business_rules.consequences: If the transaction is refinancing the lender’s same-institution debt, the lender may submit via E-Tran under non-delegated procedures. If not, the lender must process under delegated authority; processing under the wrong method increases risk of SBA rejection, post-approval repair, or lender oversight criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: The only stated exception permitting non-delegated submission by a delegated PLP-EWCP lender is when the loan refinances the lender’s same institution debt.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: Lenders with delegated PLP-EWCP authority: May only submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for approval under non-delegated procedures when the loan will refinance the Lender’s same institution debt. Except for the above, must process all loan applications using their delegated authority.

examples: A PLP-EWCP delegated lender refinances its own existing working capital line with a new EWCP line; it may submit under non-delegated procedures in E-Tran for SBA approval.
A PLP-EWCP delegated lender originates a new EWCP facility to finance export orders with no refinance of its own debt; it must process using delegated authority (not non-delegated).
A delegated lender refinances a competitor bank’s note; this is not “same institution debt,” so the lender must process under delegated authority.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing rejection/repair; SBA lender oversight finding or corrective action

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_002 governs when a delegated PLP-EWCP lender may route an EWCP application through non-delegated procedures. Rule 26_005 adds gating form requirements and states that if SBA Form 2310 is executed, the lender must process under delegated authority—functionally overriding any attempt to route non-delegated based solely on refinance circumstances.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender selects non-delegated processing without satisfying (or while violating) the executed-form constraints, SBA may reject the submission, require repair, or cite the lender in oversight reviews for improper processing channel selection.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_002 addresses when a delegated PLP-EWCP lender may use non-delegated procedures (same-institution refinance). Rule 26_004 states that after exception approval the lender must proceed under delegated authority and retain the approval email—functionally overriding the processing-method selection for that exception scenario.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender treats the exception approval as allowing non-delegated processing (or fails to keep the approval email), SBA may cite processing/documentation deficiencies and impose repair/oversight consequences.

business_rules.rule_name: Delegated SBA lenders must use the delegated-loan-applications email only for specific delegated-application questions and not for general policy questions
business_rules.description: The SBA email account 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov is restricted to lenders with delegated authority to ask questions about a specific loan application; it must not be used for general policy questions, which must instead be sent to 7aQuestions@sba.gov.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA lender with delegated authority is communicating with SBA by email about 7(a) delegated processing.
business_rules.consequences: If misrouted, inquiries may be delayed or not addressed as intended; use of the wrong channel may contribute to processing delays and potential compliance findings during SBA lender oversight if it results in improper handling of exceptions/approvals.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: The 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov email account is only for Lenders with delegated authority that have questions about a specific loan application and is not to be used for general policy questions, which should be sent to 7aQuestions@sba.gov.

examples: A delegated lender asks whether a specific borrower’s principals meet an eligibility nuance for a pending EWCP file; it may email 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov with a summary of facts.
A lender wants clarification on SOP interpretation unrelated to a specific file; it must email 7aQuestions@sba.gov rather than 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov.
A lender sends a general policy question to 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov and receives no timely response, delaying closing; this violates the stated channel restriction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; processing delays that can affect guaranty issuance

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP lender eligibility to submit depends on executed SBA Form 750 version and SBA Form 750EX or SBA Form 2310 status
business_rules.description: SBA Form 750 (Loan Guaranty Agreement (Deferred Participation)) was updated July 1, 2019. A lender with an earlier executed SBA Form 750 may submit EWCP applications only if it also has an executed SBA Form 750EX. If a lender has an earlier SBA Form 750 but no executed SBA Form 750EX, the lender must execute the July 1, 2019 version of SBA Form 750 to submit an EWCP application. All lenders with an executed July 1, 2019 version of SBA Form 750 are eligible to submit EWCP applications. Lenders with an executed SBA Form 2310 (PLP EWCP supplemental guaranty agreement) must process EWCP applications under delegated authority.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lender seeks to submit an EWCP guaranty application and must confirm appropriate guaranty agreement forms are executed (SBA Form 750, 750EX, and/or 2310) and select the correct processing authority (delegated vs non-delegated).
business_rules.consequences: If required executed forms are not in place, the lender is not eligible to submit the EWCP application for guaranty until the required form(s) are executed. If SBA Form 2310 is executed, the lender must process under delegated authority.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond the conditional allowance for earlier SBA Form 750 when SBA Form 750EX is also executed.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: SBA Form 750, “Loan Guaranty Agreement (Deferred Participation,” was updated as of July 1, 2019. Lenders that executed an earlier version of SBA Form 750, and that also have an executed SBA Form 750EX, are permitted to submit applications for an EWCP guaranty... If a Lender has an earlier version of the SBA Form 750, but no executed SBA Form 750EX, the Lender must execute a July 1, 2019, version of the SBA Form 750...

examples: Lender A has an executed SBA Form 750 dated 2018 and also has an executed SBA Form 750EX; it may submit EWCP guaranty applications.
Lender B has an executed SBA Form 750 dated 2018 but does not have SBA Form 750EX; it must execute the July 1, 2019 SBA Form 750 before submitting an EWCP application.
Lender C has executed SBA Form 2310; it must process EWCP applications under delegated authority (not non-delegated).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty application rejected or not eligible for submission; SBA lender oversight finding

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Non-delegated EWCP submissions through LGPC require E-Tran submission of the guaranty application and all attachments and SBA makes final eligibility and creditworthiness decision
business_rules.description: For EWCP loans submitted using the non-delegated process through the 7(a) Loan Guaranty Processing Center (LGPC), the lender must submit the application for guaranty and all attachments via E-Tran to SBA. SBA makes the final determination of applicant eligibility and creditworthiness, including uses of proceeds, collateral adequacy, loan structure, and equity injection, and will approve or decline the loan in E-Tran.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any EWCP loan processed under non-delegated procedures through the LGPC.
business_rules.consequences: The lender must submit the full E-Tran package (guaranty application plus all attachments). SBA—not the lender—makes the final approval/decline determination for eligibility and creditworthiness factors in the non-delegated process.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: For all loans submitted using the non-delegated process through the LGPC, the Lender must submit applications for guaranty and all attachments via E-Tran to SBA. SBA will make the final determination as to the eligibility and creditworthiness of the Applicant, including approving the uses of proceeds, the adequacy of the collateral being pledged, the structure of the loan and any equity injection...

examples: A lender sends an EWCP non-delegated request to LGPC but omits a required attachment in E-Tran; the submission is incomplete and may be delayed or declined.
In a non-delegated EWCP request, SBA determines the proposed collateral is inadequate and declines the loan in E-Tran even if the lender would have approved it internally.
SBA requires an equity injection for a non-delegated EWCP request; the lender must proceed based on SBA’s terms and conditions.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA approval denial/decline in E-Tran; SBA lender oversight finding for incomplete/incorrect submission

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_006 is explicitly scoped to EWCP loans submitted using the non-delegated process through LGPC. Rule 26_002 determines whether non-delegated submission is allowed (refinance of same-institution debt) or prohibited (must use delegated).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender incorrectly determines it can use non-delegated processing, it may submit under the wrong channel, increasing the likelihood of SBA rejection, delays, and repair/oversight criticism.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_006 requires the lender to submit the EWCP application and attachments via E-Tran under non-delegated procedures. Rule 26_005 establishes whether the lender is eligible to submit at all (correct executed versions of Form 750/750EX, and/or impacts of executing Form 2310).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Absent the required executed forms, the lender cannot validly submit a complete non-delegated EWCP package; any attempted submission may be rejected or later repaired with potential guaranty risk.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_006 requires submission of the application and all attachments via E-Tran. Rule 26_007 specifies mandatory financial statements and freshness (within 120 days) for owners/guarantors and interim business/affiliate statements, which are part of the required submission package.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If statements exceed the 120-day freshness window at time of SBA submission, the lender must obtain updates; otherwise the submission may be delayed/declined and could create oversight findings.

dependencies.rationale: Under 26_006, SBA makes final determinations on collateral adequacy/structure in the non-delegated process. Rule 22_007 defines mandatory EWCP collateral structure (at least a first security interest in transaction collateral; U.S. location). Therefore, meeting 22_007 is a prerequisite for a viable 26_006 approval outcome.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender cannot perfect the required first security interest or collateral is outside permitted locations, SBA may decline or require repair/restructuring before approval.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_006 places SBA as final decision-maker for structure/collateral in the non-delegated process. If the EWCP request supports issuance of a standby letter of credit, Rule 22_008 imposes additional collateral (cash deposit or approved ABL collateral substitution). Therefore, for those transactions, 22_008 must be satisfied to obtain approval under 26_006.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Absent the required additional collateral (or approved substitute) and documentation, SBA may require restructuring, delay approval, or decline; post-approval noncompliance can create repair exposure.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_006 prescribes requirements for non-delegated LGPC processing. Rule 26_004 instructs that, upon SBA approval of an exception for a delegated lender, the lender must proceed under delegated authority. Therefore, the non-delegated process should not be used for the exception-approved loan.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Attempting to submit the loan non-delegated after an approved delegated exception may create authority/process nonconformance, delays, and potential SBA repair exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP owners at 20% or more and proposed guarantors must provide financial statements dated within 120 days and applicant interim financials must be within 120 days of SBA submission
business_rules.description: For EWCP guaranty submissions, the lender must obtain owner financial statements (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children) and proposed guarantors (except Supplemental Guarantors). In addition, interim financial statements for the applicant and any affiliates must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when preparing an EWCP application for SBA submission and determining required financial documentation and freshness requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If the financial statements are older than 120 days at submission, the lender must obtain updated statements before submission/processing to satisfy SBA documentation requirements; failure increases risk of SBA processing delay/decline and lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental Guarantors are excepted from the owner/proposed guarantor financial statement requirement as stated.

source_reference.section_id: Non-Delegated EWCP requirements / documentation list
source_reference.source_text: Owner Financial Statements (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA, for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children), and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors... Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA.

examples: An EWCP applicant has a 25% owner whose personal financial statement is 150 days old on the planned submission date; the lender must obtain an updated statement dated within 120 days.
An EWCP applicant’s interim financials are dated 5 months before submission; the lender must collect interim financials dated within 120 days before submitting to SBA.
A proposed Supplemental Guarantor is identified; the lender does not need to collect the Supplemental Guarantor’s financial statement under this specific requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delay/decline; SBA lender oversight finding for incomplete documentation

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 small business determination uses 5 million dollars or less in gross annual revenue for preceding fiscal year
business_rules.description: For Regulation B Subpart B (CFPB Section 1071) purposes, a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year is $5,000,000 or less (subject to any rule-permitted methodology), notwithstanding SBA industry size standards. Misclassification of applicants by not applying the $5MM threshold can result in inaccurate 1071 coverage determinations and data collection/reporting errors.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the financial institution determines whether an applicant is a “small business” for Section 1071/Regulation B Subpart B coverage and data collection/reporting.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution misclassifies applicants due to not applying the $5 million threshold, it risks inaccurate 1071 coverage determinations and data collection/reporting errors, which may result in supervisory findings, mandated corrective action, and potential enforcement risk for violations of implementing regulations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt; rule states it applies notwithstanding 13 CFR 121.201 SBA size standards.

source_reference.section_id: Final § 1002.106(b)(1) Small business definition (Section-by-Section Analysis)
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.106(b)(1) further states that, notwithstanding the size standards set forth in 13 CFR 121.201, for purposes of subpart B, a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue, as defined in final § 1002.107(a)(14), for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(b)(2) Inflation Adjustment (context for $5 million threshold)
source_reference.source_text: the Bureau believes that its $5 million gross annual revenue threshold strikes an appropriate balance between covering the applications of most businesses... while minimizing the number of businesses above the SBA’s size standards whose applications will be reported to the Bureau...

examples: A business with $4.8MM gross annual revenue in its preceding fiscal year is within the small business threshold for 1071 scope (subject to other coverage elements).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding; potential public enforcement action for inaccurate 1071 data collection/reporting and related Reg B violations

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules set the same operative small business threshold for 1071 coverage: preceding fiscal year gross annual revenue of $5,000,000 or less. No differences in number, measurement period, or applicability.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_27_009 selected: more explicit interpretive framing (operative $5MM threshold notwithstanding SBA industry size standards) while preserving the same threshold value and scope.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 27_009 states the operative $5M threshold notwithstanding SBA size standards. Rule 28_002 restates the threshold and adds required adjustment every 5 years after 1/1/2025. Where adjustments take effect, the adjusted threshold governs and therefore overrides any interpretation of 27_009 as permanently fixed at $5M.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution treats $5M as permanently static after an effective CPI-U adjustment, it will mis-scope small business status and distort both applicability thresholds and reported data.

business_rules.rule_name: Small business threshold for Section 1071 is gross annual revenue of $5 million or less with CPI adjustment every 5 years rounded to $500,000
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 small business lending data collection, a business qualifies as a small business if its gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less. The $5 million gross annual revenue threshold must be adjusted every 5 years after January 1, 2025 based on changes to the CPI-U (U.S. city average, all items, not seasonally adjusted). Any adjustment must be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000 and becomes effective on January 1 of the year following the adjustment determination. Institutions must update scoping logic and retain a defensible calculation/audit trail.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether an applicant/business is a small business for Section 1071 coverage of a covered application/covered credit transaction; revenue is the preceding fiscal year gross annual revenue.
business_rules.consequences: If gross annual revenue is at or below the applicable threshold (initially $5,000,000): treat as small business for Section 1071 and apply covered-application data collection/compilation. If above: the application/transaction may fall outside Section 1071 small business scope (subject to other coverage criteria).
business_rules.exceptions: Notwithstanding SBA size standards in 13 CFR 121.201, this $5 million gross annual revenue test governs for this subpart; CPI adjustment details apply only every 5 years after 2025-01-01.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(b)(1)-(2) Small business definition; Inflation adjustment
source_reference.source_text: for purposes of this subpart, a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue... for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less. (2) Inflation adjustment. Every 5 years after January 1, 2025, the gross annual revenue threshold... shall adjust based on changes to the Consumer Price Index... Any adjustment... shall be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000... If an adjustment is to take effect... on January 1 of the following calendar year.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.106(b)(2) and comments 106(b)(2)-1 and -2 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: finalizing new § 1002.106(b)(2) to provide that every five years after January 1, 2025, the gross annual revenue threshold... shall adjust based changes to the Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers... (CPI-U). Any such adjustment will be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000... take effect... on January 1 of the following calendar year... base... is the January 2025 CPI-U... compared to... January 2030 and every five years thereafter... if publication of the CPI-U ceases... the Bureau shall substitute another reliable cost of living indicator...

examples: If an adjustment calculation yields $5.24MM, it would round to $5.0MM (nearest $500k) and may result in no threshold change depending on rounding.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and data integrity violations if institution misclassifies small business status and fails to collect/report required Section 1071 data

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules describe the same five-year CPI-U adjustment requirement (base January 2025 CPI-U, compare every five years, round to nearest $500,000, effective Jan 1 following adjustment). No differences in numeric logic or timing.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_28_002 selected: contains both (a) the base $5MM threshold statement and (b) the full CPI-U adjustment mechanics, creating a single implementable scoping rule.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA lenders must review third-party management agreements unless included in franchise disclosure documents for a Franchise Directory brand
business_rules.description: SBA lenders must review management agreements to determine whether they make the applicant an ineligible passive business. This review is required regardless of whether processing is delegated or non-delegated, unless the management agreement is part of the franchise disclosure documents (FDD) for a brand listed on the Franchise Directory.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA lender receives an application and the borrower has a management agreement with a third party; triggers at underwriting during eligibility determination for SBA 7(a) and/or 504 loans.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender fails to review the agreement, the lender may fail to identify ineligible passive businesses and risk SBA guaranty denial/repair upon oversight or purchase. If reviewed and found to grant sole discretion or otherwise create passive business status, the loan must be declined or restructured for eligibility.
business_rules.exceptions: Review requirement does not apply when the management agreement is part of the FDD for a brand listed on the Franchise Directory.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses > Management agreement review requirement
source_reference.source_text: Regardless whether processing the loan under delegated or non-delegated procedures, unless the management agreement is part of the franchise disclosure documents (FDD) for a brand listed on the Franchise Directory, SBA Lenders must review these agreements to determine whether the agreement makes the Applicant an ineligible passive business.

examples: Delegated lender processes a 7(a) request for a business with third-party operator agreement not in FDD: lender must review for passive business status.
Non-delegated lender receives an agreement embedded in FDD for a Franchise Directory brand: the 'must review' requirement in this clause does not apply (other due diligence may still be prudent).
Lender omits review and later SBA purchase review finds the agreement grants sole discretion to manager: guaranty repair/denial risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; lender oversight criticism

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB small business lending rule requires data collection by applicable tier compliance date and submission obligations by June 1 of following year
business_rules.description: Under CFPB Regulation B small business lending data collection (Section 1071), a covered financial institution must begin compiling data under §1002.107, comply with firewall requirements of §1002.108, and begin maintaining records under §1002.111 by its applicable compliance date. The institution must comply with §1002.110(c) and (d) no later than June 1 of the year after the applicable compliance date. For partial-year initial collection windows: Tier 1 institutions (compliance date October 1, 2024) must collect data October 1–December 31, 2024 and comply with §§1002.109 and 1002.110(c) and (d) by June 1, 2025; Tier 2 institutions (compliance date April 1, 2025) must collect data April 1–December 31, 2025 and comply with §§1002.109 and 1002.110(c) and (d) by June 1, 2026.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions subject to CFPB small business data collection rule; triggers based on the institution’s assigned compliance date tier and the date of covered applications.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution does not begin data compilation/firewall/recordkeeping by the compliance date or fails to meet June 1 obligations, it risks supervisory findings and CFPB enforcement (including civil money penalties) for reporting/recordkeeping violations.
business_rules.exceptions: If an institution is not a covered financial institution at the time of the compliance date, it is not required to comply for that period (see rule examples and eligibility determination outside this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002—Official Interpretations > Section 1002.114 > 114(b) Compliance date
source_reference.source_text: The applicable compliance date ... is the date by which the covered financial institution must begin to compile data ... comply with the firewall requirements ... and begin to maintain records ... In addition, the covered financial institution must comply with § 1002.110(c) and (d) no later than June 1 of the year after the applicable compliance date. ... required to collect data ... from October 1, 2024 to December 31, 2024 ... comply with §§ 1002.109 and 1002.110(c) and (d) by June 1, 2025.

examples: Tier 2 institution: starts collecting required 1071 data on April 1, 2025; ensures submission/related obligations for 2025 collection are met by June 1, 2026.
Tier 1 institution: fails to apply firewall procedures starting October 1, 2024 for covered applications; risk of CFPB violation findings.
Institution collects data for April–Dec 2025 but misses June 1, 2026 deadline for the specified obligations: reporting noncompliance exposure.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB enforcement action; civil money penalties; supervisory findings requiring remediation

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 30_010 requires the institution to begin compilation/firewall/recordkeeping by the applicable compliance date. Rule 34_001 defines when that compliance obligation begins under the general coverage test once the institution crosses the threshold.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution does not recognize the Jan 1 trigger, it may miss required start of collection, creating systematic reporting gaps.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 30_010 mandates beginning compilation on the compliance date for covered institutions. Rule 34_003 provides a specific exclusion for covered applications received before the initial compliance date (even if final action occurs after), overriding the need to apply 30_010 collection duties to that subset.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution ignores the carve-out, it may over-collect and complicate firewall/process controls; if it misapplies the carve-out, it may under-collect for post-compliance-date applications.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 30_010 outlines full compliance-date obligations for covered institutions. Rule 34_002 provides a limited-requirements regime when an institution voluntarily collects 1071 information in the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date, thereby overriding the need to comply with all other provisions for early-collected data.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution applies full requirements unnecessarily, it may increase operational burden; if it under-applies required limited provisions (e.g., firewall/recordkeeping), it risks procedural violations even during early collection.

business_rules.rule_name: Trade credit is excluded from CFPB 1071 covered credit transactions and does not include third-party financial institution financing of supplier goods
business_rules.description: For CFPB 1071 small business lending data collection, trade credit is excluded from covered credit transactions. Trade credit is defined as a financing arrangement where a business acquires goods or services from another business without making immediate payment in full. An extension of business credit by a financial institution other than the supplier to finance those items is not trade credit. Credit extended by the supplying business is not trade credit for subpart B where the supplier intends to sell or transfer its rights as a creditor to a third party, such as a financial institution.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when classifying a transaction as a covered credit transaction versus excluded trade credit for Regulation B Subpart B (1071) data collection.
business_rules.consequences: If misclassified as trade credit when it is actually a covered credit transaction (e.g., a bank loan financing inventory), the institution risks underreporting and regulatory violations. If correctly excluded, no 1071 collection/reporting is required for that transaction (though ECOA/Reg B baseline may still apply).
business_rules.exceptions: Trade credit exclusion is limited to the business providing the goods or services being financed; affiliates/facilitators that provide financing are not covered by the exclusion (as described in the final rule narrative).

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.104(b)(1) Trade Credit > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.104(b)(1) defines trade credit as a financing arrangement wherein a business acquires goods or services from another business without making immediate payment in full ... an extension of business credit by a financial institution other than the supplier ... is not trade credit, and ... credit extended by a business providing goods or services ... is not trade credit ... where the supplying business intends to sell or transfer its rights as a creditor to a third party, such as a financial institution.

examples: A supplier allows a customer to buy inventory now and pay net-60: excluded trade credit (no 1071 data required for this transaction).
A bank provides a line of credit to a retailer to purchase inventory from a supplier: not trade credit; treat as covered credit transaction if other coverage criteria met.
A supplier extends credit but immediately plans to sell the receivable to a bank: not trade credit for subpart B; may become reportable depending on structure and coverage.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding for data inaccuracies; potential civil money penalties for pattern/practice

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA E-Tran submission must list 100% direct and indirect ownership and must combine married spouses and minor children ownership percentages
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines guaranty submissions, the lender must list in E-Tran 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined when reporting ownership.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when preparing and submitting an SBA CAPLines application for guaranty in E-Tran (delegated PLP or non-delegated) and completing required E-Tran data fields related to ownership.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender fails to report 100% ownership or fails to combine married spouses/minor children ownership as required, the submission may be incomplete/incorrect, resulting in SBA processing delays, file deficiencies, or post-approval/lender oversight findings and potential guaranty repair exposure depending on impact.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > E-Tran ownership reporting
source_reference.source_text: Lender must submit to E-Tran all terms and conditions required by E-Tran data fields. Lender must list in E-Tran 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the Applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined.

examples: Applicant ownership: Spouse A 30% and Spouse B 30%—lender combines and reports 60% for the married spouses ownership grouping in E-Tran (while still documenting ownership detail in file).
Applicant has four owners totaling 90% due to missing a 10% indirect owner; lender must identify and report the missing indirect ownership so E-Tran ownership totals 100%.
Minor child holds a 5% interest and parent holds 55%; lender combines minor child ownership with the parent/spouse grouping as required for reporting.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays; SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty repair exposure depending on impact

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines require owner financial statements dated within 120 days for owners of 20% or more and proposed guarantors unless lender uses a credit score to evaluate owner financials
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines guaranty applications, the lender must obtain an owner financial statement dated within 120 days of submission to SBA for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children) and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors. If the lender uses a credit score to evaluate the owner financials, then the lender does not need to obtain owner financial statements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines applications for guaranty (file requirements). Owner threshold: 20% or more ownership. Timing: financial statement dated within 120 days of submission to SBA.
business_rules.consequences: Missing or stale owner financial statements (when required) creates an SBA documentation deficiency that can result in processing delays, adverse findings in SBA oversight, and guaranty repair exposure if it constitutes noncompliance with program requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: If the lender uses a credit score to evaluate owner financials, owner financial statements are not required per the cited text. Supplemental Guarantors are excluded from the owner financial statement requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > Owner Financial Statement requirement
source_reference.source_text: Owner Financial Statement (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA, for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children), and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors. Lenders may use SBA Form 413 or their own equivalent form... If the Lender uses a credit score to evaluate the owner financials, then the Lender does not need to obtain owner financial statements.

examples: Two owners each hold 25%: lender obtains personal financial statements dated within 120 days of SBA submission for both owners (including spouse/minor child assets if applicable).
Owner holds 19% and is not a proposed guarantor: owner financial statement not required by the 20% threshold (but may be required by lender policy).
Lender uses credit scores to evaluate owner financials: lender may omit owner financial statements, but must document credit score usage consistent with SBA requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays; SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty repair exposure

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 32_007 requires owner financial statements for all owners of 20% or more (including spouse/minor children assets). Rule 32_006 mandates that 100% direct/indirect ownership be listed in E-Tran and that married spouses/minor children ownership be combined—this determines who meets the 20% threshold.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ownership is incomplete or not combined properly, the lender may miss required owner financial statements (or request unnecessary ones), creating SBA file deficiencies and potential repair exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines require three years business financial statements or tax returns plus interim financial statements dated within 120 days and include affiliates
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines guaranty applications, the lender must obtain tax transcripts (per cross-referenced SOP section) and either business financial statements or tax returns for the last 3 years and interim financial statements for the applicant and any affiliates. Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA. The file must include year-end balance sheets for the last 3 years (with detailed debt schedule), year-end profit and loss statements for the last 3 years, and interim balance sheet (with detailed debt schedule) and interim profit and loss statement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines applications for guaranty submission. Timeframes: last 3 years year-end financials; interim financials dated within 120 days prior to submission; coverage includes applicant and any affiliates.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to obtain and retain required financial documentation and affiliate financial information may constitute SBA program noncompliance, create processing delays, increase risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial, and lead to adverse lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text (requirement is framed as required documentation for the guaranty request).

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > Financial statements/tax returns and interim statements timing
source_reference.source_text: Tax transcripts... and either business financial statements or tax returns for the last 3 years and interim financial statements for the Applicant and any affiliates. Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA... Year End Balance Sheet for the last 3 years, including detailed debt schedule; Year End Profit & Loss Statements for the last 3 years; Interim Balance Sheet, including a detailed debt schedule; and interim Profit & Loss Statement.

examples: Lender collects FY2022–FY2024 year-end balance sheets (with debt schedules) and P&Ls plus an interim statement dated 60 days before submission; meets the timing requirement.
Interim financials are dated 150 days prior to SBA submission; lender must obtain updated interim statements within 120 days to comply.
Applicant has an operating affiliate: lender must also obtain the affiliate’s interim and prior 3-year statements as required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delays; SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty repair/denial exposure

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 32_007 addresses owner/guarantor financial statements (unless credit score exception applies). Rule 32_008 addresses business and affiliate historical and interim financial statements and tax transcripts. In practice, CAPLines guaranty submission and SBA oversight expect both elements to be satisfied for a complete file (subject to exceptions).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Satisfying only one side (owners vs. business/affiliates) leaves the file incomplete, increasing processing delays and post-approval/oversight findings and repair exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 permits reliance on applicant-provided data without verification subject to Regulation B Section 1002.107(b)
business_rules.description: Under CFPB’s final rule implementing ECOA section 1071, the Bureau states that financial institutions are permitted to rely on applicant-provided data and need not verify the data, subject to the requirements of Regulation B § 1002.107(b) and its official interpretation (comment 107(b)-1). The Bureau declined to create an additional general safe harbor for reliance on applicant-provided data because the final rule already permits reliance without verification under the specified provisions.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions collecting/reporting 1071 data points when the institution receives applicant-provided data (self-reported) and is determining whether verification is required for submission.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution verifies data for its own business purposes, it must still comply with § 1002.107(b) requirements governing how data is collected/reported; failure to follow the rule may create data quality issues and expose the institution to CFPB supervisory findings or enforcement risk. The absence of a broad safe harbor means institutions should not assume immunity for inaccurate data beyond permitted reliance provisions.
business_rules.exceptions: Reliance without verification is 'subject to the requirements of § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1' (i.e., not unconditional). The cited text does not enumerate all constraints; implementers must consult § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1 for specific limitations.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.112(c) Safe Harbors > Final Rule (discussion of reliance and no verification)
source_reference.source_text: the Bureau again notes that financial institutions may rely upon applicant-provided data and need not verify such data, subject to the requirements of § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1... The Bureau believes that final § 1002.107(b) and comment 107(b)-1 strikes a reasonable balance between data quality and cost and effort incurred by lenders.

examples: Applicant reports gross annual revenue of $900,000; institution may rely on applicant-provided value without verifying, provided it follows § 1002.107(b) rules.
Institution collects applicant-reported number of workers; it may accept the applicant’s response without independent verification (subject to § 1002.107(b)).
Institution verifies a revenue figure internally for credit decisioning; it cannot assume a blanket safe harbor from 1071 accuracy obligations—must follow § 1002.107(b) requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism; potential ECOA/Reg B enforcement action for 1071 data rule violations

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 does not require precise worker counts and requires exact time in business only if the institution already obtains it
business_rules.description: In the CFPB 1071 final rule discussion of safe harbors, the Bureau states that a safe harbor for number of workers is unnecessary because § 1002.107(a)(16) permits reporting by selecting ranges rather than requiring a precise number of workers. The Bureau also states a safe harbor for time in business is unnecessary because § 1002.107(a)(17) does not require reporting of exact years in business unless the financial institution already obtains that information for its own purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions compiling and reporting 1071 data points for (a)(16) number of workers and (a)(17) time in business, particularly when determining the level of precision required for reporting.
business_rules.consequences: Institutions should implement data collection to allow selection of worker ranges rather than attempting to validate a precise headcount. Institutions are not required to report exact years in business unless they already obtain that exact information; over-collection can create operational burden and potential data inconsistency if not governed under § 1002.107 requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: If the institution already obtains exact years in business for its own purposes, then exact years may be required to be reported per § 1002.107(a)(17) as described in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.112(c) Safe Harbors > Final Rule (workers and time-in-business precision)
source_reference.source_text: final § 1002.107(a)(16) does not require the reporting of a precise numbers of workers, but rather permits the selection of ranges of numbers. Similarly, the Bureau believes that a safe harbor for the time in business data point is not necessary because final § 1002.107(a)(17) does not require the reporting of exact years in business for this data point unless the financial institution already obtains that information for its own purposes.

examples: Applicant states it employs 23 workers; institution reports using the appropriate worker range selection rather than storing/reporting '23' as a required precise value.
Institution does not track exact years in business and only collects a general 'time in business' category; it is not required to report exact years under § 1002.107(a)(17) per the cited description.
Institution already captures exact years in business in its underwriting system; it must be prepared to report the exact value for § 1002.107(a)(17) (subject to full rule requirements).
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism for misreporting or improper data precision handling under 1071

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple covered credit transaction requests at one time must be reported as separate covered applications unless only one transaction is requested
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 (Reg B Subpart B), if an applicant requests two or more covered credit transactions at one time (e.g., term loan and line of credit), the financial institution must report each request as a separate covered application. If the applicant is requesting only one covered credit transaction but is undecided between products (e.g., term loan OR line of credit), the institution reports it as a single covered application (do not split solely due to alternative product possibilities).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications under 12 CFR 1002.103(a) and reporting obligations under subpart B (1071) when multiple products/transactions are requested simultaneously.
business_rules.consequences: Incorrectly combining multiple transaction requests into one covered application, or incorrectly splitting a single request into multiple covered applications, can cause inaccurate application counts, demand measures, and fair lending analytics and may result in resubmission requirements or supervisory criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None beyond the clarified scenario where a single covered credit transaction is requested but product selection is undecided (report as one).

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-5 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: if an applicant makes a request for two or more covered credit transactions at one time, the financial institution reports each request as a separate covered application. The Bureau believes this approach furthers the purposes of section 1071 by better capturing demand for credit, including demand for different covered credit transactions

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to § 1002.103(a), comment 103(a)-5
source_reference.source_text: Assuming the requirements of a covered application are met, if an applicant makes a request for two or more covered credit transactions at the same time, the financial institution reports each request as a separate covered application. For example, if an applicant is seeking both a term loan and a line of credit and requests them both on the same application form, the financial institution reports the requests as two separate covered applications... If, on the other hand, the applicant is only requesting a single covered credit transaction, but has not decided on which particular product, the financial institution reports the request as a single covered application.

source_reference.section_id: Final comment 107(a)(5)-1 (multiple products vs alternative products)
source_reference.source_text: if an applicant requests more than one credit product at the same time, the financial institution reports each credit product requested as a separate application... changed the language... to clarify that when the applicant is seeking only one transaction but is open to alternative product types, the financial institution reports only one application.

examples: Applicant requests a $1MM term loan and a $500k line simultaneously: report two covered applications.
Applicant requests $500k of credit but is undecided between term vs line: report one covered application.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination and potential enforcement action for inaccurate 1071 reporting

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

deduplication_info.rationale: All three rules impose the same 1071 reporting logic: simultaneous requests for two or more covered credit transactions/products must be reported as separate covered applications; a single request where applicant is undecided between products is reported as one covered application. Differences are purely presentation (reporting vs MIS implementation framing).
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_reporting_33_003 selected: most detailed articulation of the split-vs-single logic and the reporting risk of incorrect combining/splitting.

business_rules.rule_name: Duplicate covered applications may be treated as a single covered application only if the institution would treat them as duplicates under its own procedures
business_rules.description: A financial institution may treat two or more duplicate covered applications as a single covered application for purposes of subpart B reporting, but only if, for credit decisioning purposes, the institution would also treat one or more of those applications as a duplicate under its own established procedures. This is a conditional permission, not a requirement, and is constrained by the institution’s internal duplication procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when more than one covered application is received that the institution identifies as duplicates based on its procedures.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution consolidates applications that it would not treat as duplicates in its own procedures, reporting may be deemed inaccurate; if it fails to consolidate true duplicates and over-reports demand, data quality and analytics may be distorted.
business_rules.exceptions: Only applicable to “true duplicates” that the institution would otherwise treat as duplicates under its own procedures.

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-8 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: The Bureau is adopting new comment 103(a)-8 to address reporting of duplicate covered applications. Under new comment 103(a)-8, a financial institution may treat two or more duplicate covered applications as a single covered application for purposes of subpart B, so long as for purposes of determining whether to extend credit, the financial institution would also treat one or more of the applications as a duplicate under its procedures.

examples: Applicant submits the same request twice due to system error; bank policy treats the second as a duplicate and does not re-underwrite: bank may report as one covered application.
Applicant submits a second application with a materially different amount or product and bank policy treats it as a new request: bank must not consolidate as a duplicate for 1071 reporting.
Applicant submits via broker and directly; bank procedures flag as duplicate and merge into one underwriting file: bank may report as one.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for inaccurate application counts and duplication handling in 1071 reporting

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Application must be reported as covered only if covered product is requested prior to final action taken
business_rules.description: If an applicant initially requests a product that is not a covered credit transaction but, prior to final action taken, decides to seek a product that is a covered credit transaction, the application becomes a covered application and must be reported under § 1002.109. Conversely, if an applicant initially requests a covered credit transaction but, prior to final action taken, decides to seek a product that is not a covered credit transaction, the application is not a covered application and must not be reported.
business_rules.conditions: Applies during the application process up to the point of final action taken when the requested product type changes between covered and non-covered credit transaction status.
business_rules.consequences: Reporting an application that becomes non-covered (or failing to report one that becomes covered prior to final action) leads to register inaccuracies and potential supervisory findings for incorrect inclusion/exclusion logic.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; the rule hinges on the timing ‘prior to final action taken.’

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-9 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 103(a)-9 provides that if an applicant initially requests a product that is not a covered credit transaction, but prior to final action taken decides to seek instead a product that is a covered credit transaction, the application is a covered application and must be reported pursuant to final § 1002.109. However, if an applicant initially requests a product that is a covered credit transaction, but prior to final action taken decides instead to seek a product that is not a covered credit transaction, the application is not a covered application and thus is not reported.

examples: Applicant starts with an inquiry that becomes a non-covered product application; before final action, applicant switches to a covered term loan: report the application as covered.
Applicant applies for a covered open-end line; before final action, applicant switches to a non-covered product: do not report it as a covered application.

Applicant applies for a covered product, receives a counteroffer for a non-covered product, and accepts the non-covered product prior to final action: do not report (consistent with the covered/non-covered outcome prior to final action).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential enforcement action for incorrect covered-application scoping in 1071 reporting

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 33_005 governs reporting when a product type changes between non-covered and covered before final action. Rule 35_002 determines that certain factoring-adjacent arrangements are covered credit transactions (other sales-based financing) and therefore can trigger 33_005’s 'becomes covered' logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 35_002 is not applied, institutions may fail to flip the reporting status when the applicant’s request effectively becomes covered credit before final action.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 33_005 requires determining whether the requested product is a covered credit transaction at different points in time. Rule 30_011 defines trade credit as excluded and clarifies when bank financing is not trade credit. That classification is necessary to decide whether a change converts the application into a covered application or out of coverage.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassifying a bank loan financing goods/services as excluded trade credit may lead to incorrect non-reporting even after the request becomes a covered credit transaction.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 33_005 depends on distinguishing covered vs non-covered products when the requested product changes prior to final action. Rule 39_001 states MCAs are covered credit transactions unless excluded, which is needed to correctly apply the flip logic.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution misclassifies MCA as non-covered, it may fail to report applications that become covered before final action, causing systematic underreporting.

business_rules.rule_name: When multiple non-affiliate business co-applicants apply, report subpart B data for only one designated small business using consistent procedures
business_rules.description: If a covered financial institution receives a covered application from multiple businesses that are not affiliates, the institution must compile, maintain, and report data under §§ 1002.107 through 1002.109 for only a single applicant that is a small business. The institution must establish consistent procedures for designating which small business is reported (e.g., first small business listed on the application form).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications with multiple business co-applicants who are not affiliates (as defined in § 1002.102(a)).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to consistently designate a single small business (or attempting to report multiple co-applicants without an approved method) can create duplication, inconsistent reporting, and potential supervisory criticism for unreliable data.
business_rules.exceptions: If co-applicants are affiliates, this commentary does not apply; affiliate handling is governed by other provisions and institution procedures consistent with the rule.

source_reference.section_id: Comment 103(a)-10 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 103(a)-10 provides that if a covered financial institution receives a covered application from multiple businesses who are not affiliates ... it shall compile, maintain, and report data pursuant to final §§ 1002.107 through 1002.109 for only a single applicant that is a small business ... A covered financial institution shall establish consistent procedures for designating a single small business for purposes of collecting and reporting data

examples: Two unrelated small businesses jointly apply for one line of credit: report data for only one designated small business under the institution’s consistent procedure.
Joint venture application lists two non-affiliate small business members as co-applicants: report for only one designated small business (e.g., first listed).
If the co-applicants are affiliates under the rule’s affiliate definition, the institution does not use this one-of-many designation approach and follows affiliate treatment rules instead.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for inconsistent co-applicant handling and 1071 reporting integrity issues

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 33_006 applies when multiple non-affiliate business co-applicants submit a covered application and requires reporting for only a single applicant that is a small business. Rule 28_002 provides the small business definition (gross annual revenue threshold) needed to determine which co-applicant(s) qualify.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If small business status is misapplied, the institution may select an ineligible applicant for reporting or inconsistently designate applicants, degrading data reliability and creating supervisory risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 coverage begins when a lender has at least 100 small business covered credit originations in each of the prior two calendar years
business_rules.description: A financial institution becomes a covered financial institution for CFPB Section 1071 when it originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years. When this two-year threshold is met for calendar years (Y-2) and (Y-1), the institution’s compliance obligation begins on January 1 of year Y; if the threshold is not met, the institution is not covered for that period and is not required to collect/report 1071 data.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution did not qualify for a specified earlier tiered compliance date but later originates enough covered credit transactions to small businesses to meet the general coverage test. Determine small business originations using the Section 1071 small business definition (gross annual revenue of $5 million or less).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution meets the 100-origination threshold in each of the two preceding calendar years, it must begin Section 1071 data collection and reporting as of January 1 of the next calendar year (its initial compliance date). If it does not meet the threshold, it is not yet required to comply under the general coverage test.
business_rules.exceptions: Tier 1/Tier 2/Tier 3 compliance schedules under § 1002.114(b)(1)-(3) may set an earlier initial compliance date for lenders that meet higher origination thresholds; this rule addresses the cross-referenced general coverage path described in § 1002.114(b)(4) via § 1002.105(b) as illustrated by the commentary examples.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, Section 1002.114(b)(4) examples (vi)-(vii)
source_reference.source_text: Because Financial Institution F originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of 2024 and 2025, it is a covered financial institution under § 1002.105(b) and is required to comply with the rule beginning January 1, 2026... Because Financial Institution G originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of 2026 and 2027... is required to comply with the rule beginning January 1, 2028.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.105(b) Covered financial institution
source_reference.source_text: Covered financial institution means a financial institution that originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years.

examples: If an institution originated 120 covered transactions in 2024 and 105 in 2025, it becomes covered on January 1, 2026.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding and potential enforcement action for failure to collect/report required Section 1071 data by the initial compliance date

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules define coverage based on originating at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years; the primary rule adds the explicit compliance start date. No conflict; the shorter rule is redundant.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REPORTING_34_001 selected: includes the same threshold test while also specifying the operational start date (Jan 1 of year Y after meeting Y-2 and Y-1).

dependencies.rationale: Rule 34_001 applies when an institution later meets the 100-origination threshold and sets the compliance obligation start (Jan 1 of year Y). To determine that it met the threshold in Y-2 and Y-1, the institution must classify which transactions were to 'small businesses' under Rule 28_002.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misclassification can shift the institution’s initial compliance date, resulting in late implementation of systems/processes and heightened supervisory risk.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 34_001 relies on correctly determining whether Y-2 and Y-1 met the 100-threshold. Rule 34_004 constrains the methodology when small business revenue information is readily accessible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution incorrectly uses estimation despite having accessible information, it may mis-time its initial compliance date and face supervisory findings.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 34_001 sets the start date of compliance obligations when threshold is met. When data needed to compute the threshold is not readily accessible, Rule 34_005 provides the estimation method used to determine whether the threshold is met in Y-2 and Y-1.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If estimation is not performed (or is undocumented/unsupported), the institution may mis-time compliance start and face supervisory remediation demands.

business_rules.rule_name: Optional early collection in the 12 months before initial Section 1071 compliance date requires only limited rule components
business_rules.description: If a financial institution voluntarily collects Section 1071 information on covered applications during the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date, it need comply only with the specified limited requirements: § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), § 1002.108, and § 1002.111(b) and (c) for the information it collects during that early-collection period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies only if the institution chooses to collect covered-application information as permitted in the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date under § 1002.114(b)(1), (2), or (3).
business_rules.consequences: During this 12-month pre-compliance collection period, the institution is not required to comply with the remaining provisions of § 1002.107 (other than (a)(18) and (19)), § 1002.109, § 1002.110, § 1002.111(a), or § 1002.114. This limits operational obligations to the listed provisions for early-collected data.
business_rules.exceptions: This rule does not require early collection; it applies only when the institution elects to collect. After the initial compliance date, the institution must comply with the full set of applicable requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(1)
source_reference.source_text: a financial institution that chooses to collect information... in the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date... need comply only with the requirements set out in §§ 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), 1002.108, and 1002.111(b) and (c)... need not comply with... § 1002.107 (other than §§ 1002.107(a)(18) and (19)), 1002.109, 1002.110, 1002.111(a), or 1002.114.

examples: A Tier 2 institution starts collecting applicant demographic responses 6 months early → it must follow only the cited limited provisions for that early-collected data, not full LAR reporting rules yet.
A Tier 1 institution performs a 9-month pilot of certain data fields → the pilot is governed only by §§ 1002.107(a)(18)-(19), 1002.108, and 1002.111(b)-(c).
An institution does not do any early collection → this rule is not applicable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding if institution collects early but fails to follow the limited applicable provisions (e.g., firewall or handling requirements referenced by the cited sections)

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

dependencies.rationale: Rule 40_009 permits collection of protected demographic information in the second year after exceeding the threshold in the first year, provided the institution complies with Subpart B requirements. Rule 34_002 governs the limited requirements applicable when the institution chooses to collect in the 12 months prior to its initial compliance date—often aligning with the 'second year' timing described in 40_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution begins early collection under 40_009 without mapping to the applicable early-collection rule set, it may omit required safeguards (firewall/recordkeeping/notices) and incur procedural violations.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 40_008 allows certain creditors (previously required to report in last five years but not currently covered) to collect protected demographic information if they comply with Subpart B requirements. When such collection occurs prior to an initial (re)compliance date, Rule 34_002’s early-collection framework informs what must be complied with for the data collected during that window.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the creditor collects under 40_008 but does not align operational controls to the early-collection framework (firewall/recordkeeping/notices), it risks creating noncompliant data handling practices.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 34_002 limits early-collection obligations to specified provisions for the information collected. Rule 40_009 conditions early demographic collection on compliance with a broader set that explicitly includes §1002.112. Implementations must reconcile whether 112 applies in the 40_009 scenario even if 34_002 would otherwise suggest it is not required in early collection.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution follows only 34_002 and omits controls required by 40_009 (e.g., 112), it may violate conditions of the early-collection permission; if it applies 40_009 broadly without need, it may overbuild controls.

business_rules.rule_name: No Section 1071 collection or reporting required for applications received before initial compliance date even if action occurs after
business_rules.description: If a covered financial institution receives a covered application before its initial compliance date, but takes final action on or after the initial compliance date, it is not required to collect Section 1071 data for that application under § 1002.107 and is not required to report it under § 1002.109.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications received prior to the institution’s initial compliance date specified in § 1002.114(b). Final action must occur on or after the compliance date.
business_rules.consequences: The institution may exclude the application from Section 1071 data collection and from submission to the Bureau, even though the decision occurs after the compliance date.
business_rules.exceptions: If the application is received on or after the initial compliance date, full data collection and reporting requirements apply. This transition relief is limited to the timing scenario described.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(2)
source_reference.source_text: If a covered financial institution receives a covered application... prior to its initial compliance date... but takes final action on or after that date, the financial institution is not required to collect data... pursuant to § 1002.107 nor to report... pursuant to § 1002.109. For example... compliance date of October 1, 2024... receives an application on September 15, 2024... final action... October 5, 2024... not required to collect... nor to report...

examples: Compliance date 2024-10-01; application received 2024-09-15; final action 2024-10-05 → not reportable under Section 1071.
Compliance date 2026-01-01; application received 2025-12-30; final action 2026-01-10 → not reportable under Section 1071.
Compliance date 2026-01-01; application received 2026-01-02; final action 2026-01-10 → must be collected and reported.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding for improper inclusion/exclusion logic leading to inaccurate LAR submissions

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Readily accessible small business status exists when lender can determine whether gross annual revenue is 5 million dollars or less from ordinary-course data
business_rules.description: A financial institution has readily accessible information needed to determine whether its covered credit originations were to small businesses if, in the ordinary course of business, it collects precise gross annual revenue, collects information sufficient to determine whether an applicant had gross annual revenues of $5 million or less, or collects and reports similar data to Federal or State agencies under other legal requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining whether the institution meets origination thresholds for coverage/compliance timing and must determine which originations were to small businesses as defined in § 1002.106 (gross annual revenue $5 million or less).
business_rules.consequences: If information is readily accessible, the institution is expected to use that information (rather than estimation shortcuts intended for institutions without accessible data) to determine small business status for originations when calculating applicability thresholds.
business_rules.exceptions: This definition is illustrative (“if, for instance”) and does not limit other fact patterns that may qualify as readily accessible; institutions should document their basis for concluding data is readily accessible.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(3)
source_reference.source_text: . A financial institution does not ha ve readily accessible the information needed to determine whether its originations of covered credit transactions were for small businesses as defined in § 1002.106 if it did not in the ordinary course of business collect either precise or approximate information

examples: Bank captures exact gross annual revenue for every business borrower in its LOS → it has readily accessible information for threshold calculations.
Nonbank lender stores a field that flags whether applicant revenue is ≤ $5,000,000 → readily accessible for small business determination.
Credit union reports comparable revenue metrics to a state program and retains those records in a searchable system → readily accessible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for unsupported applicability determinations and resulting failure to comply on time

examples: State-chartered commercial bank with $8B in assets and elevated CRE concentration
Federally regulated thrift with growing construction and development exposure

business_rules.rule_name: Reasonable estimation of small business originations may annualize one quarter of self-reported revenue data by multiplying by four for both 2022 and 2023
business_rules.description: When a financial institution lacks readily accessible information to determine small business status for originations, it may use reasonable, documented estimation methods. One permitted example methodology is: during October 1 through December 31, 2023, ask every applicant (prior to closing approved transactions) to self-report whether preceding fiscal year gross annual revenue was $5 million or less; determine small business originations in that quarter; annualize by quadrupling the quarter’s small business originations; and apply the annualized figure to both calendar years 2022 and 2023 for purposes of determining compliance tier/date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies for estimating 2022 and 2023 small business originations when the institution does not have readily accessible revenue information. The method requires collection of self-reported revenue status in the period October 1 through December 31, 2023, and annualization by multiplying by 4.
business_rules.consequences: If used, the resulting annualized originations count is used to determine the institution’s tier and initial compliance date. The institution must maintain written documentation supporting the estimation methodology and results.
business_rules.exceptions: Other reasonable and documented methods are allowed, including assuming every covered credit transaction to a business customer in 2022 and 2023 was to a small business. The listed methods are non-exclusive.

source_reference.section_id: Supplement I to Part 1002, 114(c)(5)(i)-(iii)
