business_rules.exceptions: Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects and Projects for Small Manufacturers have different maximums described separately.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Debenture Limits (Page 351)
source_reference.source_text: the eligible administrative costs . The Gross Debenture is subject to the following dollar limits and conditions: The minimum dollar amount for a debenture is $25,000. 13 CFR § 120.930(b) For a ll 504 Projects except for Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects and Projects for Small Manufacturers , the gross debenture is limited to an outstanding

examples: Borrower group has $4.8MM outstanding SBA 504 gross debentures; requests new $400k gross debenture (non-energy, non-manufacturer) → would exceed $5.0MM aggregate; not eligible unless reduced.
Applicant requests gross debenture $20,000 → below $25,000 minimum; not eligible as structured.
Applicant has $4.7MM SBA exposure comprised of 504 + 7(a) guaranties; requests new $500k 504 gross debenture (non-exempt) → total $5.2MM; must reduce/decline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing denial; post-approval eligibility exception findings

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: Gross Debenture min/max limits are applied after confirming the deal is a valid SBA 504 request for an eligible applicant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If eligibility fails, the loan cannot be processed as SBA 504 even if the debenture size is within limits.

business_rules.rule_name: Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects debenture limit $5,500,000 per project and $16,500,000 aggregate per borrower group
business_rules.description: For Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects, the maximum Gross Debenture is $5,500,000 per eligible project. Additionally, the outstanding Gross Debentures issued for a small business concern (including affiliates) for Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects must not exceed $16,500,000 in the aggregate. The $5,500,000 per-project limit is not reduced by other outstanding SBA loan guaranties through other SBA loan programs, and energy projects do not reduce the $5,000,000 limit for other 504 projects.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the SBA 504 project qualifies as an Eligible Energy Public Policy Project under SOP criteria referenced in the debenture limit section.
business_rules.consequences: If the gross debenture for an energy project exceeds $5,500,000, or if the borrower group’s aggregate outstanding energy-project gross debentures exceed $16,500,000, the project is not eligible as structured and must be reduced/restructured/declined.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated beyond category-specific treatment (not reduced by other SBA guaranties; energy projects do not reduce non-energy 504 limits).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Eligible Energy Public Policy Projects (Page 351-352)
source_reference.source_text: for other 504 Projects. Proje cts for Small Manufactur ers $5,500,000 maximum for each eligible project , defined as a business with its primary NAICS Code in Sectors 31, 32, and 33, and

examples: Energy project request gross debenture $5.7MM → exceeds $5.5MM per project; must reduce or restructure.
Borrower group has $16.2MM outstanding energy-project gross debentures; requests new $500k energy debenture → aggregate $16.7MM; not eligible unless reduced.
Borrower group has $5MM non-energy 504 exposure; requests $5.5MM energy debenture → allowed if energy-eligible and within $16.5MM energy aggregate (and per-project limit met).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing denial; post-approval eligibility exception findings

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 8_006 sets the general 504 caps; BR 8_007 replaces/augments these caps when the project qualifies as an Eligible Energy Public Policy Project (per-project and aggregate energy limits, and interaction rules with other SBA exposure).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the energy classification branch is missed or misapplied, debenture sizing may violate SBA maximums, forcing restructure/decline and risking SBA processing denial.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 maturity must be 10, 20, or 25 years and third-party loan minimum term must be 7 or 10 years depending on 504 maturity
business_rules.description: SBA 504 loan maturity must be 10, 20, or 25 years based on remaining useful life of the property financed, including: maximum 25 years for real estate and minimum 10 years for machinery and equipment; if both real estate and equipment are financed, maturity is based on the asset category that constitutes the majority of loan proceeds. Additionally, the Third Party Loan must have a term of at least 7 years when the 504 loan term is 10 years, and at least 10 years when the 504 loan term is 20 or 25 years; if multiple Third Party Loans exist, an overall maturity must be calculated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 projects at origination/structuring when setting the 504 debenture maturity and Third Party Loan term(s).
business_rules.consequences: If maturities do not comply (e.g., Third Party Loan term too short), the loan structure is not SBA-compliant and must be modified before approval/closing.
business_rules.exceptions: If more than one Third Party Loan exists, overall maturity is calculated considering maturities and amounts of each loan; balloon payments must be justified and identified (additional requirement stated in source).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Loan Maturities (Page 352)
source_reference.source_text:
Maturity of the 504 loan is 10 , 20, or 25  years based upon the remaining useful
life of the property being financed as follows :
i. A maximum of 25 years for real estate;
ii. A min imum of 10 years for machinery and equipment; ...
A Third Party Loan must have a term of at least 7 years when the 504 loan is for a
term of 10 years and 10 years when the 504 loan is for 20  or 25  years.

examples: 504 loan term 20 years → Third Party Loan term must be at least 10 years; a 7-year bank loan would be noncompliant.
Project proceeds majority to real estate → 504 term can be up to 25 years; structure sets 25-year debenture and 10-year third-party loan → compliant (meets minimum).
Mixed project with majority equipment proceeds → 504 term must be at least 10 years; a proposed 7-year 504 maturity would be noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing/closing deficiency; required restructuring prior to approval/closing

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: You must first be in-scope of SBA 504 eligibility to apply maturity selection logic (10/20/25 years) and Third Party Loan term minimums.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the borrower/project is not eligible, maturity structuring cannot cure eligibility and the SBA structure must be abandoned/reworked.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinancing without expansion requires qualified debt with 75 percent eligible fixed asset use and 6-month seasoning
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 refinancing projects that do not involve an expansion, the debt being refinanced must meet the definition of “Qualified Debt.” Qualified Debt is a commercial loan where (1) substantially all (75% or more) of proceeds were used to acquire Eligible Fixed Assets and the remaining amount (25% or less) was incurred for the benefit of the small business, including where the original loan was later refinanced and the existing debt is the most recent refinancing; (2) the debt was incurred not less than 6 months prior to the application date; (3) it was incurred for the benefit of the small business seeking refinancing; and (4) it has been secured by Eligible Fixed Assets for at least 6 months.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a CDC submits an SBA 504 application for a Refinancing Project of Qualified Debt that does not involve expansion (13 CFR § 120.882(g); SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1).
business_rules.consequences: If any Qualified Debt criterion is not met (e.g., proceeds usage below 75% for Eligible Fixed Assets, or debt incurred <6 months before application, or collateral not secured for at least 6 months), the refinancing is not eligible under this provision and the CDC/lender must not proceed as structured.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 75%/25% proceeds and 6-month incurred/secured requirements; these are definitional eligibility requirements.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1: 504 Eligibility through Submission of Application — Definitions — Qualified Debt
source_reference.source_text: “Qualified Debt” means a commercial loan that: a) Either: i) Substantially all (75% or more) of the proceeds of the existing debt was used to acquire an Eligible Fixed Asset(s) and the remaining amount (25% or less) was incurred for the benefit of the small business seeking refinancing; or ii) If the Eligible Fixed Asset(s) was originally financed through a commercial loan ... (a) Substantially all (75% or more) of the proceeds of the original loan was used to acquire an Eligible Fixed Asset(s) ... and (b) The existing debt is the most recent refinancing ... b) Was incurred not less than 6 months prior to the date of application. ... d) Has been secured by Eligible Fixed Asset(s) for at least 6 months.

examples: Eligible: Existing bank note from 10 months ago used 82% to buy building and 18% for business benefit; collateral has been the building for 10 months.
Ineligible: Existing loan incurred 4 months before the 504 application date, even if it financed eligible real estate.
Ineligible: Original loan qualified, but the debt presented for refinancing is not the most recent refinancing of the original loan.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair risk due to ineligible use/eligibility noncompliance; regulatory criticism in SBA lender oversight

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: Qualified Debt testing for 504 refinance-without-expansion presumes the applicant is an eligible SBA borrower (operating, for-profit, etc.) at application and through closing/disbursement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core eligibility is not met/maintained, the loan is ineligible for SBA support regardless of whether Qualified Debt criteria are satisfied.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinancing of federally guaranteed debt requires 10 business day lender notice and payment reduction unless D FA exception
business_rules.description: Qualified Debt for SBA 504 refinancing may include debt subject to a federal guarantee only if: (1) for an existing 504 loan, both the Third Party Loan and the 504 loan are being refinanced or the Third Party Loan has been paid in full; (2) the CDC has notified the existing CDC or 7(a) Lender in writing (letter or email) no less than 10 business days to advise them the existing loan is being refinanced; (3) the refinancing provides a “substantial benefit” to the borrower, meaning the portion of the new installment attributable to the refinanced debt must be less than the existing installment amount(s). In calculating percentage reduction, prepayment penalties, financing fees, and other financing costs must be added to the amount being refinanced, but not Eligible Business Expenses. The CDC may request D/FA or designee approval for an exception to the reduction requirement for good cause; PCLP CDCs may not use delegated authority to approve a loan requiring this exception.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA 504 Refinancing Project includes debt subject to a federal guarantee (including SBA-guaranteed debt) as part of the Qualified Debt being refinanced.
business_rules.consequences: If required written notice is not sent at least 10 business days in advance, or substantial benefit/payment reduction test is not met without a D/FA exception, the refinancing is not eligible as structured and should not be submitted/approved under the standard process.
business_rules.exceptions: D/FA or designee may approve an exception to the reduction requirement for good cause; PCLP CDCs may not approve this exception under delegated authority.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 — Qualified Debt definition — federally guaranteed debt and substantial benefit
source_reference.source_text: May include debt subject to a federal guarantee under these conditions: i) An existing 504 loan if both the Third Party Loan and the 504 loan are being refinanced or the Third Party Loan has been paid in full, and ii) The CDC has notified the existing CDC or 7(a) Lender in writing by letter or email no less than 10 business days to advise them the existing loan is being refinanced. iii) The refinancing of a Federally-guaranteed loan provided a substantial benefit ... “Substantial benefit” will mean that the portion of the new installment amount attributable to the debt being refinanced must be less than the existing installment amount(s). ... The CDC may request the D/FA or designee to approve an exception ... PCLP CDCs may not use their delegated authority to approve a loan requiring this exception.

examples: Eligible: CDC emails existing 7(a) lender 12 business days before closing; new payment attributable to refinanced SBA debt is lower after adding prepayment penalty to refinanced amount.
Ineligible: CDC sends notice 5 business days before closing and proceeds without cure; fails the 10 business day requirement.
Eligible with exception: Payment reduction is not achieved but CDC documents good cause and obtains D/FA or designee approval; the CDC is not a PCLP CDC using delegated authority for the exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair risk; SBA lender oversight finding; required corrective action and potential indemnification

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 9_002 is a conditional branch: if the Qualified Debt includes federally-guaranteed debt, additional notice/substantial benefit tests are required.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the base Qualified Debt framework is not met, the special federally-guaranteed debt conditions do not cure eligibility; the refinance cannot proceed under these provisions.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinancing eligible business expenses limited to operating expenses incurred but unpaid or due within 18 months and exclude personal charges
business_rules.description: When an SBA 504 Refinancing Project includes Eligible Business Expenses (EBE), EBE are limited to Operating Expenses or Other Secured Debt as defined by SOP. Operating Expenses are expenses incurred but not paid prior to the 504 application date or that will become due for payment within 18 months after the application date (e.g., salaries, rent, utilities, inventory, and other non-capital expenditures). Business lines of credit and business credit cards may be included as EBE only if: proceeds are not used to cover any personal expenses; if personal expenses occurred, the Applicant must identify and deduct them from the amount to be refinanced as EBE; the line/credit card is in the name of the small business; and the Applicant and CDC certify in the application that the refinanced debt was incurred exclusively for EBE.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinancing applications where the project includes refinancing of Eligible Business Expenses, including business lines of credit or business credit cards as EBE.
business_rules.consequences: If EBE includes capital expenditures, personal expenses, expenses outside the timing window, or the required certifications are not obtained, those amounts must be excluded from the refinancing project and the application may be declined or restructured.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; personal expense components must be deducted, not refinanced as EBE.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 — Eligible Business Expenses definition
source_reference.source_text: “Eligible Business Expenses (EBE)” are payments of the business for either Operating Expenses or Other Secured Debt that: a) Are limited to the “Operating Expenses” which are expenses of the business that were incurred but not paid prior to the date of the 504 application or that will become due for payment within 18 months after the date of application. Examples include salaries, rent, utilities, inventory ... b) Business Lines of credit and business credit cards may be included, if: i) Loan proceeds are not used to cover any personal expenses; ii) ... identify which purchases were for personal expenses and deduct that amount ... iii) ... in the name of the small business; and iv) The Applicant and the CDC certify ... incurred exclusively for EBE.

examples: Eligible: Applicant refinances $120,000 of unpaid rent and utilities incurred before application and payable within the next 6 months.
Partially eligible: Business credit card balance includes $3,000 of personal travel; applicant identifies and deducts $3,000 so only business EBE are refinanced.
Ineligible: Applicant seeks to include $80,000 of equipment purchases as EBE (capital expenditures).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA repair/denial risk for ineligible use of proceeds; SBA oversight finding requiring cure and possible guaranty denial on purchase

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 9_003 permits EBE categories but BR 9_004 supplies mandatory eligibility constraints for the 'Other Secured Debt' subset; without BR 9_004, EBE cannot be validated.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR 9_004 conditions are not met, the 'Other Secured Debt' portion must be excluded, which can change project size and invalidate LTV calculations and approvals.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinancing other secured debt requires same collateral for 6 months and borrower must be current 12 months with no payment over 30 days past due
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 refinancing, “Other Secured Debt” included as Eligible Business Expenses must be debt incurred prior to the 504 application that is secured by the same Eligible Fixed Assets securing the Qualified Debt and incurred for the benefit of the Borrower and/or Operating Company; it does not include debt for capital expenditures. Existing liens must be released or subordinated to the amount of the debt being refinanced by the 504 loan. The debt must have been secured for at least 6 months prior to the application date by the same Eligible Fixed Assets. The Borrower must have been current on all payments due for not less than 12 months preceding the application date; “current” means no payment more than 30 days past due from original or modified payment terms (including deferments), and any such modification must have been agreed to in writing by the Borrower and existing lender not less than one year prior to the application date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA 504 refinancing project includes refinancing of Other Secured Debt as part of Eligible Business Expenses.
business_rules.consequences: If the debt has not been secured by the same Eligible Fixed Assets for at least 6 months, if liens are not released/subordinated, or if the borrower has had payments more than 30 days past due in the preceding 12 months (as defined), the Other Secured Debt component is ineligible and must be excluded; the refinancing project may be declined or restructured.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; definitional eligibility criteria. Modified payment terms count only if agreed to in writing at least one year prior to application.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 — Eligible Business Expenses — Other Secured Debt
source_reference.source_text: “Other Secured Debt” is debt incurred prior to the 504 loan application that has been secured by the same Eligible Fixed Assets securing the Qualified Debt ... Other Secured Debt does not include debt incurred for the purposes of capital expenditures, and any existing liens must be released or subordinated ... i) This debt must be secured for at least 6 months prior to the date of application by the same Eligible Fixed Asset(s) ... and ii) The Borrower has been current on all payments due for not less than 12 months ... “Current on all payments due” means that no payment was more than 30 days past due ... modified payment terms ... agreed to in writing ... not less than one year prior to the date of application.

examples: Eligible: Equipment loan secured by the same building as the Qualified Debt for 14 months; borrower has had no payments over 30 days past due in the last 12 months.
Ineligible: Borrower had a payment 45 days late 6 months ago; not “current on all payments due” for the preceding 12 months.
Ineligible: Loan modification/deferment was agreed in writing 6 months before application; modification must be at least one year prior to application to be counted under the definition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA repair/denial risk for ineligible refinancing; SBA oversight criticism and corrective action

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA core eligibility requires applicant operating business and organized for profit at application through closing and disbursement
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) and 504 loans, the applicant must meet all core program eligibility requirements at the time of application and throughout loan closing and disbursement. Core requirements include that the applicant is an Operating Business (except for Eligible Passive Companies) and is organized for profit; nonprofit businesses are not eligible, though for-profit subsidiaries of nonprofits may be eligible. Program eligibility should be determined as early as possible in the application process and properly documented.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA 7(a) and SBA 504 loans during underwriting, closing, and disbursement.
business_rules.consequences: If the applicant is not an operating business (as required) or is not organized for profit, the loan is ineligible for SBA guaranty and should not be approved/disbursed as an SBA loan; lender/CDC faces guaranty denial/repair risk and oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligible Passive Companies are an exception to the Operating Business requirement (not detailed in this excerpt). For-profit subsidiaries of nonprofits may be eligible.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements
source_reference.source_text: The Applicant must meet all core requirements at the time of application and throughout loan closing and disbursement. ... A. OPERATING BUSINESS 13 CFR § 120.100(a) The Applicant must be an Operating Business (except for Eligible Passive Companies (EPCs)). B. ORGANIZED FOR PROFIT 13 CFR § 120.100(b) All Applicants must be organized for profit. Non-profit businesses are not eligible for SBA business loan assistance. For-profit subsidiaries of non-profits may be eligible.

examples: Ineligible: A nonprofit charity applies directly for an SBA 7(a) loan; it is not organized for profit.
Eligible (subject to other requirements): A for-profit subsidiary of a nonprofit applies for financing for its operating activities.
Ineligible: A holding entity with no operations applies as borrower without meeting EPC structure requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA lender oversight finding and corrective action plan

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_9_006 requires operating business/for-profit eligibility throughout application/closing/disbursement. BR_30_003 provides a concrete test (management agreements) that can render the business ineligible as passive, thereby validating the BR_9_006 determination.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If management agreements are not reviewed, the lender may incorrectly certify SBA program eligibility and face guaranty denial/repair and oversight findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Working Capital CAPLine refinancing same institution short-term revolving debt cannot use delegated authority and default within 90 days triggers loss shift presumption
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Working Capital CAPLines, a lender may not use delegated authority to refinance same institution debt; such refinancing must be processed under non-delegated authority. CAPLine proceeds may refinance existing short-term revolving debt only if: the short-term revolving debt is terminated after payoff; the borrower has a borrowing base or collateral sufficient to support the Working Capital CAPLine plus any other short-term debt not being refinanced; and the lender must not use proceeds to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss (no shifting potential loss to SBA). If the application includes refinancing of same-institution short-term revolving debt and the applicant defaults on the SBA-guaranteed CAPLine within 90 days of initial disbursement, there will be a presumption that proceeds were used to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss and SBA may deny liability on the guaranty.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) CAPLines (Working Capital CAPLine) applications that include refinancing of existing short-term revolving debt, particularly same-institution debt.
business_rules.consequences: If processed under delegated authority when same-institution debt is refinanced, the loan is noncompliant; if default occurs within 90 days, SBA may presume an improper loss shift and may deny liability on the guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the 90-day presumption; it is a presumption tied to same-institution short-term revolving debt refinancing. Separate non-delegated procedures apply when refinancing same-institution SBA-guaranteed short-term revolving debt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Debt Refinancing under Working Capital CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: a) ... a Lender may not use delegated authority to refinance same institution debt. Refinancing same institution debt must be processed under non-delegated authority. b) Loan proceeds may not be used to pay a creditor ... in a position to sustain a loss. ... c) Proceeds from a Working Capital CAPLine may refinance existing short-term revolving debt under the following conditions: i) The short-term revolving debt must be terminated after it is paid off ... ii) ... borrowing base or collateral sufficient ... iii) If the application includes the refinancing of same-institution short-term revolving debt, and if the Applicant defaults ... within 90 days ... there will be a presumption ... and SBA may deny liability on the guaranty

examples: Noncompliant processing: A PLP lender refinances its own existing operating LOC into a Working Capital CAPLine using delegated authority; must be non-delegated.
Eligible structure: Borrower’s existing short-term revolver at another bank is paid off and terminated; borrower demonstrates sufficient borrowing base for new CAPLine.
Guaranty denial risk: Same-institution revolver is refinanced; borrower defaults 60 days after first disbursement—SBA may presume prohibited loss shift and deny liability.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of liability on guaranty; SBA lender oversight finding; potential indemnification demands

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Standard 7(a) refinancing must reduce installment payment by at least 10 percent including escalating payment structures
business_rules.description: For Standard 7(a) debt refinancing (loans greater than $350,000), the new installment payment amount must be at least 10% less than the existing installment amount(s). If the refinanced note has an escalating payment structure, the new installment amount must be at least 10% less than the expected installment amount within the next 12 months. If other debt is refinanced at the same time, it may be included in the installment payment improvement calculation. Certain debt types are excepted from the 10% improvement requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Standard 7(a) loan (loan amount > $350,000) is used to refinance debt.
business_rules.consequences: If the 10% installment payment improvement test is not met and no stated exception applies, the refinancing use of proceeds is not compliant with SOP requirements and may result in SBA guaranty denial/repair or non-approval.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception applies for: demand notes or balloon payment debt (short-term or long-term), credit card obligations and HELOC used for business-related purposes, and revolving lines of credit (short-term or long-term) where the original lender is unable/unwilling to renew the line or the Applicant is restructuring financing to obtain a lower interest rate or longer term.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1: Standard 7(a) Loans — Ten Percent Improvement to Installment Payment Amount
source_reference.source_text: Ten Percent Improvement to Installment Payment Amount. When refinancing debt, the new installment payment amount must be at least 10 percent less than the existing installment amount(s)... If the note terms include an escalating payment structure, the new installment amount must be at least 10 percent less than the expected installment amount within the next 12 months.

examples: Existing term debt monthly payment is $10,000. New SBA refinance payment is $9,000 (10% reduction). Condition satisfied.
Existing payment is $10,000 but escalates to $11,000 within 12 months. New payment must be ≤ $9,900 (10% less than $11,000) to satisfy the rule.
Refinancing a balloon note: lender documents balloon structure and treats as exception (10% improvement test not required).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; non-delegated application deficiency; regulatory criticism in SBA lender review

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: The 10% payment improvement test applies only to eligible Standard 7(a) refinancing transactions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core eligibility is not met, passing the 10% test does not make the loan SBA-eligible.

dependencies.rationale: BR 10_001 is a quantitative SOP test; BR 10_002 requires narrative and itemization that substantiates the refinance decision and guards against loss-shift/refinancing ineligible debt.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If documentation is missing, even if the payment test is met, the file is deficient and may be denied/delayed or subject to guaranty repair/denial post-closing.

business_rules.rule_name: Standard 7(a) refinancing file must include written analysis and supporting documentation including itemization of creditors paid 10,000 dollars or more
business_rules.description: When refinancing debt with a Standard 7(a) loan, the loan application must include a written analysis addressing: (a) why the debt was incurred; (b) factors supporting that refinancing will not pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss; (c) reason for restructuring; (d) factors showing debt is not currently on reasonable terms; (e) how new loan improves financial condition; (f) itemization of all debts repaid when individual creditor is paid $10,000 or more and/or SBA loan number and amount of any SBA debt refinanced; and (g) reasons lender believes debt no longer meets borrower needs. Lenders must retain copies of notes and related evidence of debt being refinanced and, for non-delegated processing, include such documentation with the application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a Standard 7(a) loan is used for debt refinancing (loan amount > $350,000).
business_rules.consequences: Missing analysis, missing itemization threshold documentation, or failure to retain/include underlying debt evidence creates an SOP documentation deficiency that can result in processing delays, SBA denial, or post-closing guaranty repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the documentation requirements (other than 'when applicable' for credit card statement copy).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1: Standard 7(a) Loans — Refinancing documentation requirements
source_reference.source_text: When refinancing debt, the loan application must include: i. A written analysis that addresses... f) An itemization of all debts being repaid by loan proceeds when the individual creditor is to be paid $10,000 or more... ii. Supporting documentation for each debt to be refinanced: Lenders are required to: a) Retain copies of the note(s) being refinanced... If submitting to LGPC for non-delegated processing, Lenders must include this documentation with the application.

examples: Refinance pays Creditor A $8,500 and Creditor B $25,000. File must itemize Creditor B (≥$10,000) and retain both underlying notes.
Refinancing includes an existing SBA loan. Application must list the SBA loan number and dollar amount being refinanced.
Non-delegated submission to LGPC: lender includes copies of refinanced notes and security agreements with the application package.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing hold/decline; SBA guaranty repair/denial in purchase review

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Complete change of ownership must include at least 10 percent equity and seller debt counts only if full standby for life of SBA loan and limited to half of required injection
business_rules.description: For changes of ownership resulting in a new owner (complete change of ownership), SBA considers a minimum equity injection of at least 10% of total project costs necessary. Total project costs include all costs required to complete the change of ownership regardless of source of funds, except for lines of credit and 504 loans. Seller debt cannot be counted as equity injection unless it is on full standby for the life of the SBA loan and does not exceed one-half of the required equity injection.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions that result in a new owner (complete change of ownership).
business_rules.consequences: If the equity injection threshold is not met, or seller financing is improperly counted (not full standby for life and/or >50% of required injection), the loan fails SBA equity rules and may be declined or subject to SBA guaranty denial/repair.
business_rules.exceptions: Loans to ESOPs purchasing a controlling interest are not subject to SBA equity injection requirement (covered in separate rule context); business expansion with same 6-digit NAICS, identical ownership, and same geographic area may avoid minimum equity injection (as described in SOP note).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2: 7(a) Small & SBA Express — Changes of ownership (complete change) equity injection and seller debt standby
source_reference.source_text: requirement for equity injection. (b) Resulting in a new owner (complete change of ownership): At a minimum, SBA considers an equity injection of at least 10 percent of total project costs (all costs required to complete the change of ownership, rega rdless of the source of funds , except for lines of credit

examples: Purchase price/project costs are $1,000,000. Required equity is at least $100,000. Seller note can count only up to $50,000 and only if on full standby for life of SBA loan.
Borrower contributes $80,000 cash and proposes $20,000 seller note not on standby. Equity injection counted is $80,000 (fails 10% minimum).
Business expansion case within same 6-digit NAICS, identical ownership, same geographic area, and co-borrowers: lender documents as expansion and no minimum equity injection is required per SOP note.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA credit policy noncompliance finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 10_004 allows limited counting of seller debt as injection only if standby conditions are met; BR 10_006 defines the mandatory full-standby terms, use of SBA Form 155/equivalent, note attachment, and subordination/no-action provisions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If standby debt is improperly documented, the injection is overstated, creating an equity shortfall and SBA noncompliance with likely guaranty repair/denial exposure.

dependencies.rationale: BR 10_004 is an equity standard within a COI deal; BR 14_007 is a hard structural eligibility rule for COI transactions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR 14_007 fails, satisfying BR 10_004 injection thresholds does not make the transaction SBA-eligible; restructure is required.

business_rules.rule_name: Partner buyout financing above 90 percent requires 24-month active participation certification and pre-transaction debt-to-worth no greater than 9 to 1 or cash contribution
business_rules.description: If a 7(a) Small Loan finances more than 90% of the purchase price of a partner buyout, the lender must confirm and retain borrower certification that remaining owner(s) actively participated in operations and held the same or increasing ownership interest for at least the past 24 months, and must document that the business balance sheets for the most recent completed fiscal year and current quarter reflect a debt-to-worth ratio no greater than 9:1 prior to the ownership change. If the lender cannot document both requirements, the remaining owner(s) must contribute cash sufficient to reflect a debt-to-worth ratio no greater than 9:1 on the current-quarter balance sheet prior to the change, or at least 10% of the purchase price (per purchase and sale agreement), whichever is less.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to 7(a) Small Loans used for a change of ownership structured as a partner buyout when SBA loan financing exceeds 90% of the purchase price.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are unmet and required cash contribution not provided, transaction is not compliant with SBA ownership-change equity/financial condition standards and may be declined or subject to SBA guaranty denial/repair.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited paragraph for partner buyouts when >90% financed; separate rules apply for other ownership-change types.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2: 7(a) Small & SBA Express — Partner buyout requirements (>90% financed)
source_reference.source_text: If the 7(a) Small Loan will finance more than 90% of the purchase price of a partner buyout... held the same or an increasing ownership interest... for at least the past 24 months... balance sheets... reflect a debt-to-worth ratio of no greater than 9:1... If the Lender is unable to document... the remaining owner(s) must contribute cash... or... at least 10% of the purchase price... whichever is less.

examples: Purchase price $200,000; SBA loan finances $190,000 (95%). Owners certify 24-month participation and balance sheet shows 8:1 debt-to-worth; acceptable.
SBA loan finances 92% but lender cannot document debt-to-worth ratio. Owners contribute cash to bring ratio to 9:1 (or $20,000 (10%) whichever is less).
Lender obtains certification but balance sheets show 12:1 debt-to-worth and no cash contribution is made to correct; loan is noncompliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA lender oversight finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 10_005 applies to partner buyouts (a COI form). BR 14_007 ensures the business is borrower/co-borrower and the COI promotes preservation/sound development, establishing the deal is in-scope before applying BR 10_005’s >90% financing constraints.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR 14_007 is not met, satisfying BR 10_005 certifications/ratios cannot create SBA eligibility for the partner buyout.

business_rules.rule_name: Standby debt may count as equity only with full standby for term of 7(a) loan using SBA Form 155 or equivalent and creditor subordination of liens
business_rules.description: Debt may be treated as equity injection for SBA purposes only if it is on full standby for the term of the 7(a) loan (no payments of principal or interest for the term). The lender must use SBA Form 155 (or an equivalent standby agreement) and attach a copy of the standby note. Standby debt may accrue interest and be added to the standby balance and amortized only after the 7(a) loan is paid in full. The standby creditor must subordinate any lien rights in collateral to the lender and agree to restrictions consistent with SBA standby requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the borrower proposes standby agreements as a source of equity injection for 7(a) equity requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If standby debt is not fully standby for the entire 7(a) loan term, lacks required form/documentation, or lacks lien subordination/no-action provisions, it cannot be counted as equity injection, potentially causing equity shortfall and SBA noncompliance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited paragraph.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2: 7(a) Small & SBA Express — Source of equity injection: Standby agreements
source_reference.source_text: debt that is on full standby (no payments of principal or interest for the term of the 7(a) loan) may be considered as equity for SBA’s purposes. Lender must use SBA Form 155 or its own equivalent Standby Agreement form, and a copy of the note

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Equity Injection — Standby Agreements (iii)(a)
source_reference.source_text: Standby Agreements - only debt that is on full standby (no payments of principal or interest for the term of the 7(a) loan) may be considered as equity... Lender must use SBA Form 155... and a copy of the note must be attached... The standby debt may accrue interest... after the 7(a) loan is paid in full. Standby Creditor must subordinate any lien rights... and take no action... without Lender’s consent.

examples: Seller note counted as equity must be full standby for the entire SBA loan term; interest may accrue but cannot be paid currently.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA lender oversight documentation finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules state the same SBA condition for treating debt as equity injection: full standby for the entire 7(a) loan term (no P&I payments), use SBA Form 155 (or equivalent), attach note, allow accrual, and require subordination/no-action constraints. No material differences.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_10_006 selected: slightly more complete articulation of standby debt mechanics and required documentation elements (Form 155 + note attachment + subordination/no-action concepts).

business_rules.rule_name: Guaranty requirements require full guaranty by each 20 percent owner with six-month lookback and spouse aggregation to 20 percent
business_rules.description: Each SBA loan must be guaranteed by at least one individual or entity. Any individual with direct and/or indirect ownership of 20% or more of the Applicant must provide an unlimited full guaranty. If no individual/entity owns 20% or more, at least one owner must provide a full unconditional guaranty. Each spouse owning less than 20% must personally guarantee in full when combined ownership of both spouses and minor children is 20% or more. Entity owners with direct/indirect ownership of 20% or more must provide an unlimited full guaranty. A six-month lookback applies: any Person subject to guaranty requirements 6 months prior to application continues to be subject even if ownership drops below 20%, unless complete divestiture occurs prior to application with severance of any relationship for life of the loan; for 7(a) partial changes of ownership, guaranty determinations are based on post-sale ownership percentage.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans at application/closing for determining required guarantors.
business_rules.consequences: If required guaranties are not obtained consistent with SBA rules, SBA may deny the guaranty or seek repair, and the lender may face adverse findings in SBA oversight.
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions to six-month lookback: (i) complete divestiture prior to application including severance of any relationship with Applicant (and associated EPC) in any capacity for the life of the loan; (ii) for 7(a) partial changes of ownership, guaranty is based on post-sale ownership percentages.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Guaranties — 20% owners, spouse guaranty, six-month lookback
source_reference.source_text: Each loan must be guaranteed by at least one individual or entity... Any individual who has direct and/or indirect ownership of 20% or more... must provide an unlimited full guaranty... Each spouse owning less than 20%... must personally guarantee... when the combined ownership interest... is 20% or more... Any Person... subject to the guaranty requirements 6 months prior... would continue to be subject...

examples: Borrower has two owners at 30% and 70%: both must provide unlimited full personal guaranties (≥20%).
No owner is ≥20% (e.g., five owners at 19% each): at least one owner must provide a full unconditional guaranty even though no one meets 20%.
Owner reduced from 25% to 10% two months before application: still required to guarantee due to six-month lookback unless complete divestiture and severance occurred.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA lender enforcement action in oversight review

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: If Standard 7(a) loan is not fully secured, life insurance must cover collateral shortfall for key principals with collateral assignment and borrower-paid premiums
business_rules.description: For Standard 7(a) and specified 7(a) programs, lenders may follow internal policy for similarly-sized non-SBA loans, except that if the loan is not fully secured, life insurance is required in the amount of the collateral shortfall for principals of sole proprietorships, single member LLCs, or businesses otherwise dependent on one owner’s active participation. For each required policy, the lender must obtain a collateral assignment identifying the 7(a) lender as assignee acknowledged by the insurer’s home office and must ensure the applicant/borrower pays premiums. If principal is uninsurable, lender must obtain written documentation from a licensed insurer.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Standard 7(a), EWCP, CAPLines, International Trade loans, and pilot programs unless specified otherwise, where the loan is not fully secured and borrower type is sole proprietorship/single-member LLC/or otherwise dependent on one active owner.
business_rules.consequences: If life insurance is required but not obtained/assigned/maintained, SBA guaranty may be denied/repaired; collateral shortfall is not mitigated as required.
business_rules.exceptions: For 7(a) Small Loans, SBA Express, and Export Express loans, lenders may follow internal written policy for similarly-sized non-SBA loans (no specific shortfall-based mandate stated in the cited paragraph). SBA lenders may accept pledge of existing life insurance policy. Credit life or whole life insurance should not be required.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Life Insurance — 7(a) loans not fully secured
source_reference.source_text: 7(a) Lenders may follow their internal policy... except if the loan is not fully secured, life insurance is required in the amount of the collateral shortfall for the principals of sole proprietorships, single member LLCs, or for businesses otherwise dependent on one owner’s active participation... SBA Lenders must obtain a collateral assignment... acknowledged by the Home Office of the Insurer. SBA Lenders must ensure... pays the premiums...

examples: Loan is under-secured by $200,000 and borrower is a single member LLC dependent on one owner: life insurance required in amount of $200,000 with collateral assignment to lender.
Owner is determined uninsurable: lender obtains written documentation from a licensed insurer and documents file.
SBA Express loan: lender applies internal policy for similarly-sized non-SBA loans for life insurance determination (no SOP shortfall mandate under the cited SBA Express paragraph).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; adverse SBA oversight finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 10_008 applies when the loan is not fully secured; BR 14_002 defines how to determine 'fully secured' under SBA collateral rules.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 'fully secured' is mis-determined, life insurance may be incorrectly omitted or over-required, causing SBA compliance exceptions and potential guaranty repair/denial.

dependencies.rationale: BR 14_004 requires taking personal real estate equity; BR 10_008 requires life insurance for certain borrowers to cover the collateral shortfall. Together they form a combined shortfall-mitigation control set.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either is missed when triggered, the loan may be under-mitigated and subject to SBA collateral findings and possible guaranty repair/denial.

business_rules.rule_name: 504 life insurance is based on discounted collateral and must run at least 10 or 20 years depending on debenture term
business_rules.description: For 504 loans where life insurance is required and the SBA loan is not fully collateralized, the loan is considered fully collateralized when discounted collateral value is equal to or greater than the net debenture amount. When not fully collateralized, required life insurance equals (net debenture amount − discounted collateral value). Discounted collateral for life insurance only is calculated as: improved real estate at 85% of fair market value; new machinery/equipment (excluding furniture/fixtures) at 75% of price minus prior liens; used/existing machinery/equipment (excluding furniture/fixtures) at max 50% of Net Book Value or 80% with an Orderly Liquidation Appraisal minus prior liens. Minimum life insurance term is 10 years for a 10-year debenture, and 20 years for a 20- or 25-year debenture.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans when CDC determines viability is tied to an individual(s) and SBA loan is not fully collateralized (triggering life insurance requirement).
business_rules.consequences: If discounted collateral is miscalculated or life insurance is not obtained for the calculated shortfall and minimum term, SBA may identify closing/servicing deficiency and may deny/repair guaranty or require corrective action.
business_rules.exceptions: CDC may accept pledge of existing life insurance policy. Credit life or whole life insurance should not be required. If principal is uninsurable, CDC must obtain written documentation from a licensed insurer.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Life Insurance — 504 discounted collateral and minimum term
source_reference.source_text: For the purpose of life insurance calculation, the loan is considered fully collateralized when the value of the discounted collateral is equal to or greater than the net debenture amount... the calculation of discounted collateral is as follows: a) Improved real estate at 85%... b) New machinery and equipment... at 75%... c) Used... at a maximum of 50%... or 80% with an Orderly Liquidation Appraisal... When required, the minimum term... 10 years... 20 years...

examples: Net debenture amount is $1,000,000; discounted collateral is $850,000. Life insurance required is $150,000.
Improved real estate FMV is $2,000,000; discounted collateral value for life insurance is $1,700,000 (85%).
20-year debenture: required life insurance term must be at least 20 years.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA closing deficiency; corrective action requirement; potential guaranty repair

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 10_009 calculates discounted collateral value (e.g., 85% of FMV for improved real estate) to determine life insurance amount; BR 12_009 provides the appraisal standard needed to support FMV inputs where CRE collateral is involved.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If appraisal support is missing or noncompliant, the discounted collateral value and resulting life insurance requirement may be wrong, creating SBA closing/servicing deficiencies.

business_rules.rule_name: No collateral is required for SBA 7(a) loans of 50,000 dollars or less
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans with a loan amount of $50,000 or less, the lender is not required to take collateral (but guaranties are still required per SBA rules).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans when the SBA loan amount is $50,000 or less.
business_rules.consequences: If condition is met, collateral is not mandatory under SBA minimum requirements; lender may still take collateral under its own policies. If condition is not met (loan amount > $50,000), lender must follow SBA minimum collateral requirements and commercially reasonable practices.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the $50,000 threshold; guaranties still required even when collateral is not required.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Collateral — General Collateral Requirements (i)
source_reference.source_text: General Collateral Requirements i. For loans of $50,000 or less, the Lender is not required to take collateral. (Guaranties still must be obtained in accordance with Section A, Ch. 5, Para. A.)

examples: A $45,000 SBA 7(a) CAPLine is approved: lender is not required to perfect a lien on business assets, but must still obtain required guaranties.
A $50,000 SBA 7(a) working capital loan is approved: collateral is not required by SBA minimum standards.
A $75,000 SBA 7(a) CAPLine is approved: the $50,000 no-collateral rule does not apply; lender must follow SBA collateral minimum requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair at guaranty purchase for failure to follow SBA Loan Program Requirements

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 11_001 states collateral is not required for 7(a) ≤$50k but guaranties are still required per SBA rules; BR 10_007 defines who must provide those guaranties.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If guaranties are not obtained, the loan is noncompliant even though collateral may be waived, risking SBA guaranty repair/denial.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) loans must be fully disbursed within 48 months of approval or SBA cancels undisbursed balance and a line of credit is fully disbursed at first disbursement
business_rules.description: The SBA 7(a) loan disbursement period must be stated in E-Tran Terms & Conditions. The loan must be fully disbursed within 48 months of approval; any remaining undisbursed balance will be canceled by SBA. SBA considers a line of credit as fully disbursed at the time of first disbursement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all SBA 7(a) loans at closing/disbursement; applies specifically to lines of credit for the definition of 'fully disbursed.'
business_rules.consequences: If not fully disbursed within 48 months, SBA cancels the undisbursed balance. For lines of credit, the loan is treated as fully disbursed upon first advance, affecting eligibility for actions that depend on being 'fully disbursed' (e.g., secondary market sale).
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions to the 48-month disbursement policy are permitted only if requested per SOP 'Exceptions to Policy' process, including SBA loan number, detailed explanation, and number of months requested to be added.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 5: Loan Closing and Disbursement — Disbursement Period
source_reference.source_text: The disbursement period must be stated in the E-Tran Terms & Conditions. The loan must be fully disbursed within 48 months of approval, or any remaining undisbursed balance will be canceled by SBA. SBA considers a line of credit as fully disbursed at the time of first disbursement.

examples: A 7(a) term loan approved on 2025-07-01 has $50,000 not disbursed by 2029-07-01: SBA cancels the $50,000 undisbursed amount absent an approved exception.
A CAPLine makes its first $10,000 draw one month after approval: SBA treats the line as fully disbursed at that first draw for SBA purposes.
A lender requests a disbursement extension to 54 months due to cost increases: lender must follow the SOP exceptions process and specify how many months to add.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Undisbursed balance canceled by SBA; SBA oversight criticism if lender fails to follow required exceptions process

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Escrow for commercial real estate taxes and insurance may not exceed 105 percent of current year charges and must pay at least the greater of money market or typical escrow rate
business_rules.description: For SBA loans, if the borrower and lender establish an escrow account for real estate taxes and insurance (including hazard and, when applicable, flood and earthquake insurance), the amount collected may not exceed 105% of the current year amount charged by the taxing authority/insurance company for the annual requirement. The escrow account must be FDIC-insured and must pay the borrower a money market rate of interest, or the rate typically paid on escrow accounts for commercial real property on non-SBA guaranteed loans, whichever is greater.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an escrow account is established for real estate taxes and/or insurance for an SBA loan secured by commercial real estate.
business_rules.consequences: Escrow collection methodology must cap collections at 105% of the current year annual requirement and must meet FDIC-insured and interest-rate payment requirements. Over-collection or failure to credit required interest can lead to SBA compliance findings and potential borrower remediation.
business_rules.exceptions: Borrower and lender may agree whether to establish escrow ('may agree'); rule applies only if escrow is established.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 5: Escrow Policy for Commercial Real Estate Taxes and Insurance (ii)-(iii)
source_reference.source_text: Escrow Policy for Commercial Real Estate Taxes and Insurance: i. The Borrower and Lender may agree to establish an escrow account... ii. The amount of money collected for an escrow account may not exceed 105% of the amount charged in the current year... iii. The account must be FDIC-insured and pay the Borrower a money market rate of interest, or the rate typically paid on escrow accounts... whichever is greater

examples: Annual property taxes are $12,000: maximum escrow collection for taxes is $12,600 (105% of $12,000).
Hazard insurance premium is $8,000 for the current year: escrowed amount for insurance cannot exceed $8,400 if collected upfront for the annual requirement.
If the bank’s money market rate is 1.50% and its typical commercial escrow rate on non-SBA loans is 0.25%, the SBA escrow must pay at least 1.50% (the greater rate).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; potential borrower restitution/remediation for improper escrow practices

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA International Trade loans generally require first lien and second lien is allowed only with adequate assurance and not for PLP when not first lien, with piggyback defined as approvals within 90 days
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans, the lender must take a first lien on the assets financed with IT loan proceeds or other assets of the applicant. An IT loan may be secured by a second lien position on the property or equipment financed by the IT loan or on other assets of the applicant only if SBA determines the second lien provides adequate assurance of repayment. SBA treats “piggyback financing” as loans approved within 90 days of each other; for second-lien allowance in the cited example, the first-lien loan must not have been made at or about the same time as the IT loan (i.e., not approved within 90 days). IT loans may not be processed under a lender’s PLP authority when the IT loan will not have a first lien on the assets being financed.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Export Trade Finance: International Trade loans, particularly when collateral lien position is not first lien and/or lender intends to use PLP delegated processing.
business_rules.consequences: If first lien is not taken and SBA does not determine adequate assurance for second lien, collateral structure is not acceptable. If IT loan will not have a first lien on assets being financed, lender cannot process under PLP authority (must use non-delegated/other SBA-approved method).
business_rules.exceptions: Second lien may be permitted if SBA determines adequate assurance of payment; lender must document offsetting factors (e.g., other collateral paying IT loan in full, profitable operations, timely repayment, sufficient cash flow).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade > Required Lien Position
source_reference.source_text: requires that the Lender must take a first lien... An IT loan can be secured by a second lien... if the SBA determines that the second lien provides adequate assurance... SBA considers “at or about the same time” to mean loans approved within 90 days of each other... IT loans may not be processed under a Lender’s PLP authority when the IT loan will not have a first lien

examples: Bank proposes IT loan in 2nd lien behind an existing loan approved 30 days earlier—treated as piggyback; second lien scenario fails example condition and requires SBA determination/structure change; cannot be PLP if not first lien on assets being financed.

Existing first-lien loan was approved 2 years earlier; IT loan in 2nd lien is proposed with strong additional collateral and documented offsetting factors—SBA may determine adequate assurance and allow second lien (but still cannot be PLP if not first lien on assets being financed).
IT loan is secured by first lien on equipment being financed—compliant and may be processed under PLP subject to other SBA requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing restriction (cannot use PLP) and potential SBA denial or required restructuring for unacceptable lien position

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Commercial real estate securing SBA-guaranteed loans requires USPAP appraisal dated within 12 months, independent appraiser, and state-certified appraiser when value exceeds $1,000,000
business_rules.description: For SBA-guaranteed loans secured by commercial real estate, lenders must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser, compliant with USPAP, and completed appraisals must be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. The appraiser must be independent with no appearance of conflict of interest and not involved in the loan production/approval. When the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified. The lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the applicant; the appraisal must identify the lender as the client and/or intended user (subject to primary regulator FIRREA allowances for federally regulated lenders).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any SBA-guaranteed loan collateralized by commercial real estate, including International Trade loans and other 7(a) loans, at time of application for guaranty.
business_rules.consequences: Without an appraisal meeting these requirements, collateral valuation does not satisfy SBA program requirements; SBA guaranty processing/approval may be delayed or denied, and file may be criticized in oversight or guaranty purchase review.
business_rules.exceptions: For federally regulated lenders, FIRREA requirements of the primary regulator may be followed to the extent they permit otherwise regarding identifying lender as client/intended user; however, SBA states no exemption under Interagency appraisal guidance for transactions insured/guaranteed by U.S. government agency.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal and Business Valuation Requirements > Commercial Real Estate
source_reference.source_text: Estate: a) For all loans secured by commercial real estate , Lenders must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser. Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty .

examples: Loan application date is October 1, 2025; appraisal completion date is November 15, 2024 (10.5 months)—compliant.
Estimated property value is $1,200,000 and lender uses only state-licensed (not certified) appraiser—noncompliant; must use state-certified appraiser.
Borrower provides seller-ordered appraisal; lender cannot use it and must order its own compliant appraisal naming lender as client/intended user.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing/guaranty denial or delay; SBA oversight criticism and potential guaranty purchase disputes

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 12_009 requires an appraisal meeting SBA/USPAP timing and independence; BR 13_003 governs whether the appraisal is usable for SBA purposes (lender as client/intended user; cannot rely on seller/applicant appraisal).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR 13_003 is not satisfied, the appraisal may be invalid for SBA compliance, delaying/denying SBA guaranty processing and exposing guaranty repair/denial risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR 13_002 operationalizes/clarifies independence and credential requirements (including state-certified requirement for >$1,000,000 estimated value), validating that BR 12_009’s appraisal is compliant.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If independence/credential checks fail, the appraisal does not satisfy SBA requirements, risking processing delay and guaranty repair/denial.

dependencies.rationale: BR 13_007 decides whether an appraisal (vs evaluation) is required for Export Express CRE; when required, BR 12_009 provides the governing appraisal quality/timing standards.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If triggers require an appraisal but only an evaluation is obtained, or if appraisal standards are not met, SBA compliance failures may lead to oversight criticism or guaranty purchase issues.

business_rules.rule_name: USPAP appraisal by state licensed or certified appraiser required for SBA Standard 7(a) commercial real estate collateral and must be dated within 12 months
business_rules.description: For SBA Standard 7(a) loans (loans greater than $350,000) that are secured by commercial real property, the lender must obtain an appraisal performed by a state licensed or certified appraiser. The appraisal must comply with USPAP and must be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. For federally regulated lenders, SBA grants no exemption under the Interagency Guidance for transactions insured or guaranteed by a U.S. Government Agency.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) loan program is SBA Standard 7(a), (b) loan amount is > $350,000, and (c) loan is secured by commercial real property.
business_rules.consequences: If the required appraisal is not obtained, is not USPAP-compliant, or is older than 12 months at application, the loan file is non-compliant with SBA requirements and may be subject to SBA denial/repair at guaranty purchase or adverse lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for Standard 7(a) commercial real estate collateral; for federally regulated lenders, SBA explicitly states no exemption under the Interagency Guidance for U.S. Government agency-insured/guaranteed transactions.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_7aStandardSmall > Real Estate Appraisal and Business Valuation Requirements > Commercial Real Estate (i)(a)
source_reference.source_text: For all Standard 7(a) loans secured by commercial real property, all Lenders must obtain an appraisal by a State licensed or certified appraiser. Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. For federally-regulated Lenders, no exemption is granted under the Interagency Guidance Appraisal and Evaluation Guidelines dated December 2, 2010, for Transactions Insured or Guaranteed by a U.S. Government Agency.

examples: A $900,000 Standard 7(a) loan secured by a retail building: lender must obtain a USPAP appraisal by a state licensed/certified appraiser dated within 12 months of guaranty application.
A federally regulated bank makes a Standard 7(a) CRE-secured loan: bank cannot rely on an Interagency Guidance exemption; it must still obtain the appraisal meeting SBA requirements.
A Standard 7(a) guaranty application submitted with a 14-month-old appraisal: lender cannot rely on it because the appraisal exceeds the 12-month dating requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial at purchase due to collateral documentation deficiency

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR 13_001 specializes the general appraisal framework for Standard 7(a) loans >$350k and emphasizes no exemption from interagency guidance for federally regulated lenders; it is applied when the BR 13_001 conditions are met within the broader BR 12_009 appraisal requirement universe.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the specialized branch is missed, the lender may incorrectly apply a non-SBA appraisal exception, resulting in SBA noncompliance and guaranty repair/denial risk.

business_rules.rule_name: State-certified appraiser required when estimated commercial property value exceeds $1,000,000 and appraiser must be independent
business_rules.description: For SBA commercial real estate appraisals, the appraiser must be independent with no appearance of conflict of interest, independent of the loan production function, and not involved in approving the transaction. The appraiser must be state-licensed or state-certified; however, if the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be state-certified (not merely state-licensed).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loans requiring a commercial real estate appraisal (including Standard 7(a) and Export Express where an appraisal is required). Triggered when an appraisal is being ordered/accepted for CRE collateral.
business_rules.consequences: Use of a non-independent appraiser or using only a state-licensed (not state-certified) appraiser for estimated value > $1,000,000 is non-compliant; may lead to SBA review findings and potential guaranty repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the independence requirement or the >$1,000,000 state-certified requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_7aStandardSmall > Commercial Real Estate (i)(b)
source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must be: i) Independent and have no appearance of a conflict of interest (such as a direct or indirect financial or other interest in the property or transaction, independent of the loan production function, and not involved in the approval of the transaction); and ii) Either State-licensed or State-certified, with the following exception: when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal Requirements (Appraiser must be independent; state-certified over $1,000,000)
source_reference.source_text:
The appraiser must be:

Independent and have no appearance of a conflict of interest... independent of the loan production function, and not involved in the approval of the transaction); and

Either State-licensed or State-certified, with the following exception: when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified.

examples: If estimated property value is $1.2MM, a state-licensed appraiser is insufficient; a state-certified appraiser is required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial due to improper appraisal independence/qualification

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same appraiser independence requirements and credentialing rule: state-licensed or state-certified generally, but state-certified required when estimated value > $1,000,000. Wording differences only.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_13_002 selected: concise and directly operational for ordering/accepting SBA CRE appraisals, including the >$1,000,000 state-certified trigger.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA commercial real estate appraisal must identify lender as client or intended user and may not be reused from seller or applicant
business_rules.description: For SBA commercial real estate appraisals, the appraisal must identify the lender as the client and/or an intended user (as defined in USPAP), except federally regulated lenders may follow their regulator’s FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise. The lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the applicant. The appraisal cost may be passed on to the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lender obtains or relies on an appraisal for SBA commercial real estate collateral (Standard 7(a), Export Express when appraisal required, and IT loans secured by CRE).
business_rules.consequences: If the appraisal does not name the lender as client/intended user (subject to FIRREA allowance), or if the lender relies on a seller/applicant-prepared appraisal, the appraisal cannot be used for SBA purposes and the file may be subject to SBA review findings and guaranty repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: Federally regulated lenders may follow their primary regulator’s FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise regarding identifying the lender as client/intended user.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisal_Bus_Valuation_7aStandardSmall > Commercial Real Estate (i)(c)
source_reference.source_text: In order for the appraiser to identify the scope of work appropriately, the appraisal must identify the Lender as the client and/or an intended user of the appraisal, as those terms are defined in USPAP, except that federally-regulated Lenders may follow their primary regulator’s FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise. The Lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the Applicant. The cost may be passed on to the Applicant.

examples: A borrower provides an appraisal ordered by the property seller: lender may not use it and must order its own appraisal meeting SBA requirements.

A federally regulated bank follows FIRREA rules that permit a different intended user identification approach: it may do so only to the extent permitted by its regulator, otherwise must identify lender as client/intended user.
An SBA loan file shows appraisal invoice billed to applicant: permissible because the cost may be passed on to the applicant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial due to invalid appraisal reliance

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Construction or substantial renovation appraisal must value property at completion and requires post-construction deviation statement or SBA permission to close
