A city economic development agency administers a guarantee funded by a State appropriation; the lender reports it as a local government guarantee because the city is the administering entity.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding; potential civil money penalty and required resubmission/correction of 1071 data

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 loan term must be reported in months from origination to legal maturity or termination and may be not applicable only when no term exists
business_rules.description: Under final 12 CFR § 1002.107(a)(5)(iii), a financial institution must report the length of the loan term, in months, if applicable. Loan term is the number of months after which the legal obligation will mature or terminate and is generally measured from the date of origination and reported that way. If the credit product does not have a loan term (e.g., certain credit cards), report loan term as 'not applicable.' Products with an estimated loan term (e.g., merchant cash advances and other sales-based financing) are not treated as having no term; for those, report the loan term, if any, that the institution estimated, specified, or disclosed in processing or underwriting.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered credit transactions subject to CFPB Regulation B Section 1071 reporting requirements where the credit type data point requires reporting loan term in months if applicable.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to report loan term in months when applicable, or improper use of 'not applicable' for products that have an estimated/disclosed term, results in inaccurate 1071 reporting and increases risk of CFPB supervisory findings and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: Use 'not applicable' only for products that truly do not have a loan term (e.g., certain credit cards). Merchant cash advances and other sales-based financing with estimated/specified/disclosed term do not qualify for 'not applicable' solely because the term is estimated.

source_reference.section_id: Final § 1002.107(a)(5)(iii) and comment 107(a)(5)-8 loan term instructions
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.107(a)(5)(iii) requires a financial institution to report the length of the loan term, in months, if applicable... it explain[s] ... the loan term is the number of months after which the legal obligation will mature or terminate... loan term for small business credit is generally measured from the date of origination... if a credit product ... does not have a loan term, the financial institution reports loan term as “not applicable.”

examples: A small business term loan originated on 2026-01-15 matures on 2031-01-15; the lender reports a 60-month loan term.
A business credit card product has no stated maturity/termination date; the lender reports loan term as 'not applicable.'
A merchant cash advance application includes a disclosed estimated term of 8 months; the lender reports 8 months (not 'not applicable') because an estimated/disclosed term exists.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding; potential civil money penalty and required remediation of 1071 reporting controls

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 26_010 requires reporting loan term in months based on the legal maturity/termination. For SBA 7(a) loans, 21_006 constrains permissible maturity by use of proceeds, shaping the final legal term that is then reported under 26_010.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 21_006 is violated (term too long), the loan may need restructuring, changing the loan term and causing downstream 1071 reporting corrections; if 26_010 is reported without finalizing maturity, reporting may be inaccurate.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA loan applicant with third-party management agreement is ineligible if manager has sole discretion over operations
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) and 504 lending eligibility, a business is an ineligible passive business if it has entered into a management agreement with a third party that gives the management company sole discretion over the business operations. The applicant may remain eligible only if it exercises meaningful oversight and the management company does not have sole discretion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting/eligibility review identifies a third-party management agreement covering the applicant business operations for an SBA 7(a) or 504 credit request (origination, renewal, or material modification).
business_rules.consequences: If the management agreement grants sole discretion to the management company, the applicant must be treated as an ineligible passive business and the SBA loan must be declined as ineligible (or restructured so the applicant retains meaningful oversight).
business_rules.exceptions: Applicant remains eligible if the management company does not have sole discretion AND the applicant exercises meaningful oversight meeting all enumerated oversight elements (see related rule on meaningful oversight).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses
source_reference.source_text: Businesses that have entered into a management agreement with a third party that gives the management company sole discretion over the business operations are ineligible passive businesses. However, if the management company does not have sole discretion to manage the operations of the business and the Applicant exercises meaningful oversight of the business, the Applicant is eligible.

examples: A hotel is operated under an agreement where the third-party operator can set pricing, hire/fire staff, and control bank accounts without owner approval: ineligible passive business for SBA purposes.
A restaurant uses a management firm, but the owner approves budgets and capex and controls accounts: potentially eligible (evaluate meaningful oversight elements).
A borrower proposes SBA financing but provides a contract granting the manager unilateral authority to incur expenses and sign contracts: must treat as ineligible unless contract is amended.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism in SBA lender oversight

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 30_001 declares ineligibility when a management agreement gives the management company sole discretion, but allows eligibility if the applicant exercises meaningful oversight. 30_002 defines what constitutes meaningful oversight (budget approval, capex/expense approvals, account control, employee oversight). Thus 30_002 validates whether 30_001’s ineligibility determination applies.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 30_002 elements are not documented/satisfied, the applicant should be treated as ineligible under 30_001; failure to apply 30_002 can lead to originating an ineligible SBA loan.

business_rules.rule_name: Meaningful oversight requires budget approval, capex or expense approval over threshold, account control, and employee oversight by applicant
business_rules.description: For SBA eligibility where a third-party management agreement exists, the applicant must demonstrate meaningful oversight. Meaningful oversight includes a management agreement that provides for the applicant to: (i) approve the annual operating budget; (ii) approve any capital expenditures or operating expenses over a significant dollar threshold; (iii) have control over the bank accounts; and (iv) have oversight over the employees operating the business, who must be employees of the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA 7(a) or 504 applicant uses a management company and eligibility depends on whether the applicant is a passive business due to operational control delegation.
business_rules.consequences: If any required meaningful oversight element is missing (e.g., manager controls accounts or employees are not applicant employees), treat applicant as ineligible passive business unless agreement is revised to satisfy all requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt; lender may document alternative facts only if they still satisfy the four elements as written.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses > Meaningful oversight
source_reference.source_text: “Meaningful oversight” by the Applicant means involvement in the decisions made concerning the operation of the business, which include a management agreement that provides for the Applicant to do all of the following: i. Approve the annual operating budget; ii. Approve any capital expenditures or operating expenses over a significant dollar threshold; iii. Have control over the bank accounts; and iv. Have oversight over the employees operating the business (who must be employees of the Applicant).

examples: Agreement allows applicant to approve annual budget and capex over $25,000, and applicant controls operating account; employees are on applicant payroll: meets oversight elements (subject to full review).
Management company hires and pays all employees directly and applicant has no personnel authority: fails oversight requirement; applicant ineligible unless revised.
Applicant approves budget but manager can incur unlimited operating expenses without approval: fails the 'over significant dollar threshold' approval element.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism in SBA lender oversight

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Salon suites, ghost kitchens, and similar rental-by-space models are SBA-ineligible unless revenue is membership dues and space is unassigned and fully serviced
business_rules.description: Shopping centers, office suites (including salon suites), ghost kitchens, and similar business models that generate income by renting space to accommodate independent businesses are SBA-ineligible unless: (a) revenue is earned through membership dues (not rent); (b) customers do not have an assigned space they can return to and the space cannot be personalized; and (c) the business is responsible for upkeep/maintenance and supplies equipment necessary for a fully functioning office/kitchen. If the model is combined (some rent and some membership dues), the business is not eligible.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) or 504 loan eligibility determinations for applicants whose business model monetizes the use of space by independent operators (origination/renewal).
business_rules.consequences: If any of the three conditions is not met, or if revenue includes both rent and membership dues, the applicant is SBA-ineligible and cannot obtain an SBA loan for any purpose.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligibility only when all three membership-based conditions are met and there is no combined rent/membership model.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Passive Businesses > Shopping centers/office suites/ghost kitchens
source_reference.source_text: Shopping centers, office suites (aka salon suites), ghost kitchens, and similar business models that generate income by renting space ... are not eligible unless all of the following apply: a) The revenue is earned through membership dues (not rent); and b) The business’s customers do not have an assigned space ... and the space cannot be personalized; and c) The business is responsible for upkeep and maintenance and is supplying the equipment necessary...

examples: A ghost kitchen leases dedicated kitchen bays with lockable storage and monthly rent: ineligible.
A co-working space charges monthly membership dues, provides unassigned desks and shared equipment, and customers cannot personalize or reserve a specific office: potentially eligible.
A salon suite operator charges some stylists fixed rent and offers others a day-pass membership: combined model; ineligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Hotels, motels, and similar transient lodging are SBA-eligible only if more than 50% of prior-year revenue is from stays of 30 days or less
business_rules.description: Hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, marinas, campgrounds, and similar businesses are SBA-eligible only if more than 50% of prior-year revenue is derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time and the business complies with zoning and other legal requirements. For a start-up business, projections must show more than 50% of revenue will be from transients staying 30 days or less. Rentals must comply with local laws and regulations, including zoning codes and HOA/condo association rules.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) or 504 loan eligibility determinations for lodging or similar rental businesses; tested at underwriting using prior-year revenue or projections for start-ups.
business_rules.consequences: If the transient revenue test is not met (>50% from ≤30-day stays) or legal compliance is not demonstrated, the applicant is ineligible for SBA financing.
business_rules.exceptions: Start-up businesses may qualify using projections rather than prior-year revenue, but must still exceed the 50% transient revenue threshold and comply with local laws.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Limited eligibility for renting/leasing > Hotels/motels
source_reference.source_text: Hotels, motels, recreational vehicle parks, marinas, campgrounds, or similar types of businesses are eligible if more than 50% of the business’s revenue for the prior year is derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time and the business complies with all zoning and other legal requirements. If the Applicant is a Start-Up Business, the Applicant’s projections must show that more than 50% of the business’s revenue will be derived from transients who stay for 30 days or less at a time.

examples: A motel with 65% of prior-year revenue from nightly and weekly stays (≤30 days) and documented zoning compliance: eligible under this criterion.
An RV park where most tenants sign 6-month leases and only 20% of revenue is from short-stay guests: ineligible.
A start-up marina provides projections showing 55% revenue from transient slips rented by the week, plus evidence of zoning compliance: may be eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA ineligibility for legal gambling when prior-year gross revenue from gambling exceeds one-third or business purpose is gambling
business_rules.description: A business is SBA-ineligible if it obtains more than one-third of its annual gross revenue for the prior year, including rental income, from legal gambling activities. If the purpose of the business is gambling (e.g., pari-mutuel racetrack or casino), it is ineligible regardless of the percentage of gross revenue derived from gambling. A business may be eligible if it obtains one-third or less of its annual gross revenue (including rental income) from certain permitted gambling-related sources such as commissions from official State lottery ticket sales under a State license or state-licensed and supervised gambling activities in states where legal.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 applicant eligibility screening at underwriting; lender must evaluate prior-year revenue sources and business purpose.
business_rules.consequences: If >33.333% threshold is exceeded or business purpose is gambling, the SBA loan must be declined as ineligible.
business_rules.exceptions: Eligible if one-third or less of prior-year annual gross revenue (including rental income) is derived from: (i) commissions from official State lottery ticket sales under State license; or (ii) gambling activities licensed and supervised by a state authority where legal.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1: Primary Applicant Eligibility Requirements > Legal gambling
source_reference.source_text: Businesses that obtain more than one-third of their annual gross revenue for the prior year, including rental income, from legal gambling activities are not eligible. If the purpose of the business is gambling, such as a pari-mutuel betting racetrack or a gambling casino, the business is not eligible, regardless of the percentage of gross revenue derived from gambling.

examples: A convenience store earns 20% of revenue from state lottery commissions and the rest from retail sales: eligible under this criterion (assuming all other requirements met).
A restaurant earns 40% of revenue from on-premise video lottery terminals: ineligible (>1/3).
A casino hotel applies for SBA financing and claims gambling is only 25% of revenue: still ineligible because the purpose of the business is gambling.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA requires an environmental investigation for all commercial property taken as loan collateral
business_rules.description: SBA requires an Environmental Investigation of all commercial Property upon which a security interest (e.g., mortgage, deed of trust, or leasehold deed of trust) is offered as security for a loan or debenture. The depth/type of investigation varies with contamination risk, and SOP standards are minimum standards.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans when commercial property collateral is being taken via mortgage/deed of trust/leasehold deed of trust (origination and collateral substitutions).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to complete required Environmental Investigation is noncompliance with SBA environmental policies; for 7(a), failure may result in a denial of SBA guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt; SOP provides different investigation types based on risk, but the minimum requirement is that an Environmental Investigation is required for all commercial property offered as security.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Environmental Policies and Procedures > Environmental Investigations
source_reference.source_text: SBA requires an Environmental Investigation of all commercial Property upon which a security interest such as a mortgage, deed of trust, or leasehold deed of trust is offered as security for a loan or debenture. The type and depth of an Environmental Investigation to be performed varies with the risks of Contamination.

examples: SBA 7(a) loan secured by a first mortgage on a retail building: Environmental Investigation required.
SBA 504 project with leasehold deed of trust interest: Environmental Investigation required.
SBA loan secured only by business assets and no commercial real property lien: this specific rule does not trigger (no commercial property offered as security).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial; regulatory criticism in lender oversight

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Environmental investigation reports must be dated within one year of SBA loan number and include reliance letter with work by environmental professional
business_rules.description: Environmental Investigation Reports must be dated within one year of issuance of the SBA loan number. Transaction Screens, Phase I and Phase II ESAs must be performed by an Environmental Professional and be accompanied by the Reliance Letter in Appendix 5 (even if addressed to the SBA Lender). Requests for exceptions to SBA Environmental Policies and Procedures must be directed to the Environmental Committee, regardless of processing method.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 loans where an Environmental Investigation is required, including delegated and non-delegated processing.
business_rules.consequences: If report date is older than one year from SBA loan number issuance, or if required reliance letter/environmental professional requirements are missing, the file is deficient and may result in SBA guaranty denial/repair or inability to proceed under non-delegated processing without correction/exception.
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions must be requested through the Environmental Committee; rule states exception requests are required regardless of processing method.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Environmental Policies and Procedures > Submission of Environmental Investigation Reports
source_reference.source_text: of any Environmental Investigation Report in the loan file. All SBA Lenders must comply with and meet the requirements of the Environmental Policies and Procedures as set forth in this SOP. For example, all Transaction Screens, Phase I and Phase II ESAs must be performed by an Environmental Professional

examples: Loan number issued 2026-05-15; Phase I dated 2025-06-01 (older than one year): must refresh or obtain exception per SOP process.
Phase I is current but no reliance letter included: documentation deficiency; obtain reliance letter before closing/guaranty eligibility.
Delegated SBA Express loan: reports need not be submitted via E-Tran, but must be retained in file and still must meet dating/professional/reliance letter requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair; regulatory criticism in lender oversight

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_30_008 governs the required attributes (dating within 1 year of SBA loan number, reliance letter, performed by an Environmental Professional) of the Environmental Investigation required by BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_30_007. The documentation rule is not actionable unless the investigation/report exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_30_007 is not satisfied (no investigation), BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_30_008 cannot be satisfied and the loan is environmentally noncompliant, increasing risk of SBA guaranty denial/repair and inability to close under SBA requirements.

business_rules.rule_name: Section 106 review clearance must be retained or submitted depending on delegated versus non-delegated SBA processing
business_rules.description: When Section 106 review clearance is issued by local SBA counsel, SBA lenders must retain or submit the clearance based on processing method: delegated lenders must retain the clearance in the SBA loan file and provide it with any guaranty purchase request; non-delegated lenders must include the clearance with the application submitted to the SBA loan processing center.
business_rules.conditions: Applies after Section 106 review clearance is issued in connection with collateral/property review under SBA requirements for loans where this process is applicable.
business_rules.consequences: If required clearance is missing from the file or not submitted with guaranty purchase request/application as required, SBA may delay processing, request additional information, or deny/repair guaranty purchase due to documentation deficiency.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Section 106 review clearance retention/submission
source_reference.source_text: SBA Lenders processing the loan under their delegated authority must retain the clearance from local SBA counsel in the SBA Loan file. The clearance must be provided with any guaranty purchase request and may be reviewed by SBA when conducting lender oversight activities. SBA Lenders processing the loan under non-delegated procedures must include the clearance from local SBA counsel with the application for review by the SBA loan processing center.

examples: Delegated 7(a) loan: Section 106 clearance obtained but not in file at purchase: SBA may require cure before honoring guaranty purchase.
Non-delegated 504: clearance not included in application package: SBA processing center may suspend review pending receipt.
Delegated lender retains clearance and includes it with guaranty purchase request: compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA purchase denial/delay; lender oversight finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 energy reduction eligibility requires at least 10% reduction and excludes new businesses
business_rules.description: To qualify as an SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Project under the “reduction of existing energy consumption” category, the project must reduce existing energy consumption by at least 10%. This category cannot be used for projects involving new businesses. If the project involves construction or acquisition of a facility, the new facility must replace an existing facility, energy consumption must be compared on a square-footage basis, the applicant must demonstrate the new facility will use 10% less energy than the existing facility, and the new facility must be located in the same local area as the existing facility (e.g., same city/town/county/zip/MSA or as otherwise deemed appropriate by SBA).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when loan_purpose indicates SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Project eligibility is being claimed under the “reduction of existing energy consumption by at least 10%” pathway, including when proceeds involve construction or acquisition of a facility.
business_rules.consequences: If any required element is not met (e.g., <10% reduction, project is for a new business, not a replacement facility, not same local area, or comparison not on square footage basis), the project is not eligible under this 10% reduction category and must qualify under a different eligible public policy category or be processed as non-energy eligibility; otherwise SBA/CDC may not approve the project under this category.
business_rules.exceptions: Retrofit projects follow a separate rule (see related rule BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_31_002). SBA may deem the “local area” differently as appropriate. No exception stated for new businesses under this category.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1: 504 Eligibility through Submission of Application > Energy_10percent_reduction_504
source_reference.source_text: Projects for the reduction of existing energy consumption by at least 10%. This category may not be used for Projects involving new businesses. ... The new facility must replace an existing facility. ... the Applicant must be able to demonstrate that the new facility will use 10% less energy than the existing facility. The energy consumption between the two facilities must be compared for energy consumption on a square footage basis. ... The new facility must be located in the same local area (e.g., the same city, town, county, zip code, metropolitan statistical area or as otherwise deemed appropriate by SBA).

examples: Acquisition: Applicant buys a replacement facility in the same county and documents (kWh/sq ft) that the new facility will use 12% less energy than the existing facility; eligible under the 10% reduction pathway.
Construction: Applicant builds a new additional site (not replacing an existing facility); ineligible under the 10% reduction pathway even if projected to be efficient.
Acquisition: Replacement facility is in a different MSA and the applicant cannot support SBA’s acceptance as the “same local area”; fails the location condition and is ineligible under this pathway.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC eligibility rejection or requirement to re-structure the project to meet another eligibility category

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 retrofit energy projects must reduce the subject facility’s energy consumption by at least 10%
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Projects involving retrofit of an applicant’s existing facility, the retrofit must reduce the energy consumption of that facility by at least 10%. The reduction test is applied to the retrofitted facility regardless of energy usage of any other facilities the applicant may operate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 504 eligibility is being claimed under the retrofit portion of the “reduction of existing energy consumption by at least 10%” category and proceeds finance modifications/retrofits at an existing facility.
business_rules.consequences: If projected/validated reduction at the retrofitted facility is <10%, the project is not eligible under this pathway and must qualify under another eligible category or be re-scoped to meet the threshold.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. The rule explicitly ignores energy usage of any other applicant-operated facilities.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1: 504 Eligibility through Submission of Application > Energy_10percent_reduction_504
source_reference.source_text: The retrofit of an Applicant’s existing facility, the retrofit must reduce the energy consumption of that facility by at least 10%, regardless of the energy usage of any other facilities that the Applicant may operate;

examples: Applicant retrofits HVAC and lighting and documents a 10.5% reduction in annual kWh at the same facility; eligible under the retrofit pathway.
Applicant retrofits one facility by 8% but has 20% reduction at a different facility; still ineligible because the subject facility does not meet 10%.
Applicant claims a 10% reduction but presents only utility cost savings in dollars rather than actual usage metrics; fails documentation requirements (see related documentation rule).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC eligibility rejection or requirement to revise project scope to meet energy reduction threshold

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 renewable energy projects must generate more than 15% of energy used at the project facility and include all required improvements in project costs
business_rules.description: To qualify as an SBA 504 Energy Public Policy Project under renewable energy sources, each project must generate more than 15% of the energy used by the applicant at the project facility. Additionally, all improvements or equipment required to generate the renewable energy or renewable fuels must be included in the 504 project costs. Eligible examples include plant, equipment, and process upgrades for renewable energy sources such as micropower and renewable fuel producers (e.g., biodiesel and ethanol producers).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 504 eligibility is being claimed under the renewable energy sources category for the project facility and proceeds finance renewable generation or renewable fuel production-related assets.
business_rules.consequences: If the project generates 15% or less of the energy used at the project facility, or if required improvements/equipment are omitted from 504 project costs, SBA/CDC may determine the project does not meet this Energy Public Policy eligibility category and require re-scoping or alternative eligibility justification.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Threshold is “more than 15%” (i.e., strictly >15%, not ≥15%).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1: 504 Eligibility through Submission of Application > Energy_RenewableSources_504
source_reference.source_text: Renewable energy sources that generate more than 15% of energy used at Project Facility. ... Each Project must generate more than 15% of the energy used by the Applicant at the Project facility. In addition, all improvements or equipment required to generate the renewable energy or renewable fuels must be included in the 504 Project costs;

examples: Solar installation projected to generate 18% of the facility’s annual kWh, and all inverters and interconnection equipment are included in 504 project costs; eligible under this category.
Renewable fuel equipment generates 15.0% of facility energy; ineligible because the requirement is more than 15%.
Wind generation projected at 17% but key balance-of-system equipment is excluded from 504 project costs; fails the inclusion requirement and risks eligibility rejection.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC eligibility rejection or requirement to revise project budget/costs and eligibility documentation

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 amount field must report credit limit approved for open-end and amount originated for originated closed-end transactions
business_rules.description: For CFPB Section 1071 reporting under § 1002.107(a)(8), the financial institution must report (1) for open-end covered credit transactions: the credit limit approved; and (2) for originated closed-end covered credit transactions: the amount originated (rather than amount approved). For applications reported as denied, withdrawn by the applicant, or incomplete under § 1002.107(a)(9), the institution must report the amount approved/originated as “not applicable.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a covered financial institution compiles and reports data for a covered application for a covered credit transaction under 12 CFR part 1002, subpart B, including for small business CRE loans that are covered credit transactions.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution reports the wrong amount basis (e.g., amount approved instead of originated for originated closed-end) or fails to use “not applicable” for denied/withdrawn/incomplete, the submission will be noncompliant and may trigger resubmission, supervisory findings, or enforcement risk related to inaccurate 1071 data.
business_rules.exceptions: For applications reported as denied, withdrawn, or incomplete, the amount approved/originated is not required and must be reported as “not applicable.”

source_reference.section_id: 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(8) and comment 107(a)(8)-1 (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: The Bureau reads the statutory language “the amount of the credit transaction or the credit limit approved” to require the amount of the credit limit approved to be reported for open-end applications, and the amount of the credit transaction to be reported for closed-end applications. ... Accordingly, final comment 107(a)(8)-1 explains that for applications a financial institution, pursuant to § 1002.107(a)(9), reports as denied, withdrawn by the applicant, or incomplete, the financial institution reports that the amount originated or approved is “not applicable.”

examples: Open-end line of credit application approved for $250,000: report amount approved/originated = $250,000 (credit limit approved).
Closed-end term loan originated for $1,200,000 (even if approved for $1,300,000 initially): report amount approved/originated = $1,200,000 (amount originated).
Application is withdrawn by applicant before closing: report amount approved/originated = “not applicable.”
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination and potential enforcement action for inaccurate or incomplete small business lending application register reporting

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Highest approved amount must be reported when credit is approved but not accepted and multiple approval amounts exist
business_rules.description: When a covered credit transaction is approved but not accepted and there are multiple approval amounts during the approval process, the institution must report the highest amount approved for the § 1002.107(a)(8) amount approved/originated data point, because it most accurately reflects the amount of credit made available to the applicant in that scenario.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications that are approved but not accepted (i.e., the credit does not close) where the institution has generated multiple approval amounts (e.g., revised term sheets or negotiated approvals).
business_rules.consequences: If the institution reports a lower intermediate approval amount instead of the highest approved amount, the 1071 register data may understate offered credit, creating data quality issues and supervisory criticism risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; institution must follow commentary guidance for multiple approval amounts for approved-not-accepted outcomes.

source_reference.section_id: Comment 107(a)(8)-2 (Final Rule discussion excerpt)
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 107(a)(8)-2 provides guidance on reporting the amount approved or originated data point when the transaction involves multiple approval amounts. The Bureau believes that reporting the highest amount approved when credit is approved but not accepted will most accurately reflect the amount of credit that was made available to the applicant

examples: Applicant is approved at $500,000, later re-approved at $450,000, then final re-approval at $550,000, but applicant does not accept: report $550,000.
Approval is increased from $1,000,000 to $1,250,000 during negotiations and the applicant declines: report $1,250,000.

Two products are approved at different limits and the institution treats them as multiple approval amounts for the same application outcome (approved not accepted): report the highest approved amount among those approvals for that application.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination for inaccurate amount approved/originated reporting

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 33_001 defines whether to report credit limit (open-end), amount originated (closed-end), or not applicable (denied/withdrawn/incomplete). 33_002 only applies after determining that the application is approved but not accepted and there are multiple approval amounts—i.e., within the 33_001 data point framework.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 33_001 logic is not applied correctly, 33_002 cannot be applied correctly; if 33_002 is ignored, the institution may underreport offered credit, creating 1071 data integrity issues.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Export Express piggyback structure is prohibited when SBA-guaranteed loan has junior or no lien to collateral securing non-guaranteed loan approved within 90 days
business_rules.description: For SBA Export Express (7(a)) loans, the lender must not structure an SBA-guaranteed loan in a piggyback structure that creates a preference for the lender. Piggyback financing occurs when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single borrower at or about the same time (interpreted as approved within 90 days of each other), financing the same or similar purpose, and where the SBA-guaranteed loan is secured with a junior lien position or no lien position on the collateral securing the non-guaranteed loan(s).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Export Express requests under delegated authority when the borrower is receiving multiple loans for the same/similar purpose and collateral/lien position is split between guaranteed and non-guaranteed loans.
business_rules.consequences: If structured as prohibited piggyback financing, SBA may determine the lender established an improper preference and may deny liability on the guaranty or take adverse lender oversight action.
business_rules.exceptions: Not piggyback: (1) both SBA-guaranteed and non-SBA-guaranteed loans are for working capital and the non-SBA loan is secured only by working/trading assets; (2) a shared (pari passu) lien position is not piggyback when the non-SBA-guaranteed loan maturity is not shorter than the SBA-guaranteed loan maturity.

source_reference.section_id: Export Express > Adequacy of Collateral: Preference and Piggyback Financing (13 CFR 120.411)
source_reference.source_text: A Lender may not take any action in connection with an SBA-guaranteed loan that establishes a preference in favor of the Lender (13 CFR § 120.411). The Lender must not have a 7(a) loan in a “piggyback” structure. ... SBA considers “at or about the same time” to mean loans approved within 90 days of each other. ... SBA does not consider a shared lien position with the lender (pari passu) to be a piggyback structure when the maturity of the non-SBA guaranteed loan is not shorter than the maturity of the SBA-guaranteed loan.

examples: Borrower gets a non-SBA $2MM loan secured by first lien on equipment and real estate; within 60 days borrower also gets SBA Export Express loan for same expansion secured by second lien/no lien on that collateral: prohibited piggyback.
Borrower receives SBA Export Express working capital line and a separate non-SBA working capital line secured only by inventory/receivables: not piggyback (exception applies).
SBA Export Express term loan and a non-SBA term loan share pari passu first lien on collateral, and the non-SBA maturity is equal to or longer than SBA loan maturity: not piggyback.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of guaranty purchase or denial of liability; lender oversight criticism

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 19_009 addresses when collateral is required/optional and when lender follows its written non-SBA collateral policy for Export Express. 33_010 prohibits piggyback preference structures for Export Express. Together they constrain collateral selection and lien structuring for Export Express.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: A lender may satisfy collateral coverage requirements (e.g., standby LOC minimum collateral) but still violate piggyback preference prohibitions; this can lead to SBA guaranty denial/liability issues.

business_rules.rule_name: Tax assessment valuations cannot be used as the sole basis for an evaluation without demonstrated correlation to market value and required supplemental support
business_rules.description: An institution may not rely solely on data provided by local tax authorities to develop an evaluation unless the resulting evaluation is consistent with safe and sound banking practices and the appraisal/evaluation guidelines. Because tax assessment valuation (TAV) analytical methods generally need additional support, institutions must have policies and procedures specifying the level and extent of supplemental information needed to develop an evaluation and must require an alternate valuation method when the supplemental information does not support the transaction. Use of a TAV is acceptable only when the institution can demonstrate a valid correlation between tax assessment data and market value, including documenting how the jurisdiction calculates TAV and revaluation frequency, analyzing the relationship between TAV and market values within the jurisdiction, testing and documenting correlation based on contemporaneous sales at the time of assessment, and revalidating whether the correlation remains stable as of the effective date of the evaluation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution develops a real estate evaluation for a transaction and proposes to use local tax assessment data as an input or basis for the evaluation value conclusion.
business_rules.consequences: If correlation is not demonstrated/stable or supplemental support is insufficient, the institution must use an alternate valuation method (e.g., appraisal or other evaluation approach) rather than relying solely on tax authority data; failure may result in valuation noncompliance and unsafe/unsound practice findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; institutions may still use TAV as a component if supported and correlated as described.

source_reference.section_id: Appendix C > Tax Assessment Valuations (TAVs)
source_reference.source_text: An institution may not rely solely on the data provided by local tax authorities to develop an evaluation unless the resulting evaluation is consistent with safe and sound banking practices... institutions should develop policies and procedures that specify the level and extent of supplemental information... require the use of an alternate valuation method... An institution may use a TAV... when it can demonstrate that a valid correlation exists between the tax assessment data and the market value.

examples: A bank uses assessed value alone for a $2MM CRE refinance without correlation testing. Result: prohibited; must obtain supplemental support and demonstrate correlation or use alternate valuation.

A bank documents jurisdiction revaluation frequency and tests TAV-to-sale-price correlation using contemporaneous sales, then revalidates stability as of evaluation date. Result: may use TAV-supported evaluation.
A market experiences volatility and TAV correlation deteriorates. Result: policy should require alternate valuation method.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for appraisal/evaluation guideline noncompliance and unsafe and unsound valuation practices

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Leasehold and leased-fee financing must follow supervisory loan-to-value limits including 65% land advance cap and 80% to 85% commercial property SLTV caps based on interest type and construction status
business_rules.description: For ground lease/leasehold structures, supervisory loan-to-value limits apply by interest type and stage. When financing the ground lessor’s leased-fee interest, the bank may lend up to the lower of 65% of market value or cost of the land for acquisition, then fund up to the appropriate supervisory loan-to-value (SLTV) of the market value of the borrower’s leased-fee interest upon completed construction (e.g., commercial property SLTV limit of 85% upon completion). When financing the tenant’s leasehold improvements, the maximum SLTV for construction is up to the relevant SLTV of the market value of the leasehold interest for construction (e.g., 80% for a commercial property), and once construction is complete, the appropriate SLTV for the completed property type (e.g., 85% for commercial property). When holding a junior lien, the sum of the debt and all senior liens should not exceed the relevant SLTV using fee-simple market value.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to CRE loans secured by leased-fee or leasehold interests and to junior lien positions secured by real estate where supervisory loan-to-value limits are used for risk management/capital purposes.
business_rules.consequences: If calculated SLTV exceeds the relevant threshold (e.g., 65%, 80%, 85% as applicable), the institution should not book the exposure without mitigating structure (e.g., additional equity, reduced advance rate, additional collateral) and should expect supervisory criticism if SLTV guideline limits are exceeded without documented justification per real estate lending policy and appraisal/evaluation requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt provides commercial property examples; institutions must map the correct SLTV limits by property type per the applicable SLTV table referenced (appendix C).

source_reference.section_id: Comptroller’s Handbook Appendix D > Ground lease / leasehold considerations (leasehold lender; SLTV examples)
source_reference.source_text: provisions for a notice to the bank of tenant default under the ground lease and give the lender the right, but not the obligation, to cure any defaults. When the bank finances the ground lessor’s leased -fee interest, the bank may lend up to the lower of 65 percent of market value or cost of the

examples: A bank finances land acquisition for leased-fee interest at 70% of market value. Result: exceeds 65% cap; reduce advance or require more equity.
A leasehold commercial construction loan is structured at 78% of leasehold interest market value during construction. Result: within the 80% construction example cap.
A junior lien CRE loan plus senior lien totals 90% of fee-simple market value where relevant SLTV is 85%. Result: should be reduced to meet SLTV guideline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential adverse classification for unsafe and unsound real estate lending practices and guideline SLTV exceedances

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple-unit ADC loans should require accelerated unit release prices and paydowns designed to repay before all units are sold
business_rules.description: For construction loans financing multiple units or phases, loan agreements should be structured for repayment to follow unit sales, requiring adequate paydowns as collateral is sold and liens are released. A common control is an accelerated release price so the bank is fully repaid before all units are sold. The release price is typically calculated as: release price per unit = (total loan amount / total units) × acceleration factor, where acceleration factor = (total units / units required for payoff). Example: for 100 lots, $1.5MM loan, payoff by 80th lot: acceleration factor 100/80 = 1.25 (125%), release price = ($1,500,000/100) × 1.25 = $18,750 per lot (if equal value).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to CRE construction/ADC loans involving multiple-unit developments (e.g., lot development, condominiums) where collateral is released unit-by-unit upon sale.
business_rules.consequences: If release prices and paydown requirements are not adequate, the bank may remain under-collateralized late in the project and face higher loss severity if sales slow or prices fall. Examiners may criticize weak structural protections and inadequate repayment controls.
business_rules.exceptions: Where unit values differ, separate release prices can be established for each unit/lot. The specific acceleration target (e.g., payoff by 75% or 80% of units sold) is policy/underwriting-driven; not prescribed as a fixed percentage in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: ADC policies; acceleration and release price example
source_reference.source_text: For example, assume a developer is developing 100 single-family lots... The bank agrees to lend $1.5 million (75 percent of the appraised value of the project) and wants to be fully paid with the sales of 80 percent, or 80, of the lots. To be fully paid with the sale of the 80th lot, the construction loan agreement would specify a release price of 125 percent (100/80). If the lots were equal in value, the release price would be calculated as follows: $1,500,000/100 = $15,000 x 125% = $18,750.

examples: 100-lot project, $1.5MM loan, target payoff by 80 lots: acceleration 1.25; release price per lot $18,750 (equal-lot assumption).
100-unit condo project, $10MM loan, target payoff by 75 units: acceleration 100/75 = 1.34; average proportional debt $100,000; release price ≈ $134,000 per unit (before adjusting for varying values).
Lots have different values: bank sets higher release prices for premium lots and lower for standard lots but still targets full payoff before all releases.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Examination criticism for weak repayment controls and unsafe ADC loan structuring

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_COVENANT_36_010 requires adequate paydowns as units are sold and liens released (often using accelerated release prices). BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_38_007 requires appraisals for 5+ unit developments to analyze absorption periods and apply holding/marketing/entrepreneurial profit deductions supported by data. Those appraisal outputs inform whether the release price/paydown schedule is truly protective over the sellout period.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If appraisal absorption/discounting is weak, release prices may be set too low, increasing tail-end under-collateralization and examiner criticism.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 financing for a new business and limited or special purpose property requires 20% borrower contribution and limits debenture to 30%
business_rules.description: If an SBA 504 Project will finance both a New Business and a Limited or Special Purpose Property, the Applicant must contribute at least 20% of the Project cost. In that case, the SBA debenture will finance no more than 30% of the Project, and at least 50% of the Project financing must come from state or local government, banks or other financial institutions, foundations, or other non-profit institutions. The additional borrower contribution reduces SBA’s portion of the financing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 transactions where project characteristics include BOTH (i) New Business and (ii) Limited or Special Purpose Property.
business_rules.consequences: If borrower contribution is <20%, or debenture would exceed 30%, or third-party/other-eligible financing is <50%, the project financing structure is noncompliant with SBA policy and must be restructured or declined for SBA 504 processing.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 – Limited/Special Purpose Property + New Business financing structure
source_reference.source_text: If a Project will finance both a New Business and a Limited or Special Purpose Property, the Applicant must contribute at least 20% of the Project cost. The Debenture will finance no more than 30% of the Project and at least 50% of the Project financing will be from state or local government, banks or other financial institutions, foundations or other non-profit institutions. The additional Borrower’s contribution will reduce the SBA’s portion of the financing.

examples: Project is a new hotel (limited/special purpose) for a start-up operator: borrower proposes 15% equity → not allowed; must increase to ≥20% or restructure/decline.
Project qualifies as both new business and special purpose property; proposed 35% debenture, 45% bank, 20% borrower → debenture exceeds 30% → restructure.
Project: borrower 20%, debenture 30%, bank 50% → meets the financing split requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA authorization/guaranty denial; SBA repair; regulatory criticism

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_CREDIT_POLICY_38_005 applies only to SBA 504 processing; SBA 504 processing requires that the Applicant be eligible under 13 CFR 120.110/120.111 tests addressed in BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_38_003.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If eligibility is not satisfied, the bank/CDC must not submit/approve under SBA; any attempt to structure the deal under 38_005 risks guaranty denial and exam criticism for proceeding with an ineligible Applicant.

business_rules.rule_name: Borrowed borrower contribution on SBA 504 must be subordinate and cannot amortize faster than the 504 loan when secured by project property
business_rules.description: If the SBA 504 borrower’s required contribution is borrowed: (a) any lien position on the Project Property must be subordinate to the 504 loan; (b) if (and only if) the borrowed contribution is collateralized by the Project Property, the borrower may not pay the loan for its contribution at a faster rate than the 504 loan unless approved in writing by the D/FA or designee (13 CFR 120.912); and (c) if the borrowed contribution is collateralized by assets other than the Project Property, the borrower must demonstrate repayment of the contribution loan from business cash flow or other sources.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans where the borrower contribution (equity injection requirement) is sourced from borrowed funds.
business_rules.consequences: If the contribution financing lien is not subordinate, or if repayment is structured to be faster than the 504 loan without written approval (when secured by project property), or if repayment ability is not demonstrated (when secured by non-project assets), the loan structure is noncompliant and must be modified or declined for SBA 504 processing.
business_rules.exceptions: Faster repayment of a contribution loan secured by project property may be permitted if approved in writing by the D/FA or designee.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 – Borrowed borrower contribution requirements (13 CFR 120.912)
source_reference.source_text: If the Borrower’s contribution is borrowed: a) Any lien position on the Project Property must be subordinate to the 504 loan; b) Only in situations where the borrowed contribution is collateralized by the Project Property, Borrower may not pay the loan for its contribution at a faster rate than the 504 loan (13 CFR § 120.912) unless it is approved in writing by the D/FA or designee; and c) If the borrowed contribution is collateralized by assets other than the Project Property, the Borrower must demonstrate repayment...

examples: Borrower takes a second mortgage on the project property to fund its 10% contribution; the second lien is behind the 504 loan and amortizes on the same schedule → compliant (subject to other SBA rules).
Borrower contribution loan is secured by the project property but is structured as a 3-year note while the 504 debenture is 20 years → noncompliant unless written D/FA approval is obtained.

Borrower uses an unsecured personal loan (not secured by project property) as contribution; borrower provides evidence that business cash flow will service both loans → must demonstrate repayment capacity as required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA authorization/guaranty denial; SBA repair; regulatory criticism

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_CREDIT_POLICY_38_005 sets a 20% minimum borrower contribution and specific 30%/50% financing stack constraints for certain 504 projects. If the borrower meets that contribution via borrowed funds, BR_CRE_LOAN_CREDIT_POLICY_38_006 imposes lien subordination and repayment-structure restrictions depending on collateral type.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 38_006 is not applied when contribution is borrowed, the financing stack may violate SBA lien priority/repayment rules, risking SBA processing denial or required restructuring.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_CREDIT_POLICY_38_006 is an SBA 504 structural rule for borrowed borrower contribution. If the business is ineligible under 38_003, the SBA 504 loan cannot proceed and 38_006 cannot be applied as a valid mitigant/structure.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 38_003 is not met, the lender must stop SBA processing; continuing to structure borrowed equity subordination/repayment rules is non-actionable and risks compliance findings.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraisals for developments with five or more units must include holding and marketing costs and entrepreneurial profit discounts and apply a 12-month feasibility exception for individual-unit financing
business_rules.description: For tract developments with unsold units and for proposed construction and sale developments of five units or more (including attached/detached single-family homes and condominium buildings), the appraisal must analyze and report appropriate deductions and discounts—reflecting holding costs, marketing costs, and entrepreneurial profit—supported by market data and tied to the sales absorption period. The appraisal report must explain how entrepreneurial profit is treated (as a deduction line item and/or within the discount rate) and support projected sales prices and absorption rate by anticipated demand and competing supply. If an institution finances construction on an individual-unit basis in a development of five or more homes/units, it may use appraisals of the individual units only if it can demonstrate through an independently obtained feasibility study or market analysis that all units collateralizing the loan can be constructed and sold within 12 months. If any individual units are not completed and sold within the 12-month timeframe, the transaction should be supported by an appraisal that analyzes and reports appropriate deductions and discounts.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to real estate-secured lending where collateral is part of (or will be part of) a single development of five units or more, including tract developments, subdivisions (developed lots), 5+ single-family home developments, and 5+ unit condominiums—especially where units may be sold over an absorption period or where financing is on an individual-unit basis.
business_rules.consequences: If the appraisal omits required deductions/discounts or lacks required explanation/support for absorption and demand, collateral valuation is noncompliant with interagency appraisal guidance and should be remediated (e.g., obtain a revised appraisal or additional appraisal analysis). If relying on individual-unit appraisals without an independently obtained feasibility/market analysis demonstrating construction and sale of all units within 12 months, the institution should not rely on individual-unit appraisals for valuation support.
business_rules.exceptions: Individual-unit appraisals are permitted for individual-unit construction financing in 5+ unit developments only when supported by an independently obtained feasibility study or market analysis showing all units collateralizing the loan can be constructed and sold within 12 months.

source_reference.section_id: Appendix C – Tract developments and 12-month feasibility study exception for individual-unit financing (5+ units)
source_reference.source_text: for the raw land. • Developed Lots. For existing or proposed developments of five or more residential lots in a single development, the appraiser must analyze and report appropriate deductions and

examples: Bank finances a 20-unit condo construction project: appraisal must include absorption-based holding/marketing costs and entrepreneurial profit adjustments; a simple ‘as-complete’ value without discounts is insufficient.

Bank funds five spec homes with separate notes; relies on individual home appraisals and provides an independently obtained market analysis showing all five will be built and sold within 12 months → permissible reliance on individual-unit appraisals.

Same five-home project: after 12 months, two homes remain unsold → transaction should be supported by an appraisal that analyzes and reports appropriate deductions and discounts (not just unit appraisals).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism in safety-and-soundness examination; appraisal/valuation finding requiring corrective action

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 31_005 triggers obtaining an appraisal in SBA 504; 38_007 dictates additional appraisal analysis and reporting for 5+ unit development/sale scenarios. Together they determine whether the appraisal is acceptable for reliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 38_007 requirements are not met in an appraisal obtained due to 31_005, the appraisal is deficient and the lender should not rely on it until cured (revision/additional analysis).

dependencies.rationale: 29_010 governs whether an appraisal is required for OCC-regulated banks and when evaluations are permitted. If an appraisal is required and the collateral is a 5+ unit development or tract/subdivision scenario, 38_007 dictates required appraisal analyses and disclosures.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Using an appraisal that omits 38_007-required deductions/absorption analysis creates valuation noncompliance and weakens collateral protection, potentially triggering supervisory criticism.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraisal is not required when transaction value is $250,000 or less but an evaluation is required unless another exemption applies
business_rules.description: Under the Agencies’ appraisal regulations described in the guidance, an appraisal is not required for transactions with a transaction value of $250,000 or less. When an appraisal is not required and no other appraisal exemption applies, the institution must obtain an evaluation of the real property collateral.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to real-estate-related transactions subject to the Agencies’ appraisal regulations where transaction value is $250,000 or less.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution does not obtain an appraisal (because transaction value is ≤ $250,000) and fails to obtain a required evaluation when no other exemption applies, the institution may be cited for appraisal/valuation noncompliance and must remediate by obtaining a compliant evaluation (or appraisal if required by other factors).
business_rules.exceptions: If another appraisal exemption applies, the evaluation requirement is determined under the applicable exemption framework (not detailed in this excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Appendix D Glossary – Appraisal Threshold
source_reference.source_text: Appraisal Threshold – An appraisal is not required on transactions with a transaction value of $250,000 or less. As specified in the Agencies’ appraisal regulations, an institution must obtain an evaluation of the real property collateral, if no other appraisal exemption applies.

examples: Loan secured by commercial real estate with transaction value of $240,000 → appraisal not required by threshold; must obtain an evaluation unless another exemption applies.
Loan modification increases transaction value to $260,000 → threshold no longer applies; appraisal requirement must be re-evaluated under applicable rules.
Transaction value is $250,000 exactly → within threshold; appraisal not required by threshold, but evaluation required if no exemption.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism; MRIA/MRA for appraisal program weakness; corrective action to obtain evaluation

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_38_008 establishes a general regulatory appraisal threshold (≤$250k) where an appraisal is not required. BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_18_001 imposes a stricter SBA requirement for Standard 7(a) loans secured by CRE, effectively disallowing reliance on the general threshold.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution applies BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_38_008 to skip an appraisal on a 7(a) CRE-secured loan, it will create an SBA collateral compliance defect and increase guaranty repair/denial risk.

dependencies.rationale: 38_008 is a general appraisal-reg threshold concept. 31_005 is SBA 504-specific and dictates appraisal necessity for SBA package acceptance. For SBA 504, the program rule takes precedence for SBA compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If staff follow 38_008 logic alone and fail to obtain an SBA-required appraisal, SBA processing/closing is delayed or rejected.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA environmental indemnitor must have financial capacity and cannot be the applicant or operating company
business_rules.description: When environmental contamination remediation is incomplete and the lender seeks SBA consideration of approval or disbursement before completion, indemnification may be used only if a third-party indemnitor with sufficient financial resources executes the SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement. The SBA lender must analyze the proposed indemnitor’s assets/resources to confirm ability to honor the indemnity. The third-party indemnitor is prohibited from being the Applicant or the operating company.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loans where SBA is deciding whether to approve or disburse before completion of Remediation or monitoring, and the lender relies on the mitigating factor 'Indemnification'.
business_rules.consequences: If the indemnitor is the Applicant/operating company or lacks sufficient resources (based on lender analysis), SBA approval/disbursement may not be considered under the indemnification mitigating factor; lender must use other mitigating factors or delay until remediation/monitoring conditions are satisfied.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Indemnitor must be a Third Party Indemnitor (not Applicant or operating company).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5: Other Core Requirements > Indemnification > Mitigating Factors (Indemnification)
source_reference.source_text: Indemnification. If any Person (as defined in Appendix 4) who possesses sufficient financial resources to cover the costs of completing Remediation executes the SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement in Appendix 8, approval or disbursement may be considered. The SBA Lender must conduct an analysis of the proposed indemnitor to ensure that it has sufficient assets to honor an indemnification agreement. The Third Party Indemnitor cannot be the Applicant or the operating company.

examples: 7(a) loan collateral has contamination; borrower proposes its operating company as indemnitor. Not allowed—indemnitor cannot be Applicant or operating company; SBA cannot rely on this indemnification factor.

504 project has remediation not complete; a parent holding company with audited financials showing ample liquidity executes Appendix 8; lender documents analysis of capacity—SBA may consider disbursement under this factor.
Borrower proposes an individual indemnitor with limited assets relative to remediation estimate; lender analysis shows insufficient resources—cannot rely on indemnification mitigating factor.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA loan funding delay; regulatory criticism for SBA SOP non-compliance

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 13_010 requires documented NRHP due diligence and Section 106 steps when triggered. 40_001 addresses reliance on indemnification when remediation is incomplete. In many property purchase/renovation transactions, both tracks can be triggered and must be coordinated to prevent approval/disbursement delays.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If historic diligence is missed while environmental indemnification is pursued (or vice versa), SBA may delay/deny approval/disbursement and require additional documentation/clearances.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement must be unmodified, include investigation report, and be recorded in required memorandum format
business_rules.description: If using the SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement (Appendix 8) as a mitigating factor, the agreement (a) cannot be modified, (b) must be executed by the Applicant and, if applicable, the Operating Company, (c) must have a copy of the Environmental Investigation Report attached, and (d) must be properly recorded in the memorandum format in Exhibit C to Appendix 8.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an SBA lender/CDC uses the SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement (Appendix 8) in connection with remediation/monitoring timing and SBA consideration of disbursement/approval.
business_rules.consequences: If modified, missing required signatures, missing the Environmental Investigation Report attachment, or not recorded in Exhibit C memorandum format, the indemnification document package is noncompliant and SBA review/approval and/or funding may be delayed or denied; potential SBA guaranty repair/denial risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5 > The SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement requirements
source_reference.source_text: The SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement: a) Cannot be modified; b) Must be executed by the Applicant and (if applicable) Operating Company; c) Must have a copy of the Environmental Investigation Report attached to it; and d) Must be properly recorded in the memorandum format in Exhibit C to Appendix 8.

examples: Lender edits Appendix 8 indemnification wording to add carve-outs—noncompliant because the agreement cannot be modified.
Operating company is a separate entity but does not sign the Appendix 8 agreement—noncompliant; required execution by Operating Company if applicable.
Agreement is signed but Environmental Investigation Report is not attached—noncompliant; attach report before submission/recording.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA funding delay; SBA guaranty denial/repair; post-closing findings requiring cure

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_40_001 states indemnification may be used only if a third-party indemnitor executes the SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement and prohibits that indemnitor from being the Applicant/Operating Company. BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_40_002 states the Appendix 8 agreement must be executed by the Applicant and (if applicable) the Operating Company. If both refer to the same document, they cannot both be satisfied as written.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: This conflict can cause document defects, SBA rejection/delay, or incorrect reliance on indemnification as a mitigating factor, elevating guaranty repair/denial risk.

business_rules.rule_name: 7(a) lenders must submit finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement to SBA before SBA funding request unless processed under PLP, 7(a) Small Loans, SBA Express, or Export Express
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, lenders must submit the finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement to SBA for review and approval prior to requesting SBA to fund the loan, except when the request is submitted through PLP, 7(a) Small Loans, SBA Express, or Export Express.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans using the SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement (Appendix 8) and where the lender will request SBA funding.
business_rules.consequences: If submission/review/approval is not completed before the SBA funding request (and no exception program applies), SBA may delay or deny funding and the loan may face guaranty compliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception when submitting requests through PLP, 7(a) Small Loans, SBA Express, and Export Express.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5 > For 7(a) loans submission requirement
source_reference.source_text: For 7(a) loans: All 7(a) Lenders (except when submitting requests through PLP, 7(a) Small Loans, SBA Express and Export Express) must submit the finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement to SBA for review and approval prior to a request that SBA fund the loan.

examples: Standard 7(a) loan (not PLP/Express) uses Appendix 8; lender requests SBA funding without SBA review—noncompliant and may delay funding.
SBA Express loan uses Appendix 8; the stated submission requirement exception applies—lender is not required by this provision to submit prior to funding request.
PLP processed loan uses Appendix 8; exception applies under this provision, but lender still must ensure document meets Appendix 8 requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA funding delay; SBA guaranty denial/repair; SBA procedural noncompliance finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_40_003 requires submitting the finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement before requesting SBA funding (subject to exceptions). BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_40_002 defines what constitutes a compliant, finalized Appendix 8 package (no modifications, required signatures, Environmental Investigation Report attached, proper recording format).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the agreement is not compliant/finalized per 40_002, submission under 40_003 will fail or be delayed, jeopardizing funding timelines and increasing guaranty repair/denial risk.

business_rules.rule_name: 504 CDC must submit finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement to SLPC at least two weeks before closing package to be considered in that closing cycle
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, all CDCs (including PCLP CDCs) must submit each finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement (Appendix 8) to the SLPC for review and approval no less than 2 weeks in advance of submission of the loan closing package if the loan is to be considered in that closing cycle.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans using Appendix 8 indemnification and where the CDC wants the loan included in a given closing cycle.
