business_rules.consequences: If submitted fewer than 14 days before the closing package submission, SLPC review/approval may not occur in time and the loan may not be considered in the targeted closing cycle; closing delays may result.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5 > For 504 loans submission timing requirement
source_reference.source_text: For 504 loans: All CDCs, including PCLP CDCs, must submit each finalized SBA Environmental Indemnification Agreement (located in Appendix 8 of this SOP) to the SLPC for review and approval no less than 2 weeks in advance of submission of the loan closing package if they want the loan to be considered in that closing cycle.

examples: CDC submits Appendix 8 to SLPC 10 days before closing package—misses the 'no less than 2 weeks' requirement; closing cycle consideration may be delayed.
CDC submits Appendix 8 20 days before closing package—meets timing requirement for that closing cycle.
PCLP CDC assumes different timing applies—incorrect; requirement explicitly includes PCLP CDCs.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Closing cycle delay; SBA processing delay; procedural noncompliance finding

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_40_004 requires submitting each finalized Appendix 8 to SLPC at least 2 weeks before the closing package submission for inclusion in that cycle. BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_40_002 defines execution/attachment/recording requirements necessary for the document to be treated as finalized.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Defects under 40_002 prevent timely SLPC review and likely cause closing cycle slippage and package resubmission.

business_rules.rule_name: Environmental remediation escrow must be at least 150% of estimated cost, exclude SBA loan proceeds, and restrict disbursements to remediation only
business_rules.description: Where an escrow account is used as a mitigating factor for environmental remediation timing, the escrow must equal at least 150% of the total estimated required remediation cost and be controlled by the 7(a) lender or, for 504 loans, the first mortgage holder as trustee. The governmental entity must concur with the remediation scope. The escrow agreement must ensure escrow funds are used only for remediation costs. The escrow funding source cannot be SBA loan proceeds. Remaining escrow funds cannot be released until an appropriate closure letter/no further action letter is received, or (for monitoring) when all monitoring wells related to the property have been decommissioned.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the lender seeks SBA approval/disbursement consideration before completion of remediation and relies on the 'Escrow Account' mitigating factor.
business_rules.consequences: If escrow is <150%, funded with SBA loan proceeds, not properly restricted, or released prematurely, the escrow mitigating factor is not satisfied; SBA may decline to consider disbursement/approval prior to remediation completion and the lender may face SBA guaranty compliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Escrow >150% may be appropriate depending on circumstances (discretionary).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5 > Mitigating Factors > Escrow Account
source_reference.source_text: vi. Escrow Account. If an escrow account is available that equals a minimum of 150 percent of the total estimated cost of required Remediation and is controlled by a 7(a) Lender or first mortgage holder in a 504 loan as trustee, approval or disbursement may be considered... The escrow agreement for the escrow account must ensure that escrow funds will only be used for Remediation costs. The source of the escrow funds may not be SBA Loan proceeds.

examples: Estimated remediation cost is $200,000; escrow is funded at $250,000 (125%)—noncompliant; must be at least $300,000 (150%).
Escrow is $450,000 on a $250,000 estimate (180%) controlled by 7(a) lender; agreement restricts use to remediation only and government concurs with scope—meets core escrow requirements.
Borrower wants to fund escrow using SBA loan proceeds—prohibited; must use non-SBA-loan-proceeds funding source.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA funding delay/denial; SBA guaranty denial/repair; collateral/escrow control criticism

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: 13_010 governs historic eligibility/Section 106 workflow; 40_005 governs escrow mitigation for incomplete remediation timing. For SBA property purchase/renovation, both can condition closing/disbursement and need coordinated compliance sequencing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Satisfying escrow terms without historic clearance (or historic clearance without acceptable escrow/remediation posture) can still block approval/disbursement.

business_rules.rule_name: Escrow trustee role is limited to releasing funds upon satisfactory remediation completion and lender must not control or manage the remediated property
business_rules.description: When a lender (or for 504 loans, the third-party lender) serves as trustee for an environmental remediation escrow account, the trustee’s role is solely to release funds upon satisfactory completion of remediation work. The lender/third-party lender must not control or manage the property being remediated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when using an escrow account mitigating factor and the lender/first mortgage holder serves as trustee.
business_rules.consequences: If the trustee lender exercises control/management over the property beyond releasing escrow funds for completed work, the arrangement violates SBA requirements and may create ineligible control/operational risk; SBA may object and funding/guaranty support may be at risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5 > Mitigating Factors > Escrow Account (Note)
source_reference.source_text: Note: The SBA Lender’s, or for 504 loans, the Third Party Lender’s role as trustee of the escrow account is solely to release funds upon the satisfactory completion of Remediation work – the SBA Lender or Third Party Lender must not control or manage the Property being remediated.

examples: Lender as escrow trustee insists on hiring and directing the remediation contractor—impermissible control/management beyond releasing funds.
Third-party lender (504) releases escrow draws only after receiving engineer sign-off of completed milestones—consistent with limited trustee role.
Lender monitors documentation and authorizes disbursement but does not operate or manage the site—permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA procedural objection; funding delay; SBA guaranty denial/repair risk

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_40_005 establishes escrow-account mitigating factor requirements (150% funding, control, permitted uses, restrictions). When the lender/third-party lender is trustee, BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_40_006 further restricts the trustee role to releasing funds upon satisfactory completion and prohibits property control/management.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 40_006 is violated, the escrow mitigating factor may be rejected by SBA and can create ineligible control/operational risk, potentially affecting funding/guaranty support.

business_rules.rule_name: Reliance solely on other environmental mitigating factors requires clearance from the SBA Environmental Committee including delegated processing
business_rules.description: SBA may rely on factors other than (or in addition to) listed mitigating factors when considering approval or disbursement (e.g., environmental insurance, bonds, agreements not to sue, brownfields agreements, controls). However, if the lender seeks to rely solely on 'Other Factor(s)', clearance from the SBA Environmental Committee is required, and this requirement applies to both delegated and non-delegated procedures.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the lender/CDC proposes to base SBA approval/disbursement consideration solely on 'Other Factor(s)' rather than the enumerated mitigating factors.
business_rules.consequences: Without SBA Environmental Committee clearance, reliance solely on Other Factor(s) is not permitted; SBA approval/disbursement consideration may be delayed or denied until cleared or alternate mitigating factor(s) are documented.
business_rules.exceptions: If Other Factor(s) are used in addition to (not solely) the enumerated factors, committee clearance requirement stated here does not apply (as written).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 5 > Mitigating Factors > Other Factor(s)
source_reference.source_text: ix. Other Factor(s). The SBA Lender and SBA may rely on factors other than or in addition to the eight referenced above when considering approval or disbursement... However, reliance solely upon “Other Factor(s)” requires clearance from the SBA Environmental Committee. This requirement extends to loans processed under delegated and non-delegated procedures.

examples: Lender proposes relying only on existing environmental insurance paying remediation costs—must obtain SBA Environmental Committee clearance before SBA will rely solely on this factor.
Lender uses both an escrow account (150% rule met) and environmental insurance—committee clearance is not triggered by this provision because reliance is not solely on Other Factor(s).
Delegated processing (e.g., delegated authority channel) still requires Environmental Committee clearance if relying solely on Other Factor(s).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA approval/funding delay; SBA guaranty denial/repair risk; SBA environmental procedural noncompliance

examples: Nonfarm nonresidential loan where 65% of repayment is from nonaffiliated tenant rents
Land development and construction loan dependent on sale/refinance proceeds

business_rules.rule_name: Multiple SBA 7(a) loans approved within 90 days for an applicant and affiliates must be aggregated for upfront fee tier and guaranty percentage
business_rules.description: If more than one SBA 7(a) loan (with maturities exceeding 12 months) is approved for an Applicant, including loans approved to its affiliates, within 90 days of each other, the loans are considered as one loan for purposes of determining the percentage of guaranty and the SBA Upfront Fee calculation, regardless of whether the loans were approved by the same or different 7(a) Lenders. For two or more 7(a) loans approved within 90 calendar days, the upfront fee for subsequent loan(s) equals: (Upfront fee that would have been charged if all loans were combined into one) minus (upfront fee from the first loan approved). If the applicant receives both a short-term and long-term 7(a) loan, the guaranty percentage is calculated as if combined, but the upfront fee is based on the maturity of each loan. The 90-day aggregation rule also applies to subsequent increases to either loan made within the 90-day period, even if one loan is later paid in full.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (a) there are multiple SBA 7(a) loan approvals for the same applicant or its affiliates within a rolling 90-calendar-day window, and (b) at least one loan has maturity > 12 months (for aggregation of upfront fee tier/guaranty percentage).
business_rules.consequences: Lender(s) must recalculate the appropriate upfront fee tier and fee amounts for subsequent loans based on combined approvals within 90 days. Misapplication can cause fee underpayment/overpayment and SBA compliance issues, potentially affecting guaranty enforceability/purchase.
business_rules.exceptions: Short-term loans are not included in the calculation that modifies E-Tran upfront fee calculation for other loans; however, where both short and long-term loans exist, guaranty percentage is calculated as if combined while upfront fees remain maturity-based per loan.

source_reference.section_id: Upfront Fee Calculation for Multiple 7(a) Loans Within 90 Days
source_reference.source_text: If more than one 7(a) loan (with maturities exceeding 12 months) is approved for an Applicant, including loans approved to its affiliates, within 90 days of each other, the loans are considered as one loan... regardless of whether the loans were approved by the same or different 7(a) Lenders... applicable fee for the subsequent loans is equal to the Upfront Fee that would have been charged had all the loans been combined... less the amount of the fee from the first loan approved... This rule also applies to any subsequent increases... even if one of the loans subsequently is paid in full.

examples: Loan A: $200,000 approved June 1; Loan B: $600,000 approved July 15 for the same applicant. Because both are >12 months and within 90 days, determine fee tier as if $800,000 combined; charge Loan B the incremental fee (combined fee minus Loan A fee).
Affiliate loan: Parent company receives $300,000 loan and subsidiary receives $200,000 within 60 days. Treat as one for guaranty percentage and upfront fee tier purposes.

Applicant has one 10-month short-term loan and one 7-year loan approved within 90 days. Compute guaranty percentage as if combined, but compute each upfront fee based on each loan’s maturity category.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; potential repair/denial of guaranty purchase due to fee calculation errors

examples: Aggregation combining CRE term loans and interest rate swap credit exposure to the same developer group
Aggregation including affiliated borrower loans flagged as related exposure

dependencies.rationale: BR_BORROWER_EXPOSURE_AGGREGATION_REGULATORY_1_003 explicitly includes loans to affiliates in the 90-day aggregation. BR_BORROWER_EXPOSURE_AGGREGATION_REGULATORY_21_005 requires the lender to determine whether affiliates exist and document the result, enabling correct aggregation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If affiliation is not determined/documented, the lender may miss aggregation triggers, miscalculate upfront fees/guaranty tiers, and create SBA compliance/guaranty purchase risk.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) lenders must identify and document affiliates and apply SBA maximums across applicant and affiliates as one business
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade lending, lenders must determine whether the applicant has any affiliates and document the results in their credit analysis. If affiliation exists, SBA loan maximums apply to the applicant including all affiliates as if all were a single business.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when processing an SBA 7(a) International Trade loan and the applicant may have affiliates under SBA affiliation rules.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must aggregate applicant + affiliates when testing program maximums (e.g., max loan, max guaranty, working capital guarantee caps). Failure to aggregate/document may result in exceeding SBA limits and SBA guaranty eligibility risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited section.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade — Maximum Loans to Businesses with Affiliates (13 CFR § 120.151)
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must determine whether the Applicant has any affiliates and document the results in their credit analysis. If affiliation exists, SBA’s loan maximums apply to the Applicant, including all affiliates, as if all were a single business.

examples: Applicant requests an IT loan and owns 60% of another company that has an existing SBA 7(a) loan. Lender must treat both as one business when evaluating SBA maximums.
Applicant is controlled by a parent entity with multiple subsidiaries. Lender must document affiliate determination and aggregate SBA exposure for maximum guaranty testing.
Applicant certifies no affiliates; lender’s analysis finds common ownership/control that creates affiliation. Lender must document the finding and apply maximums to the combined group.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial at purchase; supervisory criticism for inadequate credit analysis documentation

examples: Aggregation combining CRE term loans and interest rate swap credit exposure to the same developer group
Aggregation including affiliated borrower loans flagged as related exposure

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) variable rate base rate must be Prime or SBA Optional Peg Rate and base rate rounding follows 0.004 down and 0.005 up
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) variable-rate loans, the base rate must be either (a) the Prime Rate or (b) the SBA Optional Peg Rate. Base rates must be rounded to two decimal places, with 0.004 rounded down to 0.00 and 0.005 rounded up to 0.01.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) variable-rate loans at origination and whenever base rate is referenced for adjustments; requires selection of a permitted base rate and correct rounding convention.
business_rules.consequences: If an unapproved base rate is used or rounding is applied incorrectly, the loan’s interest computation terms are inconsistent with SBA requirements and may require correction and borrower notification/amendment.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1 > Base Rate and spread
source_reference.source_text: There are two acceptable base rates: The Prime Rate; or The SBA Optional Peg Rate... Base Rates will be rounded to two decimal places with .004 being rounded down to .00 and .005 being rounded up to .01.

examples: A lender proposes SOFR as the base rate for an SBA 7(a) variable loan; not permitted because base rate must be Prime or Optional Peg.
Optional Peg Rate published as 5.005% rounds to 5.01% under SBA rounding guidance.
Prime rate published as 8.004% rounds to 8.00% under SBA rounding guidance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA oversight finding; potential required correction to note terms/servicing

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) lender may not split a loan into two loans to charge a higher interest rate
business_rules.description: An SBA 7(a) lender is prohibited from splitting a single credit request into two (or more) loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate to the applicant, including manipulating loan-size tiers that determine maximum allowable spreads/caps. If detected, the structure must be corrected/restructured into compliant terms and pricing and may trigger SBA oversight findings and guaranty repair/denial exposure.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) structuring decisions when a borrower request could be booked as one loan but is instead divided; focus is intent/purpose to increase interest rate.
business_rules.consequences: If detected, the loan structure is non-compliant with SBA requirements and may lead to SBA oversight findings, potential corrective action, and program participation consequences.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1 > Interest rate requirements
source_reference.source_text: The 7(a) Lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate to the Applicant.

source_reference.section_id: 7(a) CAPLines — Interest rate policy (Page 199)
source_reference.source_text: The 7(a) Lender may not split a loan into two loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate to the Applicant.

examples: A lender may not book $400,000 as two $200,000 loans to obtain a higher spread tier than would apply to a single $400,000 loan.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA oversight finding; potential suspension/revocation of delegated authority for pattern of noncompliance

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules prohibit splitting a single SBA 7(a) credit request into multiple loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate (i.e., bypassing SBA maximum interest rate limitations). Scope and consequence alignment are the same.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_regulatory_4_008 selected: most explicit about the manipulation purpose (evading tier-based maximum spreads) and provides illustrative framing.

dependencies.rationale: BR_BORROWER_EXPOSURE_AGGREGATION_REGULATORY_1_003 treats multiple 7(a) approvals within 90 days as one loan for guaranty percentage and upfront fee purposes. While aimed at fees/guaranty, it can validate whether multiple notes were used to structure around SBA limits (including interest-rate tiering concerns raised by BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_regulatory_4_008).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to aggregate can obscure de facto splitting and cause both fee miscalculations and undetected interest-rate-tier manipulation risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Borrower written consent and SBA notification required for interest rate term changes before and after disbursement
business_rules.description: Interest term changes on SBA 7(a) loans require borrower consent and must remain within SBA maximum-rate constraints tied to the application/approval timing. (1) Pre-disbursement: after SBA approval but before first disbursement, the lender may change the initial Note rate terms (base, spread, or fixed/variable selection) only if the new rate does not exceed the maximum allowable interest rate at the time of loan application, and only with the borrower’s written consent separate from executing loan documents; lender must notify LGPC or update through E-Tran/E-Tran Servicing as required. (2) Post-disbursement (variable-rate loans): the lender may change base rate or spread only if the method is permitted and consistent with SBA interest rules in effect at approval, with borrower written agreement, and with CLSC notification (email) or update via E-Tran Servicing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans with changes to note interest terms; bifurcated based on whether change occurs (a) pre-first-disbursement or (b) post-disbursement; post-disbursement guidance in cited text applies to variable rate loans.
business_rules.consequences: If borrower consent/agreement is not obtained, or SBA notification is not completed, or changes exceed/violate maximums at application/approval, the change is non-compliant and may require remediation; SBA oversight findings and guaranty enforceability risk may occur.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in cited text.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1 > Post-Approval Changes to the Interest Rate
source_reference.source_text: Pre-Disbursement Changes: After loan approval and prior to first disbursement... provided the new interest rate does not exceed the maximum allowable interest rate at the time of the loan application. The Lender must obtain the Borrower’s written consent... and must either notify the LGPC of the change or make the change through E-Tran Servicing... Post-Disbursement Changes: After the loan is disbursed, on a variable rate loan, the Lender may change the base rate or the spread... The Lender must obtain the Borrower’s written agreement and must either notify the CLSC of the change or make the change through E-Tran Servicing.

source_reference.section_id: Post-Approval Changes to the Interest Rate — Pre-Disbursement Changes
source_reference.source_text: After loan approval and prior to first disbursement, the Lender may change the initial Note rate, including changing the base rate, the spread over the base rate, or change from a fixed rate to a variable rate, or from a variable rate to a fixed rate, provided the new interest rate does not exceed the maximum allowable interest rate at the time of the loan application. The Lender must obtain the Borrower’s written consent to the change in the interest rate (separate and apart from executing the loan documents) and must either notify the LGPC of the change or make the change through E-Tran Servicing.

source_reference.section_id: Post-Approval Changes to the Interest Rate — Post-Disbursement Changes
source_reference.source_text: After the loan is disbursed, on a variable rate loan, the Lender may change the base rate or the spread over the base rate as long as the new base rate or spread is based on a method permitted when the loan was approved and is consistent with the interest rate regulations at the time the loan was approved. The Lender must obtain the Borrower’s written agreement and must either notify the CLSC by email to FSC.Servicing@sba.gov of the change or make the change through E-Tran Servicing.

examples: A lender cannot increase a spread after approval but before first disbursement without separate written consent and without ensuring the new rate remains within the max allowed at application.
After disbursement, a lender cannot switch to a different index not permitted at approval, even if the borrower agrees.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA oversight finding; potential denial of SBA liability on guaranty if note terms are not compliant/authorized

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

deduplication_info.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_002 matches the pre-first-disbursement portion, and BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_003 matches the post-disbursement portion, of BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_documentation_4_009. No thresholds or scope differences exist; only separation vs combination and wording differ.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_documentation_4_009 selected: integrates both pre-disbursement and post-disbursement interest-term change controls (including consent + SBA notification/update mechanics) into one cohesive rule.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_documentation_4_009 allows pre-disbursement changes to base/spread/fixed-variable only if the new rate does not exceed SBA maximums at time of application. Those SBA maximums for variable-rate loans are governed by BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_pricing_4_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the max spread test is not satisfied/known, the lender cannot validate that a proposed rate change is allowable; changes may become noncompliant and require amendment/borrower notice and SBA notification remediation.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_pricing_4_010 restricts changing the adjustment period without borrower written consent and limits adjustment frequency. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_documentation_4_009 establishes consent/notification requirements for interest-term changes pre- and post-disbursement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one rule is followed, the lender may obtain consent but still violate timing/frequency limitations (or comply with timing but fail consent/notification), creating SBA repair/defect risk.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) lender must decline if cash flow analysis does not show reasonable assurance of timely repayment
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Small and SBA Express credit decisions, the lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner consistent with prudent lending standards. The applicant’s cash flow is the primary source of repayment (not liquidation of collateral). If the lender’s financial analysis demonstrates that the applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from business cash flow, the loan request must be declined regardless of collateral or outside sources of repayment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small Loans and SBA Express loans during underwriting/credit approval based on lender’s financial analysis of repayment capacity.
business_rules.consequences: If analysis shows no reasonable assurance of timely repayment from cash flow, lender must decline (cannot approve based solely on collateral or guarantors). Approving contrary to this standard risks SBA purchase denial/repair and lender oversight criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2 – Credit Standards for 7(a) Small Loans & SBA Express
source_reference.source_text: A Lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner, consistent with prudent lending standards. The cash flow of the Applicant is the primary source of repayment, not any expected recovery from the liquidation of collateral. Thus, if the Lender’s financial analysis demonstrates that the Applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from the cash flow of the business, the loan request must be declined, regardless of the collateral available or outside sources of repayment.

examples: Applicant projects negative debt service coverage and recurring losses; even with a fully pledged building as collateral, the lender must decline due to lack of reasonable assurance from cash flow.

Applicant has strong guarantor liquidity but the business cash flow cannot support proposed debt; lender must decline under SBA standards rather than rely on liquidation/guarantor as primary repayment.
Applicant’s cash flow supports repayment with reasonable assurance; lender may proceed to collateral analysis and other eligibility checks.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA purchase denial/repair; lender oversight findings

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_19_008 is a threshold eligibility rule for SBA financial assistance tied to tax filing and verification. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_UNDERWRITING_7_008 applies to SBA Small/SBA Express credit decisions; those decisions must not proceed if the applicant is ineligible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Proceeding to approve/close without confirmed filed returns can result in ineligible SBA assistance and guaranty denial/repair risk.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_16_001 requires CAIVRS checks and prohibits SBA loans where an unsatisfied prior federal loss exists. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_UNDERWRITING_7_008 governs the credit decision process but is moot if the applicant is ineligible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If CAIVRS indicates unsatisfied prior loss and lender proceeds, SBA guaranty may be denied and oversight findings likely.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_16_002 requires CAIVRS checks for applicant/guarantors (excluding supplemental guarantors) and prohibits SBA lending if delinquent federal nontax debt exists. Underwriting decisions under 7_008 cannot override this eligibility bar.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If delinquent debt exists and loan proceeds, SBA may deny guaranty liability/purchase and cite the lender for eligibility failures.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDIT_POLICY_7_009 establishes upfront screening and a hard ineligibility condition (existing SBA 7(a)/504 not current at loan number issuance). If ineligible or if SBSS falls below threshold requiring alternate processing, the lender cannot proceed under the same Small Loan workflow assumed by 7_008.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 7_009 is not satisfied, the lender must not proceed with SBA Small Loan processing; proceeding may result in ineligibility at loan number issuance and guaranty risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Minimum SBSS score is 165 for SBA 7(a) Small Loans and applicant is ineligible if any existing SBA loan is not current within 29 days
business_rules.description: All SBA 7(a) Small Loan applications begin with screening for a FICO Small Business Scoring Service (SBSS) score. As of the SOP effective date, the minimum acceptable SBSS score is 165 (SBA may adjust and post updates). Regardless of SBSS score, the applicant business is ineligible if it has an existing 7(a) or 504 loan that is not current at the time the new 7(a) SBA loan number is issued. “Current” means a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days; a loan that has matured and is not paid within 29 days of maturity is not current and is not eligible for refinancing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small Loan underwriting at the time of SBSS screening and at issuance of the new SBA loan number, including checks for status of existing SBA 7(a)/504 loans.
business_rules.consequences: If SBSS < 165, the loan must be processed using Standard 7(a) procedures (or via SBA Express if lender has authority). If any existing SBA 7(a) or 504 loan is not current (as defined), applicant business is ineligible for the new SBA loan number; lender must not proceed.
business_rules.exceptions: SBSS threshold may be adjusted by SBA over time; lenders must use the currently posted minimum. Ineligibility due to non-current existing SBA loans applies regardless of SBSS score.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2 – Underwriting 7(a) Small Loans (SBSS and current SBA loan requirement)
source_reference.source_text: All 7(a) Small Loan applications will begin with a screening for a FICO® Small Business Scoring ServiceSM Score (SBSS Score)... the Applicant business is ineligible if the Applicant business has an existing 7(a) or 504 loan that is not current at the time of issuance of the new 7(a) SBA loan number. “Current” means that a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days... As of the effective date of this SOP, the minimum acceptable SBSS score is 165

examples: Applicant SBSS score is 160: lender cannot process as a 7(a) Small Loan; must process as Standard 7(a) (or possibly SBA Express if available).
Applicant SBSS score is 190 but has an existing SBA 504 loan with a payment 45 days past due: applicant is ineligible for a new SBA loan number until the existing loan is current.
Applicant has an SBA 7(a) loan that matured and is unpaid 40 days after maturity: it is not current and is not eligible for refinancing under this standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA loan number issuance prevention; SBA guaranty denial; lender oversight findings

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 repayment ability requires debt service coverage ratio at least 1.0x and applications must be declined if not supported by cash flow
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, the applicant’s cash flow is the primary source of repayment. Debt Service is defined as the future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (operating cash flow divided by debt service) must be equal to or greater than 1.0x (1:1). If financial analysis shows the applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from business cash flow, the loan request must be declined regardless of collateral available.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loan credit analysis at underwriting/approval. DSCR is calculated using operating cash flow and debt service on all business debt including the new SBA loan proceeds.
business_rules.consequences: If DSCR < 1.0x or repayment is not reasonably assured from cash flow, the request must be declined even if collateral appears sufficient.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1, Credit Standards, Repayment Ability Analysis (Page 353)
source_reference.source_text:
On 504 loans, the cash flow of the Applicant is the primary source of repayment, not
any expected recovery from the liquidation of collateral... if the CDC’s financial analysis
demonstrates that the Applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment i n a timely manner
from the cash flow of the business, the loan request must be declined, regardless of the collateral
available... The Applicant’s debt
service coverage ratio (operating cash flow divided by debt service) must be
equal to or greater than 1:1

examples: Operating cash flow $250,000; total annual P&I on all business debt incl. new SBA debt $260,000 → DSCR 0.96x; must decline regardless of collateral.
Operating cash flow $400,000; total annual debt service $380,000 → DSCR 1.05x; meets minimum DSCR requirement (subject to other credit factors).
Strong collateral coverage but DSCR 0.85x → still must decline under SBA 504 cash-flow-first standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA credit decision criticism; denial required under program rules; lender/CDC oversight findings

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_UNDERWRITING_8_009 requires DSCR ≥ 1.0x and decline if repayment not reasonably assured. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_19_008 is an upstream eligibility gate applicable to SBA assistance including 504.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If returns are not filed, the 504 request must not proceed; CDC/lender may face SBA/SLPC findings for processing an ineligible applicant.

dependencies.rationale: 16_001 blocks SBA 504 eligibility on prior federal loss grounds. 8_009 is the 504 repayment/DSCR credit rule; approval is impermissible unless eligibility is satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Proceeding to approve a 504 loan with an unsatisfied prior loss can result in SBA denial and CDC/lender enforcement exposure.

dependencies.rationale: 16_002 applies to SBA 504 eligibility. 8_009 is the 504 cash flow/DSCR credit decision rule. The loan cannot be approved under 8_009 unless 16_002 is cleared.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If delinquent debt exists, SBA may deny the 504 loan and/or pursue enforcement remedies; the file is ineligible regardless of DSCR.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 refinancing that includes eligible business expenses requires itemized expense list and CDC credit memorandum certification
business_rules.description: If the borrower requests that an SBA 504 refinancing include Eligible Business Expenses (EBE), the application must include a specific description and an itemization of the amount of each expense. The CDC must retain EBE documentation in its file: EBE must be itemized (a gross figure is not acceptable). The CDC’s credit memorandum must document the nature of the EBE, provide the itemization of EBE, and include the CDC’s certification that the EBE are eligible as defined in the SOP paragraph.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 refinancing applications that include any amount of Eligible Business Expenses (EBE).
business_rules.consequences: If EBE are not itemized, not specifically described, or the credit memorandum lacks required documentation/certification, the EBE component is not properly supported and the application/package is deficient; SBA/SLPC review may require correction, and lender/CDC faces oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section C, Ch 1 — EBE documentation retention requirements
source_reference.source_text: If the Borrower is requesting that the refinancing include EBE, the application must include a specific description and an itemization of the amount of each expense. ... The CDC must retain in its file the following EBE documentation: i) EBE must be itemized (a gross figure is not acceptable). ii) The CDC’s credit memorandum must: (a) Document the nature of the EBE; (b) Provide the itemization of EBE; and (c) Include the CDC’s certification that the EBE are eligible as defined in this Paragraph.

examples: Compliant: EBE includes an itemized list (e.g., $18,450 rent arrears, $6,120 utilities, $40,000 inventory) and credit memo certifies eligibility.
Noncompliant: Application states “EBE = $150,000” with no breakdown; this is a gross figure and not acceptable.
Noncompliant: Itemization exists, but credit memo does not include CDC certification that the EBE are eligible.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing deficiency; SBA lender oversight finding; potential repair at guaranty purchase if unsupported proceeds are included

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: Revolving line increases must meet SBA timing limits and require documented analysis when increase exceeds defined percentage or age thresholds
business_rules.description: SBA revolving line of credit increases are permitted only within specified time windows and may require additional credit/risk analysis. For SBA Express and Export Express, increases may be made at any time during the life of the loan but must be within 5 years (SBA Express) or 7 years (Export Express) of the loan approval date and comply with maximum maturities. If the increase is above 33% of the original loan amount, the lender must include an analysis of appropriate credit and risk factors consistent with procedures used for similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. For CAPLines, increases may be made at any time during the life of the loan but must be within 10 years of approval (except Builder’s CAPLines within 5 years) and comply with maximum maturities. If the increase request is more than 20% of the original loan amount or is more than 18 months after the original approval date, the lender must include analysis showing the purpose is the same as the original purpose and that borrower cash flow can support the increased payment amount.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to increases of SBA revolving lines of credit under SBA Express, Export Express, and CAPLines programs.
business_rules.consequences: If timing windows or analysis triggers are not met, the increase is not authorized under SBA requirements and may result in SBA oversight findings or guaranty repair/denial. Supporting documentation must be retained in the lender file as applicable.
business_rules.exceptions: Builder’s CAPLines have a shorter 5-year window instead of 10 years. Also, increases may not exceed the program dollar limit at time of original approval (stated elsewhere in the same excerpt but not fully reproduced).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 5: Revolving lines of credit increases (i)-(ii)
source_reference.source_text: Revolving lines of credit increases may be made: i. If made under SBA Express and Export Express: a) ... within 5 years for SBA Express and 7 years for Export Express... b) Must include an analysis... if the increase is above 33% of the original loan amount. ii. If made under CAPLines: a) ... within 10 years... (except Builder’s CAPLines... within 5 years)... b) If the request... is more than 20%... or is more than 18 months after... approval date... include... analysis... same... purpose... and ... cash flow can support

examples: SBA Express line approved 2024-01-01: an increase requested 2029-02-01 is outside the 5-year window and is not permitted under SBA Express increase rules.
Export Express line increase of 40% over original amount: lender must document credit/risk factor analysis consistent with its non-SBA process.
CAPLine approved 2025-01-01: increase requested on 2027-01-15 (more than 18 months later) even if only 10% increase requires analysis of same purpose and cash flow support.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; SBA guaranty repair/denial for unauthorized increases or insufficient documentation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade loan export eligibility requires prohibited-country screening under the Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule
business_rules.description: For SBA International Trade (IT) loans involving direct or indirect exports, the lender must determine whether U.S. companies are authorized to conduct business with the destination country pursuant to the Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule. A loan may not be made to a business that directly or indirectly exports to a foreign country listed as a prohibited country (Note #7) on the Country Limitation Schedule. Indirect exports count as exports, but the applicant must provide documentation from its domestic customer (e.g., letter, invoice, order, or contract) that the goods/services are being exported.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loans when eligibility is based on exports (including indirect exports).
business_rules.consequences: If the destination country is prohibited (Note #7), the loan may not be made under the IT loan program. If indirect export documentation is missing, eligibility support is insufficient and the application may be declined or require additional documentation.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; indirect exports are expressly included as exports for eligibility, but require documentation from the domestic customer.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade — Eligibility (1)(a)(iv) and indirect export documentation (iii)
source_reference.source_text: Indirect exports are considered exports... the Applicant must provide documentation... (typically... letter, invoice, order, or contract)... For all of the Applicant’s exports (including indirect exports), the Lender must determine if U.S. companies are authorized to conduct business with the country... pursuant to the Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule. A loan may not be made... that... exports to... a prohibited country (Note #7)

examples: Applicant sells components to a U.S. distributor who exports them to Country X: lender treats as indirect export but must obtain distributor documentation (invoice/order/contract) evidencing export and screen Country X against the Ex-Im schedule.
Applicant exports directly to a country marked prohibited (Note #7): IT loan is not permitted, regardless of borrower credit strength.
Applicant claims export expansion but cannot identify destination markets or provide export plan/documentation: lender lacks evidence to support export eligibility and must cure or decline.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility denial; SBA guaranty denial/repair if prohibited-country export was financed

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade loan refinancing requires the debt to be current for 12 months and defines current as not more than 29 days past due including maturity
business_rules.description: For SBA International Trade (IT) loan refinancing, the debt to be refinanced must be current for at least the last 12 months or for the life of the loan, whichever is less. 'Current' means a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days. A loan that has matured and is not paid within 29 days of the maturity date is not current and is not eligible for refinancing. In addition, SBA-guaranteed proceeds may not be used to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss (including same institution debt); and a lender may not use PLP delegated authority to refinance same institution debt—such requests must be processed under non-PLP delegated authority when they reduce the lender’s credit exposure to the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loans when proceeds are used to refinance business debt, including same institution debt considerations and PLP vs non-PLP processing.
business_rules.consequences: If the refinanced debt is not current under the 29-day definition for the required period, it is ineligible for refinancing with IT loan proceeds. If same institution debt refinance would reduce the lender’s exposure, it must be processed under non-PLP authority; using PLP improperly can lead to SBA denial/repair and oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Current period is the last 12 months or the life of the loan, whichever is less.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: International Trade — Eligible Uses — Debt Refinancing (iii) and same institution/PLP limitation (a)
source_reference.source_text: The debt to be refinanced must be, and must have been, current for at least the last 12 months or for the life of the loan, whichever is less. “Current” means that a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days. A loan that has matured and not been paid within 29 days... is not current and is not eligible for refinancing... a Lender may not use PLP delegated authority to refinance same institution debt

examples: Debt has one payment 35 days past due within the last 12 months: it fails the 'current' test and cannot be refinanced with an IT loan.
A term loan matured 20 days ago and remains unpaid: still within 29 days, so it may be current for this definition (other requirements still apply).
Refinancing the lender’s own existing note would reduce its exposure: lender cannot use PLP delegated authority and must process under non-PLP procedures.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA ineligibility determination; SBA guaranty denial/repair and lender oversight criticism for improper refinancing or delegated processing misuse

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 debt refinancing with expansion limited to 100% of expansion cost and requires 75% fixed-asset use test plus payment history and substantial benefit test
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 Projects that include debt refinancing with expansion, the amount of existing indebtedness that may be refinanced must not exceed 100% of the cost of the expansion. The CDC must analyze and document in its credit memorandum that: (1) substantially all (75% or more) of proceeds of the existing debt (or the original loan if refinanced multiple times) were used to acquire eligible fixed assets, with 25% or less incurred for benefit of the small business; (2) the existing indebtedness is collateralized by fixed assets and eligible fixed assets collateralizing the refinanced debt must also collateralize the 504 loan unless SLPC grants a waiver due to extraordinary circumstances (PCLP CDCs may not approve loans requiring this waiver under delegated authority); (3) borrower has been current for not less than 1 year preceding SBA approval date (not to exceed 30 days in arrears) supported by transcript of account or similar payment history; and (4) the refinancing provides a substantial benefit—new installment amounts attributable to refinanced debt must be less than existing installments after adding prepayment penalties/fees/costs to amount refinanced; seasonal payment loans use average payments over most recent 12 months; balloon-payment loans satisfy substantial benefit independent of the reduction requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loans where the Project involves expansion (acquisition, construction, or improvement of land/building/equipment for applicant use) and includes debt refinancing with expansion under 13 CFR § 120.882(e). “Date of refinancing” is the date the 504 loan is approved by SBA.
business_rules.consequences: If any required condition is not met, the debt refinancing with expansion is not permissible within the 504 Project structure; SBA approval may be denied or waiver/extraordinary circumstances approval required where applicable. Any unremedied delinquency after approval must be reported to SBA as an adverse change.
business_rules.exceptions: Balloon payments meet the substantial benefit test independent of the reduction requirement. PCLP delegated authority may not be used for SLPC collateral waiver. Seasonal payments assessed by 12-month average. If debt has been open < 1 year, payment history requirement applies for the time the debt has been open (still must be current, not to exceed 30 days in arrears).

source_reference.section_id: Permissible Debt Refinancing with Expansion (13 CFR § 120.882(e))
source_reference.source_text: If the Project involves expansion of an Applicant, any amount of existing indebtedness that does not exceed 100% of the cost of the expansion may be refinanced... Substantially all (75% or more) of the proceeds of the existing debt was used to acquire a fixed asset(s)... and the remaining amount (25% or less)... The Borrower has been current on all payments due on the existing debt for not less than 1 year... (not to exceed 30 days in arrears)... “Substantial Benefit” means... new installment amounts... must be less than the existing installment amount(s).

examples: Expansion cost is $800,000 and borrower seeks to refinance $900,000 existing debt—noncompliant because refinanced amount exceeds 100% of expansion cost.
Existing debt proceeds were 70% used for eligible fixed assets and 30% for working capital—noncompliant because it fails the 75%/25% test.

Borrower has a balloon note being refinanced; even if new installment is not lower, it can still meet the substantial benefit test because balloon payments satisfy the test independent of the reduction requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA denial of eligibility for debt refinance component; potential post-approval adverse change reporting requirement and closing delay

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Standard 7(a) loans must demonstrate DSCR at least 1.15 on historic or projected basis and at least 1.00 on global basis, with projections achieving 1.15 within 2 years
business_rules.description: For SBA Standard 7(a) loans, operating cash flow (OCF) is defined as EBITDA and debt service (DS) is defined as future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The applicant’s DSCR (OCF/DS) must be equal to or greater than 1.15 on a historical and/or projected cash flow basis and 1.00 on a global basis. For start-ups, new businesses, changes of ownership, and other applications based on projections, projections must reflect DSCR equal to or greater than 1.15 within 2 years from loan funding or, for construction projects, within 2 years from the end of construction.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) Loans (Loans Greater than $350,000) under SOP 50 10 8 credit standards for lender analysis of repayment ability.
business_rules.consequences: If lender’s financial analysis demonstrates lack of reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from business cash flow (including failure to meet DSCR requirements), the loan request must be declined regardless of collateral or outside repayment sources.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1: Standard 7(a) Loans > Credit Standards
source_reference.source_text: include detailed projections... reflect a debt service coverage equal to or greater than 1.15 within 2 years from loan funding or, for construction projects, within 2 years from the end of construction... The Applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (OCF/DS) must be equal to or greater than 1.15 on a historical and/or projected cash flow basis and 1:1 on a global basis.

examples: Existing business has historic DSCR 1.05 and projected DSCR 1.18; global DSCR 1.02—compliant if projections are supportable and documented; otherwise SBA standards require reasonable assurance of repayment.
Construction project projections reach DSCR 1.15 only 3 years after end of construction—noncompliant because projections must reach 1.15 within 2 years from end of construction.
Applicant’s business DSCR is 1.20 but global DSCR including affiliates/guarantor obligations is 0.95—noncompliant due to global DSCR minimum 1.00.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loan must be declined under SBA credit standards; potential SBA guaranty purchase denial if file does not support standards under PLP

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDITPOLICY_12_007 mandates DSCR and repayment analysis for Standard 7(a) loans. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_19_008 requires that required federal returns be filed and verified, establishing baseline reliability for that analysis and eligibility to proceed.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If required returns are not filed, the loan is ineligible regardless of DSCR outcomes; approval/processing must stop or move outside SBA.

dependencies.rationale: 16_001 is an eligibility gate; 12_007 is the repayment/DSCR credit standard. Eligibility must be satisfied before credit standards can result in an approvable SBA loan.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: A loan approved under 12_007 without clearing 16_001 can be deemed ineligible and expose the lender to guaranty denial/repair.

dependencies.rationale: 16_002 is an SBA eligibility gate. 12_007 is the Standard 7(a) credit standard; it presumes SBA eligibility is met, including no disqualifying delinquent federal debt.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Approval based on DSCR while ignoring delinquent federal nontax debt creates a likely guaranty denial/repair scenario.

dependencies.rationale: If SBSS < 165, BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDIT_POLICY_7_009 requires the loan be processed using Standard 7(a) procedures (or SBA Express if authorized). For Standard 7(a) loans, BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDITPOLICY_12_007 defines DSCR and repayment analysis requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender continues under Small Loan standards after SBSS failure, the file may lack required Standard 7(a) DSCR/global analysis and be cited in SBA review.

business_rules.rule_name: Independent appraisal required when fixed asset valuation exceeds net book value and business valuation does not satisfy SBA requirement
business_rules.description: If fixed assets are valued higher than their Net Book Value in support of equity injection or collateral value, the lender must obtain an independent appraisal/valuation by a qualified individual to support the higher valuation. The appraiser must be independent of the loan production function, not involved in approval of the transaction, and must not have the appearance of a conflict of interest. A valuation of the fixed assets provided as part of a business valuation does not meet these requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting or equity injection analysis uses fixed assets with a stated valuation greater than Net Book Value for SBA loan purposes (including IT loans and Export Express).
business_rules.consequences: If lender relies on a business valuation or internal estimate to support fixed asset value above Net Book Value without an independent appraisal, the file is non-compliant and may be criticized in SBA review/oversight; may affect eligibility of equity injection treatment and increase risk of SBA guaranty repair/denial depending on materiality.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards for International Trade > Equity injection > Assets other than cash
source_reference.source_text: Assets other than cash – An appraisal or other valuation by an independent third party is required if the valuation of the fixed assets is greater than the Net Book Value. A valuation of the fixed assets provided as part of a business valuation will not meet these requirements.

examples: Borrower contributes equipment with NBV $50,000 but claims value $120,000: lender must obtain an independent appraisal/valuation to support the $120,000 value.
Loan file includes a business valuation that lists fixed assets at higher than NBV but no separate independent appraisal: this does not meet the SBA requirement.
Independent valuation is performed by a third party who also helped originate the loan: not acceptable due to independence requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding and potential SBA guaranty repair/denial if valuation support is material to credit decision or eligibility

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA commercial property appraisers must be independent and state-certified when value exceeds $1,000,000 and lender cannot use seller or applicant appraisal
business_rules.description: For SBA commercial real estate appraisals: the appraiser must be independent with no appearance of a conflict of interest (including no direct/indirect interest in the property/transaction, independent of loan production, and not involved in transaction approval). The appraiser must be state-licensed or state-certified; when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be state-certified. The appraisal must identify the lender as the client and/or intended user (subject to limited FIRREA flexibility for federally regulated lenders), and the lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the Applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the lender obtains an appraisal for SBA loans secured by commercial real property; state-certified requirement applies when estimated property value > $1,000,000.
business_rules.consequences: If independence/licensing requirements are not met or if a seller/applicant-procured appraisal is used, the appraisal is not acceptable for SBA purposes, creating underwriting and SBA compliance deficiencies and potential guaranty risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Federally regulated lenders may follow their primary regulator’s FIRREA requirements to the extent they permit otherwise regarding identification of client/intended user; other elements are stated as requirements.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal Requirements – Appraiser independence, credentialing, client identification, and prohibited reliance (i.c and i.d)
source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must be: Independent and have no appearance of a conflict of interest... and Either State-licensed or State-certified, with the following exception: when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified. The appraisal must identify the Lender as the client and/or an intended user... The Lender may not use an appraisal prepared for the seller or the Applicant.

examples: Estimated property value is $1.5MM for an SBA-secured commercial building: lender engages a state-certified independent appraiser; a state-licensed-only appraiser would not satisfy the requirement.
Borrower provides an appraisal ordered by the seller: lender cannot rely on it and orders its own appraisal identifying lender as client/intended user.
A staff appraiser involved in loan approval prepares the appraisal: violates independence requirements and is not acceptable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program non-compliance; appraisal acceptability failure; potential guaranty repair/denial exposure.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_20_002 mandates obtaining an appraisal under specified conditions. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_15_011 sets core acceptability requirements for SBA commercial real estate appraisals (independence, licensing/certification thresholds, client/intended use identification).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 15_011 standards are not met, the appraisal obtained under 20_002 is not acceptable for SBA purposes, requiring replacement/repair and potentially delaying closing.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) and 504 ineligibility when applicant or associates have prior loss to the federal government and CAIVRS must be checked
business_rules.description: An Applicant is not eligible for an SBA 7(a) or 504 loan if there is a prior loss to the Federal government. A prior loss occurs when (i) the Applicant previously defaulted on a Federal loan or federally assisted financing resulting in a loss to the Federal government, or (ii) any other business owned/operated/controlled by the Applicant or an Associate previously defaulted on such Federal credit (or guaranteed a loan that defaulted), resulting in a loss. SBA Lenders must check CAIVRS for Applicant/Associate prior loss and retain evidence of the CAIVRS check in the loan file. If the prior loss is fully satisfied, the application may be processed (including under delegated authority) but the lender must document how the loss was fully satisfied.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and SBA 504 loan applications during eligibility/underwriting. Applies to the Applicant and any business owned/operated/controlled by the Applicant or an Associate of the Applicant (as defined by SBA).
business_rules.consequences: If CAIVRS or other documentation indicates an unsatisfied prior loss, the loan is ineligible and must be declined or restructured outside SBA guaranty. If the prior loss is fully satisfied, the lender may proceed but must document satisfaction. Failure to perform/retain CAIVRS evidence creates SBA compliance findings and can jeopardize SBA guaranty purchase or trigger lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Prior loss definition excludes unpaid/delinquent taxes and losses incurred by the FDIC when it sells a loan at a discount. If fully satisfied, eligibility may be restored with documented proof.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1, Paragraph 15 Prior Loss to the Government (13 CFR § 120.110(q))
source_reference.source_text: An Applicant is not eligible for a 7(a) or 504 loan if there is a prior loss to the Federal government. A “Prior Loss” has occurred when: i. The Applicant has previously defaulted on a Federal loan or federally assisted financing, resulting in a loss to the Federal government... ii. Any other business owned, operated, or controlled by the Applicant or an Associate of the Applicant, previously defaulted... resulting in a loss... All SBA Lenders must check the Credit Alert Verification Reporting System (CAIVRS)... SBA Lenders must retain documentation of the CAIVRS check in the loan file.

examples: Applicant’s CAIVRS check shows a prior SBA disaster loan charge-off with an unpaid deficiency recognized by the agency; lender must decline the SBA-guaranteed request.
Applicant had a prior loss but provides documented proof of full satisfaction (paid in full/settlement accepted by the agency); lender may proceed and must retain satisfaction evidence.
A related entity controlled by a 25% owner previously defaulted on a federally guaranteed business loan with a recognized loss; Applicant is ineligible unless the prior loss is fully satisfied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair at purchase; SBA lender oversight finding; potential declination of SBA eligibility

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) and 504 ineligibility when applicant or non-supplemental guarantors have delinquent federal nontax debt past 90 days and CAIVRS must be checked
business_rules.description: An Applicant is not eligible for an SBA 7(a) or 504 loan if the Applicant or any guarantor (except a Supplemental Guarantor) owes an outstanding nontax debt to the Federal Government that is in delinquent status. A debt is delinquent when it has not been paid within 90 days of the payment due date (as specified in the creditor agency’s initial written demand for payment or other applicable agreement), even if the agency has suspended/terminated collection activity. SBA Lenders must check CAIVRS for delinquent federal debt for the Applicant, any guarantor, or Associate of the Applicant and retain CAIVRS evidence in the loan file. If the delinquent federal debt is fully satisfied, the application can be processed, including under delegated authority, with documentation of satisfaction.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and SBA 504 underwriting. Applies to Applicant and any guarantor other than a Supplemental Guarantor; also requires CAIVRS checks for Applicant/guarantor/Associate tax IDs.
business_rules.consequences: If delinquent federal nontax debt exists and no exception applies, the loan is ineligible and must be declined or restructured outside SBA guaranty. If fully satisfied, lender may proceed but must document satisfaction. Missing CAIVRS check/retention is a compliance deficiency and may jeopardize SBA guaranty purchase or lead to SBA oversight criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: Debt is not delinquent if: (i) creditor agency released obligor or accepted compromise or delinquency cured under acceptable terms; (ii) obligor is in/discharged from bankruptcy and current on any court authorized plan; (iii) obligor has a satisfactory written repayment agreement and is paying as agreed; or (iv) debt is in an administrative or judicial appeal process. Note: if there was a prior loss associated with any of these debts, the Applicant remains subject to the Prior Loss rule.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 1, Paragraph 16 Delinquent Federal Debt (31 CFR § 285.13)
source_reference.source_text: An Applicant is not eligible for a 7(a) or 504 loan if the Applicant or any guarantor (except a Supplemental Guarantor) owes an outstanding nontax debt to the Federal Government... A debt is in “delinquent status” when the debt has not been paid within 90 days of the payment due date... A debt is not considered “delinquent” if: i. The creditor agency has released the obligor... ii. The obligor is subject to... bankruptcy... iii. The obligor has entered into a satisfactory written repayment agreement... iv. The debt is in an administrative or judicial appeal process.

examples: Applicant owes a federal agency nontax debt and is 120 days past the due date; loan is ineligible until satisfied or placed under an acceptable repayment agreement that is being paid as agreed.

Guarantor (not supplemental) is 150 days past due on a federal debt but is current on a court-approved Chapter 13 plan; not considered delinquent under the SOP and the lender may proceed if documented.
Applicant’s federal debt is under administrative appeal; lender documents appeal status and may proceed (subject to any separate Prior Loss findings).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial or repair at purchase; SBA lender oversight finding; potential declination of SBA eligibility

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP foreign receivable proceeds must flow through control accounts with monthly borrowing base reporting and immediate cure of any over-advance
business_rules.description: For SBA Export Working Capital Program (EWCP) loans, lenders must implement collateral controls over foreign accounts receivable proceeds. For “single transaction-specific” and “transaction based-revolving line of credit” EWCP loans, lenders are required to set up a control account to capture proceeds of foreign accounts receivable as paid by foreign buyers, and proceeds must be applied against the loan balance either in their entirety or as a percentage sufficient to pay off the initial advance for the specific transaction. For EWCP asset-based loans, LGPC (non-delegated) or the PLP-EWCP lender (delegated) determines whether a control account is required based on credit analysis; normally, 100% of foreign receivable proceeds must be applied against the loan balance with additional advances requested via Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC). If the lender allows the borrower to retain foreign receivable proceeds (not applied upon collection) while maintaining the balance within borrowing base limits, the borrower must (a) be in business at least 2 years (no start-ups) and (b) have financial records satisfactory to SBA or the PLP-EWCP lender and ability to provide a current aging of foreign receivables. At a minimum, borrower must submit a BBC at least monthly (or more frequently if that is the lender’s customary practice for similar non-SBA loans), and the lender must review the borrowing base to ensure the borrower is not over-advanced; any over-advance requires immediate borrower payment to bring the loan into compliance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) EWCP loans structured as (i) single transaction-specific, (ii) transaction based revolving line of credit, or (iii) asset-based EWCP revolving lines supported by BBC collateral reporting, where foreign accounts receivable proceeds are a primary repayment/collateral source.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must establish required control account structure and enforce proceeds application/BBC reporting. If BBC review shows an over-advance (loan balance exceeds eligible collateral per BBC), lender must require immediate payment to reduce balance into compliance. Failure to implement controls can be cited in SBA oversight and may impair SBA guaranty support for disbursements/advances inconsistent with required collateral monitoring.
business_rules.exceptions: For asset-based EWCP, requirement for a control account is determined case-by-case by LGPC (non-delegated) or PLP-EWCP lender (delegated) based on credit analysis; however, normal expectation is 100% proceeds applied unless borrower qualifies for retention option under the specified conditions.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4 EWCP, Control Accounts
source_reference.source_text: For the “single transaction-specific” and “transaction based-revolving line of credit” EWCP loans, Lenders will be required to set up a control account to capture the proceeds of foreign accounts receivable... The proceeds are required to be applied against the loan balance... For asset-based loans... Normally, Lenders must have 100% of the foreign accounts receivable proceeds applied... At a minimum, the Borrower must submit a BBC to the Lender at least monthly... The Lender will review the Borrowing Base... If the Borrower is over-advanced... immediately make a payment... the small business must: (a) Be in business for at least 2 years (no start-ups)

examples: Single-transaction EWCP advance is secured by a specific export receivable; lender establishes a control account and applies 100% of collected proceeds to pay down the advance until the initial advance is repaid.
Asset-based EWCP line: borrower submits BBC monthly; BBC shows eligible collateral supports $800,000 but loan balance is $850,000; lender requires immediate $50,000 payment to cure the over-advance.

Asset-based EWCP line where borrower requests retention of collections: borrower has 3 years operating history and provides current aging of foreign A/R; lender permits retention within borrowing base limits and continues monthly BBC monitoring.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential guaranty repair/denial for advances made outside required collateral monitoring controls

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_16_003 requires control accounts and collateral monitoring for EWCP structures and mandates cure of over-advances identified through collateral reporting. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_16_004 defines which foreign A/R is ineligible for the borrowing base absent SBA consent; this is required to correctly compute availability and identify over-advances.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If ineligible A/R is included, the borrowing base is overstated and 16_003 monitoring will fail to prevent improper advances; SBA guaranty support for advances may be impaired.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_16_003 relies on accurate collateral eligibility and reporting to manage advances and capture proceeds. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_16_005 defines when export-related inventory is eligible and how it must be valued and controlled (U.S. location until shipment, GAAP valuation, perfection/enforceability).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Including ineligible inventory inflates the borrowing base, enabling over-advances and increasing SBA compliance and loss exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP foreign accounts receivable over 180-day terms or over 60 days past due are ineligible for borrowing base and buyer-level 50% delinquency triggers full exclusion
business_rules.description: Unless SBA provides prior written consent, specified foreign accounts receivable (A/R) are not eligible for inclusion in an EWCP asset-based loan borrowing base. Ineligible categories include (among others): A/R not arising from ordinary course sales; domestic (U.S.) account debtor unless SBA approved as an indirect export; A/R with no invoice sent; A/R due from buyers in countries SBA is legally prohibited from doing business with per the current Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule; foreign A/R due and payable more than 180 calendar days from invoice date (except those supported by acceptable letters of credit or credit insurance); foreign A/R outstanding more than 60 calendar days from its original due date; uncollectible/unacceptable A/R; A/R from affiliates; and a buyer-concentration rule where, when 50% or more of the total foreign A/R for a specific buyer are over 60 calendar days past the original due date, then all foreign A/R for that buyer are excluded from the borrowing base.
