business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA EWCP asset-based borrowing base calculations that include foreign accounts receivable as eligible collateral. Applies during origination and throughout the life of the line when calculating availability.
business_rules.consequences: Ineligible foreign A/R must be excluded from the borrowing base and cannot be used to support advances. If ineligible A/R is included, the borrowing base is overstated, which can create over-advances requiring immediate cure and may create SBA compliance/guaranty support risk.
business_rules.exceptions: Foreign A/R with original terms exceeding 180 days may be eligible only if supported by acceptable letters of credit or credit insurance. SBA may consent in writing to include otherwise ineligible A/R.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4 EWCP, Foreign Accounts Receivable Restrictions
source_reference.source_text: Unless the Lender receives SBA’s prior written consent... not eligible... (e) A foreign account receivable that, by its original terms, is due and payable more than 180 calendar days from the date of the invoice, except those accounts receivable supported by acceptable letters of credit or credit insurance; (f) A foreign account receivable that is still outstanding more than 60 calendar days from its original due date... (l) A foreign account receivable due from an affiliated company; and (m) When 50% or more of the total foreign accounts receivable for a specific buyer are over 60 calendar days past the original due date, then the total foreign accounts receivable for that buyer are excluded.

examples: Borrower submits foreign invoices with 210-day payment terms and no letter of credit/credit insurance; lender must exclude those receivables from the EWCP borrowing base.
A foreign buyer has 12 invoices totaling $1,000,000; $600,000 (60%) is more than 60 days past due; lender must exclude all $1,000,000 of that buyer’s receivables from the borrowing base.
A foreign invoice is 75 days past its original due date; lender must exclude that receivable from eligibility and recalculate availability.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential guaranty repair/denial if advances were unsupported by eligible collateral

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_11_011 prohibits making IT loans involving exports to prohibited countries per Ex-Im Country Limitation Schedule. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_16_004 similarly excludes A/R due from buyers in prohibited countries from EWCP borrowing bases. A unified prohibited-country screening control supports both eligibility and collateral calculations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If prohibited-country screening is inconsistent, lenders may both (i) originate ineligible IT loans and/or (ii) overstate EWCP collateral, creating over-advances and SBA compliance failures.

business_rules.rule_name: EWCP export-related inventory must be located in the United States and valued at lower of cost or market and is ineligible without a perfected first-priority lien
business_rules.description: Export-related inventory used as EWCP collateral must meet SBA eligibility/valuation requirements. Inventory taken as collateral must be located within the United States until shipped to the foreign buyer, and must be valued at the lower of actual cost or market value (including work-in-process), determined under GAAP. Without SBA’s prior written consent, export-related inventory is not eligible for inclusion in the export-related borrowing base if it is not subject to a valid, perfected, and enforceable first priority lien in favor of the lender, if it is located at an address not disclosed to the lender in writing, or if it is not located in the United States.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA EWCP borrowing base structures where export-related inventory is included as eligible collateral for availability/advance calculations.
business_rules.consequences: Inventory failing these eligibility requirements must be excluded from the borrowing base and cannot support advances. Inclusion of ineligible inventory can cause over-advances and SBA compliance issues.
business_rules.exceptions: SBA may provide prior written consent to include inventory otherwise restricted; absent consent, restrictions apply as stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4 EWCP, Export-Related Inventory General Guidelines and Restrictions
source_reference.source_text: Export-related inventory taken as collateral must be located within the United States, until shipped to the foreign buyer. Export-related inventory must be valued at the lower of actual cost or market value (including cost of work-in-process inventory) as determined in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)... Unless the Lender receives SBA’s prior written consent... not eligible... inventory that is not subject to a valid, perfected, and enforceable first priority lien... inventory located at an address that has not been disclosed... inventory that is not located in the United States

examples: Borrower proposes including export inventory stored in Mexico; lender must exclude it because inventory must be located in the United States until shipment.
Inventory is in the U.S. but lender has only a second lien due to another secured creditor; inventory is ineligible for borrowing base absent SBA consent because it lacks a first-priority lien.
Borrower includes work-in-process inventory in BBC; lender values it at lower of cost or market under GAAP and includes it only if lien/address requirements are met.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential guaranty repair/denial if advances were unsupported by eligible inventory collateral

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 CDC interim financing requires recorded mortgages before construction and qualified inspections before each progress disbursement
business_rules.description: When interim financing for an SBA 504 project comes from a CDC, the E-Tran Terms and Conditions must require (i) mortgages recorded prior to beginning construction and (ii) inspections by a qualified engineer, appraiser, or other SBA-satisfactory party prior to all progress disbursements. Additional required conditions include a firm fixed-price construction contract with no material changes without CDC written consent; contractor-provided builder’s risk and workers’ compensation insurance; submission of one complete set of plans/specifications; using CDC/Applicant injected funds before disbursing interim financing; CDC periodic inspections documented; and assignment of lease when improving buildings on leased land with loan funds.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when interim financing for SBA 504 construction-related use of proceeds is provided by the CDC (not a third-party lender).
business_rules.consequences: Failure to include/enforce these interim financing conditions may render the interim construction controls noncompliant with SBA E-Tran conditions, creating disbursement/closing deficiencies and SBA enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited paragraph.

source_reference.section_id: Construction Loan Provisions: additional conditions when interim financing comes from a CDC
source_reference.source_text: If the interim financing comes from a CDC, the following additional conditions must be required in the E-Tran Terms and Conditions: i. Mortgages must be recorded prior to beginning construction. ii. Inspections must be made by a qualified engineer, appraiser, or other party satisfactory to SBA prior to all progress disbursements. iii. The Applicant must furnish a firm construction contract ... no material changes ... without the prior written consent of the CDC; iv. The contractor must furnish builder’s risk and workers’ compensation insurance; ... vi. ... these funds must be used prior to the disbursement of the interim financing; vii. The CDC must make and document periodic inspections of construction; and viii. ... assignment of the lease must be obtained.

examples: CDC provides interim construction financing: mortgage is recorded before the first draw; an independent engineer inspects and signs off before each progress payment.
Applicant changes contractors mid-project without CDC written consent: violates the required fixed-price contract/no material changes condition and must be remediated.
CDC disburses interim loan proceeds before the applicant’s required cash injection is used: violates the required funding sequence condition.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA closing/servicing finding; potential denial/repair of guaranty conditions for noncompliance

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA credit not available elsewhere certification prohibits SBA eligibility when applicant meets conventional cash flow and collateral standards
business_rules.description: The SBA Lender must certify that the Applicant cannot obtain some or all requested loan funds on reasonable terms from non-Federal, non-State, or non-local government sources (including from the SBA Lender or Third Party Lender) without SBA assistance. If the Applicant’s cash flow and collateral (including adequacy of any third-party guaranty) would cause the loan to meet the conventional credit standards of the SBA Lender or Third Party Lender, the project is not eligible for an SBA loan. Failure to adequately address this need in the credit memorandum may result in SBA declining a non-delegated application or denying liability on the guaranty for delegated loans; for 504, it may support a CDC Recovery Claim if fraud, negligence, or misrepresentation occurred.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 lending where SBA eligibility depends on the statutory/regulatory requirement that credit is not available elsewhere.
business_rules.consequences: Noncompliance may lead to (i) SBA decline (non-delegated) or (ii) denial of guaranty liability (delegated) and/or (iii) 504 CDC Recovery Claim pursuit under specified misconduct circumstances.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited paragraph.

source_reference.section_id: H. Credit Not Available Elsewhere (15 U.S.C. 636(a)(1)(A) and 13 CFR § 120.101), paragraph 1
source_reference.source_text: 1. The SBA Lender must certify and indicate that the Applicant does not have the ability to obtain some or all of the requested loan funds on reasonable terms from non -Federal, non-State, or non -local government sources, including from the SBA Lender or Third Party Lender, without SBA assistance. If the

examples: Applicant has strong DSCR, strong collateral coverage, and readily qualifies under bank conventional policy: SBA loan is ineligible because credit is available elsewhere.
Delegated 7(a) lender approves without documenting why conventional credit is unavailable: SBA may deny liability on the guaranty after default due to insufficient support.
A 504 CDC misrepresents the unavailability of credit elsewhere: SBA may pursue a CDC Recovery Claim if fraud, negligence, or misrepresentation is present.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA decline; denial of guaranty liability; CDC recovery claim pursuit

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA credit memo must document specific conventional policy weaknesses and cannot rely solely on credit score or CRA benefits
business_rules.description: To substantiate that credit is not available elsewhere, the SBA Lender must include in its credit memorandum the specific reasons the Applicant does not meet conventional loan policy requirements, supported by relevant documentation. The lender may not cite the Applicant’s inability to meet the SBA Lender’s or Third Party Lender’s conventional credit score policy as the sole reason. The credit memo must consider availability from owner liquidity (owners of 20% or more, their spouses and minor children, and the Applicant), while permitting reasonable funds set aside for (i) medical, (ii) educational (including owner’s children), (iii) retirement, and permitting the Applicant reasonable funds for capital expenses for the next 24 months and for working capital. The lender may not rely in any manner on improving CRA ratings/performance or improving collateral lien position to demonstrate lack of credit elsewhere.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) and 504 credit memoranda supporting the ‘credit not available elsewhere’ requirement.
business_rules.consequences: If credit memo lacks specific reasons/support or relies on prohibited rationales, SBA may decline (non-delegated) or later deny guaranty liability (delegated) for inadequate support of eligibility requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; SBA permits reasonable owner/applicant funds set aside as described, which should not be treated as fully available liquidity.

source_reference.section_id: H. Credit Not Available Elsewhere, paragraphs 2 and 4
source_reference.source_text: Substantiate that credit is not available elsewhere ... The SBA Lender must include in its credit memorandum the specific reasons why the Applicant does not meet conventional loan policy requirements, along with relevant supporting documentation. The SBA Lender may not cite the Applicant’s inability to meet the SBA Lender’s or Third Party Lender’s conventional credit score policy as the sole reason ... SBA permits the Applicant to have reasonable funds for capital expenses for the next 24 months ... The SBA Lender may not rely in any manner on ... rating or performance evaluation under the Community Reinvestment Act (CRA) ... or ... improvement of ... collateral lien position.

examples: Applicant is eligible because conventional policy prohibits lending to businesses operating less than 2 years; credit memo documents this policy limitation and includes underwriting support—acceptable.
Credit memo states only ‘credit score below our conventional minimum’ with no other weaknesses: unacceptable as sole basis and may trigger SBA decline/guaranty denial.
Credit memo claims ‘SBA will improve our CRA rating’ as justification: prohibited and must be removed and replaced with acceptable credit weakness factors.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA decline; denial of guaranty liability; regulatory criticism in SBA review

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_17_005 imposes the substantive eligibility requirement and lender certification. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_17_006 specifies how the lender must evidence and support that certification in the credit memorandum (including prohibited rationales and required considerations).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If documentation standards are not met, SBA may decline (non-delegated) or later deny guaranty liability (delegated) because the core eligibility requirement is unsupported.

business_rules.rule_name: International Trade SBA 7(a) loans prohibited for exports to Ex-Im prohibited countries and indirect exports require customer documentation
business_rules.description: For SBA International Trade (IT) loans, the lender must determine whether U.S. companies are authorized to conduct business with the destination country pursuant to the Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule. A loan may not be made to a business that directly or indirectly exports to a foreign country listed as a prohibited country (Note #7) on the Country Limitation Schedule. Indirect exports qualify as exports, but the applicant must provide documentation from the domestic customer (e.g., letter, invoice, order, or contract) showing that the goods or services are being exported.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loan eligibility determination whenever exports (direct or indirect) are part of the eligibility basis.
business_rules.consequences: If the applicant exports directly or indirectly to an Ex-Im prohibited country, the loan is ineligible as an IT loan. If indirect export documentation is not obtained, the lender lacks required support for IT eligibility and should not approve under IT eligibility basis until documentation is received.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Eligibility for International Trade Loans (exports, indirect exports, prohibited countries)
source_reference.source_text: exported. For all of the Applicant’s exports (including indirect exports), the Lender must determine if U.S. companies are authorized to conduct business with the country to which the goods or services will be shipped, pursuant to the Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule. A loan may not be made to a business that directly or indirectly

examples: Applicant sells to a U.S. distributor who exports overseas: applicant must provide a distributor letter/invoice/order/contract demonstrating export (indirect export).
Applicant exports components to a country listed as prohibited (Note #7) on the Ex-Im schedule: lender must not make an IT loan to the applicant.
Applicant exports to a permitted country: lender documents the Ex-Im schedule check in underwriting and proceeds with other IT eligibility requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility failure; potential denial of SBA guaranty or adverse lender oversight finding

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_11_011 and BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_18_009 both require checking the Ex-Im Bank Country Limitation Schedule and prohibit IT loans tied to Note #7 prohibited countries (including indirect exports). Treating one as a validation of the other supports consistency across SOP interpretations and documentation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender applies one version but not the other, documentation and compliance controls may be incomplete (e.g., indirect export documentation gaps).

business_rules.rule_name: SBA alternative size standard eligibility requires tangible net worth not exceeding $20 million and average net income not exceeding $6.5 million for two fiscal years with 25% increase for labor surplus area use
business_rules.description: An SBA applicant (including affiliates) may qualify as small under the SBA Alternative Size Standard if: (i) maximum tangible net worth does not exceed $20,000,000; and (ii) average net income after Federal income taxes (excluding any carry-over losses) for the two full fiscal years before the application date does not exceed $6,500,000. The applicable size standards are increased by 25% when the applicant agrees to use all financial assistance within a designated labor surplus area.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when determining SBA small business eligibility using the Alternative Size Standard (in lieu of industry size standard) and when assessing labor surplus area adjustment applicability.
business_rules.consequences: If either threshold is exceeded (or documentation does not support the calculations), the applicant does not qualify under the SBA Alternative Size Standard, and eligibility must be evaluated under industry size standards or the application is ineligible for SBA assistance.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited excerpt. (Other SBA eligibility pathways may exist, but this rule governs Alternative Size Standard qualification.)

source_reference.section_id: D. Small Under SBA Size Requirements > Alternative Size Standard; Labor surplus area increase
source_reference.source_text: "Alternative Size Standard." The Applicant (including affiliates) must meet the following (13 CFR § 121.301): i. The maximum tangible net worth may not exceed $20 million; and ii. The average net income after Federal income taxes... for the 2 full fiscal years before the application date may not exceed $6.5 million. The applicable size standards are increased by 25 percent when the Applicant agrees to use all of the financial assistance within a labor surplus area...

examples: Applicant tangible net worth $18.0MM and 2-year average net income $5.9MM: meets Alternative Size Standard (subject to affiliate inclusion and correct calculation).
Applicant tangible net worth $22.0MM: fails Alternative Size Standard even if average net income is below $6.5MM.
Applicant meets both thresholds and will use all assistance in a labor surplus area: lender applies a 25% size standard increase when evaluating eligibility under the Alternative Size Standard.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA eligibility failure; potential denial of SBA guaranty or adverse lender oversight finding

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDIT_POLICY_18_010 applies to an SBA applicant including affiliates. BR_BORROWER_EXPOSURE_AGGREGATION_REGULATORY_21_005 requires determining and documenting affiliates, which is necessary to correctly compute consolidated net worth/net income for size eligibility when affiliates exist.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If affiliates are missed, the lender may incorrectly qualify an ineligible borrower as 'small,' creating program eligibility and guaranty risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express underwriting must prioritize business cash flow and decline the loan if there is no timely repayment assurance regardless of collateral
business_rules.description: A lender must analyze each Export Express application in a commercially reasonable manner consistent with prudent lending standards, and must treat the applicant’s business cash flow as the primary source of repayment rather than expected recovery from collateral liquidation. If the lender’s financial analysis shows the applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from business cash flow, the loan request must be declined regardless of collateral available or outside sources of repayment.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Export Express loan or line of credit requests when performing financial analysis and credit decisioning.
business_rules.consequences: If cash flow analysis does not provide reasonable assurance of timely repayment, lender must decline the request even when collateral appears sufficient.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: 4. Credit Standards for Export Express
source_reference.source_text: A Lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner, consistent with prudent lending standards. The cash flow of the Applicant is the primary source of repayment, not any expected recovery from the liquidation of collateral. Thus, if the Lender’s financial analysis demonstrates that the Applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from the cash flow of the business, the loan request must be declined, regardless of the collateral available or outside sources of repayment.

examples: Applicant requests $350,000 Export Express term loan; projected cash flow shows inability to service debt—lender must decline even if collateral coverage is strong.
Applicant has a guarantor willing to pay if the business fails, but business cash flow is insufficient—loan must still be declined under this standard.
Applicant’s business cash flow supports repayment with reasonable assurance—standard permits approval subject to other SBA and lender requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty vulnerability if credit decision not supportable

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express underwriting must document credit not available elsewhere and lender must not make the loan if available without SBA guaranty on reasonable terms
business_rules.description: Export Express lenders must not make an Export Express loan that would be available on reasonable commercial terms from the lender itself or another source without an SBA guaranty. The credit analysis must include factors demonstrating the applicant does not have credit available elsewhere on reasonable commercial terms from non-Federal, non-State, and non-local government sources, consistent with SBA credit not available elsewhere requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when underwriting and documenting an SBA 7(a) Export Express loan/line request under delegated authority.
business_rules.consequences: If credit is available elsewhere on reasonable terms without SBA guaranty, the Export Express loan is not permissible under the program requirements; failure to document may create SBA guaranty risk in purchase/oversight reviews.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: b. Underwriting
source_reference.source_text: Lenders must not make an Export Express loan that would be available on reasonable commercial terms from either the Lender itself or another source without an SBA guaranty. The credit analysis must include the factors demonstrating the Applicant does not have credit available elsewhere on reasonable commercial terms from non-Federal, non-State, non-local government sources...

examples: Applicant qualifies for identical $250,000 revolver at market terms without guaranty—Export Express request must not be approved under program requirements.
Applicant has been declined for conventional credit due to tenor/collateral constraints; lender documents lack of credit elsewhere—requirement satisfied.
Lender approves Export Express loan but file lacks credit-elsewhere factors—creates SBA purchase/repair risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty purchase denial/repair risk; SBA lender oversight criticism

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_19_004 requires Export Express credit analysis to demonstrate lack of credit available elsewhere on reasonable terms. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_17_006 defines the required memo content and prohibited rationale patterns that substantiate this eligibility concept.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 17_006-level documentation is not present, 19_004 compliance is typically unprovable in SBA review, elevating guaranty denial/repair risk.

dependencies.rationale: Export Express cannot be made if credit is available elsewhere on reasonable commercial terms without SBA guaranty. That is the same substantive rule in BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_REGULATORY_17_005, applied within Export Express delegated authority.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender cannot truthfully certify 17_005, the Export Express request fails 19_004 and should not be made under SBA guaranty.

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express business credit scoring may not rely solely on consumer credit scores and requires documentation and validation of model predictiveness
business_rules.description: Export Express lenders may use a business credit scoring model only in addition to appropriate, prudent, and generally accepted industry credit analysis and procedures, and such procedures must be consistent with those used for similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans. A business credit scoring model cannot rely solely on consumer credit scores. If used, results must be documented in each loan file and be available for SBA review. Lenders must validate and document, using appropriate and accepted statistical methodologies, that the business credit scoring model is predictive of loan performance and must provide validation documentation to SBA upon request. SBLCs must provide credit scoring model validation to SBA annually.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an Export Express lender uses business credit scoring in underwriting (including SBLC usage).
business_rules.consequences: If scoring is used without required supplemental analysis, documentation, or validation, SBA may criticize underwriting during oversight and could impair guaranty supportability.
business_rules.exceptions: SBLCs may use credit scoring despite not making non-SBA guaranteed loans, but must provide annual validation to SBA.

source_reference.section_id: b. Underwriting v
source_reference.source_text: Export Express Lenders may use a business credit scoring model (such a model cannot rely solely on consumer credit scores)... The business credit scoring model may only be used in addition to the Lender’s appropriate... credit analysis... If used, the business credit scoring results must be documented in each loan file... Lenders must validate (and document) with appropriate and accepted statistical methodologies that their business credit scoring model is predictive of loan performance and they must provide that documentation to SBA upon request... SBLCs are required to provide credit scoring model validation to SBA on an annual basis.

examples: Lender approves Export Express loan using only consumer FICO—noncompliant because business model cannot rely solely on consumer scores and must supplement with prudent analysis.
Lender uses business scoring plus standard cash flow/credit memo and retains score output in file—compliant if model validation is documented and available.
SBLC uses a credit scoring model—must deliver validation to SBA annually.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; potential guaranty vulnerability in review

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_UNDERWRITING_19_003 establishes cash flow as primary repayment source and requires decline if repayment is not reasonably assured. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_UNDERWRITING_19_005 permits credit scoring only as a supplement to prudent analysis and prohibits reliance solely on consumer scores.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If scoring is used without prudent analysis, the underwriting standard in 19_003 is not satisfied, increasing SBA oversight findings and potential guaranty support issues.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDIT_POLICY_19_006 conditions eligibility (for <12 months operation) on using conventional commercial underwriting procedures and not relying solely on scoring. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_UNDERWRITING_19_005 provides the required constraints and documentation expectations for scoring use.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If lender uses scoring as the primary/sole basis, the borrower may be ineligible for Export Express under the <12-month exception, causing program eligibility and guaranty risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express eligibility requires at least 12 full months in operation unless export expertise and conventional underwriting conditions are met
business_rules.description: Eligibility for Export Express is limited to businesses that have been in operation (not necessarily as exporters) for at least 12 full months. Applicants in operation for less than 12 months are eligible only if both conditions are met: (1) the applicant’s key personnel have clearly demonstrated export transaction expertise and substantial previous successful business experience; and (2) the lender processes the Export Express loan using conventional commercial loan underwriting procedures and does not rely solely on credit scoring or credit matrices to approve the loan. Non-bank lenders without a conventional loan portfolio must submit underwriting procedures to the Office of Credit Risk Management for written approval prior to making an Export Express loan. Evidence of compliance with both requirements must be retained in the lender’s file.
business_rules.conditions: Applies at application/underwriting for SBA 7(a) Export Express program eligibility when business has <12 months of operating history.
business_rules.consequences: If <12 months operation and either condition is not met (export expertise/substantial experience AND conventional underwriting not solely scoring), the applicant is not eligible for Export Express; failure to retain evidence creates program compliance risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Eligibility and Eligible Uses of Proceeds for Export Express
source_reference.source_text: Eligibility for Export Express is limited to businesses that have been in operation... for at least 12 full months. However, Applicants... less than 12 months are eligible if both... The Applicant’s key personnel have clearly demonstrated Export Transaction expertise... and The Lender processes the Export Express loan using conventional commercial loan underwriting procedures and does not rely solely on credit scoring... Non-bank Lenders... must submit their underwriting procedures... for written approval... Evidence of compliance... must be retained...

examples: Business operating 9 months; management has documented export expertise and lender uses full conventional underwriting with cash flow analysis—eligible if evidence retained.
Business operating 10 months; lender approves based solely on a scorecard—noncompliant and ineligible for Export Express.
Non-bank lender with no conventional portfolio wants to originate Export Express—must obtain written approval of underwriting procedures before making the loan.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program eligibility violation; potential SBA guaranty vulnerability

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express refinancing requires debt to be current for 12 months and generally requires 10% payment reduction with 29-day delinquency definition
business_rules.description: SBA-guaranteed loan proceeds may not be used to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss (including the same institution’s debt), and refinancing that shifts potential loss to SBA is prohibited. For Export Express refinancing, the debt to be refinanced must be, and must have been, current for at least the last 12 months or for the life of the loan (whichever is less). 'Current' means a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days; a loan matured and not paid within 29 days is not current and is not eligible. When refinancing eligible debt from another lender, the new payment amount must be at least 10% less than the existing aggregate payment amount(s), and may include a balloon due within 18 months. If the refinanced note has escalating payments, the new installment must be at least 10% less than the expected installment within the next 12 months. Revolving debt refinancing is not subject to the 10% payment improvement requirement. Merchant cash advances and factoring agreements are not eligible for refinancing. Export Express lenders may not refinance their own same institution debt under Export Express and may not refinance their own Export Express or other SBA-guaranteed loans with a new Export Express loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when Export Express loan proceeds are proposed to refinance existing borrower debt.
business_rules.consequences: If the debt is not 'current' under the 29-day rule, or the payment reduction requirement is not met (when applicable), or the debt type is ineligible, the refinancing use of proceeds is not permitted for Export Express; lender must process differently (e.g., non-delegated 7(a) Small/Standard for same-institution refinancing) or decline.
business_rules.exceptions: Refinancing revolving debt is not subject to the 10% payment improvement requirement.

source_reference.section_id: Debt Refinancing
source_reference.source_text: 120.201 SBA-guaranteed loan proceeds may not be used to pay a creditor in a position to sustain a loss (including the same institution’s debt). This includes any refinancing that will shift all or part of a potential loss from the original lender to the SBA. The debt to be refinanced

examples: Borrower seeks to refinance a term loan that had a payment 35 days late two months ago—debt is not 'current' under 29-day definition; not eligible for Export Express refinancing.
Borrower refinances another bank’s term loan; new payment is 12% lower and includes a balloon due in 12 months—meets the 10% improvement and 18-month balloon allowance.
Borrower wants to refinance a merchant cash advance—categorically ineligible for Export Express refinancing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair risk if ineligible refinancing proceeds included; SBA lender oversight criticism

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: Applicant is ineligible for SBA financial assistance if required Federal tax returns were not filed
business_rules.description: If an applicant has not filed required Federal tax returns, the applicant is not eligible for SBA financial assistance. SBA’s tax verification process is intended to confirm that required returns were filed and that financial statements provided as part of the application agree with the returns submitted to the IRS.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA loan applicants when determining eligibility and performing tax verification/financial information verification.
business_rules.consequences: If required Federal returns are not filed, applicant is not eligible; lender must not proceed with SBA financial assistance under the program.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception for U.S. Territories is addressed elsewhere in the same section (territory taxing authority verification in lieu of IRS transcripts when not required to file Federal returns).

source_reference.section_id: IRS Tax Transcript/Verification of Financial Information
source_reference.source_text: The purpose of SBA’s Tax Verification process is to determine if: The Applicant filed business tax returns; and The Applicant’s financial statements... agree with the business tax returns... If an Applicant has not filed required Federal tax returns, the Applicant is not eligible for SBA financial assistance.

examples: Applicant admits it has not filed the last required Federal business return—applicant is ineligible for SBA financial assistance.
Applicant filed returns but lender’s verification shows missing required year—loan cannot proceed until resolved; if not filed, ineligible.
Applicant is in a U.S. Territory and not required to file Federal returns—must use territory tax authority receipt-stamped returns per exception elsewhere in SOP.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program eligibility violation; SBA guaranty denial risk

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: Commercial real estate appraisal is required for close-relationship transactions and must be USPAP-compliant and no older than 12 months at application
business_rules.description: For all 7(a) Small and SBA Express loans secured by commercial real property, the lender must obtain an appraisal when (1) the transaction involves parties with a close relationship (e.g., between existing owners or family members), or (2) SBA or the lender concludes an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness. Appraisals must comply with USPAP and must be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty. The lender may not rely on an appraisal prepared more than 12 months prior to the date of application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the loan is a 7(a) Small or SBA Express secured by commercial real property and either close-relationship transaction exists or an appraisal is otherwise deemed necessary by SBA/lender; also applies to any appraisal relied upon for the transaction.
business_rules.consequences: If the appraisal trigger is met, lender must obtain a USPAP-compliant appraisal dated within 12 months of the guaranty application; failure is a documentation/collateral compliance defect and may require cure prior to closing or may affect SBA guaranty enforceability.
business_rules.exceptions: If an appraisal is not required under the close-relationship/necessity trigger, the lender must instead obtain an appropriate evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices (see related rule BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_20_003).

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal and Business Valuation Requirements – Commercial Real Estate
source_reference.source_text: For all 7(a) Small and SBA Express loans secured by commercial real property: If the loan finances a transaction involving parties with a close relationship... the Lender must obtain an appraisal. Appraisals must be in compliance with the Uniform Standards of Professional Appraisal Practice (USPAP). Additionally, SBA requires that completed appraisals be dated within 12 months of the application for guaranty... In no case may the Lender rely on an appraisal that was prepared more than 12 months prior to the date of the application.

examples: Borrower buys a building from a family member using an SBA Express loan secured by that building: appraisal is required and must be USPAP-compliant and dated within 12 months of guaranty application.
Lender determines market volatility requires a full appraisal to evaluate creditworthiness even though seller is unrelated: appraisal becomes required under the SOP.
Lender has a 14-month-old appraisal in file: lender cannot rely on it for the SBA guaranty application; a new appraisal is required if appraisal is needed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk due to appraisal noncompliance

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: If appraisal is not required for SBA Express commercial real estate, an evaluation consistent with interagency appraisal guidance is required
business_rules.description: When a USPAP appraisal is not required under SBA Express commercial real estate appraisal triggers, the lender must obtain an appropriate evaluation of the commercial real estate securing the loan consistent with safe and sound banking practices. Evaluations are not required to be USPAP-compliant or performed by state-licensed/certified appraisers but should be consistent with Interagency Guidance Appraisal and Evaluation Guidelines and the Interagency Advisory on the Use of Evaluations.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to 7(a) Small and SBA Express loans secured by commercial real property when appraisal is not required under the close-relationship/necessity paragraph.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must document an evaluation process/output consistent with interagency guidance; failure may be criticized as unsafe/unsound and an SBA program compliance defect.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; if appraisal is required, this evaluation-only approach is insufficient.

source_reference.section_id: Real Estate Appraisal and Business Valuation Requirements – Evaluations
source_reference.source_text: If an appraisal is not required under the preceding Paragraph, all Lenders must obtain an appropriate evaluation of the commercial real estate securing the loan that is consistent with safe and sound banking practices. Evaluations are not required to be performed in accordance with USPAP or by State licensed or certified appraisers but should be consistent with the Interagency Guidance Appraisal and Evaluation Guidelines and the Interagency Advisory on the Use of Evaluations...

examples: SBA Express loan is secured by a small office building, not a related-party transaction, and lender does not determine a full appraisal is necessary: lender must obtain and retain a compliant evaluation.
Lender skips both appraisal and evaluation and closes the SBA Express loan: this violates the SOP evaluation requirement.
Evaluation is performed by an internal qualified staff member (not state-certified): acceptable if consistent with safe and sound practices and interagency guidance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA review findings and potential guaranty repair risk for collateral valuation noncompliance

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_20_002 defines when a USPAP appraisal is required for 7(a) Small/SBA Express CRE. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_20_003 applies only when that appraisal trigger is not met, requiring an appropriate evaluation consistent with interagency guidance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender misclassifies the appraisal trigger status, it may obtain neither a required appraisal nor a compliant evaluation, creating a valuation/documentation defect affecting SBA compliance and safety-and-soundness.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_38_008 states that for transactions ≤$250,000 an appraisal is not required under agencies’ rules, but an evaluation is required when no exemption applies. BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_20_003 similarly requires an evaluation when SBA appraisal triggers do not apply, referencing interagency evaluation guidance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If lenders ignore the interagency evaluation framework while relying on 20_003, evaluation quality may be deficient and subject to examiner/SBA criticism.

business_rules.rule_name: Appraiser independence is required and state certification is required when estimated commercial property value exceeds 1,000,000 dollars
business_rules.description: For SBA Express (and 7(a) Small) commercial real estate appraisals, the appraiser must be independent with no appearance of a conflict of interest and must be either state-licensed or state-certified. If the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be state-certified (not merely state-licensed).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an appraisal is obtained for commercial real property securing a 7(a) Small or SBA Express loan; the $1,000,000 threshold is based on estimated value of the commercial property.
business_rules.consequences: If estimated value > $1,000,000 and appraiser is not state-certified, appraisal does not meet SBA requirements and must be replaced/cured; use of conflicted appraiser violates SBA appraisal independence requirements and may jeopardize guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for independence; licensing exception is only that state-certified is specifically required when estimated value is over $1,000,000.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate – Appraiser requirements
source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must be: Independent and have no appearance of a conflict of interest... and Either State-licensed or State-certified, with the following exception: when the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified.

examples: Estimated property value is $1.2MM: lender must engage a state-certified appraiser; a state-licensed-only appraiser is not acceptable.
Appraiser is related to the borrower’s principal and has a financial interest in the property: independence requirement is violated even if state-certified.
Estimated property value is $850k: a state-licensed appraiser may be acceptable if independent and otherwise compliant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk due to invalid appraisal independence/credentialing

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: If the lender elects not to take collateral under the ≤$50,000 allowance, CRE appraisal rules may not be triggered; if collateral is taken (or required by lender policy), appraisal requirements become applicable and must be met.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misapplying the collateral-not-required threshold can lead to either (a) unnecessary appraisal activity, or (b) taking CRE collateral but failing to satisfy appraisal independence/certification requirements, creating SBA compliance risk.

dependencies.rationale: Appraiser credentialing and independence standards are SBA program rules invoked in SBA Express/7(a) Small contexts.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the applicant is ineligible for SBA 7(a), compliance with appraiser requirements cannot enable SBA guaranty eligibility.

dependencies.rationale: An appraisal can be USPAP-compliant with correct client designation but still fail SBA if the appraiser is conflicted or not properly licensed/certified (and vice versa). These are paired acceptance criteria for the same artifact (the appraisal).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either requirement is not met, the appraisal is not SBA-compliant; lender must cure/replace before SBA reliance, risking guaranty repair/denial and process delays.

dependencies.rationale: 20_005 sets SBA minimum appraiser independence/credential standards (including a >$1MM state-certified trigger). 29_010 governs when an appraisal is required and the credential expectations for regulated banks; when 29_010 is stricter, it controls for safety-and-soundness/regulatory compliance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to follow 29_010 can lead to regulatory criticism and required remediation, regardless of SBA program compliance; following only SBA thresholds may be insufficient for OCC-supervised institutions.

business_rules.rule_name: Construction or substantial renovation appraisals must be as-completed and substantial is rehab cost greater than one-third of purchase price or market value
business_rules.description: If the SBA Express (or 7(a) Small) loan finances new construction or substantial renovation of an existing building, the appraisal must estimate market value at completion. Substantial rehabilitation is defined as rehabilitation expenses of more than one-third of the purchase price or fair market value at the time of application. After construction completes, lender must obtain a statement from the appraiser, general contractor, project architect, or construction management firm confirming the building was built with only minor deviations from the plans/specifications used for the original estimate of value. If the lender cannot obtain such a statement, the lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to 7(a) Small and SBA Express loans secured by commercial real property used to finance (a) new construction, or (b) substantial renovation as defined by > 1/3 rehab expense threshold.
business_rules.consequences: Appraisal must be based on as-completed value; post-completion deviation statement is required. If deviation statement cannot be obtained, lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing; closing without permission is a program violation and may jeopardize guaranty.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the appraisal or statement requirement; SBA written permission is required if the statement cannot be obtained.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate – New construction or substantial renovation
source_reference.source_text: If the loan will be used to finance new construction or the substantial renovation of an existing building, the appraisal must estimate what the market value will be at completion of construction. (“Substantial” means rehabilitation expenses of more than one-third of the purchase price or fair market value at the time of the application.) After construction is completed, Lender must obtain a statement... that the building was built with only minor deviations... If the Lender cannot obtain such a statement, then the Lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission.

examples: Purchase price is $900,000 and planned rehab is $350,000 (> 1/3): project is substantial renovation; appraisal must be as-completed.
After completion, lender cannot obtain a deviation statement from appraiser/GC/architect/CM firm: lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing.
New construction funded by SBA Express: appraisal must estimate market value at completion rather than only current land value.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and pre-closing prohibition without SBA written permission

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: The as-completed valuation and post-completion deviation statement requirement is an SBA 7(a) collateral compliance rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core SBA eligibility fails, as-completed appraisal compliance does not permit SBA processing/closing.

dependencies.rationale: For new construction/substantial renovation, the valuation must be 'market value at completion' (20_006). That valuation must still be delivered in an SBA-acceptable appraisal report with correct client/intended user and independence from seller/applicant-prepared reports (20_004).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the appraisal is not SBA-acceptable under 20_004, the lender cannot rely on it to meet 20_006; closing may be delayed until a compliant appraisal is obtained.

dependencies.rationale: 13_010 can require Section 106 review and stop-work instructions that affect project feasibility and timing; 20_006 requires as-completed valuation and post-completion deviation documentation/permission. Both must be managed cohesively in construction/rehab projects.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 13_010 is missed, SBA may delay/deny approval/disbursement regardless of appraisal quality; if 20_006 is missed, SBA may cite appraisal/closing violations even if historic compliance is satisfied.

dependencies.rationale: BR_20_006 prohibits closing without SBA prior written permission if the deviation statement cannot be obtained. BR_26_004 is the formal delegated-lender workflow to obtain and evidence that permission/exception and retain it in the file, effectively enabling the alternate compliance pathway.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_26_004 steps are incomplete (no approval, no retained email), the lender cannot evidence the override/permission required to satisfy BR_20_006’s alternate compliance condition, increasing guaranty impairment risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Construction deviations on SBA-guaranteed construction-period loans must be reported to CLSC with remedy and collateral valuation details
business_rules.description: If the SBA-guaranteed loan was used to cover the construction period, the lender must notify the CLSC of any deviations and work with the CLSC to determine an appropriate course of action, including additional collateral. The lender’s notification must explain reasons for differences between estimated and actual values and recommend a remedy (e.g., additional equity or additional collateral). If additional collateral is required, lender must identify both fair market value and liquidation value of the additional collateral. If the lender cannot obtain a minor-deviations statement but obtains a new appraisal showing market value meets or exceeds the original estimate, no additional action is necessary.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA-guaranteed loan covered construction period and there are deviation(s) or value differences versus original as-completed estimate.
business_rules.consequences: Lender must notify CLSC and provide analysis/remedy; if additional collateral is required, FMV and liquidation value must be documented. Failure to notify or provide adequate support risks program noncompliance and potential guaranty impairment.
business_rules.exceptions: If no minor-deviations statement is available but a new appraisal demonstrates market value meets/exceeds original estimate, no additional action is necessary.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate – Construction period deviations and CLSC notification
source_reference.source_text: permission. If the SBA-guaranteed loan was used to cover the construction period, the Lender must notify the CLSC of any deviation(s) and work with the CLSC to determine an appropriate course of action, including the securing of additional collateral. The Lender’s notification to SBA must

examples: As-completed value estimated at $2.0MM but actual completion indicates $1.7MM: lender must notify CLSC with reasons and propose remedy (e.g., additional equity or collateral).
Lender requires an additional parcel as collateral: lender must document fair market value and liquidation value of the additional parcel in the submission.
Minor-deviations statement unavailable, but new appraisal shows completed market value is $2.05MM (≥ original $2.0MM): lender need not take further action beyond obtaining the new appraisal.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA review findings and potential guaranty repair/denial risk for failure to notify/mitigate deviations

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 20_006 creates the 'as-completed' benchmark and post-completion confirmation requirement; 20_007 governs what to do when actual vs estimated values deviate for construction-period SBA-guaranteed loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 20_006 is not satisfied (no as-completed estimate / no completion confirmation), deviations cannot be properly assessed or reported under 20_007, risking program noncompliance and guaranty impairment.

dependencies.rationale: The rule is explicitly tied to SBA-guaranteed construction-period usage and CLSC interaction.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the loan is not SBA-eligible, SBA-specific CLSC deviation workflow cannot be used; attempting SBA closing/disbursement risks ineligibility and findings.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Express collateral valuation must exclude rental income contributory value and intangible assets
business_rules.description: When valuing commercial real estate collateral for SBA Express (and 7(a) Small) loans, the lender must not include the contributory value of any rental income or the value of any intangible assets contained in the appraisal.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever a lender is valuing collateral (via appraisal or evaluation) for a 7(a) Small or SBA Express loan secured by commercial real property.
business_rules.consequences: Collateral value used for underwriting and SBA compliance must be adjusted to exclude rental income contributory value and intangible asset value; if included, valuation is noncompliant and may require re-underwriting and corrective action.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Commercial Real Estate – Valuation limitations
source_reference.source_text: When valuing the collateral, the Lender must not include the contributory value of any rental income or the value of any intangible assets contained in the appraisal.

examples: Appraisal assigns additional value based on projected third-party rental income from unused space: lender must exclude that contributory rental income value when valuing collateral for SBA purposes.
Appraisal includes value for goodwill/intangible brand attributes associated with property operations: lender must exclude intangible asset value from collateral valuation.
Lender relies on a blended value including intangibles to meet internal LTV: must adjust value downward for SBA-compliant valuation and reassess underwriting.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA review findings; potential guaranty repair for improper collateral valuation inputs

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 20_009 is an adjustment rule applied to the valuation result (exclude contributory rental income/intangibles). 29_008 is a primary trigger for obtaining a compliant CRE appraisal.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the appraisal/evaluation is missing or stale, the lender cannot apply 20_009 correctly; underwriting collateral value is unsupported and may require re-underwriting.

dependencies.rationale: Excluding contributory rental income and intangible value is an SBA underwriting compliance constraint.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SBA eligibility fails, SBA valuation-exclusion compliance cannot support SBA guaranty eligibility.

dependencies.rationale: To exclude contributory rental income and intangible asset value from the collateral value (20_009), the lender must first have an acceptable valuation report to analyze (20_004).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the appraisal is not compliant/usable, the lender cannot reliably calculate the SBA-compliant collateral value under 20_009, causing re-underwriting and potential closing delays.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) Small Loan applications must be screened using SBSS and minimum acceptable score is 165 as of June 1, 2025
business_rules.description: All SBA 7(a) Small Loan applications must begin with screening for a FICO Small Business Scoring Service (SBSS) Score. As of the SOP effective date, the minimum acceptable SBSS score is 165 (subject to change by SBA and published on SBA’s website). If the applicant does not receive an acceptable SBSS score, the loan must be processed under Standard 7(a) procedures; alternatively, lenders with SBA Express authority may submit via SBA Express processing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to 7(a) Small Loan applications at intake/initial screening when attempting expedited small loan processing relying on SBSS screening.
business_rules.consequences: If SBSS < minimum acceptable, lender must not process as 7(a) Small expedited path based on SBSS; must route to Standard 7(a) or SBA Express (if authorized).
business_rules.exceptions: Minimum score may be adjusted by SBA from time to time; lenders should reference SBA’s posted minimum acceptable SBSS score.

source_reference.section_id: Underwriting 7(a) Small Loans — SBSS screening and minimum score
source_reference.source_text: All 7(a) Small Loan applications will begin with a screening for a FICO® Small Business Scoring ServiceSM Score (SBSS Score)... As of the effective date of this SOP, the minimum acceptable SBSS score is 165... If the Applicant does not receive an acceptable SBSS credit score, the loan must be processed following the procedures for Standard 7(a) Loans. Alternatively, Lenders with SBA Express authority may submit the application via that processing method.

examples: Applicant SBSS is 172. Lender may continue processing as a 7(a) Small Loan under the expedited SBSS screening approach (subject to other SBA eligibility rules).
Applicant SBSS is 160. Lender must process under Standard 7(a) procedures or (if it has authority) may submit via SBA Express.
SBA later raises minimum SBSS to 170 on its website; lender must apply 170 for new 7(a) Small applications after the change.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty processing issues; lender oversight criticism for improper processing method

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 21_007 is a processing rule within 7(a) Small workflows. 38_001 is an eligibility gate under 13 CFR 120.110(a) that prevents SBA loan processing regardless of screening score.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 38_001 is not satisfied (i.e., applicant is a non-profit), continuing SBSS-based processing wastes resources and risks compliance findings for processing an ineligible applicant.

dependencies.rationale: 21_007 governs initial SBSS screening for 7(a) Small. 38_002 is an SBA eligibility restriction that bars SBA processing for prohibited business types regardless of credit score.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 38_002 is violated, the lender may generate an SBSS-compliant file that is still ineligible, risking denial/withdrawal and potential SBA oversight criticism.

dependencies.rationale: SBSS screening determines the processing path within SBA 7(a) Small vs Standard/Express; only relevant if SBA-eligible.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core eligibility fails, SBSS routing cannot be used to obtain SBA loan number/guaranty.

business_rules.rule_name: Applicant is ineligible for a new SBA 7(a) loan number if any existing SBA 7(a) or 504 loan is not current and current means no required payment unpaid more than 29 days
business_rules.description: Regardless of SBSS score, the applicant business is ineligible for a new SBA 7(a) loan number if the applicant business has an existing 7(a) or 504 loan that is not current at the time of issuance of the new 7(a) SBA loan number. “Current” means a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days. A loan that has matured and is not paid within 29 days of the maturity date is not current and is not eligible for refinancing.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small Loan processing at the time a new SBA 7(a) loan number would be issued; requires verification of current status on any existing SBA 7(a)/504 loans for the applicant business.
business_rules.consequences: If any existing SBA 7(a) or 504 loan is not current (per 29-day definition), the applicant is ineligible for the new SBA 7(a) loan number; lender must not proceed under SBA guaranty and must cure delinquency or decline/withdraw application.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Underwriting 7(a) Small Loans — ineligibility if existing SBA loans not current
source_reference.source_text: Regardless of the SBSS score, the Applicant business is ineligible if the Applicant business has an existing 7(a) or 504 loan that is not current at the time of issuance of the new 7(a) SBA loan number. “Current” means that a required payment has not remained unpaid for more than 29 days. A loan that has matured and not been paid within 29 days of the maturity date is not current and is not eligible for refinancing.

examples: Applicant has an existing SBA 504 loan with a payment 35 days past due. Applicant is ineligible for issuance of a new SBA 7(a) loan number.
Applicant’s existing SBA 7(a) loan matured 20 days ago and is unpaid. The loan is still considered current under the 29-day definition (subject to any other SBA rules).
Applicant’s SBA 7(a) loan matured 40 days ago and remains unpaid. It is not current and is not eligible for refinancing; applicant is ineligible for a new SBA 7(a) loan number.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA loan number issuance denial; SBA guaranty ineligibility

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 21_007 determines whether the application can proceed in the 7(a) Small expedited path or must route to Standard 7(a)/Express. 21_008 is a later-stage eligibility gate tied to issuance of a new SBA loan number.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If sequencing is reversed or 21_008 is delayed too long, the lender may spend underwriting resources on a file that later becomes ineligible for a loan number due to non-current existing SBA debt.

dependencies.rationale: The 'must be current on existing SBA loans' restriction is an SBA gating criterion for issuance of a new SBA loan number.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core eligibility fails, SBA loan number issuance is barred regardless of current-status testing; proceeding risks guaranty denial.

business_rules.rule_name: Start-up businesses require minimum 10% equity injection for SBA 7(a) loans and start-up means 1 year or less in operation
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) purposes, a start-up business is one in operation (generating revenue from intended operations) for 1 year or less. All 7(a) loans made to a start-up business require an equity injection (Applicant contribution) of at least 10% of total project costs. Total project costs include all costs required to become operational, regardless of source of funds, except for lines of credit and 504 loans. Loans approved more than 90 days apart are treated as separate projects for this purpose.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans to start-up businesses and requires identifying total project costs and verifying injection sources; also applies when there are multiple SBA loan approvals and determining whether they are the same project (≤90 days) or separate projects (>90 days).
business_rules.consequences: If equity injection is below 10% of total project costs for a start-up, lender must increase verified equity injection or restructure/decline to meet SBA requirements; otherwise SBA guaranty eligibility risk exists.
business_rules.exceptions: Total project costs exclude lines of credit and 504 loans when calculating the required equity injection under this start-up rule.

source_reference.section_id: Underwriting 7(a) Small Loans — 7(a) Small Equity Requirements (Start-Up Businesses)
source_reference.source_text: SBA considers a business to be a “start-up”... if it has been in operation... for 1 year or less... All 7(a) loans made to a Start-Up Business require a 10% equity injection based on the project cost; however, loans approved more than 90 days apart from each other are considered to be separate projects.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2: 7(a) Small & SBA Express — Minimum equity injection for Start-Up Businesses
source_reference.source_text: for the purpose of determining equity injection requirements if it has bee n in operation (i.e., generating revenue from intended operations) for 1 year or less. SBA considers an equity injection (Applicant contribution) of at least 10 percent of the total project costs

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Equity requirements — Minimum equity injection for Start-Up Businesses
source_reference.source_text: Start-Up Businesses – At a minimum, SBA considers an equity injection (Applicant contribution) of at least 10 percent of the total project costs (all costs required to become operational, regardless of the source of funds, except for lines of credit and 504 loans) to be necessary... SBA considers a business to be a “start-up”... if it has been in operation... for 1 year or less

examples: A restaurant operating for 9 months seeking a 7(a) term loan for buildout must document at least 10% injection of total project costs.
If two SBA loans are approved 4 months apart for different phases, treat as separate projects for injection analysis.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty ineligibility/repair at purchase; lender oversight finding for insufficient equity injection documentation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

deduplication_info.rationale: All three rules define start-up as <=1 year in operation (generating revenue) and require at least 10% equity injection for all SBA 7(a) loans to start-ups, with total project costs defined consistently (costs to become operational; LOCs and 504 excluded). Differences are only wording and inclusion of the 90-day project separation nuance.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDITPOLICY_21_009 selected: includes the key definitional element (<=1 year generating revenue), the 10% minimum injection, total project cost definition, and the 'separate project' clarification tied to approvals more than 90 days apart.

dependencies.rationale: The 10% injection test is an SBA 7(a) rule; it matters only if the applicant is SBA-eligible and remains eligible through closing/disbursement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core eligibility fails, equity injection compliance does not permit SBA guaranty eligibility; proceeding risks denial/repair.

business_rules.rule_name: Change of ownership requires lender-requested business valuation and independent valuation thresholds apply at $250,000 and for special purpose properties
business_rules.description: For SBA change of ownership transactions, a business valuation (excluding any real estate, which is separately valued via real estate appraisal) is a key underwriting component. The business valuation must be requested by and prepared for the lender, include the conclusion of value, qualifications of the individual performing the valuation, and the individual’s signature certifying the information. The lender may not use a business valuation prepared for the applicant or the seller. For non-special purpose properties, if (total amount being financed including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other financing minus appraised value of real estate and/or equipment being financed) is > $250,000, the lender must obtain an independent business valuation from a Qualified Source; also required if there is a close relationship between buyer and seller. For special purpose properties (as defined), if that same financed-minus-appraised calculation is over $250,000 or there is a close relationship and the business operates from a special purpose property, the lender must obtain an independent business valuation performed by a Certified General Real Property Appraiser who has completed at least four going concern appraisals of equivalent special use property within the last 36 months and who must conduct the appraisal in compliance with current USPAP guidelines. The business valuation may be obtained after issuance of an SBA loan number but before closing (credit memo must be updated after receipt). Any amount of loan proceeds used to facilitate a change of ownership may not exceed the business valuation. Lender must obtain the financial information relied upon by the valuator and verify it against the seller’s IRS transcripts.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Small and SBA Express change-of-ownership transactions (and by cross-reference, where IT change of ownership requires business valuation compliance) when determining whether an independent valuation is required and what credentialing standards apply.
business_rules.consequences: If valuation is required and not obtained in the required form (requested/prepared for lender; independent; correct credentialing for special purpose properties; IRS transcript verification), lender must remediate prior to closing or risk SBA guaranty denial/repair and lender oversight criticism. If loan proceeds for change of ownership exceed business valuation, the transaction must be restructured to comply.
business_rules.exceptions: If financed-minus-appraised value is $250,000 or less, lender may perform its own valuation unless lender internal policy requires independent valuation. Special purpose property definition drives enhanced credential requirements.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 2: 7(a) Small & SBA Express — Additional Change of Ownership Business Valuation Requirements
source_reference.source_text: In order for the individual performing the business valuation to identify the scope of work appropriately, the business valuation must be requested by and prepared for the Lender... The Lender may not use a business valuation prepared for the Applicant or the seller... If the amount being financed... minus the appraised value of real estate and/or equipment is greater than $250,000... the Lender must obtain an independent business valuation... If... over $250,000... and the business operates from a Special Purpose Property, the Lender must obtain an independent business valuation performed by a Certified General Real Property Appraiser... completed no less than four going concern appraisals... within the last 36 months... Any amount(s) of the loan proceeds... may not exceed the business valuation... verify... against the seller’s IRS transcripts.

examples: Non-special purpose property deal: total financing $900,000; appraised real estate/equipment $700,000; difference $200,000 (≤$250,000). Lender may perform its own valuation unless internal policy requires an independent Qualified Source valuation.

Non-special purpose property deal: total financing $1,000,000; appraised real estate/equipment $600,000; difference $400,000 (>$250,000). Lender must obtain an independent valuation from a Qualified Source and it must be prepared for the lender (not the seller).

Special purpose property deal with close buyer-seller relationship: even if the difference is ≤$250,000, the close relationship triggers independent valuation; if difference >$250,000 and special purpose property, valuation must be by Certified General Real Property Appraiser meeting the 4-going-concern-in-36-months experience requirement and USPAP compliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; lender oversight criticism for valuation noncompliance

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 24_008 supplies the decision logic (net financed amount threshold and close-relationship trigger) that turns on 21_010’s documentation requirements for a lender-requested, lender-prepared valuation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 24_008 is not applied correctly, the file may omit a required independent valuation or apply the wrong credentialing standard, creating SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure.
