dependencies.rationale: Independent business valuation requirements are SBA closing/underwriting controls applicable only for SBA-guaranteed transactions.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SBA eligibility fails, obtaining a compliant valuation does not permit SBA closing/disbursement; proceeding risks guaranty denial/repair.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA EWCP loans must be secured by at least a first security interest in all transaction collateral located in the United States and transferred collateral upon refinancing
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Export Working Capital Program (EWCP) loans, the loan must be secured by no less than a first security interest in all collateral associated with the transactions financed. This includes at least export inventory and receivables, and assignment of credit insurance, letters of credit proceeds, and contract proceeds as applicable. Collateral must be located in the United States, its territories, or possessions. When EWCP loan proceeds are used for debt refinancing, the collateral for the loan being refinanced must be transferred to secure the EWCP loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA EWCP loans under SOP 50 10 8, effective June 1, 2025, including when proceeds are used for refinancing and when determining eligible collateral and lien position requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender does not perfect at least a first security interest in required transaction collateral and ensure collateral location requirements, the EWCP loan may be ineligible or subject to SBA guaranty denial/repair, and the lender faces program compliance findings. For refinancing, failure to transfer existing collateral to the EWCP loan violates SBA collateral requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: SBA (for non-delegated loans) or PLP-EWCP lender may require additional collateral by requiring a lien on other business assets; assignment of contract proceeds for an EWCP ABL line of credit may be required at the discretion of the LGPC (non-delegated) or PLP-EWCP lender (delegated).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: EWCP, d. Collateral (i) and refinancing statement
source_reference.source_text: EWCP loans must be secured by no less than a first security interest in all collateral associated with the transactions financed. This includes at least the export inventory and receivables, assignment of credit insurance, letters of credit proceeds, and contract proceeds as applicable. Collateral must be located in the United States, its territories, or possessions... When EWCP loan proceeds are used for debt refinancing, collateral for the loan being refinanced must be transferred to secure the EWCP loan.

examples: An EWCP line finances export inventory and foreign receivables; lender takes a first security interest in the inventory/receivables and assigns export credit insurance proceeds: meets SBA minimum collateral requirement.
An EWCP loan refinances an existing working capital facility secured by business A/R; lender must transfer the existing A/R collateral to secure the EWCP loan as part of the refinance.
Collateral consists of goods stored outside U.S. territories: the lender must restructure collateral to meet the location requirement (or the loan risks SBA program noncompliance).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; program compliance findings; regulatory criticism for unsafe/unsound collateral controls

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_002’s only permitted non-delegated routing scenario is same-institution debt refinance. Rule 22_007 contains special collateral handling when EWCP proceeds refinance debt (collateral transfer requirement). Thus, when 26_002’s refinance condition is met, the refinance component of 22_007 becomes applicable and must be satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If collateral securing the refinanced debt is not transferred/perfected into the EWCP structure, the EWCP loan can become noncompliant and subject to guaranty denial/repair.

dependencies.rationale: Perfecting a first security interest in EWCP transaction collateral is relevant only for SBA EWCP loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If core eligibility fails, EWCP collateral structuring cannot produce an SBA-eligible loan; proceeding risks guaranty denial/repair.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA EWCP standby letter of credit support requires minimum 25 percent cash collateral or equivalent borrowing base support for the full standby term
business_rules.description: If an SBA EWCP loan is used to support the issuance of a standby letter of credit, SBA requires additional collateral: the applicant must deposit cash into an account held by the lender in an amount equal to at least 25% of the standby letter of credit being issued, and the deposit must remain in the lender-held account for the term of the standby letter of credit. If using an asset-based loan (ABL) facility, SBA (non-delegated) or the PLP-EWCP lender may allow export inventory and/or foreign accounts receivable or other acceptable collateral to replace the cash deposit requirement, provided the lender determines that the borrowing base supports at least 25% of the total of all standby letters of credit supported by the loan. The lender must document the method used to secure the additional collateral in the file.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) EWCP loans when proceeds support issuance of a standby letter of credit, including ABL structures where collateral may replace cash deposit requirements.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to maintain the minimum 25% additional collateral (cash or approved collateral with borrowing base support) for the standby term may cause SBA program noncompliance, potential guaranty repair/denial, and heightened credit risk. Failure to document the method in the loan file is a documentation compliance deficiency.
business_rules.exceptions: ABL collateral may replace cash deposit only if permitted by SBA (non-delegated) or PLP-EWCP lender and if borrowing base supports at least 25% of total standby letters of credit supported.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4: EWCP, d. Collateral (ii) Standby Letters of Credit
source_reference.source_text: Standby Letters of Credit: SBA requires additional collateral if an EWCP loan is used to support the issuance of a standby letter of credit. ... Applicant must deposit cash ... equal to at least 25% of the standby letter of credit ... remain ... for the term... If using an asset-based loan (ABL) facility... may allow export inventory and/or foreign accounts receivable... to replace the cash deposit... Lender must determine that the borrowing base ... will support at least 25% of the total of all standby letters of credit... All Lenders must document the method...

examples: EWCP supports a $400,000 standby letter of credit: applicant must maintain at least $100,000 cash collateral on deposit for the standby term unless permitted ABL collateral replacement is documented.
EWCP supports two standby letters of credit totaling $1,000,000: borrowing base must support at least $250,000 (25%) of the total supported standby amount if using collateral instead of cash.

Lender accepts export receivables to replace cash deposit but fails to document the borrowing base calculation and method in the file: violates SBA documentation requirement even if collateral exists.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; program compliance findings; loan file exception requiring remediation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: The 25% cash deposit/approved collateral framework is an SBA EWCP-specific compliance rule.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SBA eligibility fails, holding the 25% deposit does not permit SBA guaranty eligibility; proceeding risks denial/repair.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Contract CAPLine must have assignment of contract proceeds before disbursement unless at least two enumerated exception conditions are met
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Contract CAPLines, prior to initial disbursement the contracting counterparty must be advised in writing by both the lender and borrower that an assignment of contract proceeds is required. The assignment must be in place before any disbursement for a particular contract is made, must provide for the lender’s right to receive all payments from the third party, and the lender must receive written acknowledgment from the third party. Exception: assignment of contract proceeds may be foregone if at least two of the following are met: (1) contract term being financed is 12 months or less; (2) successful track record exists between borrower and contracting authority on same or similar contracts; (3) financial analysis shows DSCR exceeds 1:1; (4) all contract proceeds are paid directly to the lender by contracting authority or funds control/escrow/third-party servicer is implemented (or, if a performance bond is in place, funds control is implemented); (5) other available and worthwhile collateral is pledged to secure the line by borrower or any owner/guarantor (excluding supplemental guarantors).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Contract CAPLines at or before initial disbursement for each financed contract, and when assessing eligibility to forego assignment of proceeds under the stated exception test.
business_rules.consequences: If assignment is required and not completed (including required third-party acknowledgment), the lender must not disburse for that contract until compliant. Noncompliance may result in SBA program findings and potential guaranty repair/denial for improper closing/disbursement. If assignment is foregone, lender must demonstrate and document that at least two exception conditions were met.
business_rules.exceptions: Assignment may be foregone only if at least two of the five enumerated conditions are satisfied.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines, Contract CAPLines (Assignment of Contract Proceeds and Exception)
source_reference.source_text: prior to initial disbursement on any Contract CAPLine, the entity the Borrower has entered into the contract with must be advised in writing by both the Lender and Borrower that an assignment of the contract proceeds is required... must be in place before any disbursement... Lender must receive written acknowledgement... Exception... may be foregone, if at least two of the following conditions are met: ... term... 12 months or less; ... successful track record...; ... Debt Service Coverage ratio that exceeds 1:1; ... proceeds... paid directly... or ... Funds Control...; ... other available and worthwhile collateral...

examples: A 24-month municipal services contract is financed with a Contract CAPLine: assignment of proceeds must be executed and acknowledged before first disbursement because the contract term exceeds 12 months and exception conditions are not met.

A 10-month contract is financed and borrower has a successful track record with the contracting authority: two exception conditions are met (term ≤ 12 months and successful track record), so assignment may be foregone if documented.
A 12-month contract lacks assignment; lender disburses based on DSCR > 1:1 only: noncompliant because at least two exception conditions are required to forego assignment.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; program compliance findings; disbursement/closing exception requiring remediation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 29_005 requires a first lien position on contracts and proceeds (via assignment and UCC). 22_009 details the notice, assignment, and third-party acknowledgment steps required before disbursement for each contract.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 29_005 is not structurally met, 22_009 cannot be completed properly; disbursements must be stopped to avoid SBA noncompliance and guaranty impairment.

dependencies.rationale: Assignment of contract proceeds and third-party acknowledgment are SBA CAPLines compliance controls.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SBA eligibility fails, a compliant assignment does not permit SBA-guaranteed disbursement; proceeding risks program violation/guaranty impairment.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA Seasonal CAPLine final disbursement timing must support 30-day clean-up period or maturity repayment
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Seasonal CAPLines, the final disbursement should be made in time for the funds to be used in the business and converted to cash that can be used to pay off the loan balance at the commencement of a 30-day clean-up period or at maturity. Principal repayments must occur as soon as cash from seasonal sales has been received by the borrower, and interest should be paid monthly.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) Seasonal CAPLines during disbursement and repayment administration and when structuring draw periods relative to seasonal build-up and clean-up requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If disbursement timing does not support repayment by the 30-day clean-up period or maturity, the lender risks SBA program noncompliance and heightened credit risk. Failure to enforce timely principal repayment from seasonal receipts and monthly interest payments increases delinquency risk and may produce program findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines, Seasonal CAPLines Disbursement and Repayment
source_reference.source_text: Seasonal CAPLines: Disbursement and Repayment: Disbursements from the loan are made continually during the seasonal build-up period... The final disbursement of any Seasonal loan should be made in time for the funds to be used in the business and converted to cash which can be used to pay off the loan balance at the commencement of a 30 day clean up period or maturity. Principal repayments... must occur as soon as the cash from the seasonal sales has been received... Interest should be paid monthly.

examples: A Seasonal CAPLine funds inventory build-up; lender schedules last advance early enough to allow sales and cash conversion before the 30-day clean-up period begins: meets SBA timing expectation.
Borrower receives seasonal receipts but does not make principal payments promptly; lender must require principal repayment as soon as cash is received under SBA guidance.
Loan structure allows advances right up to the clean-up period with no time for cash conversion: noncompliant with SBA expectation and increases repayment risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA program compliance finding; potential servicing/administration exception; increased supervisory criticism for weak repayment controls

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: The clean-up period timing and repayment controls are SBA CAPLines requirements applicable to SBA-eligible loans.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If SBA eligibility fails, adherence to clean-up timing does not enable SBA guaranty; proceeding risks program findings/guaranty issues.

business_rules.rule_name: FX revaluation pricing must use premium or discount for futures and spot rate for spot contracts and ledger accounts
business_rules.description: For foreign currency position valuation in maturity distribution and revaluation/income-loss analysis: (1) future/forward contracts must be priced at the given premium or discount rate, (2) spot contracts and currency ledger accounts must be priced at the spot rate, and (3) if one or more rates are used to price a position at a point in time, the spot rate column should state 'various'. Additionally, swap contracts must be removed before valuing positions (covered in a separate rule).
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever unrealized profit/loss is calculated for a foreign currency position using these examiner workpapers or equivalent internal methodology.
business_rules.consequences: If incorrect pricing inputs are used (e.g., pricing ledger accounts at forward rates), the computed market value and unrealized P&L will be misstated, potentially driving incorrect risk conclusions and examiner criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: If multiple rates are used at a point in time, 'various' is permitted for the spot rate column rather than a single rate.

source_reference.section_id: MATURITY DISTRIBUTION (GAP) ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: Price future contracts at the given premium or discount rate. Price spot contracts and ledger accounts at the spot rate. When one or more rates are used to price a position at a point in time, type various in the Spot Rate column.

examples: A forward EUR purchase is valued using the quoted forward points (premium/discount) while EUR ledger accounts are valued using the current spot rate.
For a position spanning multiple spot rates due to intraday pricing, the report uses 'various' in the spot rate column and retains supporting rate detail.
If the bank values a forward contract at spot instead of the forward premium/discount, the unrealized P&L is misstated and must be recalculated.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 23_004 specifies how to price instruments (spot vs forward, premiums/discounts). 23_005 specifies the required spot-rate source, quoting convention, and precision used in those pricing steps.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 23_005 is not satisfied, revaluation inputs may be rejected by audit/examiners and unrealized P&L can be misstated, requiring recalculation and control remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: Spot exchange rates for FX revaluation must be expressed as USD cost per unit with four-decimal precision
business_rules.description: For the revaluation and income/loss analysis of foreign currency positions, the spot exchange rate must be obtained from a reliable published source (e.g., Wall Street Journal or similar) and expressed as the U.S. dollar cost per unit of foreign currency. The exchange rate values must be carried to four decimal places or four-digit level of significance (e.g., 1 Euro sells for $x.xxxx; 1 Japanese yen equals $.004560).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when completing the Revaluation and Income/Loss Analysis worksheet to compute current market value and unrealized profit/loss for foreign currency positions.
business_rules.consequences: If spot rates are not expressed in the required convention or precision, calculated market values and profit/loss amounts may be inaccurate or not comparable across positions, leading to examiner criticism and rework.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: REVALUATION AND INCOME/LOSS ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: Obtain the spot exchange rate from the Wall Street Journal or similar publications containing foreign exchange rates. Express the exchange rates in terms of the U.S. dollar cost per unit of foreign currency (for example, one Euro sells for $ x.xxxx) with the values carried to four decimal places or four-digit level of significance (i.e., one Japanese yen equals $.004560). Multiply the net amount of foreign currency by the spot rate to arrive at the current market value

examples: EUR spot rate is recorded as $1.0725 per EUR (four decimals) and multiplied by the net EUR position to compute market value.
JPY rate is recorded as $0.006532 per JPY (four-digit significance) rather than JPY per USD to match the required convention.
If a report uses USD per 100 JPY without conversion, the market value calculation fails the rule and must be corrected.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: FX unrealized profit or loss must be computed using the four position rules for long and short split positions
business_rules.description: When determining spot-rate unrealized profit/loss for each foreign currency position (assuming full liquidation of the position): (1) Long foreign currency position combined with long U.S. dollar equivalent: profit equals market value minus book value; loss equals book value minus market value. (2) Long foreign currency position combined with short U.S. dollar equivalent: profit equals current market value plus the short U.S. dollar book value (profit-only outcome). (3) Short foreign currency position combined with short U.S. dollar equivalent: profit equals book value minus current market value; loss equals market value minus book value. (4) Short foreign currency position combined with long U.S. dollar equivalent: loss equals current market value plus the long U.S. dollar book value (loss-only outcome).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the revaluation schedule calculates unrealized spot-rate profit/loss for net positions, including split positions referenced in position analysis instructions.
business_rules.consequences: Incorrect rule selection (e.g., treating a split position as a simple long) misstates unrealized P&L and can misrepresent earnings sensitivity and risk exposure; examiners may require recalculation and may criticize controls.
business_rules.exceptions: Rules 2 and 4 apply to split positions; rule 2 can only result in a profit; rule 4 can only result in a loss.

source_reference.section_id: REVALUATION AND INCOME/LOSS ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: Apply the following rules when determining the spot rate profit or loss on each position: 1. Long foreign currency position combined with long U.S. dollar equivalent: profit is excess of market value over book value; loss is the excess of book over market. 2. Long foreign currency position combined with short U.S. dollar equivalent: profit is the current market value plus the short U.S. dollar book value. 3. Short foreign currency position combined with short U.S. dollar equivalent: profit is the excess of book value over current market value; loss is the excess of market value over book value. 4. Short foreign currency position combined with long U.S. dollar equivalent: loss is the current market value plus the long U.S. dollar book value.

examples: A long CHF position with long USD equivalent: book value $1.0MM, market value $1.05MM → profit $50k under Rule 1.
A long GBP position paired with short USD equivalent (split position) shows profit-only under Rule 2; the report does not record a loss for that structure.
A short EUR position with long USD equivalent is treated as loss-only under Rule 4; any computed 'profit' indicates the wrong rule was applied.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 23_006 compares market value vs book value under different position configurations; 23_004 governs how to compute market value for forwards, spots, and ledger accounts.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 23_004 is not satisfied, 23_006 will compute profit/loss off incorrect market values, leading to incorrect risk conclusions and examiner criticism.

dependencies.rationale: Even if the position logic in 23_006 is correctly selected, market values rely on spot rates that must be reliable and consistently expressed per 23_005.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Noncompliant spot-rate sourcing/precision drives rework and potential examiner findings due to inconsistent or inaccurate valuation results.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) change of ownership loan proceeds used for ownership transfer may not exceed the business valuation amount
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership financing, any amount(s) of loan proceeds used to facilitate a change of ownership may not exceed the business valuation. The valuation supports the lender’s analysis of purchase price support and avoids financing above independently supported value.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when SBA 7(a) loan proceeds are used to facilitate a change of ownership (asset purchase or stock purchase) and a business valuation is required/obtained.
business_rules.consequences: If calculated change-of-ownership proceeds exceed the concluded business valuation: restructure proceeds (reduce change-of-ownership component), require additional borrower/seller injection not funded by loan proceeds, or decline. Noncompliance risks SBA guaranty repair/denial and program findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Business Valuation Requirements – Change of Ownership, item v)
source_reference.source_text: Any amount(s) of the loan proceeds that will be used to facilitate a change of ownership may not exceed the business valuation.

examples: Business valuation concludes $900,000; loan includes $950,000 earmarked for purchase price; lender must reduce change-of-ownership proceeds by at least $50,000 or restructure.
Business valuation concludes $1,500,000; loan uses $1,200,000 for ownership transfer; compliant because proceeds do not exceed valuation.
Loan proceeds allocate $600,000 to change of ownership and $200,000 to working capital; valuation concludes $550,000; lender must reduce ownership-transfer proceeds to $550,000 or less.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty denial/repair; program compliance finding; repurchase or loss of guaranty coverage risk.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 24_006 depends on a concluded valuation figure. 21_010 defines what constitutes an acceptable valuation (prepared for lender, independent, proper signatures/qualifications).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without a compliant valuation under 21_010, the lender cannot demonstrate compliance with 24_006; SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and forced restructuring are likely.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) non-special purpose change of ownership valuation requires independent qualified source when financed amount net of appraised real estate and equipment exceeds $250,000
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions (business value excluding real estate separately appraised), valuation requirements depend on the financed amount net of appraised real estate and/or equipment. For non-special purpose properties: if (amount being financed, including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other financing) minus the appraised value of real estate and/or equipment being financed is (i) $250,000 or less, the lender may perform its own valuation unless internal policy requires an independent valuation; (ii) greater than $250,000, the lender must obtain an independent business valuation from a Qualified Source. Additionally, even if net financed amount is not stated as >$250,000, a close relationship between buyer and seller triggers the independent business valuation requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions involving a business operating from non-special purpose property where lender must determine if independent valuation is required.
business_rules.consequences: If net financed amount > $250,000 OR close relationship between buyer and seller: lender must obtain independent business valuation from a Qualified Source; absence is an underwriting noncompliance and may impact SBA guaranty eligibility. If net financed amount ≤ $250,000 and no close relationship: lender may perform internal valuation unless internal policy requires otherwise.
business_rules.exceptions: Lender’s internal policies may require an independent business valuation even when net financed amount is $250,000 or less.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Business Valuation Requirements – Change of Ownership, Non-Special Purpose Properties i)(a)-(b)
source_reference.source_text: If the amount being financed (including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other financing) minus the appraised value of real estate and/or equipment being financed is $250,000 or less, the Lender may perform its own valuation... If the amount being financed... minus the appraised value... is greater than $250,000 or if there is a close relationship between the buyer and seller... the Lender must obtain an independent business valuation from a Qualified Source.

examples: Total financing $900,000; appraised real estate/equipment $700,000; net $200,000 (≤$250,000), arms-length; lender may do internal valuation (unless internal policy requires independent).
Total financing $900,000; appraised real estate/equipment $600,000; net $300,000 (>$250,000); lender must obtain independent Qualified Source business valuation.
Net $150,000 but buyer and seller are family members (close relationship); lender must obtain independent Qualified Source business valuation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; underwriting exception finding; required remediation.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) International Trade loans must be declined when cash flow lacks reasonable assurance of timely repayment regardless of collateral
business_rules.description: For SBA-guaranteed International Trade (IT) loan requests, the lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner consistent with prudent lending standards and treat business cash flow as the primary source of repayment (not collateral liquidation). If the lender’s financial analysis shows the applicant lacks reasonable assurance of timely repayment from business cash flow, the lender must decline the loan request even if collateral or other repayment sources are available.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to any request for financial assistance to be guaranteed by SBA under the 7(a) International Trade program (SOP 50 10 8, Section B, Chapter 4).
business_rules.consequences: If reasonable assurance of repayment from business cash flow is not demonstrated, the loan request must be declined (no approval based solely on collateral). Failure to follow may result in SBA credit/underwriting deficiencies noted during SBA oversight and potential guaranty purchase issues.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Paragraph 4 (Credit Standards for International Trade)
source_reference.source_text: A Lender must analyze each application in a commercially reasonable manner, consistent with prudent lending standards. The cash flow of the Applicant is the primary source of repayment, not any expected recovery from the liquidation of collateral. Thus, if the Lender’s financial analysis demonstrates that the Applicant lacks reasonable assurance of repayment in a timely manner from the cash flow of the business, the loan request must be declined, regardless of the collateral available or outside sources of repayment.

examples: Applicant pledges a fully collateralized warehouse but historic and projected cash flow shows inability to service total debt; lender must decline despite strong collateral.
Applicant offers third-party support agreement as outside repayment source, but operating cash flow analysis shows no reasonable assurance of timely repayment; lender must decline.
Applicant proposes additional guarantor collateral to offset weak cash flow; lender must still decline if cash flow does not provide reasonable assurance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase denial or repair due to underwriting deficiency

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 27_001 prohibits approval based solely on collateral when cash flow is insufficient. 27_003 operationalizes sufficiency through DSCR ≥1.15 (historical/projected) and global DSCR ≥1.00.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 27_003 is not satisfied, the lender cannot credibly demonstrate 27_001 compliance; approvals may be cited as imprudent and program-noncompliant.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA International Trade underwriting must use three years of historical financials plus interim, or projections meeting 1.15 DSCR within two years
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans, underwriting must be based on: (1) for existing businesses, the three most recent years of historical financial information plus an interim financial statement; and (2) for start-ups, new businesses, changes of ownership, and other applications based on projections, detailed projections (with supporting assumptions) that reflect debt service coverage (DSCR) equal to or greater than 1.15 within 2 years from loan funding (or, for construction projects, within 2 years from the end of construction).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loan requests; existing businesses vs. start-ups/new businesses/changes of ownership/projection-based requests.
business_rules.consequences: If required historical financials or required projection package (including DSCR timing requirement) is missing or does not support DSCR ≥ 1.15 in the required timeframe, the underwriting record is incomplete and may not support approval consistent with SBA Loan Program Requirements; may lead to SBA oversight criticisms and guaranty purchase issues.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in excerpt (other than category differences between existing businesses and projection-based applicants).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Paragraph 4.b.i(b) (Financial analysis of repayment ability)
source_reference.source_text: For existing businesses based on the three most recent years of historical financial information (tax returns or balance sheet with debt schedule and income statement) plus an interim financial statement. (13 CFR § 120.191) For start-ups, new businesses, changes of ownership, and other applications based on projections, include detailed projections, including the supporting assumptions that reflect a debt service coverage equal to or greater than 1.15 within 2 years from loan funding or, for construction projects, within 2 years from the end of construction.

examples: Existing exporter applies for an IT term loan: lender must obtain 2022–2024 tax returns/financial statements plus a current interim statement before completing underwriting.
New business with no operating history applies for IT financing: lender must underwrite to projections that show DSCR ≥ 1.15 by month 24 after funding, supported by documented assumptions.
Construction project: projections must show DSCR ≥ 1.15 within 2 years after construction ends (not merely 2 years after initial funding).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase denial or repair due to incomplete underwriting support

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 27_002 requires projections (for start-ups/new businesses/change of ownership/projection-based requests) demonstrating DSCR timing; 21_009 affects sources/uses and capitalization, which materially impacts projected repayment capacity.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 21_009 is not met, the loan may be structurally noncompliant even if projections show adequate DSCR; SBA guaranty eligibility risk increases and underwriting assumptions may be invalid.

dependencies.rationale: 27_002 requires detailed projections for projection-based IT requests; 27_004 specifies the mandatory projection content and the justification for relying on projections instead of historical performance when coverage is weak.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 27_004 is missing when triggered, 27_002 underwriting is incomplete; SBA may cite file deficiencies and challenge approval supportability.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA International Trade DSCR must be at least 1.15 on historical or projected basis and at least 1.00 on global cash flow basis
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans, operating cash flow (OCF) is defined as EBITDA. Debt Service (DS) is defined as future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The applicant’s DSCR (OCF/DS) must be ≥ 1.15 on a historical and/or projected cash flow basis and must be 1:1 (≥ 1.00) on a global basis. To complete DS analysis, the lender should obtain a current debt schedule prepared by the applicant, including shareholder debt.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to all applicants for SBA 7(a) International Trade loans during underwriting and credit analysis.
business_rules.consequences: If DSCR thresholds are not met, underwriting does not meet SBA credit standards and approval is not supportable under prudent lending/SBA requirements; may result in SBA oversight criticisms and potential guaranty purchase repair/denial if file lacks required support.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Paragraph 4.b.i(b)(c) (OCF, DS, DSCR requirements)
source_reference.source_text: Calculation of operating cash flow (OCF) defined as earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA); Debt Service (DS) is defined as the future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The Applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (OCF/DS) must be equal to or greater than 1.15 on a historical and/or projected cash flow basis and 1:1 on a global basis.

examples: Applicant EBITDA is $230,000 and total future annual P&I on business debt after the SBA loan is $200,000; DSCR = 1.15, meets SBA threshold on historical basis.
Applicant meets 1.20 DSCR on projections but global cash flow after considering affiliate/guarantor obligations is 0.95; fails global 1.00 requirement.
Applicant omits shareholder note in debt schedule; lender must obtain a current debt schedule including shareholder debt to properly calculate DS and DSCR.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase repair/denial for failure to meet documented credit standards

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: Without 27_003’s definitions (EBITDA as OCF; DS includes all business debt including new SBA loan; global DSCR ≥1.00), 27_002’s DSCR requirement cannot be consistently measured or evidenced.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 27_003 is not applied, DSCR may be computed incorrectly (e.g., excluding other debt), producing unsupported approvals and SBA oversight criticism/repair risk.

business_rules.rule_name: When historical cash flow is insufficient, SBA International Trade underwriting must obtain two years of detailed projections with supporting assumptions
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade loans, if the historical cash flow from the most recent full year and the interim financial statements do not show sufficient debt service coverage, the lender must obtain from the applicant and analyze 2 years of detailed projections, including supporting assumptions that justify relying on projections instead of historical performance.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when an applicant’s most recent full-year historical cash flow and interim financials do not show sufficient debt service coverage after the SBA loan.
business_rules.consequences: File must include and lender must analyze 2-year detailed projections with assumptions; without them, underwriting is incomplete and may not support approval consistent with SBA requirements, increasing risk of SBA oversight finding and guaranty purchase repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 4, Paragraph 4.b.i(b)(iii)(a) (Insufficient historical cash flow)
source_reference.source_text: Historical cash flow for existing businesses that demonstrates total debt service coverage after the SBA loan; if the historical cash flow from the most recent full year and the interim financial statements do not show sufficient debt service coverage, Lender must obtain from the Applicant and analyze 2 years of detailed projections including the supporting assumptions justifying relying on projections instead of historical performance.

examples: Most recent year DSCR is 1.05 and interim shows declining revenue; lender must require and analyze 24 months of detailed projections with documented assumptions.
Historic DSCR is 0.95 due to one-time disruption; lender may not rely on verbal explanations alone and must obtain 2-year projections with assumptions supporting recovery.
Applicant provides projections without assumptions (no sales pipeline, no pricing basis); lender must obtain supporting assumptions to justify reliance.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase repair/denial for inadequate documentation supporting repayment

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 27_003 establishes how to compute coverage; 27_004 requires a projection package when that computed coverage is insufficient after the SBA loan.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 27_003 is not performed correctly, the lender may fail to trigger 27_004 when required, leaving underwriting incomplete and exposing the file to SBA repair/denial risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Change of ownership business valuation must be requested by and prepared for the lender and cannot be a seller or applicant prepared valuation
business_rules.description: For SBA Standard 7(a) loans greater than $350,000 involving a change of ownership, an accurate business valuation (excluding real estate, which is valued through a real estate appraisal) is required. The business valuation must be requested by and prepared for the lender, with scope identifying whether the transaction is an asset purchase or stock purchase and what is included (including assumed debt). The valuation must include the conclusion of value, the qualifications of the individual performing the valuation, and the individual’s signature certifying the valuation. The lender may not use a business valuation prepared for the applicant or the seller.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) loans > $350,000 with change of ownership where a business valuation is required under SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1.
business_rules.consequences: If valuation is not lender-requested/prepared-for-lender or is applicant/seller-prepared, it does not satisfy SBA requirements and creates documentation deficiency risk, potentially leading to SBA lender oversight findings and guaranty purchase repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. Cost may be passed on to the applicant.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1, Business Valuation Requirements – Change of Ownership (b)
source_reference.source_text: the business valuation must be requested by and prepared for the Lender. The scope of work should identify whether the transaction is an asset purchase or stock purchase and be specific enough for the individual performing the business valuation to know what is included in the sale (including any assumed debt). The business valuation must include the individual’s conclusion of value, the qualifications of the individual performing the business valuation and their signature certifying to the information contained in the business valuation. The Lender may not use a business valuation prepared for the Applicant or the seller.

examples: Borrower submits a valuation report the seller previously ordered; lender must obtain a valuation prepared for the lender (cannot rely on seller-prepared report).
Valuation provided lacks the valuator’s signature and qualifications section; lender must obtain a compliant valuation including these elements before closing.
Valuation scope fails to state whether the deal is asset or stock purchase; lender must require scope clarification consistent with SOP.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase repair/denial for noncompliant valuation documentation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 27_005 requires an accurate valuation for certain Standard 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions; 27_006 determines whether the lender may self-value or must obtain an independent Qualified Source valuation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to follow 27_006 can cause noncompliant valuation sourcing under 27_005, leading to SBA oversight findings and guaranty purchase repair/denial risk.

dependencies.rationale: 27_005 establishes the need for an accurate valuation; 27_007 specifies that, when triggered, the valuation must be performed by a Certified General Real Property Appraiser meeting independence and experience requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 27_007 is not satisfied, the valuation obtained may be disqualified even if a report exists, creating a hard SBA compliance failure.

business_rules.rule_name: Independent business valuation required when goodwill amount exceeds 250,000 dollars or buyer and seller are closely related for non-special purpose properties
business_rules.description: For SBA change of ownership transactions involving non-special purpose properties, calculate: (total amount being financed, including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other financing) minus (appraised value of real estate and/or equipment being financed). If this amount is $250,000 or less, the lender may perform its own valuation unless internal policy requires an independent valuation from a Qualified Source. If the amount is greater than $250,000, or if there is a close relationship between buyer and seller (e.g., existing owners or family members), the lender must obtain an independent business valuation from a Qualified Source.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) change of ownership transactions for non-special purpose properties.
business_rules.consequences: If independent valuation is required but not obtained, the file is noncompliant with SBA valuation requirements and may lead to SBA oversight findings and guaranty purchase repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: If ≤ $250,000 and no close relationship, lender may self-perform valuation unless internal policy requires Qualified Source valuation.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1, Business Valuation Requirements – Change of Ownership (i)(a)-(b) Non-Special Purpose Properties
source_reference.source_text: If the amount being financed (including any 7(a), 504, seller, or other financing) minus the appraised value of real estate and/or equipment being financed is $250,000 or less, the Lender may perform its own valuation of the business being sold... If the amount being financed... minus the appraised value... is greater than $250,000 or if there is a close relationship between the buyer and seller... the Lender must obtain an independent business valuation from a Qualified Source.

examples: Total financed is $1,200,000 and appraised real estate/equipment is $1,000,000; difference is $200,000 (≤ $250,000) so lender may self-value if internal policy allows.
Total financed is $900,000 and appraised real estate/equipment is $600,000; difference is $300,000 (> $250,000) so lender must obtain a Qualified Source valuation.
Difference is $150,000 but buyer is the seller’s family member; lender must obtain an independent valuation from a Qualified Source.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase repair/denial for failure to obtain required independent valuation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: Special purpose property change of ownership valuations must use an independent certified general appraiser with specific experience and USPAP instructions
business_rules.description: For SBA change of ownership transactions where the business operates from a Special Purpose Property, if (total financed minus appraised value of real estate/equipment) is over $250,000 or if there is a close relationship between buyer and seller, the lender must obtain an independent business valuation performed by a Certified General Real Property Appraiser. The appraiser must be independent of loan production, not involved in approval, and must not have the appearance of a conflict of interest. The valuation must allocate separate values to land, building, equipment, and intangible assets. The appraiser must have completed at least four going concern appraisals of equivalent special use property within the last 36 months, and each assignment must include specific instruction to comply with current USPAP guidelines.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) change of ownership transactions involving Special Purpose Properties meeting the $250,000/close-relationship triggers.
business_rules.consequences: If valuation is not performed by qualified independent Certified General appraiser meeting experience/USPAP/allocation requirements, file is noncompliant and may result in SBA oversight criticism and guaranty purchase repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: If ≤ $250,000 and no close relationship, lender may perform own valuation unless internal policy requires independent valuation (as stated in SOP).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1, Business Valuation Requirements – Change of Ownership (ii) Special Purpose Properties
source_reference.source_text: If the amount being financed... is over $250,000 or if there is a close relationship between the buyer and seller... and the business operates from a Special Purpose Property, the Lender must obtain an independent business valuation performed by a Certified General Real Property Appraiser... The appraiser must be independent of the loan production function... and must not have the appearance of a conflict of interest... allocate separate values... land, building, equipment, and intangible assets... completed no less than four going concern appraisals... within the last 36 months... conduct the appraisal in compliance with current USPAP guidelines.

examples: Financed-minus-appraised equals $400,000 and property is a single-purpose hotel; lender must obtain a Certified General appraiser valuation meeting USPAP and experience requirements.

Buyer is related to seller and the business operates from a specialty manufacturing facility; lender must require independent Certified General appraiser valuation even if financed-minus-appraised is $200,000.
Certified General appraiser has only two going-concern appraisals of comparable special-use properties in the last 36 months; lender must select an appraiser meeting the minimum of four.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; potential SBA guaranty purchase repair/denial for noncompliant special purpose valuation

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA change of ownership proceeds cannot exceed the concluded business valuation amount
business_rules.description: For SBA change of ownership financing, any loan proceeds used to facilitate the change of ownership may not exceed the business valuation amount. This functions as a cap on change-of-ownership financing relative to the concluded valuation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Standard 7(a) change of ownership transactions subject to business valuation requirements.
business_rules.consequences: If proceeds used for change of ownership exceed business valuation, the transaction violates SBA requirements and presents eligibility/credit policy noncompliance risk, potentially leading to SBA oversight findings and guaranty purchase repair/denial.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 1, Business Valuation Requirements – Change of Ownership (v)
source_reference.source_text: for a change of ownership . An accurate business valuation is required because the change in ownership will result in new debt unrelated to business operations and potentially the

examples: Business valuation concludes $1,000,000; the portion of the loan used to buy ownership interests cannot exceed $1,000,000.
Valuation is $750,000 but purchase price financing request is $900,000 (ownership transfer portion); lender must reduce eligible proceeds to ≤ $750,000 or restructure with non-loan funds.
Valuation is updated downward after issuance of SBA loan number (PLP scenario); lender must ensure change-of-ownership proceeds remain ≤ revised valuation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty eligibility/credit standard violation; potential SBA repair/denial upon guaranty purchase review

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 27_005 creates the acceptable valuation artifact for Standard 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions; 27_008 uses that valuation amount as a binding ceiling for proceeds used to facilitate ownership change.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 27_005 is missing/defective, 27_008 compliance cannot be demonstrated; file is vulnerable to SBA repair/denial and required restructuring.

dependencies.rationale: 24_006 is framed as underwriting for 7(a) change-of-ownership; 27_008 is framed as credit policy for Standard 7(a). Together they reinforce a consistent cap control and documentation expectation.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If only one is applied, the institution may pass one internal checkpoint while failing SBA program expectations for another processing track, increasing audit/SBA oversight issues.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines asset-based lines require borrowing base eligibility determination at least monthly and a borrowing base certificate at least monthly
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines asset-based sub-programs where the lender uses a borrowing base certificate (BBC) to determine disbursement availability, the lender should determine the amount of eligible assets for the borrowing base no less than monthly. A BBC is required at least monthly to determine the amount that can be disbursed; the lender may require a BBC more frequently consistent with its policy for similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial lines of credit. The borrowing base amount available for disbursement is calculated as the lesser of (A/R borrowing base + inventory borrowing base) or the face amount of the note, minus the loan balance on books.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines asset-based sub-program revolving lines when using a BBC to determine availability of funds for disbursement; applies after initial disbursement and during ongoing advances.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: advances are controlled to eligible collateral availability. If violated: advances may exceed permitted availability, increasing credit risk and exposing lender to SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and examination criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: Frequency may be more than monthly (but not less) based on lender’s policies for similarly-sized non-SBA lines; this text uses 'should' but also states 'A BBC is required at least monthly.'

source_reference.section_id: CAPLines-Specific Loan Closing and Disbursement Requirements (BBC requirements)
source_reference.source_text: No less than monthly, Lender should determine the amount of eligible assets for the borrowing base... A BBC is required at least monthly to determine the amount that can be disbursed. Lender may require a BBC more frequently consistent with its policy and procedures on similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial lines of credit... Borrowing base (Lesser of Total A/R & Inv BB or Note)... Subtract loan balance on books... Equals Amount available for disbursement

examples: Borrower submits a BBC on the last business day of each month; lender recalculates eligible A/R and inventory and limits new advances to the reported availability.
If the lender skips BBC monitoring for two months and continues to advance, it risks exceeding collateral availability and facing SBA guaranty repair if default occurs.
Lender policy requires weekly BBCs for higher-risk borrowers; this is permitted because it is more frequent than monthly.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial for noncompliance with CAPLines servicing/monitoring requirements; regulatory criticism

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: 28_010 defines eligibility screens (aging >90 days, customer delinquency concentration) that must be applied before computing eligible assets used in the BBC required by 28_009.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 28_010 is not satisfied, 28_009 BBCs can authorize over-advances, creating elevated loss severity and SBA guaranty repair/denial exposure.

dependencies.rationale: The CAPLines control environment requires collateral perfection first, then ongoing monitoring of availability through periodic BBCs.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 29_006 is not satisfied, advances should not occur, and the lender will not reach the compliant operating state where 28_009 controls disbursements.

dependencies.rationale: 29_002 establishes the collateral/lien foundation for a BBC-driven structure; 28_009 governs the monitoring cadence and availability determination.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If either is missing, the lender may either advance without enforceable collateral (no first lien) or without availability discipline (no monthly BBC), increasing SBA guaranty and safety-and-soundness risk.

dependencies.rationale: 28_009 establishes a general CAPLines asset-based cadence/requirement for monthly BBCs. 34_010 imposes similar monthly BBC requirements with EWCP-specific advance rates and overadvance remediation steps.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not aligned, a lender may meet CAPLines BBC cadence but fail EWCP-specific advance/overadvance rules, or vice versa, leading to inconsistent controls and compliance findings.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines accounts receivable eligibility exclusions and maximum advance rate of 80% with limited 90% exceptions
business_rules.description: When advancing against receivables in SBA CAPLines asset-based lines, the lender should eliminate ineligible receivables from the borrowing base, including: invoices more than 90 days past due (exceptions over 90 days permitted only with SBA prior written concurrence); if a customer is delinquent on more than 50% of its total outstanding invoices, all accounts due from that customer are ineligible until all delinquent accounts are paid in full (exceptions permitted only with SBA prior written concurrence); all re-billed accounts (except same-day clerical correction); foreign receivables unless backed by documentary/standby letters of credit, factor’s guarantee, credit insurance, or government enhancements; contra accounts; accounts due from affiliates; and accounts requiring subordination (e.g., certain governmental contracts). The maximum advance rate for eligible receivables cannot exceed 80%, but may be up to 90% if the receivable is a prime Federal contract with an assignment under the Assignment of Claims Act of 1940 (31 U.S.C. 3727) (including specified subcontractor/controlled disbursement scenarios) or if foreign A/R is insured by the Export-Import Bank or a major private insurer. Additional exceptions may be permitted only with SBA prior written concurrence.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines asset-based sub-programs when calculating the accounts receivable borrowing base for disbursement availability; applies throughout the life of the line when receivable-based advances are made.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: borrowing base includes only eligible collateral and advances are capped at allowable advance rates. If violated: over-advances and inclusion of ineligible receivables increase loss severity and create SBA compliance exposure, including guaranty repair/denial and examiner criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: Invoices >90 days past due may be included only with SBA prior written concurrence; delinquent-customer exclusion may be waived only with SBA prior written concurrence; advance rate may exceed 80% up to 90% only for the enumerated federal contract assignment/controlled disbursement or insured foreign A/R scenarios; further exceptions require SBA prior written concurrence.

source_reference.section_id: CAPLines—Ineligible receivables and advance rate for A/R
source_reference.source_text: Any invoice more than 90 days past due. Exceptions are permitted over the 90 day with SBA’s prior written concurrence... If a customer is delinquent on more than 50% of its total outstanding invoices, ALL of the accounts due from that customer are ineligible... Foreign receivables not backed by... letters of credit... credit insurance... or Government enhancements... Contra accounts... Accounts due from affiliate companies... Advance Rate for Accounts Receivable: The maximum advance rate cannot exceed 80%... may go up to 90%... if the receivable is a prime Federal contract... assignment... 31 U.S.C. 3727... or the foreign accounts receivable are insured by the Export-Import Bank or a major private insurer.

examples: Borrower has $500,000 eligible A/R after removing invoices >90 days and affiliate receivables; with an 80% cap, the A/R borrowing base cannot exceed $400,000 absent a qualifying 90% exception.

One customer has 60% of its invoices delinquent; lender must exclude all receivables from that customer until the delinquent invoices are paid in full (unless SBA gives prior written concurrence for an exception).
Borrower has prime federal contract receivable with Assignment of Claims Act assignment in place; lender may advance up to 90% of that eligible receivable (subject to other eligibility criteria).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial for ineligible collateral advances or exceeding maximum advance rates; supervisory criticism

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines loans of 50,000 dollars or less do not require collateral to be taken
business_rules.description: For SBA CAPLines with loan amount of $50,000 or less, the lender is not required to take collateral. This does not remove guaranty requirements; guaranties must still be obtained per SBA requirements referenced in the SOP.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA CAPLines when the gross loan amount is $50,000 or less.
business_rules.consequences: If loan amount is $50,000 or less, collateral is optional (not required) under SBA CAPLines minimum requirements; lender may still take collateral based on prudent practices. If lender incorrectly requires collateral as a blanket rule, it may create unnecessary friction but is not an SBA compliance violation; if lender fails to obtain required guaranties, SBA guaranty eligibility may be impacted.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated for the collateral waiver threshold; guaranty requirements still apply.

source_reference.section_id: General Collateral Requirements
source_reference.source_text: For loans of $50,000 or less, the Lender is not required to take collateral. (Guaranties still must be obtained in accordance with Section A, Ch. 5, Para. A.)

examples: A $35,000 Working Capital CAPLine is approved: the lender may close without taking collateral, but must still obtain required guaranties.
A $50,000 CAPLine renewal: collateral is not required solely due to SBA rules; lender may still take a lien if it chooses under prudent practices.
A $60,000 CAPLine: this rule does not apply; lender must follow the collateral identification and minimum requirements in the SOP.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk if related mandatory requirements (e.g., guaranties) are not met.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

dependencies.rationale: A BBC-based structure inherently relies on collateralized availability; SBA’s requirement for a first lien on A/R and inventory (29_002) should supersede the optional-collateral allowance in 29_001 when both conditions coexist.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If a lender incorrectly applies 29_001 to avoid collateral on a BBC-based line, the structure may violate SBA minimum collateral requirements and increase guaranty repair/denial exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: Working Capital CAPLines using a borrowing base certificate must take first lien on accounts receivable and inventory
business_rules.description: When a Working Capital CAPLine is disbursed based on a Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC), the lender must obtain and perfect a first-lien security interest in the applicant’s working/trading assets—specifically accounts receivable and inventory.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Working Capital CAPLines where disbursement is based on a BBC.
business_rules.consequences: If the line is structured to advance on a BBC, failure to obtain a first lien on A/R and inventory violates SBA CAPLines minimum collateral requirements and may jeopardize SBA guaranty enforceability/eligibility.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Working Capital CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: this section. When loan proceeds from a Working Capital CAPLine loan will be used to refinance existing debt, the loan should be secured with at least the same collateral and

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Collateral — Working Capital CAPLines (i)
source_reference.source_text: Working Capital CAPLines i. If the Lender will disburse the line based on a BBC, the Lender must obtain a first lien on the Applicant’s working/trading assets (i.e., accounts receivable, inventory).

examples: If availability is calculated from a BBC and advances are made against A/R, the lender must have a perfected first lien in A/R and inventory before advancing.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial for failure to follow mandatory collateral requirements.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules require that when a Working Capital CAPLine is disbursed based on a Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC), the lender must take a first lien on A/R and inventory. No differences in scope, triggers, or consequences.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_29_002 selected: clean statement of the CAPLine BBC condition and the required first-lien collateral set.

business_rules.rule_name: Working Capital CAPLines without a borrowing base certificate must maintain a 1 to 1 collateral coverage including personal real estate equity rules
business_rules.description: For Working Capital CAPLines not disbursed using a BBC, the lender must assume full usage of the revolving line and secure the line with sufficient eligible collateral to ensure a 1:1 collateral ratio. The lender must obtain a first lien on working/trading assets (accounts receivable and inventory). If those assets are insufficient to achieve 1:1 coverage, the lender must take additional collateral (including available personal real estate equity of required owners/guarantors, subject to SBA limitations) to meet the 1:1 requirement.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Working Capital CAPLines when the lender will not use a BBC to disburse the line.
business_rules.consequences: If the loan is structured without BBC advances, lender must document and perfect collateral sufficient for 1:1 coverage, including required personal real estate equity liens when business assets are insufficient (subject to the 25% equity threshold). Failure can result in SBA guaranty repair/denial and examiner criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: SBA does not require real estate collateral when equity in the real estate is less than 25% of the property’s fair market value. Personal real estate liens may be limited to the amount of the collateral shortfall and may be limited to 150% of equity.

source_reference.section_id: Working Capital CAPLines (If the Lender will not use a BBC to disburse the line)
source_reference.source_text:
If the Lender will not use a BBC to disburse the line:

The Lender must assume full use of the revolving line of credit and secure the line with sufficient collateral to ensure there is a 1:1 collateral ratio... If business assets do not fully secure the loan, the Lender must take available equity in personal real estate... of any owners of 20% or more... Liens... may be limited to the amount of the collateral shortfall... may be limited to 150% of the equity... SBA does not require... when the equity... is less than 25% of the property’s fair market value.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Collateral — Working Capital CAPLines (ii)(a)-(b)
source_reference.source_text: If the Lender will not use a BBC to disburse the line: a) The Lender must assume full use of the revolving line of credit and secure the line with sufficient collateral to ensure there is a 1:1 collateral ratio. Lender must obtain a first lien position on the working/trading assets (accounts receivable and inventory) financed with the line... b) If business assets do not fully secure the loan, the Lender must take available equity in personal real estate... of any owners of 20% or more... except Supplemental Guarantors... Liens... may be limited to... the collateral shortfall... [and] to 150% of the equity in the collateral.

examples: If a $500,000 Working Capital CAPLine is not BBC-controlled, total eligible collateral must be at least $500,000 and include a first lien on A/R and inventory.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial and safety-and-soundness criticism for collateral shortfall versus SOP minimums.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules apply to Working Capital CAPLines not disbursed using a BBC and impose the same 1:1 collateral ratio requirement with a first lien on A/R and inventory and additional collateral if needed. No material variance.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_29_003 selected: more complete statement of the 1:1 collateral ratio logic and the requirement to add collateral when trading assets are insufficient.

business_rules.rule_name: Working Capital CAPLines used to refinance debt should take at least the same collateral and lien priority as refinanced debt
business_rules.description: When Working Capital CAPLine proceeds refinance existing debt, the SBA loan should be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt being refinanced. If the refinanced debt is over-collateralized under SBA collateral requirements and the SBA loan will remain fully secured, the lender is not required to take excess collateral and may release excess collateral. Substitute collateral is permitted only if it is comparable in value and useful life and acceptable to SBA (or acceptable to the lender under PLP authority where applicable).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Working Capital CAPLines when loan proceeds refinance existing debt.
business_rules.consequences: Collateral packages for refinancing must, at a minimum, preserve existing collateral and lien priority; failure may create SBA guaranty enforcement/eligibility risk. Unnecessary excess collateral is not required when the SBA loan is fully secured.
business_rules.exceptions: Excess collateral not required if refinanced debt is over-collateralized and SBA loan remains fully secured; substitute collateral allowed if comparable value/useful life and acceptable (SBA or PLP lender).

source_reference.section_id: General Collateral Requirements (Refinance)
source_reference.source_text: When loan proceeds from a Working Capital CAPLine loan will be used to refinance existing debt, the loan should be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt that is being refinanced... the Lender is not required to take excess collateral. Substitute collateral may be offered providing it is of comparable value and useful life...

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 3: 7(a) CAPLines — Collateral — General Collateral Requirements (iii)
source_reference.source_text: When loan proceeds from a Working Capital CAPLine loan will be used to refinance existing debt, the loan should be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt that is being refinanced... When the debt being refinanced is considered as being over collateralized... and the SBA loan will remain fully secured, the Lender is not required to take excess collateral. Substitute collateral may be offered providing it is of comparable value and useful life

examples: If the refinanced revolver had a first lien on A/R and inventory, the CAPLine refinance should maintain at least that lien position and collateral set.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial and credit administration criticism for improper lien priority/collateral substitution.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

deduplication_info.rationale: Both rules impose the same CAPLine refinance collateral principle: the SBA line should be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt, with limited release and substitution flexibility when fully secured remains true.
deduplication_info.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_29_004 selected: clearer articulation of permitted excess-collateral release and substitute collateral allowance in the CAPLine refinance scenario.

business_rules.rule_name: Contract CAPLines must perfect first lien on contract and contract proceeds through assignment and UCC filing
business_rules.description: For Contract CAPLines, applicants must be able to provide the lender with a first lien position on the contract(s) and the proceeds of the contract(s) financed with the line. This is accomplished by assignment to the participating lender and proper UCC filing. The SOP references that exceptions may apply in limited circumstances (cross-referenced elsewhere).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA Contract CAPLines.
business_rules.consequences: If the lender cannot obtain and perfect the required first lien on contract(s) and proceeds, the structure does not meet SBA CAPLines minimum collateral controls and may jeopardize guaranty enforceability/eligibility.
business_rules.exceptions: Exceptions to assignment requirements may exist as referenced in another paragraph in the chapter (not included in provided excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: Contract CAPLines
source_reference.source_text: CAPLines, below for further guidance. Contract CAPLines Applicants must be able to provide the Lender with a first lien position on the contract(s) and the proceeds of the contract(s) financed with the line, by assignment to the participating Lender and proper UCC filing. See Paragraph D.3.b.i.b), Exception

examples: A Contract CAPLine financing a government services contract: lender must obtain assignment of contract proceeds and file a UCC to perfect the first lien.
A Contract CAPLine for a construction contract: lender perfects first lien on contract rights and proceeds prior to disbursement.
Applicant refuses assignment of proceeds: unless a documented SOP exception applies, the loan is not eligible under the minimum requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial for failure to perfect required lien on primary collateral source.

examples: Standard requiring max 70% LTV and minimum 1.25x DSCR for stabilized multifamily
Construction standard limiting interest-only periods and requiring stronger guarantees

business_rules.rule_name: SBA CAPLines liens must be perfected and lien position verified before initial disbursement and before each new season contract or project
business_rules.description: All CAPLines liens must be perfected and the lien position verified prior to the initial disbursement of the line. For seasonal, contract, or builder lines that revolve for more than one season, contract, or construction/renovation project, liens must be perfected prior to the initial disbursement for each season, contract, or project.
