Appraisal engagement letter identifies only the borrower as intended user: lender must obtain corrected appraisal identifying lender as client/intended user (subject to FIRREA allowance for federally regulated lenders).
Lender orders an Appraisal Report compliant with USPAP and charges the borrower the appraisal fee at closing: permitted.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial risk and documentation exception findings

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

dependencies.rationale: 20_004 requires lender as client/intended user except where FIRREA permits otherwise; 29_010 governs appraisal vs evaluation permissibility and related regulator constraints that can alter documentation expectations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If regulator allowances are misunderstood, the lender may either (a) improperly rely on an evaluation where an appraisal is required, or (b) misdocument appraisal client/intended user, triggering SBA exceptions.

dependencies.rationale: 18_003 applies to SBA Standard 7(a) CRE appraisals; 20_004 applies to SBA Express and 7(a) Small CRE appraisals. A lender’s appraisal controls should treat these as a unified compliance requirement set (client/intended user, no seller/applicant reports, USPAP Appraisal Report).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If controls are implemented only for one product line, the lender may remain exposed to the same deficiency under another SBA product, creating audit/exam exceptions.

dependencies.rationale: 21_010 requires a business valuation excluding real estate, which must be valued via a real estate appraisal. When that appraisal is obtained for 7(a) Small/Express CRE, it must meet 20_004 (lender as client/intended user; not applicant/seller-prepared; USPAP Appraisal Report).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 20_004 is not satisfied in an applicable change-of-ownership transaction, the lender cannot document the real estate value component correctly, risking pre-closing remediation and potential SBA guaranty issues.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA agent packaging and referral fees must be documented over 2,500 dollars and percentage-based fees are capped at 3 percent, 2 percent, and 0.25 percent with 30,000 dollar aggregate maximum
business_rules.description: Agent fees charged to an applicant for packaging and other services must be reasonable and customary for services actually performed. Fees may be hourly (no maximum, but hourly rate and time spent on each service must be documented) or percentage-based, subject to caps: 3% on loans of $50,000 or less; 2% for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25% on the portion over $1,000,000; and the maximum fee in the aggregate on a percentage basis is $30,000. In either hourly or percentage approach, all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documenting the service performed. SBA prohibits agents from charging a standard/flat fee to all applicants, contingency fees, or fees for unnecessary services; SBA may require refunds of unreasonable or impermissible fees.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504-related agent fees (and generally SBA ethics/fees guidance) when agents charge applicants for packaging or other services in connection with obtaining SBA financing.
business_rules.consequences: If fees exceed caps or are impermissible, SBA may deem them unreasonable or impermissible and require refund; CDCs must review fees when there is a complaint or indication fees might be excessive; excessive fee findings may require agent to reduce/refund and refrain from collecting excess.
business_rules.exceptions: Hourly fees have no maximum, but must be reasonable/customary and documented; percentage fee caps apply to combined services provided to the applicant (combined fee cannot exceed stated maximums).

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section A, Ch 4: Ethics, Fees, and Agents – Agent fees
source_reference.source_text: The fees must be reasonable and customary for the services actually performed... all fees over $2,500 must be supported by documenting the service performed... For fees charged... based on a percentage of the loan amount, the fee may not exceed... 3 percent on loans of $50,000 or less; 2 percent for loans between $50,000 and the first $1,000,000 and 0.25 percent on the portion over $1,000,000... The maximum fee... is $30,000... SBA does not allow an Agent to charge... a standard or flat fee... contingency fees... or for services that are not reasonably necessary...

examples: Agent charges 3% on a $40,000 loan ($1,200): permissible if reasonable and services performed; if total agent fees exceed $2,500, the services must be documented.

Agent charges 2% on $800,000 ($16,000) plus 0.25% on $500,000 ($1,250) for a $1.3MM loan, total $17,250: within percentage caps and below $30,000 aggregate maximum; documentation required because > $2,500.
Agent charges a non-refundable $5,000 flat fee to all applicants regardless of work performed: impermissible; SBA may require refund.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA refund requirement for unreasonable/impermissible fees; CDC escalation to OCRM; processing delays and program compliance findings

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

dependencies.rationale: Both rules describe caps/reasonableness and documentation expectations; mapping them as validation reduces duplicate/conflicting interpretations in controls and audits.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If alignment between the two is not maintained, fee monitoring can produce inconsistent results (false positives/negatives) and complicate remediation/refund decisions.

business_rules.rule_name: OCC appraisal and evaluation thresholds allow evaluations up to 500,000 dollars and require state certified appraisers at 1,000,000 dollars and nonresidential 500,000 dollars
business_rules.description: Under 12 CFR 34 subpart C as summarized in the Comptroller’s Handbook, OCC-supervised banks may use evaluations in lieu of appraisals for certain transactions, including when the loan amount is $500,000 or less; when the transaction is a business loan of $1 million or less and income from sale or rental of real estate is not the primary source of repayment; and certain existing extensions of credit (no new money beyond reasonable closing costs, or new money advanced when no obvious/material market or property change threatens collateral adequacy). State-certified appraisers are required for all loans/transactions of $1 million or more and for nonresidential loans/transactions of $500,000 or more (including one-to-four family construction loans), and for complex residential loans/transactions of $500,000 or more.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to OCC-regulated banks’ real estate-related financial transactions subject to 12 CFR 34 subpart C, including CRE-secured and other real-estate related business loans.
business_rules.consequences: If a transaction meets a threshold requiring an appraisal and state-certified appraiser, the bank must obtain one; using an evaluation instead (or using an appraiser without required credential) is noncompliant and may result in supervisory criticism and remediation requirements. Where evaluations are permitted, bank must still follow safe and sound valuation practices.
business_rules.exceptions: Regulation exempts certain transactions (not fully enumerated in the excerpt), including examples such as federal government/federal agency guaranteed loans (referenced generally). OCC may require an appraisal/evaluation for safety and soundness concerns even if an exemption might otherwise apply.

source_reference.section_id: Appraisals and Evaluations
source_reference.source_text:
the appraisal regulations permit the use of evaluations in lieu of appraisals for transactions

• in which the loan amount is $500,000 or less,
• in which the transaction is a business loan of $1 million or less and income from the sale or rental of real estate is not the primary source of repayment,
• representing an existing extension of credit when there is no new money advanced...

The services of a state-certified appraiser are required for

• all loans or other transactions of $1 million or more,
• nonresidential loans... of $500,000 or more...

examples: A $450,000 noncomplex CRE-secured renewal with no material market change: an evaluation may be used instead of an appraisal if the transaction meets the regulatory conditions and bank policy supports it.
A $1,500,000 nonresidential real estate transaction: bank must obtain an appraisal performed by a state-certified appraiser.

A $800,000 business loan where repayment is primarily from operating cash flow (not real estate rents/sale): evaluation may be permitted if other conditions are met; if repayment is primarily from rents, appraisal requirements may apply.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; Matters Requiring Attention for appraisal program deficiencies.

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

dependencies.rationale: 29_010 describes when evaluations may substitute for appraisals under OCC/FIRREA; 31_006 can still require an appraisal for SBA 504 even when an evaluation might otherwise be permissible by bank regulator rules.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the bank relies on an evaluation where SBA 504 requires an appraisal due to a trigger, the SBA/CDC package can be rejected/delayed and create program noncompliance findings.

dependencies.rationale: For SBA 504 projects >$500k, both SBA and FIRREA/OCC generally expect an appraisal (though scope/details differ). Mapping them as complementary helps unify valuation controls.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If not harmonized, the institution may implement inconsistent valuation thresholds across SBA and conventional portfolios, increasing audit exceptions and rework.

dependencies.rationale: 29_010 allows evaluations for certain transactions (including ≤$500k) under OCC rules. 15_009 states that for SBA loans >$500k secured by CRE, the lender must obtain a USPAP appraisal and SBA provides no exemption under Interagency appraisal/evaluation guidance for government-insured/guaranteed transactions for federally regulated lenders. In other words, SBA program requirements supersede the evaluation option even where general banking rules might otherwise allow flexibility.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If a lender relies on 29_010 to use an evaluation where 15_009 requires an appraisal, the file becomes SBA-noncompliant with heightened guaranty repair/denial exposure (and likely internal credit policy violations).

dependencies.rationale: 38_008 states an appraisal is not required at $250,000 or less (evaluation required). 29_010 reflects OCC-permitted evaluation use up to $500,000 (and other criteria). If both are treated as applicable simultaneously, staff may choose the wrong requirement, producing compliance violations.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Misapplication can lead to either (a) missing an appraisal when required, or (b) unnecessary appraisals and process friction. Either way can cause exam criticism and remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 requires a real estate appraisal when estimated project property value exceeds $500,000
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 loans, SBA requires a real estate appraisal if the estimated value of the project property is greater than $500,000.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 transactions involving commercial real estate project property where an estimated value is determined as part of the application/credit package.
business_rules.consequences: If an appraisal is not obtained when required, the package is incomplete for SBA 504 standards and may be delayed or rejected until a compliant appraisal is provided.
business_rules.exceptions: If estimated value is $500,000 or less, appraisal may still be required under specific circumstances (see related rule BR_REAL_ESTATE_LENDING_POLICY_COLLATERAL_31_006).

source_reference.section_id: Collateral and Appraisals > Appraisals > Commercial Real Estate
source_reference.source_text: SBA requires a real estate appraisal if the estimated value of the Project Property is greater than $500,000.

examples: Estimated project property value is $750,000; an appraisal is mandatory and must be obtained before submission/approval.
Estimated project property value is $500,001; appraisal is required because value is greater than $500,000.
Estimated project property value is $450,000; appraisal is not automatically required but may be required under special circumstances (close relationship transaction, OREO, etc.).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC processing delay or declination pending completion of required appraisal

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

dependencies.rationale: 15_009 sets an appraisal requirement for SBA loans >$500k secured by CRE with USPAP and timing constraints. 31_005 independently triggers a 504 appraisal requirement when property value >$500k. In practice, for many 504 deals both triggers may apply and should be reconciled to ensure the stricter requirement is satisfied.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If one is applied without the other, appraisal timing/USPAP or value-based triggers may be missed, increasing SBA findings risk or delaying approval.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 requires an appraisal at $500,000 or less when land equity, OREO, close relationship, seller carryback, ownership change, or SBA/CDC necessity applies
business_rules.description: When the estimated value of SBA 504 project property is $500,000 or less, an appraisal is still required if any of the following circumstances apply: (1) equity in land owned for 2 years or more is being contributed as part of borrower’s contribution; (2) the real estate is the third party lender’s OREO property; (3) the loan finances a transaction involving parties with a close relationship (e.g., existing owners or family members); (4) the seller is carrying back a loan that is part of the borrower’s contribution; (5) the project includes a change of ownership; or (6) SBA or the CDC otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary to appropriately evaluate creditworthiness. If an appraisal is not required under these triggers, the CDC must obtain an appropriate evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 project property with estimated value <= $500,000.
business_rules.consequences: If a trigger condition exists and an appraisal is not obtained, the collateral valuation will be noncompliant with SBA 504 standards and may cause SBA/CDC to delay/decline or require corrective action. If no trigger condition exists, failure to obtain an appropriate evaluation may also be cited as not consistent with safe and sound practices.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated. SBA/CDC discretion explicitly applies to requiring an appraisal when otherwise necessary to evaluate creditworthiness.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral and Appraisals > Appraisals > If estimated value is $500,000 or less
source_reference.source_text: If the estimated value of the Project Property is $500,000 or less: The CDC must obtain an appraisal under the following circumstances: Equity in land owned for 2 years or more is being contributed... The real estate is Third Party Lender’s OREO property... transaction involving parties with a close relationship... The seller... carrying back a loan... When the project includes a change of ownership; or If SBA or the CDC otherwise concludes that an appraisal is necessary...

examples: Property value estimated at $400,000 and borrower contributes land held for 3 years as equity; appraisal is required despite value <= $500,000.
Property value estimated at $350,000 and seller carries back a note treated as borrower contribution; appraisal is required.
Property value estimated at $475,000 with no listed triggers; appraisal not required but an evaluation consistent with safe and sound banking practices must be obtained.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC processing delay or required remediation to obtain appraisal/evaluation

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 appraisal must be USPAP-compliant, dated within 12 months, performed by an independent licensed appraiser, and state-certified when value exceeds $1,000,000
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 commercial real estate appraisals: (1) the appraiser must be independent with no appearance of conflict of interest, independent of the loan production function, and not involved in approving the transaction; (2) the appraiser must be State-licensed or State-certified, and when the estimated project property value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified; (3) the appraisal report must be prepared in compliance with USPAP and dated no more than twelve months prior to the date of application; (4) the appraisal must identify SBA as the client or an intended user (CDC may be client/intended user; acceptable if third party lender is client and SBA is intended user); and (5) the CDC may not use an appraisal prepared for the applicant.
business_rules.conditions: Applies whenever an appraisal is required/obtained for SBA 504 project property collateral.
business_rules.consequences: A noncompliant appraisal (conflicted appraiser, wrong credential level, older than 12 months, not USPAP, or appraisal prepared for applicant) may be rejected by SBA/CDC, causing delays, re-ordering costs, or eligibility/credit processing interruption.
business_rules.exceptions: Evaluations (when permitted instead of appraisals) are not required to be USPAP or performed by State licensed/certified appraisers, but should be consistent with interagency appraisal/evaluation guidance (addressed separately in policy procedures).

source_reference.section_id: Collateral and Appraisals > Appraisals and Evaluations > Appraiser requirements and appraisal report requirements
source_reference.source_text: The appraiser must be: Independent... and Either State-licensed or State-certified with the following exception: when the Project Property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be State-certified. The “Appraisal Report” must be prepared in compliance with... (USPAP) and dated no more than twelve months prior to the date of application. ... the appraisal must identify SBA as the client or an intended user... It is acceptable... if the appraisal identifies the Third Party Lender as the client and SBA as intended user. The CDC may not use an appraisal prepared for the applicant.

examples: Estimated value $1.2MM: appraisal completed by a State-licensed (not certified) appraiser; noncompliant and must be redone by a State-certified appraiser.
Appraisal dated 14 months before the application date; noncompliant because it exceeds the 12-month limit.
Appraisal ordered by borrower for its own purposes and delivered to CDC; noncompliant because the CDC may not use an appraisal prepared for the applicant.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA/CDC rejection of appraisal leading to rework, delay, or denial pending compliant valuation support

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

dependencies.rationale: 31_005 triggers the requirement to obtain an appraisal; 31_007 sets the acceptability criteria (independence, credentialing, USPAP, age, not applicant-prepared).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 31_007 criteria are not met, SBA/CDC can reject the appraisal, delay processing/closing, and require re-ordering.

dependencies.rationale: 31_006 identifies appraisal trigger scenarios for projects ≤ $500k; once triggered, 31_007 governs the required appraisal’s acceptability and format.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If a trigger exists and the appraisal is missing or noncompliant, the SBA 504 package may be delayed/declined until corrected.

business_rules.rule_name: Construction loan policy should set tenors not shorter than completion and align extension options to construction plus absorption period
business_rules.description: Real estate lending policy should define acceptable tenors for construction loans based on the time needed for construction and stabilization or sale, and the tenor should not be shorter than required for completion. The policy may provide construction financing that covers the expected construction period with conversion to bridge financing for the expected stabilization period. Any extension options should be consistent with expected construction time plus projected absorption period.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to ADC/construction lending policy design and to structuring construction and bridge facilities for CRE projects with sale/lease-up components.
business_rules.consequences: If construction loan tenor is shorter than completion needs or extension options are misaligned to absorption, the institution may face maturity defaults, repeated extensions, and heightened credit risk; examiners may criticize weak structuring standards.
business_rules.exceptions: No explicit exception stated; policy design is expected to be commensurate with project characteristics and realistic timelines.

source_reference.section_id: ADC policies; construction loan tenor discussion
source_reference.source_text: The bank’s lending policy typically defines acceptable tenors for various types of construction loans. The appropriate tenor is generally based on the time needed for construction and stabilization or sale and would not be shorter than that required for completion. The lending policy may include extension options, but the length of the extension options should be consistent with the expected construction time plus the projected absorption period.

examples: Project needs 14 months to complete construction and 6 months to stabilize: policy tenor should cover at least 14 months, and any extension should reasonably cover the projected 6-month absorption/stabilization period.
Bank structures a 12-month construction facility that converts to a 12-month bridge period after CO; aligns with ‘construction + stabilization’ concept in policy.
If a project cannot reasonably be completed in the proposed 9-month term, policy requires restructuring (longer term or staged facilities) rather than approving an unrealistic tenor.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Examination criticism and required policy enhancement for unsafe ADC structuring practices

examples: Real Estate Lending Policy v4.2 with CRE property-type sublimits and exception authority
Board-approved policy requiring periodic concentration reporting to management and the board

business_rules.rule_name: CDC must review Agent fees when a third party indicates excessiveness or an Applicant complains
business_rules.description: CDCs must review the Agent’s services and related fees to determine if fees are necessary and reasonable when there is an indication from a third party that fees might be excessive or when an Applicant complains about fees charged by an Agent. Where fees appear unreasonable, CDCs should contact the D/OCRM to report the fees.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 lending operations involving Agents charging Applicants; triggered by (1) third-party indication of potentially excessive fees or (2) Applicant complaint.
business_rules.consequences: Required review of services/fees; if fees appear unreasonable, CDC escalates to D/OCRM; SBA investigation may require fee reduction/refund and prohibition on collecting amounts above SBA-deemed reasonable.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Review of Agent Fees
source_reference.source_text: CDCs must review the Agent’s services and related fees to determine if the fees are necessary and reasonable when: There is an indication from a third party that an Agent’s fees might be excessive; or When an Applicant complains about the fees charged by an Agent. In cases where fees appear to be unreasonable, CDCs should contact the D/OCRM to report the fees.

examples: A competing lender informs the CDC that an Agent is charging unusually high packaging fees on 504 deals: CDC performs a necessity/reasonableness review and documents results.
An Applicant complains that the Agent charged for services not performed: CDC reviews invoices, workpapers, and outputs, and reports to D/OCRM if unreasonable.
A CDC receives no complaint and no third-party indication: this specific review trigger is not activated (but SBA may still review fees at any time).
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA investigation outcomes can include mandated fee reduction/refund; program integrity escalation to D/OCRM.

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

dependencies.rationale: 15_004 requires CDCs to review services/fees when complaints/indications arise; 15_002 provides the principal quantitative limits and documentation expectations used to judge whether fees appear excessive.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without applying 15_002 standards, CDC reviews become inconsistent and may fail to detect excessive fees that require escalation/refund.

dependencies.rationale: 15_004’s review/escalation is triggered by potential excessiveness; 15_005 defines categories that are impermissible regardless of amount and therefore must be detected during review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 15_005 prohibitions are not applied, CDC may fail to escalate or require refunds for fees that are impermissible even if within percentage caps.

business_rules.rule_name: Prior D/FA approval required before a CDC contracts out specified SBA 504 functions
business_rules.description: Pre-approval of a professional services contract by the D/FA is required prior to engaging a contractor for specified CDC functions, including: managing (with a waiver request if manager not directly employed), marketing, packaging, processing, closing, servicing, liquidating, legal services connected with loan liquidation or litigation, independent loan reviews performed by another CDC (with restrictions to avoid bias), and co-employment contracts. Certain functions may be contracted without SBA approval (e.g., accounting, most legal services not tied to liquidation/litigation, IT, independent loan review performed by a non-CDC).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a CDC intends to engage a contractor to perform any function listed as requiring pre-approval under 13 CFR § 120.824 as reflected in the SOP.
business_rules.consequences: Without required pre-approval, the CDC is non-compliant (and related consequences may include OCRM reporting and SLPC processing delays as described elsewhere in the SOP).
business_rules.exceptions: No SBA approval required for contracting: accounting; legal services (except liquidation/litigation); information technology; independent loan review services performed by a non-CDC.

source_reference.section_id: Professional Services Contractors (13 CFR § 120.824)
source_reference.source_text: Pre-approval of a professional services contract by the D/FA is required prior to engaging the services of a contractor for any of the following functions: Managing... Marketing; Packaging; Processing; Closing; Servicing; Liquidating; Legal services in connection with loan liquidation or litigation; Independent Loan Reviews to be conducted by another CDC... or Co-employment contracts... CDCs may contract for the following functions without SBA approval: Accounting; Legal services (except in connection with loan liquidation or litigation); Information technology; and Independent loan review services performed by a non-CDC.

examples: A CDC plans to outsource packaging to a third-party contractor: D/FA pre-approval is required before the contractor is engaged.
A CDC hires a law firm to handle a liquidation lawsuit: pre-approval is required because it is legal services connected with liquidation/litigation.
A CDC contracts with a non-CDC firm for independent loan review services: SBA approval is not required under the stated exception.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA non-compliance finding; potential OCRM reporting and SLPC processing delays if engaged without approval.

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

dependencies.rationale: 15_006 requires D/FA pre-approval prior to contracting for specified CDC functions; 15_008 defines the mandatory, timed submission package to OFA to obtain that approval.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the 60-day submission package is not provided, approval can be delayed or the CDC may improperly engage a contractor without pre-approval, causing noncompliance and downstream processing delays.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 repayment ability analysis requires debt service coverage ratio at least 1.00x using all business debt including new SBA proceeds and current financials within 120 days
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 credit memoranda, repayment ability analysis must address debt service coverage where Debt Service is defined as future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (operating cash flow divided by debt service) must be equal to or greater than 1.00x based on calculations acceptable to SLPC. The analysis must include balance sheet and income statement dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA, including analysis of debt service coverage and A/R and A/P aging. If historical cash flow does not show sufficient coverage after the SBA loan, projections must be analyzed; projection-based projects require a minimum of 2 years of projections, and if projections show repayment in Year 2 but not Year 1, sufficient liquidity must be shown to cover the Year 1 shortfall.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loan program credit memoranda (CDC credit memorandum) prepared for submission to SBA for applicant repayment ability analysis, including when the deal relies on projections or construction period performance.
business_rules.consequences: If DSCR < 1.00x using the defined debt service, the credit memorandum does not meet SBA policy and may be declined or require restructuring (reduced debt, increased equity, longer amortization where allowed, additional collateral, or other mitigants). If Year 1 projection shortfall exists, lack of sufficient liquidity is a policy deficiency requiring mitigation before submission/approval.
business_rules.exceptions: DSCR calculation methodology must be acceptable to SLPC; if historical cash flow is insufficient, projections are required per SOP rather than an outright prohibition, provided assumptions and liquidity support are documented.

source_reference.section_id: CDC Credit Memorandum > Repayment Ability Analysis > Projection-based projects
source_reference.source_text: Debt Service is defined as the future required principal and interest payments on all business debt inclusive of new SBA loan proceeds. The Applicant’s debt service coverage ratio (operating cash flow divided by debt service) must be equal to or greater than 1:1... Balance sheet and income statement dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA... For projection-based projects, the analysis must include a minimum of 2 years of projections... If the projections show repayment in Year 2 but not in Year 1, sufficient liquidity must be shown to cover the shortfall in Year 1.

examples: Historical operating cash flow is $500k and total P&I on all business debt including new SBA proceeds is $520k. DSCR = 0.96x. Result: does not meet 1.00x minimum; restructure or mitigate before submission.
Financial statements are dated 150 days before SBA submission. Result: noncompliant; update statements to be within 120 days.
Projections show Year 1 DSCR 0.90x and Year 2 DSCR 1.10x. Result: must document sufficient liquidity to cover the Year 1 shortfall.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA submission rejection/decline risk and post-closing program eligibility/guaranty repair risk for nonconforming credit analysis

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

dependencies.rationale: 35_010 requires DSCR ≥ 1.00x; 35_011 defines minimum borrower contribution and structure constraints. The required injection affects debt sizing and therefore DSCR feasibility and mitigants.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If injection rules are not met, DSCR may be artificially improved by impermissible structure; SBA may require restructure or decline.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 504 borrower contribution minimums are 10% standard, 15% for new business or limited/special purpose property, and 20% when both apply with corresponding debenture percentage caps
business_rules.description: For SBA 504 projects, the borrower must contribute (excluding administrative costs) at least 10% of project cost for all borrowers. New businesses must contribute at least 15%; in that case, the debenture will finance no more than 35% of the project and at least 50% of project financing will be from banks/other financial institutions, state/local government, or foundations/non-profits. Businesses with a Limited or Special Purpose Property must contribute at least 15%; if the project involves a new business with a Limited/Special Purpose Property, the applicant must contribute at least 20%, and if the borrower contributes at least 20%, the debenture will finance no more than 30% of the project with at least 50% coming from eligible third-party sources. If the borrower contribution is borrowed, it must be subordinate to the third-party loan and the 504 debenture; and when collateralized by the project property, absent SBA written approval, it may not be repaid faster than the 504 loan.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 loan applications/projects requiring borrower injection and determining financing structure percentages and eligibility, including when property type is Limited/Special Purpose and/or applicant is a New Business.
business_rules.consequences: If borrower contribution is below the applicable minimum (10%/15%/20%), the project structure does not meet SBA policy and must be restructured (increase equity/injection, adjust debenture/third-party portions). If borrowed contribution is not properly subordinated or has impermissible repayment terms, it is ineligible absent SBA written approval where required.
business_rules.exceptions: Borrower contribution may be borrowed if subordinate to third-party loan and 504 debenture; repayment faster than the 504 loan may be permitted only with SBA written approval as specified. The excerpt includes specific treatment where project involves new business and Limited/Special Purpose property; other SBA rules may apply outside the excerpt.

source_reference.section_id: CDC Credit Memorandum > Borrower’s Contribution
source_reference.source_text: All Borrowers must contribute at least 10%... New businesses must contribute at least 15%. The Debenture will finance no more than 35% of the Project... Businesses with a Limited or Special Purpose Property: Must contribute at least 15%... Must contribute at least 20%, if the Project involves a new business... If the Borrower contributes at least 20%... the Debenture will finance no more than 30% of the Project...

examples: An existing business buying standard-use real estate proposes 8% equity injection. Result: must increase to at least 10% to meet SBA 504 minimum.

A start-up (new business) financing a hotel (limited/special purpose) proposes 15% injection with 40% debenture. Result: does not meet 20% contribution minimum and debenture cap should be no more than 30% if 20% contribution is used.

Borrower contribution is financed by a seller note secured by the project property with payments amortizing faster than the 504 loan. Result: prohibited without SBA written approval; must subordinate and adjust repayment speed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA ineligibility, approval denial, or guaranty repair/denial risk for nonconforming injection and structure

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

business_rules.rule_name: Firewall access eligibility must treat protected demographic access as permitted when an employee might need it to perform assigned duties
business_rules.description: When implementing the Section 1071 firewall and evaluating eligibility for the exception, the institution’s determination of which employees or officers “should have access” to protected demographic information must be based on whether the employee/officer may need to collect, see, consider, refer to, or otherwise use the information to perform assigned job duties. The employee/officer does not have to be required to use the information or actually use it; it is sufficient that they might need to do so to perform assigned duties.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution is determining access to protected demographic information under final § 1002.108(a)(2) and the exception framework in § 1002.108(c).
business_rules.consequences: Improperly restricting the exception to only employees who actually used the information (or who are formally required to use it) can create noncompliance with the rule’s definition and may result in inconsistent firewall implementations and supervisory criticism of controls and procedures.
business_rules.exceptions: No safe harbor is provided in the excerpt; institutions may make different determinations as permitted by the rule’s flexibility, but must apply the definition of “should have access.”

source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.108(a)(2) defines the phrase “should have access,” which is used in § 1002.108(c) and related commentary. This phrase means that an employee or officer may need to collect, see, consider, refer to, or otherwise use the information to perform that employee’s or officer’s assigned job duties. ... The employee or officer does not have to be required to collect, see, consider, refer to, or use such information or to actually collect, see, consider, refer to or use such information ... It is sufficient if the employee or officer might need to do so to perform the employee’s or officer’s assigned job duties.

examples: Credit analyst occasionally resolves application discrepancies and may need to view protected demographic responses to address data integrity: analyst can be designated as ‘should have access’ even if rarely used.
Loan officer never collects or uses protected demographic information and has no duties requiring access: should not be designated as ‘should have access.’
Branch manager may step in to complete applications when staff is absent: may be designated as ‘should have access’ even if this occurs infrequently.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism requiring revised access-control determinations and monitoring

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

business_rules.rule_name: Firewall access determinations may be applied to all staff sharing a job title when assigned tasks may require protected demographic access
business_rules.description: The institution may determine that all employees or officers with the same job description or assigned duties should have access for purposes of final § 1002.108. If tasks that may require access to protected demographic information are assigned to a particular job title, the institution may designate all employees/officers in that job title as having access for § 1002.108 purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when senior management establishes access-control scope for protected demographic information under § 1002.108 based on job roles and job duties.
business_rules.consequences: If management fails to align access permissions to role-based assigned duties, the institution may either (i) over-grant access (increasing privacy risk and firewall weaknesses) or (ii) under-grant access (causing operational failures and inconsistent application processing). Supervisory criticism may result for weak access governance.
business_rules.exceptions: Permissive (may) rather than required; institutions may choose more granular individual-based determinations if preferred.

source_reference.section_id: Commentary 108(a)-2.ii
source_reference.source_text: Final comment 108(a)-2.ii explains that a financial institution may determine that all employees or officers with the same job description or assigned duties should have access for purposes of final § 1002.108. If a financial institution assigns one or more tasks that may require access to one or more applicants’ protected demographic information to a particular job title, the financial institution may determine that all employees and officers who share that job title should have access for purposes of § 1002.108.

examples: All ‘Small Business Underwriter’ roles are assigned a duty to validate demographic completeness; bank may grant the entire job title access instead of individual-by-individual approvals.
Only ‘Data Quality Specialist’ job title may correct application data; bank may limit access to that job title group.
A bank chooses to limit access to only senior underwriters even though the job title is shared across levels; permitted if aligned to assigned duties.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding requiring strengthened access governance if role-based access is misaligned to duties

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

business_rules.rule_name: Firewall access determinations may consider lawful operational factors and do not require hiring staff or upgrading systems solely to decide access
business_rules.description: When determining whether an employee/officer should have access to protected demographic information for purposes of § 1002.108, the institution is permitted to choose lawful factors to consider, including operational factors and lawful business practices (e.g., institution size, number of staff in the line of business or at a branch, number of covered applications received or expected, staffing levels, systems, processes, policies, and procedures). The institution is not required to hire additional staff, upgrade systems, change lending/operational processes, or revise policies/procedures solely to determine who should have access.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to management’s feasibility and operational determination process for firewall exception implementation under comment 108(a)-2.iii and related final rule text.
business_rules.consequences: If management assumes it must hire staff or upgrade systems solely to meet the access determination requirement, it may incur unnecessary costs; if it uses unlawful factors, it risks regulatory violations and supervisory criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: Permissive framework; the institution may still choose to improve systems/policies for broader compliance reasons, but not solely for access determination.

source_reference.section_id: Commentary 108(a)-2.iii
source_reference.source_text: A financial institution is permitted to choose what lawful factors it will consider when determining whether an employee or officer should have access to protected demographic information. ... a financial institution may consider its size, the number of employees and officers within the relevant line of business or at a particular branch or office location, and/or the number of covered applications the financial institution has received or expects to receive. ... A financial institution is not required to hire additional staff, upgrade its systems, change its lending or operational processes, or revise its policies or procedures for the sole purpose of determining who should have access.

examples: A small community bank with two underwriters at one branch may designate both as ‘should have access’ based on staffing constraints; permitted if lawful and operationally justified.
A large bank uses expected application volumes to decide which centralized team members require access; permitted.
Bank declines to upgrade LOS permissions solely to decide access; permitted, but it must still implement a lawful access determination using existing controls.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism if unlawful factors are used or access determinations are undocumented/unsupported

examples: Chief Credit Officer overseeing CRE concentration monitoring and limit breaches
ALCO reviewing stress testing impacts on CRE portfolio performance

business_rules.rule_name: CDC must submit professional services contract package at least 60 days before engagement including board justification
business_rules.description: At least 60 days prior to the date a CDC intends to engage a contractor’s services for functions requiring SBA approval, the CDC must submit to OFA a request from a responsible CDC management official to review draft materials, an unsigned draft of the contract, and a justification from the CDC’s Board of Directors explaining why the Board believes contracting for CDC functions is in the CDC’s best interest.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 504 professional services contracts requiring prior SBA approval under the SOP’s submission process.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to submit timely and with required materials can delay approval and increase risk of engaging without approval (which triggers OCRM reporting and SLPC processing delays).
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt specifies the rule for contracts requiring prior approval; it does not apply to functions explicitly not requiring SBA approval.

source_reference.section_id: Submission Process
source_reference.source_text: At least 60 days prior to the date on which the CDC intends to engage the contractor’s services, the CDC must submit to OFA at 504Requests@sba.gov: A request from a responsible CDC management official to review the draft materials; An unsigned draft of the contract; and A justification from the CDC’s Board of Directors explaining its reasoning for why the Board believes it is in the best interest of the CDC to contract for CDC functions.

examples: A CDC plans to outsource servicing starting September 1: it submits the contract package to OFA no later than July 3 (60 days prior), including board justification.
A CDC submits a signed contract instead of an unsigned draft: the submission is deficient and may delay D/FA approval.
A CDC submits only management’s rationale but not the Board’s justification: non-compliant package under the stated requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Approval delays; risk of downstream SBA non-compliance if contractor engaged without approval.

examples: Board Risk Committee approving CRE sublimits by property type
Board receiving quarterly CRE concentration and market condition reporting

business_rules.rule_name: Borrower operating licenses must be obtained within 90 days after final disbursement and loan cannot be sold until obtained
business_rules.description: The lender must obtain evidence from the borrower of all licenses required to operate the business within no more than 90 days after final disbursement of the loan. The SBA loan may not be sold into the secondary market until all required licenses are obtained.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans subject to E-Tran Terms and Conditions through Disbursement; triggered upon final disbursement and any intent to sell the loan into the secondary market.
business_rules.consequences: If evidence of required licenses is not obtained within 90 days after final disbursement, lender is noncompliant with SBA terms. Additionally, the lender must not sell the loan into the secondary market until license evidence is obtained; attempting to sell would violate SBA requirements and could impair sale eligibility/guaranty transfer.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: SOP 50 10 8 Section B, Ch 5 – Licenses
source_reference.source_text: The Lender must obtain evidence from the Borrower of all licenses required to operate the business within no more than 90 days after final disbursement of the loan. The loan may not be sold into the secondary market until all required licenses are obtained.

examples: Final disbursement occurs on July 1: lender must obtain required operating licenses by September 29 (within 90 days) and must not sell the loan before license evidence is in file.
Borrower provides local business license but not required state contractor license: loan cannot be sold into secondary market until all required licenses are obtained.
Loan is otherwise eligible for sale, but borrower has not provided license evidence by day 60: lender must continue to pursue and cannot sell until completed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Secondary market sale prohibition; SBA compliance findings and potential guaranty transfer/sale issues

examples: Use of participations to reduce single-segment exposure without stopping originations
Whole loan sale program for noncore CRE segments during market downturn

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 bona fide error presumption requires error rates to be below Appendix F tolerance thresholds based on random samples sized by register volume
business_rules.description: For Section 1071 enforcement related to bona fide errors, a financial institution is presumed to maintain procedures reasonably adapted to avoid errors for a given data field when the number of errors found in a random sample of its small business lending application register submission for that data field does not equal or exceed the Bureau-specified threshold for that field. The thresholds appear in Appendix F (renumbered from proposed Appendix H), and the random sample size depends on the size of the institution’s small business lending application register (as shown in the appendix table).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution (or examiners) assess whether Section 1071 reporting errors qualify as bona fide errors and whether the institution is presumed to have procedures reasonably adapted to avoid such errors, based on review sampling and tolerance thresholds.
business_rules.consequences: If sample errors for a data field are below the applicable Appendix F threshold, the institution receives a presumption that it maintained procedures reasonably adapted to avoid errors for that field (supporting bona fide error treatment). If errors equal or exceed the threshold, the presumption does not apply, increasing supervisory and enforcement risk.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt indicates minor revisions from proposal; specific numeric tolerances are contained in Appendix F (not in this text excerpt).

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.112(b) Bona Fide Errors and Appendix F (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: financial institutions that report a number of errors equal to or below the applicable thresholds would have been presumed to have in place procedures reasonably adapted to avoid errors... those that report a number of errors above the applicable thresholds would not... a financial institution is presumed to maintain procedures reasonably adapted to avoid errors... if the number of errors found in a random sample... does not equal or exceed a threshold specified by the Bureau... thresholds appear... in the table in... appendix... and... the size of the random sample shall depend on the size of the financial institution’s... register...

examples: An institution with a large register volume undergoes a random-sample review; its errors for a specific data field are below the Appendix F threshold: it receives the presumption of procedures reasonably adapted to avoid errors for that field.
A lender’s random sample shows errors for action taken values at or above the Appendix F threshold: the presumption does not apply, increasing examination and potential enforcement exposure.

A vendor-run MIS produces a submission with elevated error counts in one data field; because the error count equals/exceeds the tolerance, the institution must enhance controls and may not rely on the presumption.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination; potential CFPB enforcement for deficient data quality controls

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 22_006’s presumption analysis uses error counts found in a random sample of the institution’s application register submission for a data field; that submission is governed by 31_010.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If annual submission populations are inaccurate or incomplete, error-rate assessment will be unreliable and the institution may lose the presumption of procedures reasonably adapted to avoid errors.

business_rules.rule_name: FX maturity gap schedule must be daily for at least two weeks after exam start date
business_rules.description: When preparing a maturity distribution (gap) schedule for foreign currency positions, the schedule should detail at least the first two weeks of activity after the examination start date on a daily basis. For active departments, daily enumeration for the first month after the examination start date may be appropriate. After the initial daily period, the schedule may use semi-monthly or monthly intervals depending on how the institution prices forward commitments and the volume of activity. Longer-range maturities may be grouped by years. This rule is used to set minimum time-bucket granularity for the maturity distribution output.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when MIS/workpapers produce a maturity distribution (gap) analysis for foreign currency positions during an examination or equivalent internal risk review; applies per currency schedule (major currencies and, at examiner discretion, material other currencies).
business_rules.consequences: If the schedule is not produced with the minimum required granularity (daily buckets for at least two weeks, or one month for active departments), examiners may consider the maturity distribution incomplete/insufficient and require follow-up workpapers or criticize risk measurement practices.
business_rules.exceptions: Daily enumeration for the first month is discretionary for active departments (not mandatory). Semi-monthly/monthly interval selection depends on institution’s forward pricing method and activity volume.

source_reference.section_id: MATURITY DISTRIBUTION (GAP) ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: In arranging the maturity distribution, it is recommended that at least the first two weeks of activity subsequent to the examination start date be detailed on a daily basis. (In active departments, a daily enumeration for the first month following the examination start date may be appropriate). Thereafter, semi-monthly or monthly intervals may be used depending on the institution’s method of pricing forward commitments and the volume of activity. Longer-range maturities may be grouped by years.

examples: A bank with FX trading activity produces a EUR maturity distribution with daily buckets for days 1–14 after exam start date, then monthly buckets for months 2–12, then annual buckets thereafter.
An active FX department produces daily buckets for days 1–30 after exam start date; the remainder uses semi-monthly buckets because forward commitment pricing occurs on mid-month cycles.
If the maturity distribution starts with monthly buckets immediately after the exam start date, the workpaper fails this rule and is likely to be questioned by examiners.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Swap contracts must be excluded from FX position revaluation because profit or loss is fixed at inception
business_rules.description: When valuing foreign currency positions for revaluation and income/loss analysis, all swap contracts must be removed/excluded prior to valuing the position because the profit/loss is fixed at transaction inception and is reflected in the return on the swapped asset. Institutions should identify swap transactions through records review, ensure they are not included in revaluation schedules, and recognize that swap profit (or cost) is typically applied to the rate of interest on the underlying asset to determine true yield and is accrued to income and expenses monthly rather than allocated to the exchange function.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when MIS or workpapers calculate unrealized spot-rate profit/loss or revaluation adjustments for foreign currency positions and the reported net position includes swap contracts.
business_rules.consequences: If swaps are included in revaluation schedules, unrealized profit/loss reporting may be misstated and examiners may require corrections and may criticize risk measurement/financial reporting controls.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; swaps should not be included in the revaluation schedule in the described circumstances.

source_reference.section_id: MATURITY DISTRIBUTION (GAP) ANALYSIS and REVALUATION AND INCOME/LOSS ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: All swap contracts should be removed before valuing the position since the profit/loss is fixed at the time of the transaction and reflected in the return on the asset for which the swap was effected. ... the institution has realized a $3,000 profit on the transaction at the inception of the loan. ... The profit (or loss) is accrued to income and expenses monthly. In these circumstances, it is inappropriate to allocate the profit to the exchange function. ... these amounts should not be included in the revaluation schedule.

examples: A USD/GBP swap used to fund a 3‑month GBP loan is excluded from the FX revaluation schedule; the locked-in forward points are reflected in the loan’s yield and accrued monthly.
If an FX revaluation report includes swap legs as open positions, the report overstates market P&L volatility; the bank must remove swap contracts and recompute unrealized P&L.
A treasury analyst identifies swaps by reviewing deal tickets and confirms the net position used for revaluation is “less any swap contracts included in the position.”
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 previously collected data may be reused for data points 13 through 20 only if collected within 36 months and not known to be inaccurate
business_rules.description: A financial institution is permitted (but not required) to reuse previously collected data to satisfy CFPB 1071 reportable data fields § 1002.107(a)(13) through (20) if (1) the data were collected within the 36 months preceding the current covered application (with a special rule for gross annual revenue), and (2) the financial institution has no reason to believe the data are inaccurate.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when compiling reportable data for a current covered application and the institution has previously collected the same § 1002.107(a)(13)-(20) data for the same applicant.
business_rules.consequences: If conditions are met, MIS may auto-populate the current application dataset using the prior values to reduce re-collection burden; if conditions are not met, MIS must require fresh collection for the impacted data fields.
business_rules.exceptions: For gross annual revenue (§ 1002.107(a)(14)), reuse is further restricted to data collected within the same calendar year as the current covered application (see separate rule BR_MANAGEMENT_INFORMATION_SYSTEM_REPORTING_25_002). For demographic data reuse limits, see BR_MANAGEMENT_INFORMATION_SYSTEM_REPORTING_25_003.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule—§ 1002.107(d) Previously Collected Data
source_reference.source_text: The Bureau is finalizing § 1002.107(d) ... to permit, but not require, a financial institution to reuse previously collected data to satisfy § 1002.107(a)(13) through (20) if (a) the data were collected within the 36 months preceding the current covered application (except that to satisfy § 1002.107(a)(14), on gross annual revenue, the data must be collected within the same calendar year as the current covered application) and (b) the financial institution has no reason to believe the data are inaccurate.

examples: Applicant provided number of workers 18 months ago on a covered application; the institution has no reason to believe it changed, so MIS reuses the value for the current covered application.
Applicant provided NAICS code 30 months ago; MIS reuses it because it is within 36 months and no inaccuracy flags exist.
Applicant provided certain data 40 months ago; MIS blocks reuse and prompts for fresh collection because the 36-month window has expired.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential enforcement for inaccurate or incomplete 1071 reporting

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 25_001 permits reuse of certain data within 36 months; 25_002 narrows that permission specifically for gross annual revenue by requiring same-calendar-year collection.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the override is not enforced, the institution will reuse stale revenue data, producing noncompliant 1071 reporting and potential exam findings.

dependencies.rationale: 25_001 allows reuse if collected within 36 months and believed accurate; 25_003 prohibits reuse of demographics unless originally collected pursuant to Subpart B, limiting reuse sources.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If legacy demographic data is reused, the institution will violate 1071 demographic collection/reporting requirements and increase privacy/fair lending risk.

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 gross annual revenue data may be reused only if collected in the same calendar year as the current covered application
business_rules.description: Even though § 1002.107(d) permits reuse of certain previously collected data within 36 months, gross annual revenue (§ 1002.107(a)(14)) has a stricter condition: the data must have been collected within the same calendar year as the current covered application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when compiling § 1002.107(a)(14) gross annual revenue for a current covered application and a prior gross annual revenue value exists for the applicant.
business_rules.consequences: If prior gross annual revenue data was collected in a different calendar year, MIS must prevent reuse and require updated gross annual revenue collection for the current covered application.
business_rules.exceptions: Reuse is still conditioned on the institution having no reason to believe the previously collected revenue data are inaccurate; if there is reason to believe it is inaccurate, reuse is prohibited even within the same calendar year.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule—revenue exception within § 1002.107(d)
source_reference.source_text: permit, but not require, a financial institution to reuse previously collected data to satisfy § 1002.107(a)(13) through (20) if (a) the data were collected within the 36 months preceding the current covered application (except that to satisfy § 1002.107(a)(14), on gross annual revenue, the data must be collected within the same calendar year as the current covered application)

examples: A borrower applied in November 2025 and again in February 2026; the November 2025 gross annual revenue value may not be reused for the 2026 covered application because it is not the same calendar year.
A borrower applied in March 2026 and again in October 2026; MIS may reuse March 2026 gross annual revenue for October 2026 if there is no reason to believe it is inaccurate.
A borrower applied in January 2026 and reports opening several new stores in May 2026; even within the same calendar year, MIS flags potential inaccuracy and requires updated revenue.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and mandated data corrections for 1071 reporting errors

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 demographic data may not be reused unless originally collected under Subpart B in connection with a prior covered application
business_rules.description: For CFPB 1071 demographic data elements in § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19), a financial institution may not reuse previously collected data unless it was collected in connection with a prior covered application pursuant to Subpart B. This limits reuse to data gathered using the rule’s required protections and response/selection options.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when MIS seeks to reuse previously collected applicant demographic data for women-owned/minority-owned/LGBTQI+-owned business status and principal owner ethnicity/race/sex for a current covered application.
business_rules.consequences: If demographic data was gathered outside Subpart B (e.g., from other onboarding forms or legacy demographic programs), MIS must not reuse it to satisfy § 1002.107(a)(18)-(19) for 1071 reporting; it must instead collect demographic data anew under Subpart B processes for the covered application.
business_rules.exceptions: If demographic data was collected in connection with a prior covered application under Subpart B and within the applicable reuse window, it may be reused subject to the institution having no reason to believe it is inaccurate.

source_reference.section_id: Final comment 107(d)-9 (demographic reuse limitation)
source_reference.source_text: final comment 107(d)-9 provides that a financial institution may not reuse data to satisfy § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19) unless the data were collected in connection with a prior covered application pursuant to subpart B. The Bureau believes that reuse of applicant demographic data should be limited in this manner to ensure that data reported were collected in a manner that aligns with the protections and selection options set forth in final § 1002.107(a)(18) and (19).

examples: A bank previously collected owner race/ethnicity during unrelated marketing outreach; it must not reuse that data for 1071 and must collect it under Subpart B for the covered application.

A lender collected women-owned/minority-owned status under 1071 Subpart B on a covered application 24 months ago; it may reuse it for a new covered application if there is no reason to believe it is inaccurate.

A lender has demographic data from a prior non-covered application; it cannot reuse that data for 1071 reporting because it was not collected in connection with a prior covered application under Subpart B.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB/agency enforcement for ECOA/Reg B violations and improper handling of protected demographic information; supervisory findings and potential civil money penalties

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: E-Tran submission must disclose 100% of direct and indirect applicant ownership and must combine married spouses and minor children ownership percentages
business_rules.description: When inputting applicant data into SBA E-Tran, the lender must list 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined for reporting in E-Tran.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA E-Tran submissions for EWCP applications (and, by extension within the SOP excerpt, lender’s application for guaranty content requirements) where ownership is entered into E-Tran.
business_rules.consequences: If 100% ownership (direct and indirect) is not disclosed or required combinations are not made, the submission is incomplete/incorrect and may lead to SBA processing delays, rework, or lender oversight criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty
source_reference.source_text: Lender must list in E-Tran 100% of the total direct and indirect ownership of the Applicant. The ownership percentage of married spouses and minor children must be combined.

examples: Applicant has three owners: A 60%, B 30%, C 10%; E-Tran must show all owners totaling 100%.
Applicant is owned 40% by one spouse and 40% by the other spouse, plus a third owner at 20%; E-Tran must combine the spouses as 80% for ownership reporting purposes (per the instruction to combine).
Applicant ownership includes a minor child at 5% and parent at 45%; E-Tran must combine those ownership percentages when required by the instruction.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delay/rejection; SBA lender oversight finding for inaccurate submission data

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB Section 1071 requires reporting the initial amount applied for and midpoint when a range is requested
business_rules.description: For CFPB Regulation B Subpart B (Section 1071) reporting, the financial institution must collect and report the amount applied for regardless of whether the application is approved or originated. The institution must report the initial amount of credit or credit limit initially requested by the applicant at the application stage and is not required to report amounts discussed before an application is made. If the applicant requests a range, the institution must report the midpoint of the range. If the applicant does not request a specific amount at the application stage but the institution underwrites for a specific amount, the institution reports the amount considered in underwriting. If a particular credit product does not involve a specific amount requested, the institution reports “not applicable.”
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered credit transactions subject to CFPB 1071 data collection/reporting requirements, at the application stage and in the final reported dataset.
business_rules.consequences: MIS/workflows must be capable of capturing and retaining the initial requested amount (and midpoint logic for ranges) and distinguishing pre-application discussions from the application-stage request. Errors or omissions create 1071 data integrity risk, potential supervisory findings, and remediation requirements.
business_rules.exceptions: If no specific amount is requested and product type does not involve a specific amount requested, report “not applicable” (as described).

source_reference.section_id: Final § 1002.107(a)(7) Amount applied for and commentary 107(a)(7)-1 and -2 (Section-by-Section Analysis)
source_reference.source_text: the Bureau reads section 1071 to require collection and reporting of the amount applied for regardless of whether the application is ultimately approved or originated... the financial institution reports the initial amount of credit or the credit limit initially requested by the applicant at the application stage and is not required to report credit amounts or limits discussed before an application is made... for amounts that were requested as a range of numbers, the financial institution reports the midpoint of the range... where the applicant does not request a specific amount... but the financial institution underwrites... the financial institution reports the amount that was considered in underwriting... the financial institution reports “not applicable.”

examples: Applicant requests a line of credit for $200,000–$300,000 at application; institution must report $250,000 (midpoint).

Applicant never states an amount for a credit card product and the institution assigns a limit through its process; institution reports “not applicable” for amount applied for (per commentary scenario).
Applicant applies for a term loan and initially requests $500,000; later agrees to $400,000 during underwriting—institution must report $500,000 as the amount applied for.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding; potential enforcement for inaccurate or incomplete 1071 data collection/reporting and related Reg B violations

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Covered financial institutions must compile and maintain Section 1071 covered application data in the CFPB-prescribed manner including required data items
business_rules.description: A covered financial institution must compile and maintain data regarding covered applications from small businesses. Data must be compiled in the manner prescribed in § 1002.107 and the Filing Instructions Guide (FIG) for the appropriate year, and must include the items described in § 1002.107(a)(1) through (20). MIS must be able to capture, store, and retrieve these required data points per covered application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions (per § 1002.105(b)) for each covered application from a small business within Section 1071 scope; compilation must follow the FIG for the appropriate year.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: institution has a compliant dataset for downstream reporting/submission and auditability. If not satisfied: incomplete or nonconforming data compilation creates reporting violations, data integrity findings, and potential enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; item-level exceptions/collection nuances are addressed in other subsections/commentary.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.107(a) Data format and itemization
source_reference.source_text: A covered financial institution shall compile and maintain data regarding covered applications from small businesses. The data shall be compiled in the manner prescribed herein and the Filing Instructions Guide for this subpart for the appropriate year. The data compiled shall include the items described in paragraphs (a)(1) through (20) of this section.

examples: Institution captures application date, method, product, amount requested, action taken, and pricing fields for every covered application and stores them in a retrievable record keyed by a unique identifier.

Institution uses the CFPB FIG for the applicable year to format and validate the dataset before submission; missing a required item (e.g., action taken date) is flagged as an exception requiring remediation.
Institution retains compiled records in a system that supports exam retrieval by application unique identifier.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential civil money penalties for incomplete or inaccurate Section 1071 compilation/maintenance

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 35_003 enumerates mandatory 1071 data points and rejects incremental implementation; that capability is necessary to satisfy the broader dataset compilation mandate in 28_003.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the MIS cannot capture required adopted data points, the institution cannot compile a compliant register, creating reporting violations and remediation risk.

dependencies.rationale: 28_003 requires compiling and maintaining prescribed data points; 34_009 ensures the data architecture avoids direct natural-person identifiers, reducing risk that compilation inadvertently contains prohibited PII.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If systems are not designed with PII minimization, compilation may repeatedly introduce prohibited fields, increasing ongoing remediation workload and compliance risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 unique identifier must start with the institution’s LEI and be unique per covered application
business_rules.description: For each covered application, the compiled dataset must include a unique identifier that is an alphanumeric identifier starting with the financial institution’s legal entity identifier (LEI), unique within the financial institution to the specific covered application, and usable to identify and retrieve the specific file(s) corresponding to the application for or extension of credit. MIS must generate, store, and enforce uniqueness of the identifier per covered application.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to each covered application under Section 1071; unique identifier must be associated to the application record and linked file repository.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: supports traceability, audit retrieval, and error correction. If violated (non-unique, missing LEI prefix, or not retrievable): creates data integrity and recordkeeping deficiencies.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.107(a)(1) Unique identifier
source_reference.source_text: Unique identifier. An alphanumeric identifier, starting with the legal entity identifier of the financial institution, unique within the financial institution to the specific covered application, and which can be used to identify and retrieve the specific file or files corresponding to the application for or extension of credit.
