examples: Institution LEI is 5493001KJTIIGC8Y1R12; a valid unique identifier begins with that LEI and then appends an internal application key (e.g., LEI + '-2026-000123').
If two covered applications share the same unique identifier due to a system defect, the institution must remediate because it cannot reliably retrieve the correct file(s) for an exam.
If the identifier does not start with the LEI, the institution’s Section 1071 dataset is nonconforming even if the internal key is otherwise unique.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism for recordkeeping and data integrity weaknesses; required remediation and potential resubmission

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 28_003 requires compiling prescribed fields for each covered application; 28_004 specifies the unique identifier rules that operationalize traceability within that dataset.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without a compliant unique identifier, records cannot be reliably retrieved or audited, creating recordkeeping and data integrity deficiencies.

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 action taken must be one of five enumerated outcomes with an action taken date
business_rules.description: For each covered application, the compiled dataset must include (1) the action taken by the financial institution reported as one of: originated, approved but not accepted, denied, withdrawn by the applicant, or incomplete; and (2) the action taken date, which is the date of the action taken by the financial institution. MIS must restrict action taken values to the enumerated set and record the corresponding action taken date.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to each covered application under Section 1071 when the institution takes final action or otherwise records an action outcome; action and date must be captured.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: compliant disposition reporting. If violated: misreporting of outcomes and dates leading to inaccurate reporting, fair lending analytics distortion, and regulatory criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.107(a)(9)-(10) Action taken; Action taken date
source_reference.source_text: Action taken. The action taken by the financial institution on the covered application, reported as originated, approved but not accepted, denied, withdrawn by the applicant, or incomplete. Action taken date. The date of the action taken by the financial institution.

examples: Application is approved but applicant declines the offer; action taken = 'approved but not accepted' and action taken date = date of that approval/decision record.
Application is denied; action taken = 'denied' and the date the denial decision is made is recorded as the action taken date.
Applicant withdraws before underwriting completes; action taken = 'withdrawn by the applicant' with the withdrawal date recorded.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and required data correction for inaccurate action taken reporting

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 pricing information must include interest rate structure and enumerated fee and penalty fields
business_rules.description: For a covered credit transaction that is originated or approved but not accepted (as applicable), the institution must compile pricing information including: (i) interest rate information—if fixed, the interest rate; if adjustable, the margin, index value, initial rate period in months (if applicable), and index name; (ii) total origination charges in dollars; (iii) broker fees in dollars (subset of total origination charges); (iv) initial annual charges in dollars; (v) for merchant cash advances or other sales-based financing, the additional cost (amount to be repaid minus amount advanced) in dollars; and (vi) prepayment penalties—(A) whether the institution could have included a prepayment charge under applicable policies and procedures, and (B) whether the terms in fact include a prepayment charge.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when compiling Section 1071 data for originated covered credit transactions and covered applications that are approved but not accepted where pricing fields are applicable; interest rate fields depend on fixed vs adjustable structure; MCA/sales-based field applies only to those products.
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: complete pricing dataset for regulatory reporting and analysis. If violated: pricing misreporting may lead to supervisory findings, resubmission requirements, and heightened fair lending scrutiny.
business_rules.exceptions: MCA/sales-based additional cost applies only to those transaction types; initial rate period expressed in months is 'if applicable'; prepayment penalty 'could have included' is reported notwithstanding whether actually included.

source_reference.section_id: § 1002.107(a)(12)(i)-(vi) Pricing information
source_reference.source_text: Pricing information. The following information regarding the pricing of a covered credit transaction that is originated or approved but not accepted... (i) Interest rate... If the interest rate is fixed... If the interest rate is adjustable, the margin, index value, initial rate period expressed in months... and index name... (ii) Total origination charges... expressed in dollars; (iii) Broker fees... expressed in dollars; ... (v) Additional cost for merchant cash advances... the difference between the amount advanced and the amount to be repaid... (vi) Prepayment penalties... whether the financial institution could have included... and ... whether the terms ... do in fact include a charge

examples: Adjustable-rate line: margin 3.00%, index name SOFR, index value 4.50%, initial rate period 12 months; total origination charges $2,500; broker fees $1,000; initial annual charges $300; prepayment penalty could-have-included = Yes, actually-included = No.
Fixed-rate term loan approved but not accepted: interest rate 9.25%; total origination charges $1,200; broker fees $0; initial annual charges $0; prepayment penalty fields populated as required.
Merchant cash advance: amount advanced $100,000; amount to be repaid $135,000; additional cost reported as $35,000.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential civil money penalties for inaccurate pricing reporting; required correction/resubmission

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 principal owner ethnicity, race, and sex must be reported as provided, declined, or not provided without verification and with specific conflict handling
business_rules.description: For principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex under § 1002.107(a)(19): (1) if the applicant does not provide the requested information for a principal owner, report the field(s) as 'not provided by applicant'; (2) if the applicant declines by selecting 'I do not wish to provide this information' (or similar) on a form, or orally declines via telephone/other medium, report that the applicant did not wish to provide the information; (3) if the applicant provides both a substantive response and also checks 'I do not wish,' report the substantive information provided; and (4) notwithstanding verification or other sources, the institution must report the applicant’s substantive responses, decline, or nonresponse and is not to substitute verified information.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when compiling Section 1071 covered application data that includes principal owners’ demographic fields; applies regardless of collection channel (paper, electronic, oral).
business_rules.consequences: If satisfied: compliant reporting of applicant-provided demographic information and nonresponse handling. If violated (e.g., institution overwrites with verified info or miscodes nonresponse): creates reporting violations and fair lending data integrity issues.
business_rules.exceptions: Guarantors are excluded unless also a principal owner (not addressed in this excerpt’s rule text, but noted in the same commentary section); reporting for fewer than four principal owners uses 'not applicable' for unused owner slots (not included in this rule to keep scope focused).

source_reference.section_id: Commentary 107(a)(19)-6 through -9 (selected)
source_reference.source_text: if an applicant does not provide the information... the financial institution reports the ethnicity, race, or sex (as applicable) as “not provided by applicant”... A financial institution reports that the applicant responded that it did not wish to provide the information... if the applicant orally declines... If the applicant both provides a substantive response... and also checks... “I do not wish to provide this information”... the financial institution reports the information... provided by the applicant... Notwithstanding... a financial institution must report the applicant’s substantive responses... even if the financial institution verifies or otherwise obtains the ethnicity, race, or sex

examples: Applicant indicates two principal owners; completes demographics for owner 1 but leaves owner 2 blank; institution reports owner 2 ethnicity/race/sex as 'not provided by applicant'.
Telephone application: applicant declines to provide race for the principal owner; institution records 'I do not wish to provide this information' for race (not 'not provided').
Paper form: applicant writes 'female' and also checks 'I do not wish'; institution reports sex as female (substantive response controls).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential civil money penalties for inaccurate demographic reporting; required data correction/resubmission

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: To correctly report 'not provided' vs 'I do not wish to provide' outcomes, the system must allow the enumerated codes and avoid forcing verification—requirements established in 35_004.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If response options are not supported, the institution may miscode demographic outcomes, creating 1071 reporting violations and fair lending analytics distortion.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) International Trade delegated PLP lenders may use non-delegated submission only for same-institution debt refinance or non-first-lien collateral
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans: Lenders without delegated PLP authority must submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures. Lenders with delegated PLP authority may submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for approval under non-delegated procedures only when (1) the loan will refinance the lender’s same institution debt, or (2) the collateral securing the loan will not be in first lien position. Except for those cases, delegated PLP lenders must process all loan applications using their delegated authority. Requests for exceptions to policy must be sent to 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov with specified information; if approved, the lender must retain the approval email in the loan file and document that the decision applies only to the specific request.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) International Trade loan applications when the lender is determining processing method (delegated vs non-delegated) and submitting through E-Tran.
business_rules.consequences: If a delegated PLP lender improperly submits a non-delegated application outside the two permitted scenarios, the submission may be deemed procedurally noncompliant, causing SBA processing delays, re-submission requirements, or heightened scrutiny. If an exception approval email is not retained, the lender risks a documentation deficiency for SBA program compliance.
business_rules.exceptions: Non-delegated submission is permitted for delegated PLP lenders only in the two stated circumstances. Policy exceptions are case-by-case and apply only to the specific request; general policy questions must be sent to 7aQuestions@sba.gov (not the delegated submissions inbox).

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for International Trade
source_reference.source_text: Lenders that do not have delegated PLP authority: Must submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for SBA approval under non-delegated procedures. Lenders with delegated PLP authority: May only submit applications to SBA via E-Tran for approval under non-delegated procedures when the loan will refinance the Lender’s same institution debt or if the collateral securing the loan will not be in first lien position. Except for the above, must process all loan applications using their delegated authority... If SBA approves the exception... the Lender must retain the approval email in the loan file.

examples: Delegated PLP lender submits non-delegated because the IT loan refinances the same bank’s existing debt; permitted.
Delegated PLP lender submits non-delegated for a standard first-lien collateral IT loan that does not refinance same-institution debt; not permitted and must be processed under delegated authority.
Lender obtains SBA exception approval via email but fails to retain the email in the file; documentation noncompliance risk during SBA review.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delay, requirement to re-process under correct authority, or program compliance findings

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: IT loans are a 7(a) product; the passive-business ineligibility screen must be cleared before choosing an SBA processing method and submitting via E‑Tran.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If passive-business ineligibility is present, the loan cannot be submitted for SBA guaranty; proceeding risks guaranty denial/repair and program findings.

dependencies.rationale: Whether delegated or non-delegated, IT submissions require a complete, current credit file; stale/missing owner/interim financials undermine compliant E‑Tran submission and SBA approval processing.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If statements are missing/stale, the lender risks SBA processing delays, resubmission requests, and adverse program compliance findings.

dependencies.rationale: 31_008 governs how the IT loan is submitted/processed in E‑Tran; 26_008 defines the ownership listing completeness rules that must be satisfied to make a compliant submission record.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Incorrect/incomplete ownership (not totaling 100% or failing spouse/minor-child aggregation) can trigger SBA delays, corrections, and potential lender oversight criticism.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) International Trade submissions require owner financials and interim statements dated within 120 days and apply to 20% or greater owners
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) International Trade (IT) loans, the lender must obtain and retain an Owner Financial Statement (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children) and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors. Interim financial statements must also be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to IT loan applications being submitted/processed for SBA guaranty, including PLP delegated processing where lender retains required documentation.
business_rules.consequences: If financial statements are stale (older than 120 days) or missing for required owners/guarantors, the lender’s application file is deficient and may lead to SBA processing delays, requests for additional information, or adverse program compliance findings.
business_rules.exceptions: Supplemental Guarantors are excluded from the owner financial statement requirement. “Owners of 20% or more” includes spouse and minor children assets aggregation as specified.

source_reference.section_id: Contents of Lender’s Application for Guaranty > Owner Financial Statement; Interim financial statements
source_reference.source_text: Owner Financial Statement (business or personal, as appropriate) dated within 120 days of submission to SBA, for all owners of 20% or more (including the assets of the owner’s spouse and minor children), and proposed guarantors, except Supplemental Guarantors... Interim financial statements must be dated within 120 days prior to submission to SBA.

examples: Borrower has three owners at 25%, 25%, and 50%: each must provide an Owner Financial Statement dated within 120 days; missing one owner’s statement is noncompliant.
Owner financial statement dated 150 days before submission: must be updated to within 120 days before SBA submission.
A proposed supplemental guarantor is included; lender does not collect an Owner Financial Statement for that person and remains compliant with this specific requirement.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA processing delay or file deficiency cited in SBA program review

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: CFPB 1071 requires annual reporting by final action year and requires reporting even if originated credit is later sold
business_rules.description: Under CFPB Regulation B small business lending data collection (12 CFR 1002.107(a) and related commentary), a covered financial institution must: (i) report the enumerated data even if the credit originated pursuant to the reported application was subsequently sold by the institution; (ii) annually report data for covered applications for which final action was taken in the previous calendar year; and (iii) annually report data for a covered application on the institution’s small business lending application register for the calendar year during which final action was taken, even if the institution received the application in a previous calendar year. MIS must therefore capture final action dates and ensure register assignment follows final action year logic.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions subject to CFPB 1071 reporting for covered applications from small businesses and when compiling/maintaining the small business lending application register for annual reporting.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution assigns applications to the wrong calendar year (e.g., by application receipt year rather than final action year) or omits sold-originations, the annual register will be inaccurate/incomplete, increasing risk of supervisory findings, required resubmission/correction, and potential enforcement exposure for reporting violations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited commentary for these operational instructions.

source_reference.section_id: Final Rule > Comment 107(a)-1 operational reporting instructions
source_reference.source_text: Comment 107(a)-1 explains that a covered financial institution (i) reports the data enumerated in § 1002.107(a) even if the credit originated pursuant to the reported application was subsequently sold by the institution; (ii) annually reports data for covered applications for which final action was taken in the previous calendar year; and (iii) annually reports data for a covered application on its small business lending application register for the calendar year during which final action was taken on the application, even if the institution received the application in a previous calendar year.

examples: Application received December 2025, final action taken January 2026: it must be reported on the 2026 register (final action year), not 2025.
Loan originated from a covered application and later sold: the institution must still report the application data for that covered application.

Final action taken November 2026: it is included in the annual report for the previous calendar year when reporting is performed (i.e., included in the dataset covering the calendar year in which final action occurred).
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory finding requiring correction/resubmission; potential civil money penalties for persistent or material reporting violations

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 31_010 requires annual reporting of covered applications for which final action was taken in the prior calendar year; 28_005 defines the required action-taken field values and the action-taken date needed to assign records to the correct reporting year.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If action taken or action-taken date is missing/incorrect, the institution will mis-assign records to years, omit required records, and face resubmission/enforcement risk.

dependencies.rationale: 31_010 imposes annual reporting requirements; 28_003 defines the underlying compilation requirement (items 1–20 per FIG) needed to produce an accurate register for submission.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If compilation is incomplete/nonconforming, annual reporting will be inaccurate, creating regulatory reporting violations and remediation burden.

dependencies.rationale: 31_010 requires annual reporting; 35_005 clarifies that the Bureau is not adopting a semiannual requirement, shaping compliant scheduling and process design for the annual register.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If institutions design around an incorrect cadence, they may misallocate resources, mis-time controls, or misunderstand submission expectations (even if internal monitoring is more frequent).

dependencies.rationale: 31_010 requires annual reporting of the application register; 33_008 governs the allowed contents of the register submission and retained inspection copy, prohibiting direct PII.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If PII is included in the annual register or retained copies, the institution faces privacy violations, examination criticism, and potential enforcement exposure.

business_rules.rule_name: 1071 firewall exception applies only per employee or officer and requires notice when access to protected demographic information cannot feasibly be limited
business_rules.description: Under § 1002.108(c), the firewall prohibition in § 1002.108(b) does not apply to a specific employee or officer involved in making determinations on covered applications if (1) the financial institution determines it is not feasible to limit that employee’s/officer’s access to protected demographic information (responses under § 1002.107(a)(18) or (19)); and (2) the institution provides the notice required under § 1002.108(d) to the applicant. A determination that an employee/officer should have access constitutes the feasibility determination for that individual, and the exception must not be applied institution-wide; access must be limited only to those determined individuals or determined groups.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when configuring system role-based access and workflows involving protected demographic information for covered applications from small businesses and when certain staff involved in credit determinations may need access.
business_rules.consequences: If MIS access controls permit broad access by determination-makers without an individual/group feasibility determination and applicant notice, the institution may violate the firewall requirements, creating elevated fair lending and privacy compliance risk and supervisory criticism.
business_rules.exceptions: Exception is allowed only for the specific employee/officer (or group) the institution has determined should have access and only if the applicant notice under § 1002.108(d) is provided.

source_reference.section_id: 12 CFR 1002.108(c) and related commentary (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.108(c) establishes an exception to the prohibition in final § 1002.108(b) and states that the prohibition does not apply to an employee or officer if the financial institution determines that it is not feasible to limit that employee’s or officer’s access ... and the financial institution provides the notice required under final § 1002.108(d) ... the exception (i.e., that it applies on an individual employee or officer basis, not an institution-wide basis)

examples: A small lender cannot segregate demographic responses in its LOS for one underwriter role; it documents that specific underwriter group needs access and provides the § 1002.108(d) applicant notice: exception can apply for that group only.

A lender determines one credit officer must see demographic data for operational reasons; it cannot then allow all credit officers access unless each officer (or a defined group) is covered by a documented determination and notice.
A lender can technically restrict access through user roles; it must implement the firewall and may not rely on the exception merely because implementing controls is inconvenient.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential enforcement action for firewall and notice noncompliance

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 33_007 addresses access by determination-makers to protected demographic responses under 1002.107(a)(18)-(19); 28_008 governs compilation of those demographic fields and therefore is the functional trigger for firewall controls.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If demographic data is collected without implementing firewall/exception controls, the institution risks direct violations of 1002.108 requirements and supervisory criticism.

dependencies.rationale: 33_007 permits an exception when limiting access is not feasible; 36_002 defines how to determine which employees/officers should have access based on potential need for job duties (not actual use).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 'should have access' is interpreted too narrowly, the institution may implement inconsistent/invalid exception determinations and weaken firewall compliance.

dependencies.rationale: 33_007 requires an institution determination regarding feasibility/access; 36_003 allows designating all employees in the same job title as having access when duties may require it, enabling scalable governance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without role-based access logic, institutions may implement ad hoc exceptions that are hard to audit and prone to over/under-granting access.

dependencies.rationale: 33_007’s exception hinges on infeasibility; 36_004 clarifies permissible factors (size, staffing, systems, processes) and that institutions need not hire staff solely for this determination.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If unlawful/incorrect factors are used (or institutions believe staffing changes are mandatory), feasibility determinations may be unsound and invite supervisory criticism.

business_rules.rule_name: Small business lending application register and related retained records must exclude direct personally identifiable information except as specifically required
business_rules.description: When reporting the small business lending application register under § 1002.109, maintaining the register copy under § 1002.111(a), and maintaining the separate protected demographic information record under § 1002.111(b), the institution must not include direct PII such as name, specific address (other than census tract), telephone number, email address, or any other personally identifiable information concerning any individual who is or is connected with an applicant, except as required by § 1002.107 or § 1002.111(b). The prohibition is limited to the register submitted to the Bureau, the retained copy for inspection, and the separate demographic record; it does not prohibit PII in the application or other loan files generally.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to the MIS extract/transform/load logic and data retention for 1071 reporting files and separately maintained protected demographic information records.
business_rules.consequences: Including prohibited PII in the register submission or retained inspection copy can be a direct violation of ECOA section 704B(e)(3) as implemented by § 1002.111(c), elevating privacy risk, examination criticism, and potential enforcement exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: PII may be maintained in the institution’s application or other files; the prohibition targets the submitted register, its retained copy, and the separately maintained demographic information record, subject to limited ‘as required’ carve-outs in § 1002.107 and § 1002.111(b).

source_reference.section_id: 12 CFR 1002.111(c) (Final Rule discussion)
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.111(c) ... provides that ... a financial institution may not include ... the name, specific address (other than the census tract), telephone number, electronic mail address, or any other PII ... The Bureau ... does not interpret ... as prohibiting a financial institution from including PII in its application or other files, but only the small business lending application register submitted ... the copy ... retained for inspection, and the separately maintained record of protected demographic information

examples: 1071 register file includes applicant principal owner’s name and phone number: prohibited; must be removed from the register submission and retained inspection copy.

Institution retains protected demographic information record containing a full street address of a principal owner: prohibited unless specifically required; keep only allowable fields and link via non-PII identifier.
Loan file contains borrower contact name and email for servicing: allowed in the loan file, but not in the 1071 register submission/copy.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and potential enforcement action for PII inclusion and recordkeeping violations

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 34_009 drives upstream data-model design to avoid direct natural-person identifiers; 33_008 prohibits including direct PII in the 1071 register submission and retained copies (with limited exceptions).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If direct identifiers are captured and flow into reporting datasets, the institution may commit a direct regulatory violation and elevate privacy/re-identification risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Credit product must be compiled and maintained for the product applied for or originated under Section 1071
business_rules.description: For Section 1071 reporting, the financial institution must compile and maintain a data field for the credit product applied for or originated. The system must be able to capture the credit product applied for and, when the application results in an extension of credit, treat the type of credit “applied for” as including the type of credit actually originated.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications and covered originations subject to Section 1071. The MIS/LOS must capture the credit product at application and/or origination such that it can be reported accurately.
business_rules.consequences: If the credit product is not captured, the institution’s Section 1071 application register will be incomplete and may be deemed noncompliant; data quality errors may occur in CFPB submissions.
business_rules.exceptions: Where the reporting regime provides flexibility when information is not available at processing, the institution should follow the official instructions; however, the core requirement remains to compile and maintain the data on credit product applied for or originated.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.107(a)(5)(i) Credit product
source_reference.source_text: Final § 1002.107(a)(5)(i) requires financial institutions to compile and maintain data on the credit product applied for or originated... the Bureau... interpret[s] type of credit “applied for” to include the type of credit actually originated when an application results in an extension of credit.

examples: Applicant requests an unsecured term loan; institution originates a secured term loan → MIS must report the originated credit product consistent with the rule’s interpretation of “applied for.”
Applicant requests a line of credit and is approved for a line of credit → MIS captures and reports that credit product for the application register line.
Applicant withdraws before origination → MIS still must capture the credit product applied for for reporting purposes if the application is covered and reportable.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding for incomplete/incorrect LAR data and potential enforcement for inaccurate reporting

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: When the credit product is not on the CFPB list, report credit product as Other and provide the specific product in free-form text
business_rules.description: When compiling and reporting the Section 1071 credit product field, the financial institution must select the credit product from the CFPB-provided list. If the product does not appear on the list, the institution selects “other” and reports the specific product using free-form text.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to Section 1071 covered applications when the product offered/requested is not represented in the standardized list in the official commentary instructions.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to use “other” plus free-form text for non-listed products can result in misclassification, data quality defects, and inaccurate reporting. The system must support both the enumerated list and a free-text field for “other.”
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; institution should follow any additional CFPB validation rules for free-form text if later specified.

source_reference.section_id: Final comment 107(a)(5)-1 (Other + free-form text)
source_reference.source_text: Comment 107(a)(5)-1... if the credit product for an application does not appear on the list... the financial institution selects “other” as the credit product and reports the specific product via free-form text.

examples: Applicant requests a specialized supply-chain finance product not on the list → select “other” and enter “supply-chain finance” in free-form text.
Applicant requests a bespoke receivables purchase facility not represented in the list → select “other” and record the specific facility name in text.
Institution mislabels a non-listed product as “secured term loan” instead of “other” → violates the instruction and reduces data quality.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory finding for systematic miscoding and inaccurate LAR reporting

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 34_006 creates the requirement to capture the credit product applied for/originated; 34_007 defines the standardized coding rule (list selection vs other + free text).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the product is not captured at intake/origination, the institution cannot correctly classify products for reporting, leading to register defects and resubmission risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Do not collect direct natural person identifiers such as name, address, date of birth, or Social Security number for Section 1071 publication purposes
business_rules.description: For purposes discussed in the CFPB’s Section 1071 privacy assessment, information that directly identifies natural persons—such as name, address, date of birth, or Social Security number—would not be collected. Data systems supporting Section 1071 should be designed to avoid capturing these direct natural-person identifiers as Section 1071 reportable fields and to prevent inclusion in public disclosure datasets.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to design and configuration of Section 1071 data collection/reporting systems and disclosure outputs where the institution or the Bureau prepares application-level data for publication or transmission.
business_rules.consequences: If direct identifiers are collected or propagated into Section 1071 datasets, it increases privacy and re-identification risk and may trigger regulatory criticism, remediation requirements, and potential consumer harm considerations.
business_rules.exceptions: Institutions may still collect identifying information for non-1071 purposes (e.g., CIP/KYC) in other systems/fields; the control objective is to prevent such identifiers from being treated as Section 1071 collected/published data fields.

source_reference.section_id: VIII.B.4.i Proposed Approach (privacy risk)
source_reference.source_text: while information that directly identifies natural persons, such as name, address, date of birth, or Social Security number would not be collected, publication of application-level data in an unmodified format potentially could be used to re-identify small business applicants and related natural persons...

examples: LOS stores SSN for CIP in a customer-identification module → Section 1071 extraction logic excludes SSN from the Section 1071 dataset.
Analyst attempts to include business owner home address in Section 1071 data feed → control blocks inclusion as a direct identifier.
Vendor build includes a free-text field that could capture DOB → institution enforces validation to prevent DOB from being included in Section 1071 reporting payloads.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism and required corrective action for privacy-control weaknesses; potential enforcement if disclosure practices violate applicable law or CFPB directives

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 requires collection and reporting of specified additional data points including pricing and denial reasons without phased implementation
business_rules.description: The MIS and related data collection processes must support compilation of the final rule’s adopted data points under ECOA section 704B(e)(2)(H): pricing, time in business, NAICS code, number of workers, application method, application recipient, denial reasons, and number of principal owners. The Bureau did not adopt an incremental or phased-in approach for these data points; they should be collected and reported together as soon as possible in accordance with the final rule’s compliance framework.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution is a covered financial institution and is compiling reportable data under § 1002.107 for covered applications/covered credit transactions within the scope of ECOA section 1071 / Regulation B subpart B.
business_rules.consequences: MIS must be capable of capturing, storing, and outputting these data fields for Section 1071 reporting; incomplete system build-outs that omit these required fields create reporting gaps and potential noncompliance.
business_rules.exceptions: The Bureau is not adopting certain suggested additional data points (e.g., credit score, applicant’s disability status, business structure) as part of this final rule; therefore MIS is not required to capture those as 1071 reportable data points solely for 1071.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.107(a) > Issues regarding data points adopted pursuant to ECOA section 704B(e)(2)(H)
source_reference.source_text: Pursuant to its statutory authority set forth in ECOA section 704B(e)(2)(H), the Bureau is adopting data points for pricing, time in business, NAICS code, number of workers, application method, application recipient, denial reasons, and number of principal owners... The Bureau did not choose to take an incremental approach to adding data points... or permit collecting and reporting of certain data points to be phased in over time.

examples: A covered lender’s LOS captures pricing (rate/APR-equivalent fields as defined), NAICS code, and denial reasons at disposition. Result: can populate Section 1071 submissions.

A lender builds systems to collect only statutory core fields but omits application method and number of principal owners. Result: noncompliance risk because these are adopted data points under 704B(e)(2)(H).
A lender chooses to collect credit score for internal underwriting; result: permitted internally, but not required as a Section 1071 data point based on this excerpt.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and potential ECOA/Regulation B enforcement action (including civil money penalties) for missing required Section 1071 fields

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 compilation permits certain response options and does not require verification of reported data points
business_rules.description: For data points in the Section 1071 final rule, the institution’s compilation process is not required to verify applicant-provided information and must support reporting response options when appropriate, including “I do not wish to respond” (or similar), “not applicable,” and “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined.” The final rule also provides safe harbors intended to facilitate compliance and reduce costs.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when collecting and reporting Section 1071 data points under Regulation B subpart B where the data point is applicant-provided or otherwise may be unavailable or inapplicable in a specific transaction/application context.
business_rules.consequences: MIS and procedures must (i) allow storage/reporting of the enumerated non-response/inapplicability codes, and (ii) avoid imposing a system rule requiring verification as a precondition to reporting; failing to support permitted response codes can force inaccurate reporting or operational breakdowns.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the excerpt; specific safe harbor conditions and which fields allow which responses may vary by data point and should be cross-validated against the final regulatory text and associated commentary.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.107(a) > Issues regarding data points adopted pursuant to ECOA section 704B(e)(2)(H)
source_reference.source_text: In addition, the final rule does not require verification, allows for responses of “I do not wish to respond” or similar, “not applicable,” and “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined” when appropriate, and provides several safe harbors to facilitate compliance and reduce costs in the compilation and reporting of the data points.

examples: Applicant refuses to provide number of principal owners. Result: lender reports “I do not wish to respond” for that field (where applicable).
A data point is not relevant to the application type. Result: report “not applicable” rather than forcing a default value.
Applicant does not provide time in business and lender cannot determine from file. Result: report “not provided by applicant and otherwise undetermined.”
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and potential ECOA/Regulation B enforcement action for inaccurate compilation/reporting practices

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Section 1071 does not impose semiannual reporting submissions and contemplates annual submission cadence
business_rules.description: The Bureau is not adopting a 6-month reporting requirement for small business lending data submissions because multiple submissions per year may be administratively challenging for financial institutions and the Bureau to process. This indicates that covered institutions should not design required 1071 submission processes around a mandated semiannual cadence; submission cadence follows the final rule’s annual reporting framework (while the Bureau may consider publishing aggregate data more often than once a year in the future).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered financial institutions planning Section 1071 data submission operations and system reporting schedules.
business_rules.consequences: Institutions should implement annual submission workflows as required, and should not treat semiannual submissions as mandatory under the final rule based on this commentary; however, institutions may choose more frequent internal monitoring.
business_rules.exceptions: The Bureau may consider publishing aggregate data more often than once a year in the future if administratively feasible; this does not create a current obligation for institutions to submit every six months.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > 1002.114 > Requests to publish data quickly and frequently
source_reference.source_text: Regarding the request to publish data as soon as possible, and to publish data every six months so that stakeholders can monitor progress and utilize data, the Bureau is not adopting a 6-month reporting requirement because requiring financial institutions to provide multiple data submissions a year may be administratively challenging...

examples: A covered institution configures its Section 1071 submission controls for one annual submission. Result: aligned with the Bureau’s decision not to adopt a 6-month requirement.

A vendor proposes a mandatory semiannual submission module as a compliance requirement. Result: treat as not required based on the final rule commentary (annual submission remains required elsewhere in the rule).
A bank chooses to run internal quarterly 1071 data quality checks. Result: allowed as internal control even though semiannual submissions are not required.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism if the institution fails to meet actual required submission deadlines (annual cadence) due to misdesigned reporting schedules

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Protected demographic responses must be maintained separate from the application and collected on a form limited to specified 1071 data elements
business_rules.description: The institution must maintain the record of an applicant’s responses regarding principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex (and related protected demographic inquiries) separate from the application and accompanying information. If the institution provides a paper or electronic data collection form, the form must not be part of the application form or any other document used to collect or provide information other than the specified protected demographic and related data elements (minority-owned/women-owned/LGBTQI+-owned business status, principal owners’ ethnicity/race/sex, and number of principal owners). If the form is sent via email, it should be provided as a separate attachment or accessed through a separate mechanism, consistent with separation requirements and firewall implementation.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to covered applications where demographic data are requested under 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(18) and/or (a)(19). Applies to recordkeeping and system design under 12 CFR 1002.111(b).
business_rules.consequences: If demographic response records are commingled with the application file or included in general underwriting documents, the institution may violate the separation requirement and increase risk of improper access by underwriters, resulting in supervisory findings and mandated remediation (including reconfiguration of systems, forms, and access controls).
business_rules.exceptions: The cited text indicates flexibility: separation does not always require separate physical/digital files in all instances; implementation may vary depending on how the institution complies with firewall requirements. (Details on firewall feasibility are discussed elsewhere in 1002.108/111 commentary.)

source_reference.section_id: Commentary to 1002.107(a)(19); comment 5
source_reference.source_text: A financial institution must maintain the record of an applicant’s response... separate from the application and accompanying information... If the financial institution provides a paper or electronic data collection form, the data collection form must not be part of the application form or any other document... For example, if the financial institution sends the data collection form via email, the data collection form should be a separate attachment...

examples: LOS stores demographic responses in a segregated table with separate permissions; underwriters cannot view it; compliant.

Lender embeds demographic questions inside the main application PDF that also requests financial statements and collateral details; noncompliant because the data collection form is part of the application form.
Email workflow: lender sends underwriting checklist in the email body but attaches the demographic form as a separate file; compliant with the example approach.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and mandated system/control remediation; potential enforcement for failure to maintain required separation

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 37_007 establishes when 1071 applies. 37_005 is a recordkeeping/system-design rule requiring demographic responses to be maintained separately from the application and accompanying information.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If covered-application triggering is not consistently recognized across channels, the institution will commingle protected demographic responses with credit files, raising supervisory and fair lending exposure.

dependencies.rationale: 37_008 makes incomplete covered applications reportable; 37_005 requires separated maintenance of demographic responses as part of compliant recordkeeping.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If incomplete/withdrawn applications are stored only as intake artifacts and not subject to the separation rule, commingling occurs and reportability may be lost.

dependencies.rationale: 37_009 affects whether those online records are 'covered application' records. If yes, 37_005’s separated maintenance requirement applies to those records as well.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Institutions may fail to retain separated demographic records for abandoned applications that were nonetheless evaluated, causing commingling and underreporting.

dependencies.rationale: 37_005 requires maintaining demographic responses separate from the application and accompanying information. 39_006 specifies that data collection processes/systems must support keeping responses separate and controlling access to decisioning personnel.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Without firewall-capable systems/processes, the institution will likely commingle data in LOS, document repositories, or CRM notes, leading to exam findings and remediation.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 30_010 requires a covered institution to begin maintaining records under §1002.111 by its compliance date. Rule 37_005 specifies a key recordkeeping/system design requirement: demographic responses must be maintained separate from the application and not embedded in other underwriting documents.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If recordkeeping begins without separation controls, the institution risks firewall/separation violations, improper underwriter access, and supervisory findings requiring system remediation.

business_rules.rule_name: Use of the CFPB Appendix E sample data collection form is optional and not required by the rule
business_rules.description: The institution is not required to use CFPB’s Appendix E sample data collection form. Appendix E is finalized as a compliance resource to help institutions collect applicant-provided protected demographic information and maintain required separation (firewall) of responses; institutions may choose to use it to facilitate compliance, but alternative formats are permitted.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when designing or implementing applicant data collection tools (paper, electronic, or oral scripting) for 1071 protected demographic information collection under Subpart B.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution uses the sample form, it can facilitate consistent collection and required notices. If it does not use the form, it must still ensure its own tools and processes satisfy the underlying data collection, notice, and separation requirements; failure may lead to compliance gaps and supervisory findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None; optionality applies broadly (the form itself is not mandated).

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > Appendix E to Part 1002 > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: the rule does not require use of the form itself. Rather, the sample form is intended as a compliance resource for lenders who choose to use it.

examples: A bank uses a custom online portal instead of Appendix E; it must still provide required notices and maintain firewall separation, even though it did not use the sample form.
A fintech adopts Appendix E verbatim for paper applications to streamline staff training; this is permitted but not required.
A lender uses Appendix E only as a script for oral applications while keeping an internal digital record of responses; use is optional and must still satisfy underlying requirements.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism if alternative tools fail to meet underlying 1071 data collection, notice, and firewall requirements

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

business_rules.rule_name: Applicant protected demographic responses must be kept separate from the credit application and include right-to-refuse, firewall, and non-discrimination notices
business_rules.description: When collecting applicants’ protected demographic information for 1071 purposes, the institution’s data collection process and systems must support keeping an applicant’s responses separately from the credit application and accompanying information (firewall). The sample form’s introductory language includes right-to-refuse, firewall, and non-discrimination notices designed to facilitate compliance with these requirements.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution collects applicants’ protected demographic information (e.g., principal owners’ ethnicity, race, sex/gender) under Subpart B and stores it in systems accessible to personnel involved in credit decisioning or underwriting.
business_rules.consequences: If responses are not kept separate or notices are not provided, the institution risks violating 1071/Reg B firewall and notice requirements, creating fair lending and compliance exposure, and may face supervisory criticism and mandated corrective actions.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > Appendix E to Part 1002 > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: appendix E to help financial institutions comply with requirements to collect applicants’ protected demographic information and to keep an applicant’s responses to inquiries for such information separately from the credit application and accompanying information. The introductory language in the sample form includes right to refuse, firewall, and non-discrimination notices.

examples: A lender’s LOS displays principal owner ethnicity/race/sex responses on the underwriter’s screen; the system must be redesigned to keep those responses separate consistent with the firewall requirement.

A bank uses an electronic intake form that collects protected demographic information; it must include right-to-refuse, firewall, and non-discrimination notices in the user flow and store responses in a segregated table or restricted-access module.
For oral applications, staff record protected demographic responses in a separate module not visible to the credit decision-maker, and deliver the required notices verbally.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory criticism for firewall violations; potential ECOA/Reg B enforcement requiring system/process remediation and lookback review

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: If 37_007 applies, then demographic data collection may occur via any channel; 39_006 requires systems/processes capable of keeping responses separate from the application/accompanying information (firewall).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If channel-specific workflows (e.g., oral intake) bypass firewall controls, demographic responses may be exposed to underwriters/decision-makers, creating 1071/Reg B firewall violations.

dependencies.rationale: Because 37_008 treats incomplete requests as covered applications, 39_006 firewall controls must still be applied to intake-stage records where incomplete applications often reside.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Institutions may mistakenly store demographic responses in 'pre-underwriting' folders/notes that are accessible to decision-makers, violating firewall requirements.

dependencies.rationale: 37_009 can cause covered-application status to attach earlier than 'submit.' 39_006 requires systems/processes to separate demographics from application/accompanying information; that requirement must be designed into the pre-submit workflow if the institution evaluates it.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Underwriters/decision tools may access demographic responses stored in the same pre-submit dataset, creating firewall violations.

dependencies.rationale: 39_006 addresses the collection process and firewall separation; 39_007 is a specific required statement that must be delivered when requesting the demographic data that must be firewalled.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the statement is omitted, the demographic collection process is incomplete and exposes the institution to Reg B/1071 notice violations even if technical data separation exists.

dependencies.rationale: 39_005 clarifies the form is not mandatory, but it is designed to facilitate the separation/firewall and notice requirements described in 39_006. Using it reduces implementation ambiguity and process variance.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution does not use the sample form, it must design equivalent tooling; poorly designed alternatives increase risk of firewall/control defects and inconsistent notices.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 30_010 requires covered institutions to comply with firewall requirements of §1002.108 by their applicable compliance date. Rule 39_006 details the operational/system requirement to keep protected demographic responses separate from the credit application and restrict access by decisioning personnel.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution begins compiling 1071 data without the firewall, underwriters may access protected demographic information, triggering Reg B Subpart B violations and heightened fair lending risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Applicants must be told that Federal law requires collection of principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex to ensure fair treatment and fulfill community credit needs
business_rules.description: When requesting principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex/gender information for 1071, the institution must inform the applicant that Federal law requires asking for this information to help ensure small business applicants for credit are treated fairly and that communities’ small business credit needs are being fulfilled. The CFPB includes sample language for this statement in Appendix E and commentary.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution requests applicant-provided protected demographic information for principal owners in connection with a covered application under 1071/Reg B.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution fails to provide the required informational statement, it may violate Reg B notice requirements and create applicant confusion, reduced response rates, and heightened supervisory and fair lending risk.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text.

source_reference.section_id: V. Section-by-Section Analysis > Appendix E to Part 1002 > Final Rule
source_reference.source_text: comments 107(a)(18)-4 and 107(a)(19)-4 provide that a financial institution must inform an applicant that Federal law requires it to ask for the principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex/gender to help ensure that all small business applicants for credit are treated fairly and that communities’ small business credit needs are being fulfilled.

examples: An online demographic module begins with: 'Federal law requires us to ask...' before the ethnicity/race/sex questions; this satisfies the required informational statement when properly delivered.
A lender collecting data by phone reads the required statement to the applicant before requesting principal owners’ ethnicity, race, and sex/gender.
A bank’s customized form omits the 'Federal law requires...' statement; the form must be revised to include the statement to mitigate Reg B compliance risk.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Supervisory criticism and required corrective action for deficient applicant notices under Reg B 1071 implementation

examples: MIS producing weekly concentration dashboards by property type, geography, and risk rating
System that aggregates loan and derivative-related credit exposure to the same borrower

dependencies.rationale: 37_007 establishes that 1071 requirements apply regardless of intake method once the covered-application trigger is met; 39_007 is one of the required notice elements tied to requesting demographic information for that covered application.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to recognize channel-agnostic coverage can lead to missing the required statement in phone/in-person channels, creating a Reg B/1071 notice violation.

dependencies.rationale: 37_008 clarifies that covered-application status (and 1071 obligations) can exist even without sufficient underwriting information. When demographic info is requested for these covered applications, 39_007 still applies.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution waits to provide the statement until the application is 'complete,' it risks missing required notices and underreporting incomplete/withdrawn applications.

dependencies.rationale: 37_009 governs whether partially-entered online information becomes a covered application based on institutional procedures and whether evaluation begins. If it is treated as a covered application and demographics are requested, 39_007 applies.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution evaluates pre-submit data but does not apply the 1071 notice framework, it can create unreported covered applications and missing required statements.

dependencies.rationale: 39_005 indicates Appendix E is a compliance resource; 39_007 requires informing the applicant why the information is requested. The sample form language is directly aligned to this notice requirement.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Custom forms/scripts may omit required language or materially alter it, increasing Reg B/1071 notice-compliance risk.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 30_010 triggers the start of 1071 data compilation for covered applications. Rule 39_007 defines a mandatory notice that must be provided when requesting principal owners’ protected demographic information during that data collection.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to provide the statement can constitute a notice violation under Reg B Subpart B and may reduce response rates and increase supervisory/fair lending risk.

business_rules.rule_name: FX maturity gap analysis must include all currency ledger accounts and reconcile to position sheet totals
business_rules.description: A maturity distribution (gap) schedule used to evaluate foreign currency positions must include all currency ledger accounts. Ledger accounts not bearing a maturity date must be placed in the first day’s maturities. Spot contracts must be shown based on the expected settlement date. The totals of assets and purchases (long), liabilities and sales (short), and the net amount must correspond to the foreign currency amounts shown in the position sheet. The net gap for each period must be compared to management-imposed limits, and the cumulative gap position must be reviewed for policy conformance and excessive positive or negative gap periods that may create exposure relative to liquidity, earnings, or capital.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when producing/using a maturity distribution (gap) analysis workpaper/report for each currency position.
business_rules.consequences: If totals do not reconcile to the position sheet or gaps are not compared to limits/cumulative policy conformance, the workpaper is deficient; excessive gaps may require examiner comment and may drive supervisory findings regarding liquidity/earnings/capital exposure.
business_rules.exceptions: At examiner discretion, material 'other currencies' may be detailed; otherwise focused on major currencies.

source_reference.section_id: MATURITY DISTRIBUTION (GAP) ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: The preparation of this schedule requires the inclusion of all currency ledger accounts. Show ledger accounts not bearing a maturity date in the first day’s maturities. Show spot contracts as of the date the settlement is expected to occur. The total of assets and purchases (long), liabilities and sales (short), and the net amount of these two columns should correspond to the foreign currency amounts shown in the position sheet. Compare the net gap for each period to limits imposed by institution management. Further, review the cumulative gap position ... for conformance to policy

examples: A JPY maturity distribution places a non-maturity demand deposit ledger balance into day-1 maturities and dates spot purchases to their settlement date; totals match the JPY position sheet.
A cumulative 1–3 month negative gap repeatedly breaches management limits; the report flags the breach and documents potential exposure relative to liquidity and capital.
If the long/short net totals differ from the position sheet, the institution must correct ledger account inclusion or contract dating before relying on the gap analysis.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: Quarterly board package showing construction concentration, policy exceptions, and stress test outcomes
Ad hoc report requested after rapid CRE origination growth in a single geography

business_rules.rule_name: FX maturity distribution workpaper normally omits revaluation P&L unless position creates material profit or loss
business_rules.description: Within the maturity distribution (gap) analysis workpaper, it is normally unnecessary to complete the profit and loss revaluation columns unless a position results in a material profit or loss. When materiality is present, the schedule may be used to calculate the future profit/loss adjustment in the revaluation and income/loss analysis worksheet.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when completing the maturity distribution (gap) analysis worksheet that includes optional right-side revaluation and income/loss columns.
business_rules.consequences: If material P&L exists and revaluation is omitted, the workpaper may be incomplete; if immaterial P&L exists, performing revaluation is optional and not expected.
business_rules.exceptions: Materiality determination is not quantified in the excerpt; institutions/examiners apply judgment based on the size of profit/loss relative to the institution.

source_reference.section_id: MATURITY DISTRIBUTION (GAP) ANALYSIS
source_reference.source_text: It is normally unnecessary to complete a profit and loss revaluation on this worksheet (in the right three columns) unless a position results in a material profit or loss. ... In any event, the schedule can be used as a workpaper to calculate the future profit/loss adjustment in the Revaluation and Income/Loss Analysis worksheet.

examples: An institution with small CAD exposure treats the revaluation P&L as immaterial and leaves the right-side columns blank.
A large EUR short position produces a material unrealized loss; the examiner completes the revaluation columns to quantify potential impact.
If a maturity distribution report shows significant P&L volatility but the revaluation columns are blank, examiners request completion and support.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Regulatory criticism at examination

examples: Quarterly board package showing construction concentration, policy exceptions, and stress test outcomes
Ad hoc report requested after rapid CRE origination growth in a single geography

dependencies.rationale: 23_003 states revaluation columns are generally unnecessary unless material P&L exists; when materiality is present, the schedule may be used to calculate future profit/loss adjustments. That calculation presupposes the 23_002 requirements: inclusion of all ledger accounts, correct bucket placement, settlement-date treatment, and reconciliation to the position sheet and limits.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the base maturity distribution is deficient, the institution may misstate FX exposure and any resulting revaluation/income analysis, increasing examiner criticism and risk-limit breaches.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA delegated lenders must request exceptions to policy by email with required details, retain the approval email, and then process under delegated authority
business_rules.description: A lender with delegated authority must request an exception to policy for any type of 7(a) loan by emailing 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov with detailed information including principal names, project address, the policy for which the exception is requested, and an explanation of why it should be granted. If SBA approves, the lender receives an approval email, must proceed to process the application under delegated authority, and must retain the approval email in the loan file. The exception decision is case-by-case and applies only to the specific request; the decision must be documented in the appropriate loan file. SBA will not review the entire case file or multiple attachments; the lender must provide a summary of facts.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a lender with delegated authority needs an exception to SOP/policy for a 7(a) loan application (including EWCP).
business_rules.consequences: Without SBA approval, the lender should not proceed as though an exception has been granted. If approval is granted, the lender must (1) process under delegated authority and (2) retain the approval email and document the decision in the loan file; failure risks SBA lender oversight findings and potential guaranty repair/denial implications.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; exceptions are evaluated case-by-case and are limited to the specific request.

source_reference.section_id: Submission of Application for Guaranty for EWCP
source_reference.source_text: Must make a request for an exception for any type of 7(a) loan to 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov, providing detailed information about the application including the names of the principals, address of the project, and a discussion of the policy for which the exception is being requested... If SBA approves the exception... the Lender must proceed to process the application under delegated authority. The Lender must retain the approval email in the loan file.

examples: A delegated 7(a) lender needs an exception to an SOP requirement for a specific EWCP request; it emails 7aDelegatedLoanApps@sba.gov with the principal names, project address, and exception rationale, and retains SBA’s approval email in the loan file.
A delegated lender receives SBA approval for an exception; it must still process the loan under delegated authority (not shift it to non-delegated solely because an exception was granted).
A lender requests an exception by sending multiple attachments and no summary; SBA response is delayed; the lender should instead provide a summary of facts as instructed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight finding; possible guaranty repair/denial exposure if exception handling is deficient

examples: Exception approving 78% LTV against a 70% standard due to additional collateral and recourse
Exception to minimum DSCR due to stabilized cash flow projection timing

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_004 directs lenders to email the delegated mailbox with detailed information to request an exception. Rule 26_003 establishes that the delegated mailbox is restricted to delegated lenders and should not be used for general policy questions—ensuring the exception request is properly routed and handled.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the lender misroutes the request (e.g., sends general questions to the delegated mailbox or sends exception content to the wrong mailbox), SBA response may be delayed, and exceptions may not be properly documented/approved.

dependencies.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_006 prohibits balloons and requires full amortization by maturity. If proposed terms cannot meet these constraints, the lender must either restructure or (if an exception path exists for a specific nuance) invoke BR_POLICY_EXCEPTION_DOCUMENTATION_26_004 to request SBA approval before proceeding under delegated authority.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If amortization is noncompliant and the lender neither restructures nor obtains an approved exception where applicable, SBA may cite a violation and pursue guaranty repair/denial.

dependencies.rationale: When BR_20_006’s deviation statement is unavailable, the lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing. For delegated lenders, BR_26_004 governs how to request and document such an exception/permission (email request, retain approval, document in file).
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_26_004 is not invoked/executed when BR_20_006 triggers the permission requirement, the lender risks an improper closing, program violation, and potential guaranty repair/denial.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) guaranty is obtained and maintained only if the lender pays required upfront and annual service fees to SBA
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) loans, the 7(a) Lender is responsible for payment to SBA of the SBA Guaranty Fee (Upfront Fee) and the 7(a) Lender’s Annual Service Fee in order to obtain and maintain the SBA guaranty (13 CFR § 120.220).
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loans where the lender extends credit with an SBA guaranty component.
business_rules.consequences: Failure to pay required SBA guaranty fees can result in cancellation of the SBA guaranty (per applicable timing rules) and/or SBA servicing findings; loss of guaranty protection increases loss severity and can impact capital and credit risk treatment.
business_rules.exceptions: Specific fee waivers or temporary fee changes may be announced by SBA via Information Notice (e.g., SBA Express upfront fee waiver for certain veteran-related categories).

source_reference.section_id: Fees the Lender Pays SBA (13 CFR § 120.220)
source_reference.source_text: SBA Guaranty Fee (also known as Upfront Fee) and the 7(a) Lender’s Annual Service Fee (also known as the SBA On-Going Guaranty Fee). (13 CFR § 120.220) The 7(a) Lender is responsible for payment to SBA of the Upfront Fee and the Lender’s Annual Service Fee in order to obtain and maintain the SBA guaranty.

examples: A lender closes an SBA 7(a) loan but does not pay the upfront fee by the applicable deadline; SBA cancels the guaranty, leaving the lender without SBA coverage.
A lender continues to service an SBA 7(a) loan but fails to remit the annual service fee via Form 1502; SBA may assess late fees and cite servicing deficiencies.
A lender applies a temporary SBA Information Notice fee waiver correctly for an eligible SBA Express veteran-owned business; this satisfies the fee obligation consistent with the waiver.
business_rules.enforcement_action: Loss/cancellation of SBA guaranty; SBA program compliance finding and potential denial/repair of guaranty purchase

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

dependencies.rationale: 19_011 involves submitting a guaranty request under delegated authority. 1_006 requires the lender to pay the upfront and annual service fees to obtain/maintain the SBA guaranty associated with that 7(a) loan.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Even if the delegated underwriting/structure is compliant, failure to pay required fees can result in loss/cancellation of the guaranty protection.

dependencies.rationale: 26_004 establishes the required SBA approval and file documentation for exceptions under delegated authority. If the exception is needed to originate/close the loan, then proceeding to fee payment under 1_006 before exception approval creates rework and guaranty eligibility uncertainty.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Paying fees before resolving exceptions can lead to processing errors, rework, and increased SBA oversight scrutiny if the exception was required for compliance.

dependencies.rationale: Rule 26_006 culminates in SBA approve/decline in E-Tran under non-delegated processing. Once a 7(a) EWCP loan is approved/numbered, Rule 1_006 governs fee responsibilities that are necessary to obtain and maintain guaranty protection.
