dependencies.impact_if_fails: If fees are not paid timely/accurately after approval, the SBA guaranty may be cancelled or compromised, increasing credit loss exposure and creating servicing/oversight findings.

dependencies.rationale: BR_1_006 requires the lender to pay upfront and annual service fees to obtain and maintain the SBA guaranty. BR_21_008 establishes a hard ineligibility condition for obtaining a new SBA 7(a) loan number if existing SBA debt is not current. Without eligibility and a valid loan number, the guaranty fee/payment process cannot properly proceed for that transaction.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If BR_21_008 is not satisfied, the lender risks pursuing guaranty-related steps on an ineligible credit, resulting in cancellation/denial risk and significant remediation (cure delinquency, re-issue, or withdraw).

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) Small Loan collateral rules include no collateral at 50,000 dollars or less and mandatory liens on financed assets for loans over 50,000 dollars
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) Small Loans: (1) For loans of $50,000 or less, lenders are not required to take collateral. (2) When loan proceeds will be used to refinance existing debt, the new 7(a) Small Loan must be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt (release of excess collateral permitted only when the refinanced debt is over-collateralized under SBA requirements and the 7(a) loan remains fully secured). (3) For loans over $50,000, at a minimum the lender must take a first lien on assets financed with loan proceeds, with exceptions including vehicles: SBA does not require a lien on vehicles that already have a lien or unless the vehicle value is greater than $20,000 at the time the SBA loan number is assigned; lender must document the valuation source and dollar amount in the credit memorandum. (4) When 50% or more of loan proceeds are for working capital, lender must take a lien on all applicant business fixed assets (including real estate) up to the point the loan is fully secured, with specific SBA valuation limits for determining “fully secured” (e.g., improved real estate valued up to 85% of market; unimproved 50%; new machinery and equipment valued up to 75% of price; furniture/fixtures up to 10% of net book or appraised value).
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the regulated financial institution extends an SBA 7(a) Small Loan (including refinance transactions) and is determining required collateral and lien positions; additional fixed-asset lien requirement applies when ≥50% of proceeds are working capital.
business_rules.consequences: If minimum SBA collateral/lien requirements are not met, SBA may repair or deny the guaranty or require corrective action. Lender must document required valuations and lien decisions (e.g., vehicle valuation threshold documentation) to support compliance.
business_rules.exceptions: Vehicle lien exception: lien not required if vehicle already has a lien or unless vehicle value is > $20,000 at SBA loan number assignment. Lender is not required to take a lien against applicant business real estate when equity is less than 25% of fair market value (per SBA fixed-asset lien guidance) even when pursuing full security; however the lender may limit real estate lien to amount necessary to ensure full security when applicable.

source_reference.section_id: Collateral for 7(a) Small Loans – Minimum collateral requirements and thresholds
source_reference.source_text: Para. A. for guaranty requirements. For loans of $50,000 or less, Lenders are not required to take collateral. When loan proceeds from a 7(a) Small Loan will be used to refinance existing debt, the loan must be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt that is being refinanced.

examples: A $45,000 7(a) Small Loan for working capital: lender may proceed without taking collateral (though may choose to).
A $200,000 7(a) Small Loan refinancing an existing term note secured by a first lien on equipment: the new SBA loan must have at least the same collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt.

A $300,000 7(a) Small Loan used 60% for working capital: lender must take liens on all fixed assets up to full security, and must file a lien on a financed vehicle valued at $35,000 (value > $20,000) unless it already has a lien, documenting valuation source in the credit memo.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA guaranty repair/denial; lender oversight findings

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

dependencies.rationale: Both rules address 7(a) Small loans at/under $50,000. BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_3_001 provides the baseline SBA statement; BR_EXTENDS_COLLATERAL_7_010 expands it with refinance and >$50,000 requirements. Implementations frequently validate the broader rule’s logic against the baseline threshold rule to prevent mis-coding collateral requirements.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the threshold rule is misapplied, the lender may incorrectly require collateral (process friction) or, more critically, fail to take collateral when required above the threshold.

business_rules.rule_name: SBA 7(a) lender must not decline a loan request solely due to inadequate collateral and must not use SBA guaranty as a substitute for available collateral
business_rules.description: For SBA 7(a) lending, a loan request must not be declined solely on the basis of inadequate collateral when repayment ability is demonstrated. However, SBA does not permit its guaranty to be a substitute for available collateral; the lender must still identify and take available collateral consistent with SBA requirements and commercially reasonable/prudent practices comparable to similarly-sized non-SBA guaranteed commercial loans.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to SBA 7(a) loan underwriting and credit decisioning where collateral is inadequate relative to loan amount but borrower demonstrates repayment ability, and where collateral is available but not taken.
business_rules.consequences: Improperly declining solely for collateral may conflict with SBA program purpose and requirements; failing to take available collateral while relying on guaranty may violate SBA requirements and can lead to SBA repair/denial on guaranty purchase and lender oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; collateral decisions remain based on individual loan circumstances and must meet SBA minimum requirements. SBA explicitly states guaranty is not a substitute for available collateral.

source_reference.section_id: Credit Standards > Collateral (general collateral approach)
source_reference.source_text: A loan request is not to be declined solely on the basis of inadequate collateral... Applicants that demonstrate repayment ability but lack adequate collateral... However, SBA does not permit its guaranty to be a substitute for available collateral.

examples: Borrower demonstrates cash flow repayment but has limited fixed assets: lender may proceed with SBA 7(a) rather than declining solely for collateral, while still taking all available collateral per SBA rules.
Borrower has available equipment and real estate collateral but lender chooses not to perfect liens to streamline closing: not permitted to rely on SBA guaranty in place of available collateral.
Two similarly sized non-SBA loans require UCC filings and mortgages; SBA 7(a) must use at least as thorough collateral identification/perfection practices.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender review criticism; SBA guaranty repair/denial if available collateral not taken; program compliance findings

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

dependencies.rationale: 14_002 prohibits declines solely due to inadequate collateral when repayment is demonstrated, but simultaneously requires taking available collateral consistent with SBA requirements. 7_010 provides specific SBA collateral/lien rules for 7(a) Small Loans, enabling compliant execution of 14_002 in that product context.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 7_010 is not applied where it governs, the lender may fail to take required liens or maintain required priority in refinance scenarios, elevating SBA repair/denial risk even if the credit decision was otherwise consistent with 14_002.

business_rules.rule_name: Export Express loans are processed only under delegated authority and SBA does not review lender credit analysis before issuing a loan number
business_rules.description: Export Express loans are only processed via an Export Express lender’s delegated authority. When a lender submits an SBA 7(a) loan guaranty request under its Export Express authority, SBA does not review the lender’s credit or loan structure analysis before issuing a loan number. The lender must analyze creditworthiness in accordance with SBA loan program requirements and properly document its file. The lender’s analysis remains subject to SBA review and adequacy determination when the lender requests SBA to purchase its guaranty or during SBA lender oversight activities.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the institution extends (originates) an Export Express loan/line under delegated authority and submits the guaranty request to SBA.
business_rules.consequences: Lender remains responsible for compliant underwriting and file documentation; SBA can later determine analysis is inadequate during guaranty purchase request or oversight, increasing risk of guaranty repair/denial and oversight findings.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated.

source_reference.section_id: a. Processing Method
source_reference.source_text: Method Export Express loans are only processed via an Export Express Lende r’s delegated authority. When a Lender submits an SBA 7(a) loan guarant y request under the Lender’s Export Express authority, the Agency does not review the Lender’s

examples: Lender submits Export Express guaranty request; SBA issues a loan number without reviewing credit memo—lender still must retain a complete underwriting file for later review.
After borrower default, lender requests SBA to purchase guaranty—SBA reviews file and may determine underwriting was inadequate.
SBA conducts routine lender oversight—delegated lender must evidence commercially reasonable analysis and proper documentation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: SBA lender oversight criticism; SBA guaranty repair/denial during purchase review

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

dependencies.rationale: 19_011 states SBA will not review the lender’s credit/structure analysis before issuing a loan number; therefore, lender underwriting must still comply with SBA requirements such as the collateral/repayment-ability decisioning standard in 14_002.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 14_002 is not embedded in Export Express underwriting, the lender may make impermissible collateral-driven declines or fail to take available collateral, increasing guaranty repair/denial exposure upon later SBA review.

dependencies.rationale: 19_011 describes delegated processing where SBA does not pre-review. 26_004 governs how delegated lenders must request/retain SBA exception approvals. If an exception is required, delegated processing must incorporate the exception workflow and documentation before the lender proceeds as though the exception exists.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Proceeding without an approved exception (or failing to retain the approval email) can result in SBA oversight findings and increased guaranty repair/denial risk.

dependencies.rationale: 19_011 states SBA does not pre-review delegated credit/structure, increasing lender responsibility; therefore, eligibility screens like passive-business ineligibility must be satisfied before the lender submits/relies on delegated authority.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If 38_003 is not satisfied (or not performed), an ineligible applicant could be submitted/closed, creating high guaranty repair/denial exposure upon SBA review or purchase request.

dependencies.rationale: Where the applicant is a lodging/transient-stay business, SBA eligibility turns on the >50% transient revenue test, ≤30-day stay criterion, and zoning/legal compliance; 19_011 requires lender to perform and document compliant underwriting/eligibility despite no SBA pre-review.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: Failure to apply 38_004 can cause an ineligible lodging/rental business to be originated under delegated authority, materially increasing SBA guaranty denial/repair risk.

business_rules.rule_name: Covered application data collection applies regardless of the institution’s application intake channel once a covered application is triggered
business_rules.description: The institution is not required to accept applications through every possible channel, but the section 1071 data collection and reporting requirements apply regardless of how the institution accepts applications. Whatever intake means the institution uses (e.g., in person, by telephone, by electronic transmission), once a covered application is triggered, the institution must collect and report required data under final 12 CFR 1002.107.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when a request for a covered credit transaction (including a CRE loan) becomes a covered application under 12 CFR 1002.103(a) based on the institution’s procedures for the type of credit requested, regardless of whether the application is oral, phone-based, in-person, or electronic.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution fails to collect/report 1071 data because the application was taken orally or via a non-standard channel, the institution risks inaccurate reporting, supervisory findings, and potential enforcement for noncompliance with 1071 obligations.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; rule clarifies that institutions are not required to accept applications through all channels, only that obligations apply regardless of channel once triggered.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.103(a) Covered application; method of accepting applications
source_reference.source_text: The Bureau notes that this was not the intent... Rather, the Bureau interprets the statutory language to mean that data collection and reporting requirements apply regardless of a financial institution’s method of accepting applications. Thus, whatever the means used... (e.g., in person, by telephone, by electronic transmission, etc.), once a covered application is triggered, the financial institution has a duty to collect and report on the application.

examples: Borrower calls a relationship manager and orally requests a CRE line of credit; once it meets the institution’s covered application procedures, the institution must collect and report 1071 data.
Applicant completes an application in person at a branch; covered application triggers, so demographic and other reportable data must be collected regardless of the channel.
Applicant submits via an online portal; covered application triggers; 1071 data must be collected and recorded even if the bank’s usual process is paper-based.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism and potential civil money penalties for failure to collect/report required 1071 data

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

dependencies.rationale: BR_EXTENDS_regulatory_37_007 applies 'once a covered application is triggered.' BR_EXTENDS_regulatory_37_010 defines a major exclusion (inquiries/prequalification) that prevents covered-application status and therefore prevents downstream data collection/reporting steps from being required.
dependencies.impact_if_fails: If the institution misclassifies inquiries as covered applications, it may collect protected demographic information unnecessarily (operational and fair lending risk). If it misclassifies true covered applications as inquiries, it may underreport and violate 1071.

business_rules.rule_name: Incomplete covered applications must still be collected and reported even if insufficient to make a credit decision
business_rules.description: If the business has requested a covered credit transaction in accordance with the institution’s procedures for the type of credit requested, the institution must collect and report 1071 data even if the information provided is insufficient to render a credit decision (i.e., the covered application is incomplete). Capturing incomplete or withdrawn applications is treated as essential to section 1071 purposes.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when (1) applicant requests a covered credit transaction, (2) request aligns with institution’s procedures such that a covered application is triggered under 12 CFR 1002.103(a), and (3) the file lacks sufficient information for a credit decision.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution delays data collection until it has enough information to underwrite, it may fail to report incomplete/withdrawn covered applications, creating material reporting gaps and risk of supervisory criticism and enforcement.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated in the cited text; the timing can depend on each institution’s procedures, but obligation applies once covered application is triggered.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.103(a) Covered application; incomplete applications
source_reference.source_text: Assuming the business has requested a covered credit transaction in accordance with procedures used by a financial institution for the type of credit requested, the financial institution would be required to collect and report data, even if there is insufficient information to render a credit decision. Indeed... the Bureau believes capturing incomplete or withdrawn applications is essential...

examples: Applicant submits a CRE loan request with only basic identification and requested amount, then never provides financials. The lender must still treat it as reportable if the request met its covered application procedures.
Applicant begins a covered application and later withdraws before underwriting; the lender must still collect/report data for the covered application once triggered.
A broker submits a loan request missing key fields; if it meets the bank’s covered application procedure, it is reportable even if underwriting cannot proceed.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism, required data corrections/resubmission, and potential civil money penalties

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

business_rules.rule_name: Online partial entries are reportable only if the institution begins evaluating the information before an official submit action under its procedures
business_rules.description: Whether a business’s partial information input into an online form constitutes a reportable covered application depends on the institution’s procedures for the type of credit requested. If the institution’s procedures require submission of a paper/digital form to be considered, there is no covered application until submission. If the institution regularly begins evaluating applicant information even when the form is not officially submitted, there is likely a reportable covered application. If the institution does not track or begin to evaluate until the business presses “submit,” it is not required to begin tracking partial information entered online for purposes of the final rule.
business_rules.conditions: Applies to online application channels where applicants may enter information but abandon the process before a formal submit step; applicability turns on the institution’s established procedures and actual practices for initiating evaluation/tracking.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution begins evaluating pre-submit information but fails to treat it as a covered application, it risks underreporting. Conversely, tracking every preliminary inquiry without evaluation could create data quality issues; the rule indicates tracking is not required when no evaluation/tracking begins until submit.
business_rules.exceptions: None stated; determination is procedure- and practice-dependent.

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.103(a) Covered application; online form not submitted scenarios
source_reference.source_text: If... procedures require an applicant to submit a paper or digital form... then there is no covered application... until the business submits the form... If... the financial institution regularly begins evaluating information... even if the form is not “officially” submitted... there is likely a reportable covered application... if a financial institution... does not track or begin to evaluate applications until the business presses a “submit” button, the financial institution would not be required to begin tracking partial information inputted online...

examples: Bank’s portal stores entries but does not create an application record or run any checks until 'Submit' is clicked; abandoned entries are not reportable covered applications.

Bank’s portal runs automated eligibility checks and starts underwriting workflow once key fields are entered (before 'Submit'); those instances likely become reportable covered applications under the bank’s procedures/practices.
Applicant enters contact and loan amount, then abandons; the bank’s system triggers a credit pull and assigns to underwriting; the bank should treat as reportable once its process begins evaluation.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism for underreporting or inconsistent covered application definitions; mandated policy/system alignment

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

business_rules.rule_name: Inquiries and prequalification requests are excluded from the covered application definition for 1071 reporting
business_rules.description: The definition of covered application for 1071 reporting excludes inquiries and prequalification requests; therefore, institutions are not required to collect and report 1071 data solely because a business inquires about credit or seeks prequalification. Covered application status is not triggered by purely preliminary or informal expressions of interest when they fall within the inquiry/prequalification exclusion.
business_rules.conditions: Applies when the interaction is an inquiry or prequalification request rather than a request for a covered credit transaction in accordance with the institution’s procedures that triggers a covered application under 12 CFR 1002.103.
business_rules.consequences: If the institution treats excluded inquiries/prequalification requests as covered applications, it may collect unnecessary protected demographic information and create operational/data-quality risk. If the institution improperly categorizes true applications as inquiries to avoid reporting, it risks underreporting and supervisory findings.
business_rules.exceptions: The excerpt references that details are addressed in § 1002.103(b); final determination should be validated against the full text of 1002.103(b).

source_reference.section_id: Section 1002.103(a) Covered application; reference to exclusions in 1002.103(b)
source_reference.source_text: As discussed below in the section-by-section analysis of § 1002.103(b), the Bureau is excluding inquiries and prequalification requests from the definition of a covered application; many of the reasons for those exclusions are relevant here as well...

examples: Business asks, 'Do you offer CRE loans and what rates are available?' with no request per bank procedures; treated as inquiry and not reportable.
Borrower requests a prequalification estimate for a potential purchase but does not submit information required by the bank to request credit; not a covered application.
If a business provides the required information and requests credit per the bank’s procedures, the interaction becomes a covered application even if earlier it began as an inquiry.
business_rules.enforcement_action: CFPB supervisory criticism for misclassification of inquiries vs applications; potential enforcement for underreporting

examples: A bank extends a land development loan to a developer dependent on sale/refinance proceeds
A thrift extends a multifamily loan where repayment is primarily tenant rental income

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_PRICING_6_005 selected: most complete standalone statement of the 7(a) variable-rate base-rate constraint and all four spread tiers, with explicit initial note-rate compliance framing.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All three rules impose the same tiered maximum spread caps for SBA 7(a) variable-rate loans using Prime or Optional Peg as the base rate: <=$50k +6.5%; $50,001-$250k +6.0%; $250,001-$350k +4.5%; >=$350,001 +3.0%. Differences are phrasing and minor context notes (e.g., CAPLines mention), not business logic.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA 7(a) variable-rate loans (including CAPLines where applicable), the note rate must use an acceptable base rate (Prime Rate or SBA Optional Peg Rate) and the spread above the base rate must not exceed the SBA tier caps by gross loan amount: (1) $50,000 or less: +6.5%; (2) $50,001 through $250,000: +6.0%; (3) $250,001 through $350,000: +4.5%; (4) $350,001 and greater: +3.0%. The initial Note rate (base + spread) must not exceed the applicable SBA maximum; if proposed terms exceed the cap, pricing must be reduced/corrected to remain SBA-compliant.
merged_examples: If a $200,000 7(a) variable-rate loan is priced at Prime + 6.25%, the spread exceeds the +6.0% cap and must be reduced.
If a $600,000 7(a) variable-rate CAPLine is priced at Optional Peg + 3.25%, the spread exceeds the +3.0% cap and must be reduced.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 13 CFR § 120.214; SOP 50 10 (interest rate requirements for 7(a) variable-rate loans)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_documentation_4_009 selected: integrates both pre-disbursement and post-disbursement interest-term change controls (including consent + SBA notification/update mechanics) into one cohesive rule.
duplicate_groups.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_002 matches the pre-first-disbursement portion, and BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_003 matches the post-disbursement portion, of BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_documentation_4_009. No thresholds or scope differences exist; only separation vs combination and wording differ.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Interest term changes on SBA 7(a) loans require borrower consent and must remain within SBA maximum-rate constraints tied to the application/approval timing. (1) Pre-disbursement: after SBA approval but before first disbursement, the lender may change the initial Note rate terms (base, spread, or fixed/variable selection) only if the new rate does not exceed the maximum allowable interest rate at the time of loan application, and only with the borrower’s written consent separate from executing loan documents; lender must notify LGPC or update through E-Tran/E-Tran Servicing as required. (2) Post-disbursement (variable-rate loans): the lender may change base rate or spread only if the method is permitted and consistent with SBA interest rules in effect at approval, with borrower written agreement, and with CLSC notification (email) or update via E-Tran Servicing.
merged_examples: A lender cannot increase a spread after approval but before first disbursement without separate written consent and without ensuring the new rate remains within the max allowed at application.
After disbursement, a lender cannot switch to a different index not permitted at approval, even if the borrower agrees.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (interest changes pre/post disbursement); SBA servicing submission requirements (CLSC/LGPC; E‑Tran Servicing)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_004 selected: more complete operational detail (includes allowance to delay initial adjustment period as an option) while retaining the core monthly adjustment constraints.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules impose the same core mechanics: first adjustment timing framework, adjustment frequency must be specified in the Note, subsequent adjustments no more frequently than monthly, and adjustment period cannot be changed without borrower written consent. Differences are additive guidance (delay option) rather than conflicting requirements.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA variable-rate loans: (1) the first interest rate adjustment may occur on the first calendar day of the month following initial disbursement using the base rate in effect on the first business day of the month, and the lender may delay the initial adjustment period as permitted by SBA guidance; (2) the Note must specify the frequency of interest rate adjustment; (3) the adjustment period/frequency identified in the Note may not be changed without the borrower’s written consent; and (4) subsequent adjustments may occur no more frequently than monthly.
merged_examples: If the Note states monthly adjustments, the lender cannot adjust weekly even if Prime changes mid-month.
If the lender wants to change from monthly adjustments to quarterly, borrower written consent is required.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (variable-rate adjustment mechanics for 7(a) loans)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_PRICING_7_007 selected: clearer lifecycle framing (drafting/closing and servicing) and explicitly ties prohibition to default scenarios.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules prohibit default interest rates on SBA 7(a) loans and require removal/amendment if present. No scope, threshold, or consequence differences exist.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: SBA 7(a) loan notes may not include or apply default interest rates. In a default scenario, lenders must not increase the interest rate solely due to default through a default-rate provision. Loan documents and servicing system setup must not impose an increased interest rate triggered by default status; if present, the provision must be removed/amended to comply.
merged_examples: A Note cannot state 'default rate = note rate + 5%'; such a clause must be removed.
Servicing systems must not automatically re-price a loan upward upon default coding.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (7(a) pricing limitations—default rate prohibition)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_regulatory_4_008 selected: most explicit about the manipulation purpose (evading tier-based maximum spreads) and provides illustrative framing.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules prohibit splitting a single SBA 7(a) credit request into multiple loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate (i.e., bypassing SBA maximum interest rate limitations). Scope and consequence alignment are the same.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: An SBA 7(a) lender is prohibited from splitting a single credit request into two (or more) loans for the purpose of charging a higher interest rate to the applicant, including manipulating loan-size tiers that determine maximum allowable spreads/caps. If detected, the structure must be corrected/restructured into compliant terms and pricing and may trigger SBA oversight findings and guaranty repair/denial exposure.
merged_examples: A lender may not book $400,000 as two $200,000 loans to obtain a higher spread tier than would apply to a single $400,000 loan.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (interest rate limitations and anti-evasion policy)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_29_002 selected: clean statement of the CAPLine BBC condition and the required first-lien collateral set.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require that when a Working Capital CAPLine is disbursed based on a Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC), the lender must take a first lien on A/R and inventory. No differences in scope, triggers, or consequences.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When a Working Capital CAPLine is disbursed based on a Borrowing Base Certificate (BBC), the lender must obtain and perfect a first-lien security interest in the applicant’s working/trading assets—specifically accounts receivable and inventory.
merged_examples: If availability is calculated from a BBC and advances are made against A/R, the lender must have a perfected first lien in A/R and inventory before advancing.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (CAPLines—Working Capital CAPLine collateral controls)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_29_003 selected: more complete statement of the 1:1 collateral ratio logic and the requirement to add collateral when trading assets are insufficient.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules apply to Working Capital CAPLines not disbursed using a BBC and impose the same 1:1 collateral ratio requirement with a first lien on A/R and inventory and additional collateral if needed. No material variance.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For Working Capital CAPLines not disbursed using a BBC, the lender must assume full usage of the revolving line and secure the line with sufficient eligible collateral to ensure a 1:1 collateral ratio. The lender must obtain a first lien on working/trading assets (accounts receivable and inventory). If those assets are insufficient to achieve 1:1 coverage, the lender must take additional collateral (including available personal real estate equity of required owners/guarantors, subject to SBA limitations) to meet the 1:1 requirement.
merged_examples: If a $500,000 Working Capital CAPLine is not BBC-controlled, total eligible collateral must be at least $500,000 and include a first lien on A/R and inventory.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (CAPLines—collateral requirements when BBC is not used)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_COLLATERAL_29_004 selected: clearer articulation of permitted excess-collateral release and substitute collateral allowance in the CAPLine refinance scenario.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules impose the same CAPLine refinance collateral principle: the SBA line should be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the refinanced debt, with limited release and substitution flexibility when fully secured remains true.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: When Working Capital CAPLine proceeds refinance existing debt, the SBA loan should be secured with at least the same collateral and lien priority as the debt being refinanced. If the refinanced debt is over-collateralized under SBA collateral requirements and the SBA loan will remain fully secured, the lender is not required to take excess collateral and may release excess collateral. Substitute collateral is permitted only if it is comparable in value and useful life and acceptable to SBA (or acceptable to the lender under PLP authority where applicable).
merged_examples: If the refinanced revolver had a first lien on A/R and inventory, the CAPLine refinance should maintain at least that lien position and collateral set.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (CAPLines refinance collateral expectations)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_10_006 selected: slightly more complete articulation of standby debt mechanics and required documentation elements (Form 155 + note attachment + subordination/no-action concepts).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules state the same SBA condition for treating debt as equity injection: full standby for the entire 7(a) loan term (no P&I payments), use SBA Form 155 (or equivalent), attach note, allow accrual, and require subordination/no-action constraints. No material differences.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: Debt may be treated as equity injection for SBA purposes only if it is on full standby for the term of the 7(a) loan (no payments of principal or interest for the term). The lender must use SBA Form 155 (or an equivalent standby agreement) and attach a copy of the standby note. Standby debt may accrue interest and be added to the standby balance and amortized only after the 7(a) loan is paid in full. The standby creditor must subordinate any lien rights in collateral to the lender and agree to restrictions consistent with SBA standby requirements.
merged_examples: Seller note counted as equity must be full standby for the entire SBA loan term; interest may accrue but cannot be paid currently.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (equity injection and standby debt requirements); SBA Form 155

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_5_006 selected: consolidates both (a) third-party lender documentation-at-application requirement and (b) third-party loan minimum sizing rules into one SBA 504 capital stack rule, enabling a single validation point.
duplicate_groups.rationale: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_2_007 is fully contained in BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_5_006 (third party lender must be in place and evidenced by LOI/term sheet/commitment at application). BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_2_008 is also fully contained (third party loan must be at least net debenture proceeds; and at least 50% of project costs under the stated triggers). No numeric or scope differences.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA 504 loan applications, the Third Party Lender must be in place at the time of application and evidenced by a letter of intent/term sheet or commitment letter included in the application package that outlines Third Party/Interim loan terms. Third Party Loan sizing must meet SBA minimums: it must be at least as much as the net debenture proceeds; and it must total at least 50% of project costs if (i) the Borrower (or OC if Borrower is an EPC) has operated for 2 years or less, or (ii) the project involves acquisition, construction, conversion, or expansion of a limited or single purpose asset.
merged_examples: If net debenture proceeds are $900,000, the third-party loan must be at least $900,000.
If the borrower has operated 18 months, third-party financing must be at least 50% of project costs.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (504 Third Party Loan requirements); 13 CFR Part 120 (504 program structure)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_5_007 selected: includes the same prohibited-demand-trigger requirement plus additional liquidation/intercreditor nuance not present in the shorter rule.
duplicate_groups.rationale: The prohibited cross-default/deem-at-risk/early-demand provisions are identical. BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_5_007 adds additional collateral and liquidation mechanics, so the shorter rule is redundant for the shared requirement.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA 504 Third Party Loans, the Third Party Lender’s note and loan documents must not include cross-default, “deem-at-risk,” or other provisions allowing the Third Party Lender to demand payment prior to maturity unless the loan is in default. Where additional collateral and intercreditor/liquidation terms apply, documentation must also comply with SBA requirements governing collateral exhaustion, recoveries, and permissible intercreditor provisions.
merged_examples: A third-party note cannot allow acceleration solely because the SBA/CDC debenture is in default if the third-party loan itself is not in default.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (504 Third Party Loan documentation limitations)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_13_002 selected: concise and directly operational for ordering/accepting SBA CRE appraisals, including the >$1,000,000 state-certified trigger.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules impose the same appraiser independence requirements and credentialing rule: state-licensed or state-certified generally, but state-certified required when estimated value > $1,000,000. Wording differences only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA commercial real estate appraisals, the appraiser must be independent with no appearance of conflict of interest, independent of the loan production function, and not involved in approving the transaction. The appraiser must be state-licensed or state-certified; however, if the commercial property’s estimated value is over $1,000,000, the appraiser must be state-certified (not merely state-licensed).
merged_examples: If estimated property value is $1.2MM, a state-licensed appraiser is insufficient; a state-certified appraiser is required.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (SBA appraisal independence and credentialing requirements); USPAP (credentialing and ethics context)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_13_004 selected: provides the same construction/substantial rehab appraisal and post-completion statement requirements in a cleaner compliance workflow form and includes the 'cannot close without SBA permission' consequence.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require (i) an as-completed market value appraisal for new construction or substantial renovation (rehab costs > 1/3 of purchase price/FMV) and (ii) a post-completion statement regarding minor deviations, with SBA prior written permission required if the statement cannot be obtained. No substantive differences.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: If an SBA loan finances new construction or substantial renovation of an existing building, the appraisal must estimate market value at completion. “Substantial” means rehabilitation expenses of more than one-third of the purchase price or fair market value at the time of application. After construction is completed, the lender must obtain a statement (from the appraiser, general contractor, project architect, or construction management firm) that the building was built with only minor deviations (if any) from the plans/specifications used for the original value estimate. If the statement cannot be obtained, the lender may not close the loan without SBA’s prior written permission; if the SBA-guaranteed loan covered the construction period, deviations must be escalated to CLSC as required by SBA servicing guidance.
merged_examples: If rehab budget is 40% of purchase price, an 'as-completed' appraisal is required (not only 'as-is').
If the contractor refuses to provide the deviation statement, the lender must obtain SBA prior written permission before closing.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (construction/substantial renovation appraisal and completion certification requirements)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_DOCUMENTATION_18_006 selected: most complete statement of the 90% appraisal-at-closing comparison rule, including the SBA prior written permission requirement and explicit PLP exception handling.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules impose the same decision thresholds and documentation requirements when an estimated value was used pending an appraisal at closing: (i) appraised value >= 90% of estimated requires explanation if lower; (ii) appraised value < 90% generally requires SBA prior written permission (subject to stated program exceptions). Differences are program callouts (IT) and phrasing only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: If the lender uses an estimated real estate value in the credit memorandum and obtains the appraisal at closing, then at closing: (i) if the appraised value is 90% or more of the estimated value, the lender may close but must include a written explanation in the file as to why the appraisal is less than the estimate; (ii) if the appraised value is less than 90% of the estimated value, the lender may not close without SBA’s prior written permission unless a documented program exception applies (e.g., PLP handling as allowed by SBA guidance). This procedure applies to SBA CRE-secured loans using the estimate-pending-appraisal workflow, including IT loans when applicable.
merged_examples: Estimated value $1,000,000; appraisal $930,000 (93%): may close with written explanation in file.
Estimated value $1,000,000; appraisal $880,000 (88%): must obtain SBA prior written permission (absent an applicable exception) before closing.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (appraisal-at-closing exception workflow and 90% test); SBA delegated lender guidance (PLP exception parameters)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_COLLATERAL_24_009 selected: more explicit SBA 7(a) scoping and clearer condition triad (transfer timing, loan amount threshold, collateral involvement).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules require the same enhanced controls for certain change-of-ownership cases: if business transferred within 36 months and loan amount > $500,000, obtain a compliant business real estate appraisal plus either an appraisal review by another appraiser selected by lender or a senior staff site visit with documentation. Wording differences only.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership transactions where the business has been transferred within 36 months prior to the loan application date and the loan amount is more than $500,000, SBA requires: (a) an appraisal of the business real estate meeting SBA appraisal requirements; and (b) either an appraisal review by another appraiser selected directly by the lender or a site visit by a senior member of the lender’s staff. If a site visit is used, the lender must document the file with the date of the visit and items reviewed.
merged_examples: Loan amount $750,000 and the business changed hands 18 months ago: lender must obtain the appraisal plus either appraisal review or senior staff site visit documentation.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (change-of-ownership enhanced valuation/review controls)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CREDIT_UNDERWRITING_STANDARD_CREDITPOLICY_21_009 selected: includes the key definitional element (<=1 year generating revenue), the 10% minimum injection, total project cost definition, and the 'separate project' clarification tied to approvals more than 90 days apart.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All three rules define start-up as <=1 year in operation (generating revenue) and require at least 10% equity injection for all SBA 7(a) loans to start-ups, with total project costs defined consistently (costs to become operational; LOCs and 504 excluded). Differences are only wording and inclusion of the 90-day project separation nuance.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA 7(a) purposes, a start-up business is one in operation (generating revenue from intended operations) for 1 year or less. All 7(a) loans made to a start-up business require an equity injection (Applicant contribution) of at least 10% of total project costs. Total project costs include all costs required to become operational, regardless of source of funds, except for lines of credit and 504 loans. Loans approved more than 90 days apart are treated as separate projects for this purpose.
merged_examples: A restaurant operating for 9 months seeking a 7(a) term loan for buildout must document at least 10% injection of total project costs.
If two SBA loans are approved 4 months apart for different phases, treat as separate projects for injection analysis.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (equity injection requirements for start-ups; total project cost definition)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_CRE_LOAN_REGULATORY_7_004 selected: most complete (explicit stated-maturity requirement plus the shortest-appropriate-term standard) while preserving the 10-year cap for working capital/inventory/intangibles.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules set the same maturity constraint for SBA 7(a) working capital, inventory, and intangible asset financing: maximum 10 years, with a general 'shortest appropriate term' standard. No differences in thresholds or scope.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: SBA 7(a) loans must have a stated maturity, and the term must be the shortest appropriate term based on use of proceeds and the borrower’s ability to repay. For working capital, inventory, or financing intangible assets (including goodwill), the maturity must not exceed 10 years.
merged_examples: A 7(a) loan used solely for working capital cannot be structured as a 15-year term loan.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: SOP 50 10 (7(a) maturity rules by use of proceeds)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_reporting_33_003 selected: most detailed articulation of the split-vs-single logic and the reporting risk of incorrect combining/splitting.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All three rules impose the same 1071 reporting logic: simultaneous requests for two or more covered credit transactions/products must be reported as separate covered applications; a single request where applicant is undecided between products is reported as one covered application. Differences are purely presentation (reporting vs MIS implementation framing).
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For CFPB Section 1071 (Reg B Subpart B), if an applicant requests two or more covered credit transactions at one time (e.g., term loan and line of credit), the financial institution must report each request as a separate covered application. If the applicant is requesting only one covered credit transaction but is undecided between products (e.g., term loan OR line of credit), the institution reports it as a single covered application (do not split solely due to alternative product possibilities).
merged_examples: Applicant requests a $1MM term loan and a $500k line simultaneously: report two covered applications.
Applicant requests $500k of credit but is undecided between term vs line: report one covered application.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 12 CFR 1002.103(a); 12 CFR 1002.109; CFPB 1071 commentary (covered application treatment when multiple products requested)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REPORTING_34_001 selected: includes the same threshold test while also specifying the operational start date (Jan 1 of year Y after meeting Y-2 and Y-1).
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules define coverage based on originating at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years; the primary rule adds the explicit compliance start date. No conflict; the shorter rule is redundant.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: A financial institution becomes a covered financial institution for CFPB Section 1071 when it originated at least 100 covered credit transactions for small businesses in each of the two preceding calendar years. When this two-year threshold is met for calendar years (Y-2) and (Y-1), the institution’s compliance obligation begins on January 1 of year Y; if the threshold is not met, the institution is not covered for that period and is not required to collect/report 1071 data.
merged_examples: If an institution originated 120 covered transactions in 2024 and 105 in 2025, it becomes covered on January 1, 2026.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 12 CFR 1002.105(b); 12 CFR 1002.114 (coverage and compliance timing)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_27_009 selected: more explicit interpretive framing (operative $5MM threshold notwithstanding SBA industry size standards) while preserving the same threshold value and scope.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules set the same operative small business threshold for 1071 coverage: preceding fiscal year gross annual revenue of $5,000,000 or less. No differences in number, measurement period, or applicability.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For Regulation B Subpart B (CFPB Section 1071) purposes, a business is a small business if its gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year is $5,000,000 or less (subject to any rule-permitted methodology), notwithstanding SBA industry size standards. Misclassification of applicants by not applying the $5MM threshold can result in inaccurate 1071 coverage determinations and data collection/reporting errors.
merged_examples: A business with $4.8MM gross annual revenue in its preceding fiscal year is within the small business threshold for 1071 scope (subject to other coverage elements).
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 12 CFR 1002.106(b); CFPB 1071 final rule section-by-section analysis (small business definition)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_28_002 selected: contains both (a) the base $5MM threshold statement and (b) the full CPI-U adjustment mechanics, creating a single implementable scoping rule.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules describe the same five-year CPI-U adjustment requirement (base January 2025 CPI-U, compare every five years, round to nearest $500,000, effective Jan 1 following adjustment). No differences in numeric logic or timing.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For CFPB Section 1071 small business lending data collection, a business qualifies as a small business if its gross annual revenue for its preceding fiscal year is $5 million or less. The $5 million gross annual revenue threshold must be adjusted every 5 years after January 1, 2025 based on changes to the CPI-U (U.S. city average, all items, not seasonally adjusted). Any adjustment must be rounded to the nearest multiple of $500,000 and becomes effective on January 1 of the year following the adjustment determination. Institutions must update scoping logic and retain a defensible calculation/audit trail.
merged_examples: If an adjustment calculation yields $5.24MM, it would round to $5.0MM (nearest $500k) and may result in no threshold change depending on rounding.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 12 CFR 1002.106(b)(1); CPI-U adjustment provisions (Reg B Subpart B)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REPORTING_36_001 selected: more explicit about reporting both the census tract and the basis/indicator and frames the operational unknown-location waterfalls.
duplicate_groups.rationale: Both rules implement the same three-step waterfall for census tract reporting under 1071 and require an indicator of which step was used. Differences are merely 'reporting' vs 'MIS design' orientation.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For CFPB Section 1071 reportable data, the institution must collect and report the census tract using a waterfall: (1) the census tract for the location where proceeds will be principally applied; (2) if unknown, the applicant’s main office/headquarters census tract; (3) if both unknown, the census tract of another address/location associated with the applicant. The institution must also indicate which basis (step 1/2/3) was used. Failure to collect/report the tract and basis indicator creates incomplete/incorrect 1071 submissions.
merged_examples: If proceeds location is unknown at action taken, report census tract of headquarters and code that headquarters was used.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 12 CFR 1002.107(a)(13); CFPB FIG/Commentary (census tract waterfall and indicator)

duplicate_groups.primary_selection_reason: BR_REGULATED_FINANCIAL_INSTITUTION_REGULATORY_14_001 selected: clearest definition structure (elements + 90-day timing definition) and strongest linkage to SBA program permissibility consequences.
duplicate_groups.rationale: All rules define and prohibit SBA 7(a) piggyback financing structures that create an improper preference: multiple loans at/about same time (approvals within 90 days) for same/similar purpose where SBA-guaranteed loan is junior/no lien relative to non-SBA loan collateral. Differences are phrasing and inclusion of an explicit 13 CFR cite in one version; no business logic differences.
duplicate_groups.merged_description: For SBA-guaranteed 7(a) loans, the lender must not structure credit as a prohibited “piggyback” arrangement that creates a preference in favor of the lender. Piggyback financing exists when one or more lenders provide more than one loan to a single borrower at or about the same time (defined as approvals within 90 days), financing the same or similar purpose, and the SBA-guaranteed loan is secured with a junior lien position or no lien position on the collateral securing the non-guaranteed loan(s). Such a structure is not permitted and may result in SBA denial of guaranty purchase, repair/denial of liability, or program oversight findings; the lender must restructure lien positions/collateral or otherwise correct the transaction.
merged_examples: If the bank makes a non-SBA senior lien term loan and an SBA 7(a) junior lien loan within 90 days for the same project, the structure may be a prohibited piggyback.
duplicate_groups.merged_references: 13 CFR § 120.411 (preference prohibition); SOP 50 10 (piggyback financing restrictions)

deduplication_details.analysis_notes: Deduplication was performed conservatively: rules were merged only where loan/program scope, thresholds, timing windows, and consequences aligned. Several near-duplicates were intentionally NOT merged due to meaningful scope variations (e.g., 7(a) Small vs Standard vs Express vs Export Express vs IT; appraisal thresholds at $350k vs $500k vs >$500k; change-of-ownership controls with/without the >$500k trigger). Where a longer rule fully subsumed a shorter rule without altering obligations, the longer rule was selected as primary and enhanced with any phrasing/citation details from the duplicate.

dependencies.strength_justification: Tier assignment and whether the institution is covered are gating determinations for when 1071 compliance must be operational.
dependencies.rationale: BR_25_001 defines the activity threshold and lookback methodology used to determine (i) whether the institution is a covered financial institution and (ii) which tier/compliance date applies. BR_24_004 is only actionable once tier is determined.
dependencies.impact: If coverage/tier is calculated incorrectly, the institution may implement late/early, causing either noncompliance (late) or unnecessary operational burden (early), plus reporting/data integrity failures.
examples: Institution incorrectly includes PPP volumes in 2022-2023 counts, misclassifies itself as covered and accelerates implementation unnecessarily.
Institution meets the 100-loan threshold but fails to recognize coverage, misses Tier 3 build date, and cannot collect/report required fields on covered applications.

dependencies.strength_justification: 1071 covered-application logic only applies if the institution is a covered financial institution (activity threshold met).
dependencies.rationale: BR_24_003 governs what interactions are excluded from 'covered application' treatment, but that determination is only relevant when the institution is within 1071 scope per BR_25_001.
dependencies.impact: If BR_25_001 is not satisfied/assessed, the institution may wrongly apply (or fail to apply) covered-application exclusions, resulting in systematic over/under-reporting.
examples: Non-covered FI builds 1071 workflow and begins collecting protected demographic data unnecessarily.
Covered FI fails to tag renewals without new money as excluded and incorrectly reports them as covered applications.

dependencies.strength_justification: You must first determine whether the applicant is a 'business' before evaluating whether an interaction is a covered application under 1071.
dependencies.rationale: BR_22_002 defines 'business' using SBA business concern criteria. If the applicant is not a business (e.g., nonprofit), then BR_24_003 covered-application exclusions are moot because 1071 does not apply to that applicant as a small business applicant.
dependencies.impact: Misclassifying applicant type leads to erroneous creation or suppression of covered application records and demographic collection steps.
examples: Nonprofit applies for credit; institution incorrectly treats it as a business and applies covered-application logic and data collection.
For-profit JV with U.S. place of business is misclassified as non-business and excluded from 1071 workflow.

dependencies.strength_justification: Covered application treatment depends on whether the request is for a covered credit transaction; leases are excluded.
dependencies.rationale: BR_22_001 defines when a transaction is a lease (excluded). Without determining transaction type first, the institution cannot correctly decide whether to open a covered application record or apply exclusions under BR_24_003.
dependencies.impact: Lease misclassification causes collecting/reporting 1071 data when prohibited/unnecessary, or failing to report covered credit transactions.
examples: Equipment lease is incorrectly treated as a covered credit transaction and a covered application record is created.
True loan is incorrectly treated as lease to evade coverage; institution under-reports.

dependencies.strength_justification: The exclusions in BR_24_003 limit/supersede when institution-initiated evaluations (BR_24_002) should be treated as covered applications.
dependencies.rationale: BR_24_002 clarifies that institution-initiated solicitations are not covered applications absent a business request. BR_24_003 further excludes inquiries and prequalification requests (and certain renewals). Operationally, BR_24_003 must be applied as an exclusion layer after/alongside BR_24_002.
dependencies.impact: If override/exclusion layering is not implemented, inquiry/prequal interactions may be mistakenly treated as covered applications, corrupting reporting datasets.
examples: Business asks about rate/terms (inquiry); system opens a covered application record due to misapplied institution-initiated logic.
Renewal without new money is incorrectly treated as a covered application.

dependencies.strength_justification: Principal owner collection rules are only executed for covered applications.
dependencies.rationale: BR_22_003 governs who is a principal owner for 1071 reporting when trusts are involved. The institution should only collect/report principal owner data when a covered application exists; BR_24_003 determines whether the interaction is excluded.
dependencies.impact: If BR_24_003 is not satisfied, the institution may collect sensitive demographic data for non-covered interactions or miss required fields for covered ones.
examples: Inquiry mistakenly treated as covered; institution requests principal owner demographic data improperly.
Covered application involving a trust is mishandled because covered status was never established.

dependencies.strength_justification: Ownership tracing (including trusts) is necessary to correctly apply the two-prong minority-owned definition.
dependencies.rationale: BR_25_002 requires >50% ownership/control AND >50% net profits/losses accruing to minority individuals. BR_25_003 specifies tracing ownership through indirect structures and treating trustees as owners when trusts are present, which directly affects whether prong (A) is satisfied.
dependencies.impact: Without correct tracing, the institution misclassifies minority-owned status, creating data integrity issues and potential exam findings.
examples: Applicant is indirectly owned via holding company and trust; without tracing, system reports not minority-owned when it should be.
Trustee is minority individual; institution ignores trustee ownership and fails prong (A).

dependencies.strength_justification: Minority-owned status collection is only relevant/triggered for covered applications; BR_24_003 determines covered vs excluded.
dependencies.rationale: BR_25_002 governs the definition used when requesting/recording minority-owned business status under 1071. The institution should request/record this only when BR_24_003 indicates the interaction is a covered application (not an excluded inquiry/prequal/renewal without new money).
dependencies.impact: If BR_24_003 is not applied, minority-owned questions may be asked/recorded for excluded interactions or omitted for covered ones.
examples: Prequalification request is incorrectly treated as covered and minority-owned status is solicited.
Covered application is mistakenly excluded and minority-owned status is never collected.

dependencies.strength_justification: LGBTQI+-owned status definition/provision is part of covered-application data collection; it depends on covered-application determination.
dependencies.rationale: BR_18_011 defines the two-prong LGBTQI+-owned standard and requires providing the definition when requesting the data. This request step should be performed only when BR_24_003 indicates the interaction is a covered application under 1071.
dependencies.impact: Improper execution leads to either unlawful/unsupported demographic collection in excluded contexts or missing required definitional disclosures in covered contexts.
examples: Institution requests LGBTQI+-owned status during an inquiry; creates privacy and compliance risk.
Institution requests status on covered applications but omits the definition, violating 1071 data collection requirements.

dependencies.strength_justification: Eligibility certification regarding owners/guarantors requires complete direct/indirect ownership data in E-Tran.
dependencies.rationale: BR_25_005 requires listing 100% of direct/indirect ownership (including combined spouse/minor child ownership). BR_17_010 restricts SBA financing to businesses whose required owners/guarantors meet citizenship/LPR and residency criteria and requires lender certification in E-Tran. Accurate certification depends on complete ownership capture.
dependencies.impact: If ownership is incomplete or mis-aggregated, ineligible persons may be missed, creating severe SBA eligibility and guaranty enforceability risk.
examples: Spouse ownership not combined; true indirect ownership crosses control threshold and an ineligible person is overlooked.
Indirect ownership chain not fully entered; lender incorrectly certifies eligibility in E-Tran.

dependencies.strength_justification: Guaranty exposure aggregation relies on correctly identifying the borrower 'one business' and affiliate/EPC-OC relationships, which depends on ownership data capture.
dependencies.rationale: BR_7_001 requires including affiliates and treating EPC/OC as one business for the $3,750,000 max guaranty cap. Accurate aggregation commonly requires complete ownership reporting in E-Tran per BR_25_005.
dependencies.impact: If ownership/affiliate data is incomplete, the lender may exceed the guaranty cap, risking repair/denial and SBA refusal to purchase.
examples: EPC and OC structure not fully captured; lender fails to combine exposures and breaches the $3.75MM guaranty cap.
Revolving line exposure is not aggregated due to missing affiliate linkage.

dependencies.strength_justification: Guaranty percentage tiering must still respect the absolute maximum guaranty amount cap; both must be applied together.
dependencies.rationale: BR_7_002 establishes percentage limits (85%/75%), but BR_7_001 imposes a hard cap on outstanding guaranty amount. Determining compliant guaranteed portion requires both rules.
dependencies.impact: If BR_7_001 is not satisfied while applying BR_7_002, guaranteed portion may be tier-compliant but still exceed the $3.75MM cap, exposing guaranty denial/repair risk.
examples: Loan >$5MM structured at 75% would imply >$3.75MM guaranteed; lender must reduce guaranteed amount.
Borrower has existing 7(a) exposure; new loan at 75% exceeds remaining guaranty availability.

dependencies.strength_justification: When multiple loans are approved within 90 days, BR_7_003 modifies how the guaranty percentage tier is applied (combined gross amount logic).
dependencies.rationale: BR_7_002 provides the base tiering threshold ($150,000). BR_7_003 requires combining gross amounts of multiple loans within 90 days to determine the applicable tier and may force reduction of guaranty percentage on a subsequent loan.
dependencies.impact: If BR_7_003 is not applied, the lender may issue a subsequent loan at 85% when combined gross exceeds $150,000, leading to SBA repair/denial risk.
examples: Two $100,000 loans approved 30 days apart; second loan cannot remain at 85% because combined exceeds $150,000.
Term loan plus line approved within 90 days; combined guaranty percentage must not exceed 75% when combined >$150,000.

dependencies.strength_justification: Identifying borrower/affiliate relationships is required to correctly combine loans within 90 days; accurate ownership data is a key input.
dependencies.rationale: BR_7_003 applies to borrower and/or affiliates. BR_25_005 requires full direct/indirect ownership listing in E-Tran, enabling correct affiliate determination and proper application of 90-day combining logic.
dependencies.impact: Failure to capture ownership/affiliate relationships can prevent correct combining, causing guaranty percentage violations and SBA repair/denial exposure.
examples: Affiliate entity also receives a 7(a) loan within 90 days; missing affiliate linkage results in improper 85% guaranty on both loans.
Married spouse ownership not combined, affiliate relationship not flagged, combining not performed.

dependencies.strength_justification: Program-specific maximum loan amount/aggregate caps must be enforced alongside the general max guaranty exposure cap to ensure overall SBA compliance.
dependencies.rationale: BR_10_010 includes specific caps for 7(a) Small and SBA Express aggregates, while BR_7_001 imposes maximum outstanding guaranty to one business. Structuring must satisfy both sets of limits.
dependencies.impact: If BR_10_010 is ignored while applying BR_7_001, the loan may meet guaranty exposure rules but violate product caps, risking guaranty reduction/denial and oversight criticism.
examples: SBA Express aggregate exceeds $500,000 although guaranty cap is not breached.
7(a) Small project exceeds $350,000 due to multiple loans within 90 days.

dependencies.strength_justification: Franchise Directory eligibility is a process gate; if not satisfied, the lender cannot proceed/approve (including under delegated authority).
dependencies.rationale: BR_21_011 states an application cannot proceed if required franchise agreements are not listed. BR_25_004 governs whether a delegated lender must process using delegated authority vs non-delegated. The delegated/non-delegated decision is only relevant after eligibility-to-proceed (including franchise directory) is confirmed.
dependencies.impact: If BR_21_011 is not satisfied, proceeding to delegated/non-delegated routing creates an ineligible submission/approval path and elevated guaranty denial risk.
examples: Delegated lender attempts PLP approval without confirming franchise listing; SBA later finds agreement not on Directory and rejects eligibility.
Applicant has multiple franchise agreements; one missing listing halts processing regardless of delegated status.

dependencies.strength_justification: BR_19_001 applies to SBA Express/Export Express processing; BR_25_004 governs when a lender may or may not process as Express under non-delegated procedures (e.g., same-institution debt refinance).
dependencies.rationale: If a transaction is routed away from Express (because Express may not be processed non-delegated for same-institution refinance), the BR_19_001 Express-specific post-disbursement transcript verification workflow may no longer apply.
dependencies.impact: Misrouting can lead to executing the wrong verification/documentation standards, increasing SBA guaranty repair/denial risk.
examples: Lender mistakenly processes same-institution refinance as SBA Express; later cannot meet Express transcript follow-up standards and risks repair.
Properly routed non-delegated refinance should not use Express 'immediate close/disburse' assumptions.

dependencies.strength_justification: Fee permissibility/caps require determining whether multiple facilities are a prohibited fee-splitting structure and whether combined amounts must be used.
dependencies.rationale: BR_9_008 prohibits splitting to charge additional fees and requires using combined loan amounts for fee maximums when a legitimate split exists. BR_9_007 sets fee caps and Form 159 triggers; those caps/triggers must be applied to the combined amount if BR_9_008 conditions are met.
dependencies.impact: If BR_9_008 is not applied first, the lender may apply BR_9_007 caps separately to each facility and overcharge/over-document improperly, creating refund and oversight findings.
examples: Term loan + LOC approved together; lender charges two $2,500 flat fees without combining amounts (impermissible).
Lender structures two loans solely to charge two packaging fees; violates anti-splitting rule and fees are refundable.

dependencies.strength_justification: Packaging fee reasonableness/itemization/disclosure controls must be applied on the correct fee base (combined amount when splitting is legitimate).
dependencies.rationale: BR_16_001 governs packaging fee practices (written advisories, itemization, E-Tran disclosure). BR_9_008 determines whether multiple facilities must be treated as one fee event and whether fee maximums are based on combined amounts.
dependencies.impact: If BR_9_008 is ignored, BR_16_001 disclosures may still be made but on an impermissible fee structure, resulting in refund requirements and SBA oversight criticism.
examples: Lender provides written itemization per BR_16_001 but charges duplicated fees across split facilities.
Combined-loan fee cap is exceeded because fees were assessed per facility instead of once on combined amount.

dependencies.strength_justification: Both rules jointly define the permitted fee framework: disclosures/itemization (16_001) and caps/Form 159 trigger logic (9_007).
dependencies.rationale: BR_16_001 describes packaging fee reasonableness, applicant advisories, itemization categories, and E-Tran disclosure. BR_9_007 provides the flat-fee threshold and Form 159 requirement for fees > $2,500 and percentage-based caps. Compliance requires applying both simultaneously.
dependencies.impact: If either rule is omitted, the lender may be cap-compliant but fail disclosure/itemization requirements, or disclose properly but exceed caps/skip Form 159, resulting in refunds and oversight findings.
examples: Lender charges 3% fee within cap but fails to advise applicant in writing about unwanted services (16_001 violation).
Lender discloses fee in E-Tran but skips Form 159 for fees > $2,500 (9_007 violation).

dependencies.strength_justification: When Form 159 is required by fee rules, Form 159 completion/signature/itemization requirements become mandatory execution steps.
dependencies.rationale: BR_9_007 triggers SBA Form 159 completion for fees > $2,500. BR_6_010 defines how to complete/sign Form 159, including separate forms per agent and itemization thresholds, which are necessary to satisfy the triggered requirement.
dependencies.impact: If BR_6_010 is not met after BR_9_007 triggers Form 159, the loan file is noncompliant and may result in SBA findings, delays, or refunds.
examples: Packaging fee is $3,500; lender fails to obtain applicant/agent signatures on Form 159.
Multiple agents are paid; lender files one consolidated Form 159 instead of separate forms per agent.

dependencies.strength_justification: 504 agent fee disclosure via Form 159 depends on correct Form 159 preparation requirements.
dependencies.rationale: BR_15_003 requires agent fees for 504 loans be disclosed via SBA Form 159. BR_6_010 provides operational requirements for Form 159 completion/signing and separate forms per agent, which must be satisfied to achieve compliant disclosure.
dependencies.impact: If Form 159 is not completed per BR_6_010, disclosure is defective, creating processing delays and program compliance findings.
examples: Agent charges applicant fee for 504 packaging; Form 159 is missing agent signature.
Two agents receive compensation; CDC submits only one Form 159.

dependencies.strength_justification: Third-party LSP compensation controls interact with the broader SBA fee framework and can affect fee permissibility and refunds.
dependencies.rationale: BR_8_010 restricts LSP compensation to payments from the lender under an SBA-reviewed LSP agreement and prohibits certain fees charged to applicants or paid from SBA proceeds. BR_9_007 governs permissible applicant-charged fees and disclosures; both must be aligned to ensure fees are paid/charged from permissible sources and properly documented.
dependencies.impact: If BR_8_010 is not satisfied, even otherwise cap-compliant fees under BR_9_007 may be impermissibly sourced/paid, triggering remediation and oversight actions.
examples: LSP is paid from loan proceeds without SBA-reviewed agreement; fee is deemed impermissible even if disclosed.
Applicant is charged a fee that is effectively LSP compensation, violating LSP compensation restrictions.

dependencies.strength_justification: Ongoing guaranty fee remittance presupposes an active SBA guaranty; failure of upfront fee payment can cancel the guaranty and change downstream servicing obligations.
dependencies.rationale: BR_1_001 provides that SBA cancels the guaranty if the upfront fee is not received timely for short-term 7(a). BR_1_005 applies to loans that have an SBA guaranty and require ongoing guaranty fee remittances (Form 1502 servicing). If the guaranty is cancelled, downstream obligations and calculations change materially.
dependencies.impact: If BR_1_001 is not satisfied, guaranty cancellation risk arises, and subsequent Form 1502 service fee setup/remittance may be incorrect or moot; guaranty enforceability is jeopardized.
examples: Short-term 7(a) loan number issued; lender misses 10-business-day payment window; guaranty cancelled and servicing fee setup becomes defective.
Lender charges borrower before paying upfront fee; program violation and potential guaranty jeopardy.

dependencies.strength_justification: Timely upfront fee payment is a condition to maintaining the guaranty that underlies ongoing service fee remittances.
dependencies.rationale: BR_1_002 cancels guaranty if upfront fee is not paid within 90 days of approval for maturities > 12 months. BR_1_005 presumes an SBA-guaranteed 7(a) loan exists for ongoing fee remittances and correct configuration.
dependencies.impact: Nonpayment under BR_1_002 can lead to guaranty cancellation and downstream reporting/remittance errors, plus purchase/repair risk.
examples: Lender assumes borrower will reimburse fee later and delays payment beyond 90 days; guaranty cancelled.
SBA does not send reminders; lender misses payment and later continues servicing as if guaranteed.
